Official name: Guidance on Application of the Fair Housing Act to the Advertising of Housing, Credit, and Other Real Estate-Related Transactions through Digital Platforms
Casual name: Guidance on Application of the Fair Housing Act to the Advertising of Housing, Credit, and Other Real Estate-Related Transactions through Digital Platforms
Authority: Department of Housing and Urban Development
Collections: U.S. regulations, executive orders, and agency policies
Short summary: Prohibits discriminatory use of AI in housing-related ads under the Fair Housing Act. Addresses potential violations via audience targeting, custom audiences, and algorithmic delivery. Recommends advertisers and platforms audit practices, avoid proxies for protected characteristics, and ensure equitable ad delivery.
Long summary:
Explains how the Fair Housing Act applies to housing-related advertising via AI and algorithmic systems on digital platforms.

Prohibits discriminatory advertising practices based on protected characteristics such as race, color, religion, sex, national origin, familial status, or disability.

Warns that audience categorization and algorithmic delivery tools can unintentionally lead to discrimination by excluding or targeting groups based on protected characteristics.

Requires advertisers and platforms to avoid using AI tools that segment audiences by protected characteristics or proxies like geographic location.

Urges ad platforms to disable certain targeting functions for housing ads and conduct regular audits to ensure compliance with the Act.

Recommends that advertisers and platforms manage discriminatory risks by utilizing separate processes for housing-related ads and monitoring ad delivery outcomes for bias.

Advises documenting and publicly sharing information on ad targeting functions to ensure transparency and accountability.

Official name: Increasing AI Capacity Across the Federal Government: AI Talent Surge Progress and Recommendations
Casual name: Increasing AI Capacity Across the Federal Government: AI Talent Surge Progress and Recommendations
Collections: U.S. regulations, executive orders, and agency policies
Short summary: Implements Executive Order 14110, launching a National AI Talent Surge to recruit AI professionals for federal use, policy development, and R&D. Establishes expedited hiring authorities, training initiatives, and diverse recruitment efforts to strengthen the federal AI workforce and infrastructure.
Long summary:
Mandates Federal agencies strengthen AI safety, security, privacy, equity, civil rights, and innovation through Executive Order 14110, signed by President Biden.

Launches a National AI Talent Surge to recruit AI professionals into the Federal Government, enhancing AI regulatory and policy capacity, government use of AI, and AI R&D ecosystem.

Facilitates AI hiring with Direct Hire Authority and Excepted Service Appointments granted by the Office of Personnel Management (OPM).

Establishes Chief AI Officers and enhances AI competencies with training programs for the Federal workforce.

Recommends funding increases for AI talent, institutionalizing tech talent programs, improving USAJOBS, and piloting skills-based hiring.

Proposes an AI scholarship-for-service program and public service cultivation within the tech ecosystem.

Encourages global AI talent engagement and provides guidance for improving Federal hiring experiences.

Official name: USDA Framework for State, Local, Tribal, and Territorial Use of Artificial Intelligence for Public Benefit Administration
Casual name: USDA Framework for State, Local, Tribal, and Territorial Use of Artificial Intelligence for Public Benefit Administration
Collections: U.S. regulations, executive orders, and agency policies
Short summary: Requires USDA to guide state, local, tribal, and territorial agencies on AI use in nutrition benefits, ensuring accessibility, compliance with merit systems, notification to USDA, human review options, auditability, and equitable outcomes. Encourages responsible AI innovation while protecting rights and safety.
Long summary:
The framework mandates the USDA to guide states, localities, tribes, and territories in the responsible use of AI for administering USDA nutrition benefits, as per Executive Order 14110.

Ensures AI systems maximize access for eligible recipients and comply with merit system requirements.

Requires notification to the Secretary in cases of reliance on AI, allowing appeals to human reviewers and ensuring auditability of AI decisions.

Mandates equitable outcomes from AI systems and prohibits AI from directly making adverse decisions in benefits administration.

Advocates for transparency and accountability, providing opt-out options from AI systems.

Encourages responsible innovation, requiring AI usage only with appropriate training and governance.

Requires SLTT agencies to report AI usage and its impact, with ongoing monitoring to mitigate risks and biases.

Stresses collaboration with tribes to respect data sovereignty.

Official name: Federal Register Doc. No. 2024-01580. Taking Additional Steps To Address the National Emergency With Respect to Significant Malicious Cyber-Enabled Activities
Casual name: AI Cloud KYC Proposed Rule (Department of Commerce)
Collections: U.S. regulations, executive orders, and agency policies
Short summary: Require U.S. IaaS providers to implement Customer Identification Programs (CIP) for foreign customers to prevent malicious use of AI models. Report AI training activities that could aid harmful cyber operations. Impose penalties for non-compliance or false reporting.
Long summary:
Requires U.S. Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS) providers to implement a Customer Identification Program (CIP) for verifying foreign customers' identities and maintaining relevant records to prevent malicious cyber activities. 

Obligates foreign resellers to adhere to similar CIP standards and mandates U.S. IaaS providers to report on these. 

Obligates reporting on transactions involving training of large AI models that could be misused for cyber activities. 

Authorizes the Secretary of Commerce to enforce special measures against foreign jurisdictions or persons using U.S. IaaS for cyber threats. 

Ensures compliance by conducting assessments and requiring annual certification of CIPs by IaaS providers. 

Institutes penalties for non-compliance, including civil and criminal liabilities. 

Mandates reporting to the Department of Commerce for violations or suspicious activities associated with AI model training.

Official name:
Government-wide Hiring Authorities for Advancing Federal Government
Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI)
Casual name:
Government-wide Hiring Authorities for Advancing Federal Government
Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI)
Collections: U.S. regulations, executive orders, and agency policies
Short summary: Authorizes government-wide direct hire authority for AI-related roles to enhance federal AI capabilities, effective until December 31, 2028. Allows temporary excepted service appointments for implementing AI directives under Executive Order 14110. Requires quarterly reporting of hires made under this authority.
Long summary:
Mandates the U.S. Office of Personnel Management (OPM) to conduct an evidence-based review for hiring and workplace flexibility for AI and data-science roles within 60 days of the Executive Order (EO) 14110.

Authorizes government-wide direct hire authority for specific AI-related positions, effective immediately through December 31, 2028, to address critical hiring needs in AI capabilities.

Permits use of excepted service Schedule A appointments for temporary staffing to support the implementation of EO 14110, with appointments valid for up to two years.

Requires agencies to submit quarterly reports detailing hires made under this direct hire authority, including job announcements and number of hires.

Stipulates agencies must adhere to veterans’ preference and public notice requirements when filling positions under these authorities.

Allows OPM to request information and assess the continued need for these authorities, with the possibility of modification or termination.

Official name: HHS Public Benefits and AI
Casual name: HHS Public Benefits and AI
Authority: Department of Health and Human Services
Collections: U.S. regulations, executive orders, and agency policies
Short summary: Encourages state and local governments to use AI responsibly in public benefits administration. Recommends risk assessment, fairness, transparency, human oversight, and privacy protection. Emphasizes equity, accountability, and civil rights compliance. Supports developing frameworks and resources for AI governance and innovation.
Long summary:
Requires the United States Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) to promote responsible AI use in public benefits administration by State, Local, Tribal, and Territorial Governments (STLTs).

Encourages STLTs to align with the principles of the National Institute of Standards and Technology AI Risk Management Framework and the Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights.

Recommends STLTs assess AI systems for equitable outcomes, notify recipients about AI use, and ensure human oversight and appeal processes.

Supports STLTs in developing AI policies and practices through guidance, technical assistance, and resources.

Urges STLTs to focus on safeguarding privacy, civil rights, and safety, and to provide opt-out options for AI systems.

Emphasizes the need for transparency, public engagement, and governance to manage AI-associated risks.

Directs HHS to work with STLTs and other agencies to ensure AI systems in public benefits programs are safe, equitable, and efficient.

Official name: Advancing Governance, Innovation, and Risk Management for Agency Use of Artificial Intelligence
Casual name: Advancing Governance, Innovation, and Risk Management for Agency Use of Artificial Intelligence
Collections: U.S. regulations, executive orders, and agency policies;Editors' Picks
Short summary: Mandates appointment of a Chief AI Officer within 60 days. Requires development of AI strategies. Imposes risk management requirements. Obligates annual AI use case inventory. Encourages removing innovation barriers while ensuring compliance with federal policies.
Long summary:
Requires federal agencies to appoint a Chief AI Officer (CAIO) within 60 days to enhance AI governance and innovation while managing risks, particularly those affecting public rights and safety.
Requires agencies to develop AI strategies to responsibly adopt AI, including generative AI, and to facilitate sharing and reuse of AI models, code, and data.

Imposes new requirements for agencies to manage AI-related risks, including following minimum risk management practices for AI impacting public rights and safety. Obligates agencies to annually inventory AI use cases, identifying those impacting rights and safety, and to report on associated risks and mitigation strategies. Encourages removing barriers to AI innovation related to IT infrastructure, data, and workforce while ensuring compliance with existing federal policies and risk management practices.

Official name: NSTC/OSTP Framework for Nucleic Acid Synthesis Screening
Casual name: NSTC/OSTP Framework for Nucleic Acid Synthesis Screening
Authority: Office of Science and Technology Policy
Collections: U.S. regulations, executive orders, and agency policies
Short summary: Requires providers and manufacturers to implement screening for synthetic nucleic acids and equipment, identify sequences of concern, verify customer legitimacy, report suspicious orders, retain records, and enhance cybersecurity. Mandates adherence for federally funded research, with updates based on technological advances.
Long summary:
Mandates the federal government to mitigate misuse risks of synthetic nucleic acids by establishing a procurement screening framework.

Requires synthetic nucleic acid providers and equipment manufacturers to implement comprehensive screening measures for sequences of concern (SOCs) and verify customer legitimacy.

Directs the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) to collaborate with industry to refine technical specifications for effective screening.

Obliges federal funding agencies to ensure compliance with the framework for research funded by federal life sciences grants.

Instructs providers and manufacturers to report suspicious purchase orders to relevant authorities and retain records for at least three years.

Imposes cybersecurity measures to protect sensitive data and customer information, following specific security guidelines.

Anticipates framework updates based on technological advancements and encourages the use of independent audits for compliance verification.

Official name: Pay Flexibility, Incentive Pay, and Leave and Workforce Flexibility Programs for Artificial Intelligence (AI), AI-enabling, and Other Key Technical Employees (OPM)
Casual name: Pay Flexibility, Incentive Pay, and Leave and Workforce Flexibility Programs for Artificial Intelligence (AI), AI-enabling, and Other Key Technical Employees (OPM)
Collections: U.S. regulations, executive orders, and agency policies
Short summary: Allows agencies to use various pay and workforce flexibilities to recruit and retain AI talent without OPM approval. Permits recruitment, relocation, and retention incentives, superior qualifications pay, and alternative work schedules. Authorizes telework, remote work, and student loan repayment.
Long summary:
Grants agencies discretionary authority to use various pay flexibilities, incentive pay, and leave and workforce flexibility programs to recruit, relocate, and retain AI and key technical employees.

Allows recruitment, relocation, and retention incentives up to 25% of basic pay, and provides for waivers to increase this limit to 50% based on critical needs.

Enables agencies to repay student loans as an incentive, with limits of $10,000 annually and $60,000 total per employee.

Permits setting higher pay rates for employees with superior qualifications or special needs, and requests OPM approval for special rates and critical position pay.

Authorizes alternative work schedules, telework, and remote work to enhance recruitment and retention.

Provides service credit for non-Federal work experience towards higher annual leave accrual rates.

Encourages collaboration with agency officials to design tailored recruitment and retention strategies utilizing available human resources tools.

Official name: Inventorship Guidance for AI-Assisted Inventions
Casual name: Inventorship Guidance for AI-Assisted Inventions
Collections: U.S. regulations, executive orders, and agency policies
Short summary: Requires inventors and joint inventors on U.S. patents to be natural persons, excluding AI systems as inventors. Allows AI-assisted inventions to be patented if natural persons significantly contribute. Emphasizes compliance with inventorship requirements under U.S. patent statutes.
Long summary:
Mandates that inventors named on U.S. patents and applications must be natural persons, reaffirming that AI systems cannot be recognized as inventors.

Clarifies that AI-assisted inventions are not unpatentable due to improper inventorship, provided a natural person significantly contributed to the invention's conception.

Requires that patent applications for AI-assisted inventions name natural persons who meet the Pannu factors as inventors, ensuring each claim has a human contributor.

Outlines the duty of disclosure and reasonable inquiry, emphasizing the importance of factual inventorship claims and compliance with regulations.

Establishes guidelines for correcting inventorship errors and emphasizes that AI systems cannot hold inventorship rights.

Applies guidance to design and plant patents, affirming that significant human contribution is necessary for patent eligibility.

Prohibits naming AI systems as inventors in priority claims for U.S. patents and applications, ensuring compliance with U.S. inventorship requirements.

Official name: Regulations for the Promotion of the Development of the Artificial Intelligence Industry in Shanghai Municipality
Casual name: Regulations for the Promotion of the Development of the AI Industry in Shanghai Municipality
Short summary: Promotes high-quality AI industry development, integration with economy and urban governance, and international influence. Requires municipal governments to lead AI strategies and innovation. Supports infrastructure, algorithm innovation, data usage, and ethical AI practices. Encourages AI application in various sectors and strengthens governance and security.
Long summary:
Promotes the high-quality development of the AI industry in the municipality by integrating AI with the economy, everyday life, and urban governance.

Defines AI as systems using computers to simulate and expand human intelligence and specifies the AI industry including software and hardware development and application.

Applies regulations to AI-related activities such as innovation, development, and governance within the municipality.

Mandates the Municipal People's Government to lead AI development and integrate AI plans into national economic plans.

Establishes a Municipal Artificial Intelligence Strategy Advisory Expert Committee for strategic advice on AI industry development.

Encourages innovation by citizens and organizations and explores a negative checklist for AI activities.

Supports international cooperation and participation in AI governance.

Encourages AI infrastructure development, including compute resources and big data centers.

Supports algorithm innovation and promotes the trading of algorithm models.

Strengthens AI talent cultivation and promotes AI technology standardization and intellectual property protection.

Implements regulatory measures for AI products, emphasizing ethics and security compliance.

Official name: National New Generation AI Standards System Construction Guide
Casual name: National New Generation AI Standards System Construction Guide
Short summary: Establishes a comprehensive AI standards system emphasizing basic, security, ethical, and industry-specific standards. Targets development in areas like machine learning, natural language processing, smart vehicles, and healthcare. Prioritizes international cooperation, security, and ethical considerations by 2023.
Long summary:
Establishes a comprehensive framework for developing a national AI standards system, emphasizing the integration of ethical, security, and privacy standards.

Aims to clarify top-level design and develop key standards by 2023, focusing on data, algorithms, systems, and industry-specific applications.

Outlines an AI standards architecture, comprising eight categories: basic standards, supporting technologies, software/hardware platforms, general/niche technologies, products/services, industry applications, and security/ethics.

Emphasizes the need for international cooperation and alignment with global standards to ensure high-quality AI development.

Prioritizes the development of standards for key technologies such as machine learning, natural language processing, computer vision, and quantum computing.

Addresses AI applications across various sectors, including healthcare, transportation, finance, and smart cities, ensuring cross-industry standardization.

Focuses on security and ethics, proposing standards for AI data, algorithms, models, and application security, alongside ethical guidelines for impactful AI use.

Official name: Opinion on Strengthening Science and Technology Ethics Governance
Casual name: Opinion on Strengthening Science and Technology Ethics Governance
Short summary: Strengthens governance and ethics in AI by establishing legal systems, ethical principles, and review mechanisms. Requires risk assessment, transparency, and compliance with national and international standards. Promotes education and awareness, ensuring responsible AI innovation and preventing ethical violations. Enhances global cooperation.
Long summary:
Mandates embedding ethical requirements throughout scientific and technological activities, including AI, ensuring responsible innovation.

Requires adherence to rule of law in ethics governance, constructing a legal system for AI ethics.

Promotes agile governance for AI, dynamically adjusting to ethical challenges and risks.

Emphasizes the importance of openness and cooperation in global AI ethics governance.

Directs institutions to establish ethics committees for AI and sensitive areas, ensuring compliance with ethical standards.

Urges development of norms and guidelines for AI ethics, enhancing transparency and stakeholder participation.

Establishes the National Committee for Ethics in Science and Technology to guide AI ethics governance.

Mandates strict review and regulation of AI ethics, including risk assessment and compliance with international standards.

Calls for monitoring and early-warning systems for ethical risks in AI, ensuring rapid response to challenges.

Requires education and training on AI ethics for scientific personnel, raising awareness and ensuring adherence to ethical standards.

Official name: Remarks by Ambassador Zhang Jun at the UN Security Council Briefing on Artificial Intelligence: Opportunities and Risks for International Peace and Security
Casual name: 2023 Remarks by Ambassador Zhang at the UNSC Briefing on AI
Short summary: Emphasizes the need for AI governance with principles prioritizing ethics, safety, fairness, openness, and peaceful use. Advocates for global cooperation, especially through the UN, to develop inclusive AI policies. Opposes technological hegemony and emphasizes China's commitment to responsible AI development and international collaboration.
Long summary:
Calls for adherence to ethical principles in AI development, emphasizing people-oriented AI and the need to establish ethical norms and regulatory systems tailored to national conditions.

Stresses safety and controllability, urging the international community to establish risk control mechanisms and accountability structures to prevent AI misuse and autonomous lethal technologies.

Advocates for fairness and inclusiveness, highlighting the importance of ensuring equal access to AI benefits for developing countries and opposing technological hegemony and exclusionary practices.

Promotes openness and inclusivity, encouraging international cooperation across disciplines, regions, and organizations under the UN framework to create a non-discriminatory environment for AI.

Emphasizes peaceful utilization of AI, underscoring the necessity of AI for sustainable development and responsible defense policies to prevent its misuse in military contexts.

Underlines China's commitment to international cooperation in AI governance, referencing past initiatives and ongoing efforts to establish a global framework for AI security governance.

Official name: Ethical Norms for the New Generation Artificial Intelligence
Casual name: Ethical Norms for the New Generation Artificial Intelligence
Short summary: Outlines six general guiding principles and 18 specific ethical requirements for fair, safe, and accountable AI development in China.
Long summary:
Outlines six general principles to guide ethical AI development in China, i.e. enhancing human well-being, fairness and justice, privacy and security, controllability, accountability, and improving ethical literacy.

Outlines 18 specific ethical requirements for AI development, which fall under categories of management and governance, R&D, supply, and usage.

Official name: Position Paper of the People's Republic of China on Strengthening Ethical Governance of Artificial Intelligence (AI)
Casual name: Position Paper of the People's Republic of China on Strengthening Ethical Governance of AI
Short summary: Calls for prioritizing AI ethics and establishing rules, norms, and accountability mechanisms. Emphasizes AI safety, reliability, and human control in R&D. Prohibits unlawful AI use and strengthens privacy protections. Promotes international cooperation and shared AI benefits while opposing exclusionary practices.
Long summary:
Calls for governments to prioritize AI ethics by establishing comprehensive rules, norms, and accountability mechanisms.

Emphasizes the need for AI ethical norms, laws, and policies to address potential risks and enhance AI security.

Urges governments to require R&D entities to integrate ethics throughout AI development, ensuring algorithm security, transparency, and data quality.

Prohibits the use of AI technologies that conflict with laws, regulations, and ethical standards; mandates quality monitoring and emergency measures for AI products.

Stresses safeguarding privacy and data security, advocating adherence to international norms for personal information handling.

Encourages public education on AI ethics and emphasizes the importance of public participation and awareness.

Promotes international cooperation to share AI benefits globally, participate in rule-making, and oppose exclusivity in technological development.

Advocates for international agreements on AI ethics, aiming to establish a widely accepted governance framework and standards respecting national practices.

Official name: The position paper submitted by the Chinese delegation to CCW 5th Review Conference
Casual name: The position paper submitted by the Chinese delegation to CCW 5th Review Conference
Short summary: Urges international consensus on defining lethal autonomous weapons systems (LAWS), emphasizing human involvement. Advocates for LAWS regulation under international humanitarian law and supports a legally binding protocol. Warns of LAWS' risks, urging caution to prevent civilian harm. Supports continued CCW discussions.
Long summary:
Urges extensive discussion on defining and scoping Lethal Autonomous Weapon Systems (LAWS), focusing on autonomy levels, human involvement, and distinctions between civilian and military uses.

Advocates for LAWS to be governed by international humanitarian laws, highlighting uncertainties in distinction, proportionality, and accountability with current systems.

Supports developing a legally binding protocol on LAWS, akin to the Protocol on Blinding Laser Weapons, to address legal gaps.

Warns that LAWS increase war's ease and frequency by lowering thresholds and costs, posing risks of indiscriminate harm to civilians.

Calls for caution in using LAWS, particularly to prevent indiscriminate civilian targeting.

Welcomes progress in LAWS discussions within the Convention on Certain Conventional Weapons (CCW) framework and supports continued expert meetings.

Official name: Measures of Beijing Municipality on Promoting the Innovative Development of Artificial General Intelligence
Casual name: Measures for Promoting Innovation and Development of Artificial General Intelligence in Beijing
Short summary: Promotes Beijing's AGI sector growth by boosting computing power, enhancing high-quality data supply, exploring large models, and fostering AGI applications in various sectors. Establishes regulatory policies for secure advancement, emphasizing innovation, risk prevention, security, and principles management in AI activities.
Long summary:
Mobilizes commercial computing resources to meet the requirements of the AGI sector, showcasing cooperation with cloud service providers to enhance access to diverse and high-quality computing power.

Promotes the construction of new computing facilities, including the Beijing AI Public Computing Power Center, to advance computing capabilities.

Establishes an integrated multi-cloud computing management platform to optimize computing power management and operations.

Collects high-quality training datasets, ensuring legal compliance and promoting the trading of data through legitimate channels.

Plans to set up data training bases and a crowdsourcing service platform for dataset annotation, enhancing data quality and collaboration.

Explores innovative algorithms for large models, supporting an open-source technical ecosystem and developing governance tools for data management.

Facilitates the practical application of AGI in various sectors, including government, medicine, finance, and urban governance.

Innovates regulatory policies, promoting inclusive and prudent supervision systems while strengthening network and data security.

Official name: UN General Assembly Resolution A/78/L.49 ("Seizing the opportunities of safe, secure and trustworthy artificial intelligence systems for sustainable development"
Casual name: UNGA Resolution A/78/L.49 ("Seizing the opportunities of safe, secure and trustworthy artificial intelligence systems for sustainable development"
Short summary: Resolves to bridge AI and digital divides and promote safe, secure, and trustworthy AI systems to achieve the Sustainable Development Goals. Encourages regulatory frameworks, capacity building, and international cooperation. Emphasizes human rights, data governance, and inclusive participation in AI governance.
Long summary:
Resolves to bridge AI and digital divides globally and promote safe, secure, and trustworthy AI systems to achieve Sustainable Development Goals.

Encourages Member States and stakeholders to support regulatory frameworks for responsible AI, fostering innovation and equitable access to benefits.

Calls for cooperation to assist developing countries in accessing AI benefits through capacity building, digital literacy, and infrastructure.

Emphasizes the protection of human rights throughout AI systems' life cycles and urges cessation of AI use that violates international human rights law.

Promotes inclusive governance of AI, addressing global challenges while considering developing countries' needs.

Recognizes data's importance in AI development and urges fair data governance to harness AI potential for sustainable development.

Encourages continued international cooperation, research, and mapping of AI impacts to inform effective governance.

Calls upon UN entities to assess AI challenges and assist developing countries in capacity building for achieving Sustainable Development Goals.

Official name: Global AI Governance Initiative
Casual name: Global AI Governance Initiative
Short summary: Calls for global cooperation to enhance AI governance, prevent risks, and ensure equitable access. Opposes AI misuse, disinformation, and sovereignty violations. Promotes ethical principles, legal frameworks, and equal rights in AI development. Encourages international collaboration, especially supporting developing countries.
Long summary:
Calls on countries to enhance information exchange and technological cooperation for AI governance, focusing on security, reliability, and equity.

Opposes using AI for manipulating public opinion, spreading disinformation, or interfering in other nations' affairs.

Urges adherence to international laws and prevention of AI misuse by malicious actors.

Emphasizes global collaboration to make AI technologies open-source and accessible, resisting technological monopolies.

Promotes testing and assessment systems for AI based on risk levels, ensuring AI remains under human control.

Advocates for laws ensuring privacy and data security, opposing illegal data practices.

Stresses fairness and non-discrimination in AI development processes.

Calls for ethical principles and accountability mechanisms, addressing ethical concerns timely.

Encourages wide participation and consensus-based decision-making in AI governance.

Seeks increased representation of developing countries in global AI governance.

Supports UN discussions for an international AI governing institution.

Official name: Technical Documentation of National Technical Committee 260 on Cybersecurity of Standardization Administration of China: Basic Safety Requirements for Generative Artificial Intelligence Services
Casual name: Basic Safety Requirements for Generative Artificial Intelligence Services (Technical Documentation)
Short summary: Specifies safety requirements for generative AI services, focusing on corpus and model safety. Requires providers to conduct safety assessments and adhere to cybersecurity, data security, and personal information protection laws. Emphasizes preventing illegal content and ensuring model-generated content accuracy and reliability.
Long summary:
Specifies safety requirements for generative AI services in China, focusing on corpus safety, model safety, and safety measures.

Applies to service providers conducting safety assessments and the main oversight department for evaluating generative AI services' safety levels.

Mandates safety assessments and reporting, adherence to cybersecurity, data security, and personal information protection laws.

Requires corpus source safety management, verification, and traceability, including avoiding illegal content and ensuring intellectual property rights compliance.

Demands annotators follow strict training and rule adherence for data annotation and review.

Stipulates model safety, including foundation model registration, content safety monitoring, and ensuring generated content accuracy and reliability.

Outlines safety measures for service transparency, user data collection, and complaint handling.

Imposes rigorous safety assessment procedures and compliance for corpus, generated content, and question refusal rates.

Official name: Interim Measures for the Management of Generative Artificial Intelligence Services
Casual name: Interim Measures for the Management of Generative Artificial Intelligence Services
Short summary: Regulates generative AI services in mainland China, emphasizing legal compliance, societal ethics, and security. Encourages innovation while preventing discrimination and protecting personal information. Mandates providers to ensure content accuracy, transparency, and user privacy. Enforces penalties for non-compliance.
Long summary:
Establishes legal framework for the use and governance of generative AI services in mainland China, promoting innovation while ensuring security and compliance with national values.

Applies to public generative AI services generating text, images, audio, video, but excludes non-public research and development.

Mandates adherence to laws, ethical standards, and the protection of intellectual property, personal information, and societal values.

Encourages independent innovation and international cooperation in AI technology, emphasizing lawful data handling and quality improvement.

Requires providers to ensure information security, sign service agreements with users, and prevent misuse by minors and illegal activities.

Obligates providers to label AI-generated content, maintain service stability, and establish complaint mechanisms.

Imposes oversight by relevant state departments, requiring security assessments for AI with social mobilization potential.

Specifies penalties for non-compliance, including warnings, service suspension, and legal action for serious violations.

Takes effect on August 15, 2023, with provisions for foreign investment compliance.

Official name: Position Paper of the People’s Republic of China on Regulating Military Applications of Artificial Intelligence
Casual name: Position Paper of the People’s Republic of China on Regulating Military Applications of Artificial Intelligence
Short summary: Calls countries to regulate military AI applications, ensuring responsible development, compliance with international law, and prevention of arms races. Encourages international cooperation, emphasizes human oversight, and promotes ethical AI use to avoid misuse and maintain global strategic stability.
Long summary:
Calls for countries to regulate military applications of AI to prevent strategic miscalculation and maintain global stability.

Urges countries to ensure military AI is not used to start wars or pursue dominance, respecting sovereignty and territorial security.

Emphasizes compliance with international humanitarian law, minimizing collateral damage, and preventing misuse of AI weapons.

Requires human control over AI weapon systems, ensuring data security, and restricting military use of AI data.

Recommends enhancing self-restraint in AI R&D, with accountability mechanisms and operator training.

Stresses the necessity of tiered regulation and risk management to prevent premature use of risky technologies.

Advocates for multilateral dialogue and cooperation to establish a universal governance regime for military AI.

Encourages developed countries to aid developing nations in governance capacity, opposing ideological divides and ensuring equitable technological advancement.

Official name: Artificial Intelligence Security Standardization White Paper, Section 5 ("Recommendations for AI security standardization work")
Casual name: Artificial Intelligence Security Standardization White Paper, Section 5 ("Recommendations for AI security standardization work")
Short summary: Recommends coordinating AI security standards, emphasizing ethical principles, and accelerating standards development in key areas. Promotes AI security standards application, talent training, and international collaboration. Suggests high-risk AI early warning mechanisms and improving AI security supervision and supply chain management.
Long summary:
Launch AI security standardization work by coordinating a system of AI security standards, focusing on foundational and application-specific research.

Accelerate development of AI security standards in key areas, driven by urgent needs and safety incidents, to address security risks and ethical issues.

Promote the application of AI security standards through pilot mechanisms and collaboration between academia, industry, and research institutions.

Strengthen training for AI security standardization talent by establishing multi-level training programs and fostering collaboration among universities, research institutes, and enterprises.

Participate actively in international AI security standardization by aligning Chinese efforts with global achievements, enhancing China's influence, and facilitating international cooperation.

Establish an early warning mechanism for high-security-risk AI technologies and products, including a catalog of risks and management standards.

Improve AI security supervision by developing an index evaluation system, enhancing AI supply chain security management, and formulating supportive standards.

Official name: Memorandum on Advancing the United States’ Leadership in Artificial Intelligence; Harnessing Artificial Intelligence to Fulfill National Security Objectives; and Fostering the Safety, Security, and Trustworthiness of Artificial Intelligence
Casual name: National Security Memorandum on AI
Collections: U.S. regulations, executive orders, and agency policies;Editors' Picks
Short summary: Directs the U.S. government to lead safe AI development, enhance national security through AI, and foster global AI governance. Prioritizes AI safety, security, and talent acquisition. Establishes AI governance frameworks and partnerships to ensure responsible AI use and international collaboration.
Long summary:
Develop advanced AI computational infrastructure and adopt measures to attract top technical talent to work in the United States to ensure innovation and technological superiority in AI.

Instructs relevant agencies to protect AI intellectual property from foreign threats. 

Issue guidance on testing and evaluating frontier foundational AI systems to manage safety and security risks.

Directs national security institutions to harness AI to advance national security objectives through clear policies, strategies and coordination, while safeguarding human rights, civil liberties, and privacy.

Produce a strategy for the advancement of international AI governance norms in line with safe, secure, and trustworthy AI, and democratic values.

Requires covered agencies to appoint Chief AI Officer to managing risks and opportunities of AI

Requires the formation of an interagency National Security Coordination Group to harmonize polices related to AI in National Security

Official name: Framework to Advance AI Governance and Risk Management in National Security
Casual name: National Security Memorandum on AI: Governance and Risk Management Framework
Collections: U.S. regulations, executive orders, and agency policies;Editors' Picks
Short summary: Classifies prohibited and "high-impact" AI use cases based on risks to national security, human rights, and effect on Federal personnel. Defines minimum risk assessment standards, mandates monitoring mechanisms for high-impact AI and training guidelines for the development and use of AI systems.
Long summary:
Mandates agencies to identify prohibited and high-impact AI use cases, emphasizing risks to national security, human rights, and civil liberties. 

Restricts the use of AI whose output serves as the primary basis for decisions that could pose significant risks to human rights or national security, unless accompanied by human oversight.

Requires agencies to conduct risk assessments and implement minimum risk management practices for high-impact AI uses, ensuring human oversight and accountability.

Mandates annual inventories of high-impact AI use cases and establishes data management policies prioritizing AI systems.

Obligates agencies to appoint Chief AI Officers and establish AI Governance Boards to oversee AI activities and compliance. 

Stipulates standardized training for responsible AI use and development, ensuring accountability and documentation.

Allows waivers for certain risk management practices under strict conditions and oversight.

Ensures transparency and public trust through periodic reporting on AI oversight activities, with unclassified information made publicly available where possible.

Official name: White Paper on Artificial Intelligence Standardization 2021
Casual name: White Paper on Artificial Intelligence Standardization 2021
Short summary: Promotes the coordination of AI standardization across various fields. Develops key AI standards with a focus on applications like smart transportation. Encourages pilot demonstrations to boost industrial service capabilities. Supports international collaboration to enhance China's influence in AI standards.
Long summary:
Improve the working mechanism to promote AI standardization through the National Information Technology Standardization Technical Committee’s AI Technical Subcommittee.

Develop key AI standards based on principles such as “advantages first, maturity first,” focusing on performance benchmarks and hardware virtual interfaces.

Carry out pilot demonstrations to strengthen AI standardization promotion by selecting national innovation zones for standard application demonstrations.

Create de facto standards to enhance industry competitiveness by promoting AI standards through large-scale applications and integrating them into major projects and open-source communities.

Strengthen international exchanges by connecting with organizations like ISO/IEC JTC 1, participating in international AI standards formulation, and hosting AI-related conferences to promote Chinese contributions globally.

Official name: The Global Security Initiative Concept Paper
Casual name: The Global Security Initiative Concept Paper
Short summary: Strengthens international AI security governance and risk management. Promotes dialogue on military applications and ethical governance. Advocates for international AI security mechanisms, governance frameworks, standards, and norms with broad participation and consensus.
Long summary:
Strengthens international security governance on artificial intelligence (AI) and other emerging technologies, and prevents and manages potential security risks.

Issues position papers on regulating military applications and strengthening ethical governance of AI.

Stands ready to strengthen communication and exchange with the international community on AI security governance.

Promotes the establishment of an international mechanism for AI governance with broad participation.

Develops governance frameworks, standards, and norms for AI based on extensive consensus.

Encourages responsible bioscience research and adherence to biosecurity guidelines.

Deepens international cooperation in information security, including data security initiatives.

Enhances studies on the impact of emerging technologies on global counter-terrorism efforts.

Calls for the formulation of global rules on digital governance that reflect all parties' interests.

Official name: Idaho House Bill 575
Casual name: Idaho HB 575 (Disclosing Explicit Synthetic Media)
Short summary: Criminalizes disclosing or threatening to disclose explicit synthetic media without consent if it causes distress, is intended to harass, or is used for blackmail. Defines terms, provides penalties, and exemptions. Excludes certain services from liability unless they aid disclosure of explicit synthetic material.
Long summary:
Establishes the crime of disclosing or threatening to disclose explicit synthetic media without consent, intending to cause distress or obtain valuables, punishable as a misdemeanor or felony for repeat offenders.

Defines "explicit synthetic media" as AI-altered images or videos depicting sexual conduct involving identifiable individuals without their engagement.

Exempts disclosures for lawful purposes, such as criminal investigations, public interest reporting, scientific research, or legal proceedings.

Excludes liability for interactive computer services and telecommunications providers for third-party content, unless they intentionally aid disclosures.

Declares provisions severable, ensuring unaffected sections remain valid if parts are invalidated.

Declares an emergency and sets the effective date for July 1, 2024.

Official name: North Carolina House Bill 591
Casual name: North Carolina HB 591 (Modernize Sex Crimes)
Short summary: Defines "image" to include AI-modified depictions, criminalizing sexual extortion involving such images. Criminalizes creating or modifying visual depictions of minors using AI under sexual exploitation offenses. Clarifies disclosure offenses involving AI-altered private images, making violations a felony or misdemeanor.
Long summary:
Amends offenses related to sexual extortion by making it a criminal act to use threats involving private images, including those modified by AI, to coerce victims, punishable as felonies depending on the circumstances.

Enhances definitions related to minors in sexual content, including AI-modified images, and escalates penalties for various degrees of exploitation, employing AI tools.

Mandates reporting to authorities if AI technology is used to distribute unlawful material depicting minors, extending jurisdiction over such offenses.

Incorporates AI-generated images into existing laws against the unauthorized disclosure of private images, classifying violations as felonies and establishing a civil cause of action.

Updates sex offender registration guidelines, emphasizing offenses involving digital or AI-enhanced sexual exploits of minors.

Sets effective date for enforcement of these provisions as December 1, 2024.

Official name: Maryland House Bill 622
Casual name: Maryland HB 622 (Industry 4.0 Technology Grant Program)
Short summary: Establishes a grant program for small and medium-sized manufacturers to adopt Industry 4.0 technologies, including AI. Allocates grants competitively, requiring matching funds. Creates a special fund for grants. Mandates annual budget appropriations to support the program until 2028.
Long summary:
Establishes the Industry 4.0 Technology Grant Program in Maryland's Department of Commerce to support small and medium-sized manufacturers in implementing advanced technologies, including artificial intelligence, to improve productivity and competitiveness.

Creates a special, nonlapsing Industry 4.0 Technology Grant Fund to provide financial support, with grants ranging from $25,000 to $500,000, requiring matching funds based on company size.

Reserves 20% of funds for manufacturers with 50 or fewer employees, allowing unused reserves to be reallocated to larger companies.

Requires grant recipients to report on fund usage within one year and mandates potential repayment if conditions are unmet.

Mandates the Governor to allocate $1,000,000 annually from 2024 to 2028 to the Fund.

Directs the Secretary to establish regulations for program implementation and commence the Act on October 1, 2023.

Official name: Oklahoma H 3642
Casual name: Oklahoma H 3642 (Oklahoma Law on Obscenity and Child Pornography)
Short summary: Defines "child pornography" to include computergenerated images and establishes severe penalties for production and possession. Requires registration as a sex offender for possession of 100+ depictions. Effective November 1, 2024, the act targets explicit computer-generated images involving minors.
Long summary:
Amends Oklahoma laws to enhance penalties for offenses involving child pornography, specifically targeting AI-generated images. 

Defines "child pornography" to include computer-generated images and altered images that depict minors in sexually explicit conduct, regardless of whether the image is real or synthetic. 

Establishes that possessing, distributing, or creating such images, including those utilizing AI technology, is a felony with penalties up to 20 years imprisonment and a $25,000 fine. 

Specifies that possession of 100 or more images constitutes aggravated possession, punishable by life imprisonment and a $10,000 fine, with required sex offender registration.  

Clarifies that AI-generated and computer-altered images count as independent offenses. 

Mandates that this act becomes effective on November 1, 2024.

Official name: Delaware House Bill 333
Casual name: Delaware  H 333  (Artificial Intelligence Commission )
Short summary: Establishes the Delaware AI Commission to recommend AI-related legislative and executive actions, develop guidelines, encourage AI use in agencies, and ensure AI safety and rights compliance. Requires an annual report and conducts a Generative AI inventory. Expires in 10 years.
Long summary:
Establishes the Delaware Artificial Intelligence (AI) Commission to make legislative and executive recommendations on AI.

Mandates the development of statewide AI processes, principles, and guidelines for government agencies.

Encourages the use of AI in agencies to improve service delivery and ensures AI usage does not violate individual rights.

Requires an inventory of Generative AI use within state agencies to identify high-risk areas.

Comprises a 19-member commission, with appointments from state officials, academia, and the private sector.

Requires the commission to submit an annual report on activities, goals, and policy recommendations.

Places the commission within the Department of Technology and Information, with staffing support from the Chief Information Officer.

Sets an expiration of the Act 10 years after enactment unless extended by the General Assembly.

Official name: Alabama House Bill 172
Casual name: Alabama HB 172 (Use of Materially Deceptive Media in Elections)
Short summary: Criminalizes distributing AI-generated materially deceptive media to influence elections. Exempts media with clear disclaimers. Permits injunctive relief by affected parties. Provides exceptions for certain media outlets and satire. Effective October 1, 2024, with penalties for second offenses.
Long summary:
Prohibits the distribution of materially deceptive media, produced by AI, within 90 days before an election if intended to harm a candidate's reputation or influence voting patterns.

Defines "materially deceptive media" as AI-generated content falsely depicting individuals in actions or speech they did not partake in.

Allows exceptions if disclaimers clearly state the media's manipulation and provide source citations for edited content.

Classifies violations as Class A misdemeanors, with repeat offenses within five years as Class D felonies.

Permits the Attorney General, depicted individuals, candidates, and voter-representing entities to seek permanent injunctive relief against violators.

Excludes distributors without knowledge of deception or intent to harm from violations, including media entities distributing content as part of legitimate news coverage.

Protects satire and parody, provided they don't rely on AI for impersonation.

Effective October 1, 2024.

Official name: New York Senate Bill 9678
Casual name: New York SB 9678 (Materially Deceptive Media in Political Communications)
Short summary: Amends election law to require disclosure of materially deceptive media in political communications. Exempts satire, parody, and certain news reporting. Allows candidates to seek legal remedies if their likeness is used deceptively. Establishes burden of proof for plaintiffs.
Long summary:
Amends the election law to require disclosure for political communications using materially deceptive media.

Mandates visual disclosures to be legibly printed, stating "This (image, video, or audio) has been manipulated."

Requires audio disclosures to be stated clearly at the beginning, end, and periodically for messages longer than two minutes.

Exempts satire, bona fide news reporting with clear disclosures, stations with consistent disclaimer requirements, and platforms disseminating third-party content in good faith.

Permits candidates depicted in deceptive media to seek legal relief, including court costs, attorney fees, and injunctions against unauthorized dissemination.

Grants courts authority to provide preliminary relief to plaintiffs likely to succeed in proving violations in upcoming elections.

Places the burden of proof on plaintiffs to establish use of deceptive media by clear and convincing evidence.

Clarifies that federal law takes precedence over state requirements for media labeling in political communications.

Official name: Arizona 2024 Senate Bill 1359
Casual name: Arizona SB 1359 (Deepfakes in Election Communications)
Short summary: Prohibits creating and distributing deceptive deepfakes of election candidates without clear AI-generated content disclosure within 90 days before elections. Exempts satire, parody, and interactive computer services. Imposes civil penalties for non-disclosure violations. Defines key terms including "creator" and "synthetic media."
Long summary:
Prohibits the creation and distribution of deceptive and fraudulent deepfakes of election candidates within ninety days before an election unless there is a clear disclosure that the content is generated by artificial intelligence.

Exempts satire, parody, and interactive computer services from this prohibition.

Imposes a civil penalty for each day a deceptive deepfake is distributed without the required disclosure.

Defines a "creator" as a person using AI or digital technology to generate synthetic media, excluding technology providers.

Clarifies "deceptive and fraudulent deepfake" as synthetic media intended to mislead people about a candidate's actions or statements.

Defines "synthetic media" as digitally manipulated images, audio, or video to create realistic but false impressions.

Official name: Colorado House Bill 1147
Casual name: Colorado HB 1147 (Candidate Election Deepfake Disclosures)
Short summary: Prohibits distributing AI-generated deepfakes about candidates close to elections without disclosures. Requires clear notifications in such communications. Allows candidates to seek legal remedies for unauthorized deepfakes. Effective July 1, 2024, with enforcement by the Secretary of State.
Long summary:
Prohibits the distribution of deepfakes concerning candidates for elective office without a clear disclosure, within 60 days before a primary or 90 days before a general election.

Requires a disclosure stating the content has been falsified if a deepfake is distributed.

Mandates that disclosures in visual and audio communications meet specific size and clarity standards.

Allows candidates affected by deepfakes to seek injunctive relief, damages, and other remedies against violators.

Exempts bona fide news, satire, and parody from the prohibition, as well as certain media and technology providers.

Obligates the Secretary of State to promulgate rules to enforce these provisions.

Permits civil penalties and administrative complaints for violations.

Establishes the act's effective date as July 1, 2024, with applicability to communications distributed thereafter.

Official name: Virginia Senate Bill 487
Casual name: Virginia SB 487 (Use of Artificial Intelligence by Public Bodies)
Short summary: Requires JCOTS to analyze AI use by public bodies, focusing on procurement, implementation, and discrimination prevention. Instructs JCOTS to assess creating a Commission on AI to advise on related issues. Mandates JCOTS's report submission to legislative committees by December 1, 2024.
Long summary:
Mandates the Joint Commission on Technology and Science (JCOTS) to conduct an analysis of artificial intelligence use by public bodies in Virginia.

Requires JCOTS analysis to include examination public bodies' policies & procedures on AI procurement, implementation, and assessment of AI systems. 

Requires JCOTS analysis to also ensure public bodies continually review the impact of AI systems to avoid discrimination and unlawful outcomes against an individual or a group.

Mandates consideration of establishing a Commission on Artificial Intelligence to advise the General Assembly on AI-related issues.

Requires JCOTS to evaluate the composition and duties of this commission based on specified criteria.

Obligates JCOTS to report findings and recommendations to relevant House and Senate committees by December 1, 2024.

Official name: Louisiana Senate Bill 6
Casual name: Louisiana SB 6 (Unlawful Dissemination of AI Images)
Short summary: Enacts a law criminalizing the intentional dissemination or sale of AI-generated nude images of identifiable individuals without authorization. Exempts certain service providers from liability. Defines "another person" as a recognizable individual depicted in the images.
Long summary:
Enacts R.S. 14:73.14 to criminalize the unlawful dissemination or sale of AI-generated images depicting nudity without authorization, with intent to coerce, harass, or intimidate.

Defines the crime as involving images where the distributor knows they lack the authority to distribute or sell the images.

Exempts interactive computer services and providers of telecommunications or information services from liability if their systems are used for such acts by others.

Defines "another person" as someone whose recognizable image is used in creating AI-generated content meant to depict a real individual.

Official name: South Dakota 2024 Senate Bill 79
Casual name: South Dakota SB 79 (Child Pornography)
Short summary: Defines "computer-generated child pornography" to include AI-generated images indistinguishable from minors in sexual acts. Criminalizes possession, distribution, and manufacturing of such content, with severe felony penalties. Prohibits defenses based on consent or age mistake. Recognizes mitigating sentencing circumstances.
Long summary:
Defines "computer-generated child pornography" to include AI-generated depictions indistinguishable from actual minors engaged in prohibited sexual acts.

Criminalizes possession, distribution, and manufacture of child pornography, including AI-generated content, with varying felony classes and mandatory minimum sentences for first and subsequent offenses.

Prohibits defenses based on consent or mistaken age, but allows an affirmative defense if the depiction is solely of the person charged.

Mandates court assessments for convicted individuals and allows sentence mitigation in specific circumstances.

Requires prior out-of-state convictions for similar offenses to be considered in determining repeat offenses.

Specifies that certain materials with serious value are exempt from these prohibitions.

Incorporates offenses involving child pornography into the definitions of sex crimes, impacting registration and sentencing.

Addresses juvenile sexting, deeming it a misdemeanor if minors distribute or possess nude images or participate in prohibited acts using digital media.

Official name: New Hampshire House Bill 1432
Casual name: New Hampshire HB 1432 (Deepfakes)
Short summary: Prohibits knowingly creating or distributing deepfakes for harm. Establishes civil claims for damages from such use. Exempts bona fide news, satire, or parody. Disallows lobbyist registration for convicted offenders. Effective January 1, 2025.
Long summary:
Prohibits the creation, distribution, or presentation of deepfakes intended to embarrass, harass, entrap, defame, extort, or harm reputations or finances, categorizing such acts as a class B felony.

Establishes an additional offense and liability for legal expenses if the deepfake results in an individual's arrest.

Exempts interactive services, media entities engaged in bona fide news reporting, election communications with appropriate disclaimers, and satire or parody from these prohibitions.

Allows individuals to sue those who knowingly create harmful deepfakes for damages.

Mandates that the secretary of state reject lobbyist registrations from entities convicted of deepfake-related offenses.

Specifies that provisions are severable, ensuring unaffected provisions remain enforceable if any part is invalidated.

Sets the act's effective date as January 1, 2025, following its approval on July 19, 2024.

Official name: Tennessee Senate Bill 1711
Casual name: Tennessee SB 1711 (Use of AI Technology by Students)
Short summary: Requires Tennessee educational institutions, including universities and charter schools, to adopt AI use policies for instructional purposes by specified deadlines. Mandates policy implementation and reporting to relevant authorities, with noncompliance potentially leading to accountability measures.
Long summary:
Mandates the board of trustees of the University of Tennessee, the board of regents, and local governing boards of state universities to adopt AI usage policies for students, faculty, and staff by July 1, 2025.

Encourages public higher education institutions to collaborate on policy development and implementation for AI use.

Requires these boards to post policies online and submit them to respective education committees by July 1, 2025, with noncompliance leading to mandatory reporting to the joint government operations committee.

Obliges the adoption of AI policies for students, teachers, and staff in schools by the 2024-2025 school year, with annual compliance reports due to the Department of Education starting July 1, 2024.

Instructs public charter schools to implement AI policies by the 2024-2025 school year, with annual compliance reporting to the Department of Education beginning July 1, 2024.

Takes effect immediately upon becoming law, citing public welfare.

Official name: Utah House Bill 148
Casual name: Utah HB 148 (Artificial Pornographic Images Amendments)
Short summary: Amends Utah law to define and penalize the distribution of intimate and counterfeit intimate images, including AI-generated content. Establishes offenses for sexual extortion and unlawful distribution of such images, with exemptions for certain service providers. Effective May 1, 2024.
Long summary:
Amends definitions related to child and vulnerable adult sexual abuse material, including depictions created or modified using computer-generated images.

Defines "distribute" and "produce" in the context of such materials, emphasizing the inclusion of electronic and computer-generated methods.

Criminalizes the distribution of intimate images without consent, outlining penalties, including misdemeanors and felonies for repeat offenses.

Exempts certain entities like law enforcement, legal proceedings, and internet service providers from liability if they incidentally transmit or store such images without intentional engagement.

Addresses sexual extortion, detailing offenses involving coercion through threats to distribute intimate images, with enhanced penalties for aggravated offenses involving children or vulnerable adults.

Prohibits unlawful distribution of counterfeit intimate images, specifying penalties and exemptions for certain entities and situations.

Specifies that the act takes effect on May 1, 2024, providing ample time for stakeholders to comply with the new regulations.

Official name: Utah Senate Bill 131 Information Technology Act Amendments (2024)
Casual name: Utah SB 131 (Information Technology Act Amendments)
Short summary: Requires disclosures for synthetic media in political communications, including AI-generated content labels and tamper-evident digital provenance. Imposes penalties for violations. Considers AI use in crimes as an aggravating sentencing factor. Effective May 1, 2024; sentencing provisions effective July 1, 2024.
Long summary:
Enacts requirements for disclosure of synthetic media generated by AI in election-related communications, effective May 1, 2024. 

Mandates audio communications with synthetic audio to state, "Contains content generated by AI," at both the beginning and end. 

Requires visual communications with synthetic media to display phrases such as "This content generated by AI" throughout the video or image. 

Imposes a requirement for creators and sponsors to include tamper-evident digital content provenance in online advertisements, detailing the creators, edits, and AI usage.

Allows civil penalties up to $1,000 per violation.

Considers the intentional use of AI as an aggravating factor in criminal sentencing, effective July 1, 2024. 

Permits judges and parole boards to regard AI-assisted crime as an aggravating factor during sentencing. 

Maintains judicial discretion in sentencing decisions despite AI-related aggravating factors.

Official name: Michigan HB 5143
Casual name: Michigan HB 5143 (Campaign Finance Act)
Short summary: Defines artificial intelligence as machine-based systems capable of influencing environments via predictions, recommendations, or decisions using both machine and human inputs. Specifies conditions for the definition's applicability contingent on the enactment of related legislation.
Long summary:
Define "artificial intelligence" as a machine-based system capable of making predictions, recommendations, or decisions for human-defined objectives, affecting real or virtual environments, using both machine and human inputs.

Mandate AI systems to perceive environments, abstract perceptions into models through automated analysis, and use model inference for decision-making.

Condition enactment of this amendment on the passage of House Bill No. 5141.

Official name: Florida House Bill 919
Casual name: Florida HB 919 (AI Use in Political Advertising)
Short summary: Requires political ads using generative AI to include a disclaimer. Specifies disclaimer format based on media type. Imposes first-degree misdemeanor for non-compliance. Authorizes complaints to the Florida Elections Commission with expedited hearings. Effective July 1, 2024.
Long summary:
Establishes requirements for disclaimers on political advertisements using generative artificial intelligence in Florida.

Defines "generative artificial intelligence" as a system emulating input data to create synthetic content.

Requires advertisements with AI-generated content depicting actions that did not occur to include a prominent disclaimer stating "Created in whole or in part with the use of generative artificial intelligence (AI)."

Specifies format requirements for disclaimers across various media, including print, television, internet, and audio.

Imposes first-degree misdemeanor penalties for failure to include required disclaimers, in addition to other civil penalties.

Authorizes any person to file complaints with the Florida Elections Commission for violations of this requirement.

Mandates expedited hearings for such complaints.

Sets an effective date of July 1, 2024.
Tags: Harms: Detrimental content;Risk factors: Transparency;Strategies: Disclosure;Incentives: Criminal liability

Official name: Connecticut Substitute Senate Bill 1103
Casual name: Connecticut SB 1103 (AI Automated Decision Making)
Short summary: Requires state agencies to inventory and annually assess AI systems for discrimination risks, and mandates the development of AI governance policies by February 2024. Prohibits AI implementation without impact assessments and establishes a working group to recommend AI best practices and future legislation.
Long summary:
Mandates the Department of Administrative Services to inventory AI systems used by state agencies and make this information publicly available, identifying the system's vendor, capabilities, use, and impact assessment before implementation. 

Directs the Office of Policy and Management to establish AI policies and procedures for state agencies by February 1, 2024, governing procurement and implementation to avoid discrimination.

Requires ongoing assessments of AI systems to prevent unlawful discrimination or disparate impacts, in accordance with established procedures, and prohibits state agencies from implementing AI systems without such impact assessments.

Obligates the Judicial Department to inventory its AI systems, adopt AI governance policies, and continuously monitor system use to prevent discrimination.

Prohibits state contracts with businesses unless they require compliance with applicable AI statutory requirements.

Creates a working group to recommend ethical AI use in government, assess federal AI guidelines, and propose potential legislation modeled after the OSTP AI Bill of Rights, reporting findings by February 1, 2024.
