Official name: Tennessee House Bill 2163 (Sexual Exploitation of Children)
Casual name: Tennessee HB 2163 (Sexual Exploitation of Children)
Short summary: Amends the definition of computer-generated images to include those modified by AI for sexual exploitation offenses. Defines "artificial intelligence" and "generative artificial intelligence" related to content creation. Takes effect on July 1, 2024.
Long summary:
Amends Tennessee Code to include computer-generated images created, adapted, or modified by artificial intelligence under the definition of materials related to the sexual exploitation of children.

Defines "artificial intelligence" as a machine-based system capable of making predictions, recommendations, or decisions, influencing environments, or learning autonomously, developed in any context, including software or hardware, and solving tasks requiring human-like abilities.

Includes "generative artificial intelligence" as a system capable of creating new content or data, such as text, images, audio, or video when prompted.

Takes effect on July 1, 2024, for the purpose of public welfare.

Official name: Utah House Bill 249 (Utah Legal Personhood Amendments)
Casual name: Utah HB 249 (Utah Legal Personhood Amendments)
Short summary: Prohibits governmental entities in Utah from granting or recognizing legal personhood for artificial intelligence and other non-human entities. Enacts restrictions effective May 1, 2024, reinforcing the exclusive legal personhood of human beings.
Long summary:
Prohibits governmental entities in Utah from granting or recognizing legal personhood for artificial intelligence.

Restricts legal personhood to human beings and excludes inanimate objects, bodies of water, land, real property, atmospheric gases, astronomical objects, weather, plants, nonhuman animals, or any non-human taxonomic domain.

Applies these restrictions regardless of other legal provisions.

Enacts the bill with an effective date of May 1, 2024.

Official name: Washington House Bill 1999
Casual name: Washington HB 1999 (Fabricated Sexually Explicit Images)
Short summary: Defines digitization to include AI use in creating or altering images. Criminalizes disclosing fabricated intimate images, with AI-defined digitization as a key factor. Establishes defenses and penalties. Provides victims legal recourse for unauthorized AI-altered image disclosures.
Long summary:
Amends Washington state law to address the creation, possession, and dissemination of fabricated intimate or sexually explicit images using digitization, including artificial intelligence.

Defines "fabricated depiction" as images altered to show minors or adults engaging in sexual conduct that did not occur.

Criminalizes disclosing fabricated intimate images without consent, establishing it as a gross misdemeanor or a class C felony for repeat offenders.

Specifies exemptions for disclosures made in the public interest, such as legal, law enforcement, or medical contexts.

Clarifies that interactive computer services and telecommunications providers are not liable for third-party content.

Establishes civil remedies for victims of unauthorized disclosures, including economic, noneconomic, and punitive damages.

Mandates that fabricated depictions remain in law enforcement custody and are not duplicated during legal proceedings.

Specifies timelines for filing civil actions related to unauthorized disclosures, especially for minors.

Official name: Washington Senate Bill 5152
Casual name: Washington SB 5152 (Synthetic Media)
Short summary: Defines "synthetic media" in political campaigns as manipulated media creating false impressions. Permits candidates to seek legal relief against such media use. Requires disclosure of manipulation. Holds sponsors liable, not media, unless disclosure is altered. Encourages prompt court rulings.
Long summary:
Defines "synthetic media" as manipulated images, audio, or video that create a false impression of a real individual's appearance, speech, or conduct.

Allows candidates to seek injunctive relief or damages if their appearance, speech, or conduct is altered using synthetic media in electioneering communications.

Requires disclosure for synthetic media, stating, "This (image/video/audio) has been manipulated," with specific guidelines for visibility and audibility.

Places the burden of proof on plaintiffs to demonstrate synthetic media use by clear and convincing evidence.

Encourages courts to expedite proceedings related to synthetic media disputes.

Holds sponsors, but not mediums, liable unless mediums alter content or remove required disclosures.

Exempts interactive computer services from being treated as publishers of third-party content, except under specific conditions.

Mandates the public disclosure commission to adopt rules supporting this chapter without constituting violations under existing campaign finance laws.

Official name: New Mexico House Bill 182 of 2024(Campaign Reporting Act)
Casual name: New Mexico HB 182 (Campaign Reporting Act)
Short summary: Adds disclaimer requirements for AI-generated deceptive media in political ads. Criminalizes distribution without proper disclaimers, punishable by misdemeanor or felony. Allows enforcement by authorities and affected parties. Specifies disclaimer format for various media types.
Long summary:
Amends the Campaign Reporting Act to require disclaimers on advertisements containing materially deceptive media created through artificial intelligence (AI).

Defines "materially deceptive media" as content using AI to falsely depict individuals without consent.

Mandates disclaimers stating the content is AI-manipulated, specifying whether it is an image, video, or audio.

Prohibits the distribution of materially deceptive media within 90 days before an election if intended to mislead voters, unless accompanied by the required disclaimer.

Establishes penalties for violations: a misdemeanor for a first offense and a fourth-degree felony for subsequent offenses.

Allows enforcement and injunctive relief by the attorney general, district attorneys, affected individuals, candidates, or organizations representing voters' interests.

Excludes certain media types from disclaimer requirements and clarifies non-violation conditions for broadcasting stations and distribution platforms.

Official name: Wisconsin Act 2023-123 (Artificial Intelligence Content Disclosure)
Casual name: Wisconsin Act 2023-123 (AI Content Disclosure)
Short summary: Amends Wisconsin statutes to require political ads containing AI-generated media to disclose this fact audibly and visibly. Grants rule-making authority for implementation and imposes fines for non-compliance. Exempts broadcasters from liability unless responsible for the communication.
Long summary:
Amends the title of statute 11.1303 to include synthetic media in political contexts.

Defines "synthetic media" as audio or video content substantially produced by generative AI.

Mandates disclosure in audio communications with synthetic media, stating "Contains content generated by AI" at the start and end.

Requires video communications with synthetic media to display readable notices throughout, indicating AI-generated content.

Authorizes the commission to implement rules, including possible exceptions to disclosure requirements.

Specifies that compliance does not exempt from other civil or criminal liabilities.

Establishes penalties up to $1,000 for intentional violations of disclosure requirements.

Exempts broadcasters from liability unless they are responsible committees.

Clarifies that the act does not alter the rights or obligations under 47 USC 230.

Applies disclosure requirements to communications created on or after the act's effective date.

Official name: Maryland House Bill 1128
Casual name: Maryland  H 1128  (Talent Innovation Program and Fund )
Short summary: Establishes the Talent Innovation Program to enhance AI job training using innovative financing. Creates a nonlapsing Talent Innovation Fund to support the program, including AI sector initiatives. Allows fund investments and requires annual reporting to government officials starting in 2025.
Long summary:
Establishes the Talent Innovation Program in the Maryland Department of Labor to increase access to high-quality job training, with a focus on industries such as artificial intelligence.

Creates the Talent Innovation Fund as a special, nonlapsing fund to support the program, including job training and talent financing mechanisms.

Requires the program to conduct pilots, research, and stakeholder engagement to develop sustainable talent financing approaches.

Mandates an initial pilot program focused on the cybersecurity sector to expand regional cyber ranges for job training.

Authorizes the Department to provide grants and funding to various entities and individuals for training and support services.

Directs the State Treasurer to invest the fund's money, with interest earnings credited to the fund.

Compels the Department to adopt regulations and report annually on program activities and fund utilization to state leadership.

Official name: New Hampshire House Bill 1688
Casual name: New Hampshire HB 1688 (AI Use By State Agencies)
Short summary: Prohibits state agencies from using AI for unlawful discrimination, surveillance without a warrant, and malicious deepfakes. Requires human review of irreversible AI decisions. Mandates disclosure of AI-generated content. Obligates compliance and reporting within specified timelines.
Long summary:
Prohibits state agencies from using AI to classify individuals based on behavior, socio-economic status, or personal characteristics if it results in unlawful discrimination.

Bans real-time and remote biometric identification systems for public surveillance, except by law enforcement with a warrant.

Forbids the use of deepfakes for deceptive or malicious purposes.

Mandates human review of AI-generated, irreversible recommendations or decisions before implementation.

Requires disclosure for content generated by generative AI if not reviewed by a human.

Informs users when interacting with AI systems.

Obligates state agencies to review AI use for compliance within nine months and align procedures with the chapter and ethics code.

Demands removal of prohibited AI systems and ensures new AI deployments comply with regulations.

Directs the Department of Information Technology to report annually on AI systems used by state agencies, noting compliance and changes.

Specifies the act's effective date as July 1, 2024.

Official name: Idaho House Bill 664 (Freedom From AI-Rigged Elections Act)
Casual name: Idaho HB 664 (Freedom From AI-Rigged Elections Act)
Short summary: Prohibits publishing synthetic media in electioneering communications without disclosure. Allows candidates to seek legal relief and damages. Establishes liability for content providers and certain media. Requires clear evidence and encourages expedited court proceedings. Provides exceptions and severability.
Long summary:
Enacts the "Freedom From AI-Rigged (FAIR) Elections Act" to regulate synthetic media in electioneering communications.

Defines "synthetic media" as altered audio or video intended to mislead about real events or speech through advanced digital techniques.

Allows candidates misrepresented by synthetic media to seek injunctive relief and damages against the creators or distributors.

Requires disclosures on synthetic media: a visible text or audible notice stating the media is manipulated.

Places the burden on plaintiffs to prove synthetic media use by clear and convincing evidence.

Holds information content providers liable, not the medium, unless disclosures are altered or removed.

Encourages courts to resolve cases swiftly.

Declares provisions severable to maintain the act's enforceability if parts are invalidated.

Implements the act immediately upon passage due to an emergency declaration.

Official name: Hawaii Senate Bill 2284 (Relating To A Wildfire Forecast System For Hawaii)
Casual name: Hawaii SB 2284 (Relating To A Wildfire Forecast System For Hawaii)
Short summary: Requires the University of Hawaii to establish a two-year program using AI to develop a wildfire forecast system. Appropriates $1,000,000 for this purpose. Mandates reporting on program effectiveness and recommendations before the 2026 legislative session.
Long summary:
Mandates the University of Hawaii to establish a two-year program for developing a wildfire forecast system using artificial intelligence.

Requires the system to forecast wildfire risks statewide to enhance public safety and improve preparedness and risk mitigation measures.

Obligates the University of Hawaii to report on the program's effectiveness and provide findings, recommendations, and any proposed legislation to the legislature before the 2026 regular session.

Appropriates $1,000,000 from the state's general revenues for fiscal year 2024-2025 to fund the development of the wildfire forecast system.

Specifies that the Act will take effect on July 1, 2024.

Official name: Washington Senate Bill 5838 (2024)
Casual name: Washington S 5838 (Artificial Intelligence Task Force)
Short summary: Establishes a task force to assess AI use and trends, recommending guidelines and legislation. Requires examination of AI's impact on safety, privacy, and rights. Mandates reports by 2026. Defines AI-related terms. Task force expires June 30, 2027.
Long summary:
Establishes a task force in Washington State to assess current AI uses and trends and recommend guidelines and potential legislation for AI systems.

Composes the task force of an executive committee with appointed members from legislative bodies, the attorney general's office, technology experts, industry representatives, and community advocates.

Charges the task force with examining AI development and use, focusing on safety, privacy, and civil rights, and submitting recommendations to the legislature.

Mandates a literature review on AI public policy issues, analysis of existing legal protections, and proposals for ethical AI guidelines and high-risk AI use identification.

Tasks the office of the attorney general with administering the task force, retaining consultants, and supporting subcommittee formation.

Requires the task force to deliver reports to the governor and legislature, with a final report due by July 1, 2026.

Expires the task force on June 30, 2027.

Official name: Virginia 2024 SB 731
Casual name: Virginia SB 731 (Child Pornography)
Short summary: Amends the state's definition of child pornography to explicitly include visual material where "the minor depicted does not have to actually exist". Establishes mandatory prison sentences based on the age difference and repeat offenses, with substantial penalties for violations.
Long summary:
Defines "child pornography" to include sexually explicit visual material involving identifiable minors or minors who "do not have to actually exist," thus affecting both real and digitally created images.

Establishes penalties for production, solicitation, and financing of child pornography, escalating based on the minor’s age and the offender's age difference, with mandatory minimum sentences.

Permits the age of the depicted person to be inferred by the visual material's "text, title or appearance," confirming that proof of the subject's actual age is not required for prosecution.

Allows for venue of prosecution in the jurisdiction where the unlawful act occurs, the offender resides, or where the sexually explicit visual material is produced, stored, or possessed.

Notes that the provisions of the act may result in a net increase in periods of imprisonment, requiring the Virginia Criminal Sentencing Commission to assign a minimum fiscal impact of $50,000 for adult correctional facilities due to an unknown appropriation amount.

Official name: Colorado House Bill 24-1468
Casual name: Colorado HB 1468 (Artificial Intelligence Impact Task Force)
Short summary: Creates the Artificial Intelligence Impact Task Force to develop policy recommendations against AI system risks and algorithmic discrimination affecting consumers and workers. Requires diverse membership and adherence to transparency standards. Mandates a report by February 2025 to the legislature and governor; task force sunsets in 2027.
Long summary:
Establishes the Artificial Intelligence Impact Task Force in Colorado to develop policy recommendations for protecting consumers and workers from harms posed by AI systems and automated decision systems.

Defines key terms, including "artificial intelligence system," "automated decision system," and "algorithmic discrimination."

Requires the task force to consider transparency, disclosure, and protection measures against algorithmic discrimination, and to recommend best practices for the ethical and equitable use of AI.

Mandates the task force to include diverse representation and expertise in AI and related fields, with a focus on communities that have been historically impacted by algorithmic discrimination.

Obligates the task force to convene by September 1, 2024, and submit a report of findings and recommendations to the legislature and governor by February 1, 2025.

Official name: Arizona 2024 House Bill 2394
Casual name: Arizona HB 2394 (Digital Impersonation of Candidate for Public Office)
Short summary: Prohibits digital impersonation using AI to deceive voters in Arizona elections. Allows candidates or citizens to seek declaratory relief within two years of impersonation. Shields service providers from liability for third-party content. Requires clear and convincing evidence for relief.
Long summary:
Amends Arizona Revised Statutes to prohibit digital impersonation using AI techniques.

Allows candidates or citizens to take legal action against digital impersonation within two years of awareness.

Mandates proof of unauthorized publication and lack of clear indication of impersonation to prevail in court.

Restricts legal action to originators of paid advertisements containing impersonations.

Exempts interactive computer service providers from liability for third-party content.

Permits filing for preliminary declaratory relief in superior court with expedited ruling requirements.

Specifies conditions for granting preliminary relief, such as immediate elections or significant harm.

Allows further injunctive relief and damages for impersonations involving sexual or criminal content.

Sets standards of proof as clear and convincing evidence for relief actions.

Defines "digital impersonation" as AI-manipulated media designed to deceive, excluding parody or artistic expression.

Operates as an emergency measure effective immediately to protect public peace and safety.

Official name: Illinois Senate Bill 382
Casual name: Illinois SB 382 (Civil Remedies for Nonconsensual Dissemination of Sexual Images)
Short summary: Amends the Act to include liability exceptions for good faith dissemination of digitally altered sexual images. Specifies exceptions don't apply if dissemination serves sexual arousal or commercial gain. Clarifies images with political messages aren't public concern solely due to public figures.
Long summary:
Amends the Civil Remedies for Nonconsensual Dissemination of Private Sexual Images Act to include "digitally altered sexual image" as a defined term, expanding the scope of protection to cover images that are substantially altered to falsely appear authentic.

Specifies that exceptions to liability apply to both private sexual images and digitally altered sexual images, maintaining exceptions for good faith actions by law enforcement, legal proceedings, medical education, and reports of unlawful or unwelcome conduct.

Clarifies that the dissemination of a private or digitally altered sexual image is not a matter of public concern solely because it involves a public figure or is accompanied by a political message.

Retains provisions that a parent or guardian is not liable for dissemination unless done for prohibited purposes, such as sexual gratification or commercial gain. 

Establishes that the Act takes effect upon becoming law, without acceleration or delay from previous or other legislative changes.

Official name: Florida Senate Bill 1680
Casual name: Florida SB 1680 (Advanced Technology)
Short summary: Creates the Government Technology Modernization Council to study AI and technology, recommend regulations, assess AI's legal effects, and propose ethics reforms. Requires annual legislative recommendations to modernize technology and protect against AI misuse. Prohibits creation and possession of generated child pornography.
Long summary:
Create the Government Technology Modernization Council within Florida's Department of Management Services to study and monitor new technologies, including artificial intelligence (AI).

Require the council to recommend legislative and administrative actions to promote data modernization and AI system deployment, and establish AI safety standards.

Mandate the council to assess AI's impact on legal rights and its use by governmental and private entities, focusing on potential exploitation by bad actors.

Direct the council to evaluate the need for digital literacy curricula and submit annual recommendations for government technology modernization.

Prohibit knowingly possessing, controlling, or intentionally viewing generated child pornography, with violations constituting a third-degree felony.

Forbid intentionally creating generated child pornography, imposing similar felony penalties.

Prohibit reproduction of generated child pornography in criminal proceedings, ensuring secure custody by law enforcement.

Specify the act's effective date as July 1, 2024.

Official name: Minnesota HF 1370 (Deepfake Accountability and Public Safety)
Casual name: Minnesota HF 1370 (Deepfake Accountability and Public Safety)
Short summary: Establishes legal actions for nonconsensual dissemination of deep fake sexual images, including damages and penalties. Criminalizes using deep fake technology to influence elections. Specifies exceptions and venue rules. Grants immunity to certain service providers. Effective August 1, 2023.
Long summary:
Establishes a cause of action for the nonconsensual dissemination of deep fake sexual images in Minnesota, allowing plaintiffs to seek damages, including financial losses and mental anguish.

Defines the crime of using deep fake technology to influence elections, with penalties including imprisonment and fines for violations committed within 90 days before an election.

Criminalizes the intentional dissemination of deep fake images depicting intimate parts or sexual acts without consent, with increased penalties for aggravating factors such as intent to profit or harass.

Allows for injunctions and restraining orders to prevent further harm and includes confidentiality measures for plaintiffs.

Provides exemptions for lawful purposes, such as criminal investigations, reporting unlawful conduct, or scientific research, while ensuring compliance with federal law.

Specifies that these provisions are effective from August 1, 2023, and outlines jurisdiction and venue rules for related legal actions.

Official name: New York Senate Bill 1042
Casual name: New York SB 1042 (Unlawful Dissemination of Intimate Images)
Short summary: Amends the penal law to criminalize dissemination or publication of digitized intimate images without consent. Defines "digitization" as realistic alteration using images of another person or computer-generated images. Repeals subdivision 2-a of section 245.15.
Long summary:
Amends the penal law to include the unlawful dissemination or publication of intimate images created or altered by digitization.

Defines "digitization" as altering an image realistically using other persons' images or computer-generated images.

Requires intent to cause harm to the emotional, financial, or physical welfare of another person for the offense to occur.

Mandates that the actor knew or should have known that the person depicted did not consent to the dissemination or publication.

Repeals subdivision 2-a of section 245.15 of the penal law.

Specifies that the act takes effect on the sixtieth day after becoming law.

Official name: New Hampshire House Bill 1596
Casual name: New Hampshire HB 1596 (AI Usage in Political Advertising)
Short summary: Prohibits distributing AI-generated deepfakes of candidates within 90 days of elections without disclosure. Requires clear labeling of manipulated media. Allows affected candidates to seek legal recourse. Exempts news reporting, unknowing individuals, and parody. Effective August 1, 2024.
Long summary:
Prohibits the distribution of deepfake content created using artificial intelligence or generative AI within 90 days of an election if it involves candidates, election officials, or parties.

Exempts from the prohibition any media that includes a clear disclosure indicating manipulation by AI, specifying whether it is an image, video, or audio.

Allows affected candidates or election officials to seek legal action for injunctive relief or damages.

Excludes liability for platforms providing content from third parties, individuals unaware of deepfake nature, and bona fide news reports questioning the authenticity of materials.

Excludes satire or parody content that doesn't rely on AI.

Requires disclosures to be easily readable or audible based on the media type.

Specifies the act takes effect on August 1, 2024, with severability of provisions if any are found invalid.

Official name: Maryland Senate Bill 1068
Casual name: Maryland S 1068 (Evaluation and Development of a 3-1-1 Portal)
Short summary: Requires the Maryland Department of Information Technology to evaluate AI's potential in developing a statewide virtual 3–1–1 portal for nonemergency government information and services, and if feasible, prioritize its creation by July 1, 2024.
Long summary:
Requires the Maryland Department of Information Technology to evaluate the potential use of artificial intelligence in developing a statewide virtual 3-1-1 portal for nonemergency government information and services.

Mandates the prioritization of creating the virtual 3-1-1 portal if the AI application is deemed feasible.

Sets the effective date of this Act as July 1, 2024.

Receives approval from the Governor on May 9, 2024.

Official name: Illinois House Bill 2123
Casual name: Illinois HB 2123 (Digital Forgeries Act)
Short summary: Amends the Civil Remedies for Nonconsensual Dissemination of Private Sexual Images Act to allow depicted individuals to sue for harm from intentional dissemination of private, altered sexual images without consent. Provides exceptions and specifies potential damages and remedies.
Long summary:
Amends the Civil Remedies for Nonconsensual Dissemination of Private Sexual Images Act to define terms such as "depicted individual," "consent," and "dissemination."

Establishes a cause of action for depicted individuals against those who intentionally disseminate or threaten to disseminate private sexual images without consent, knowing or disregarding the individual's lack of consent and identifiability.

Clarifies that consent to creation or previous disclosure of an image does not imply consent to further dissemination.

Specifies exceptions to liability, including good faith actions by law enforcement, legal proceedings, and medical contexts, and outlines conditions under which parents or guardians are not liable.

Allows prevailing plaintiffs to recover damages, attorney's fees, and seek injunctive relief, while considering factors like age and dissemination scope.

States that disclosure of digitally altered images is not a defense and does not impose liability on interactive computer services for third-party content.

Official name: Mississippi Senate Bill 2577
Casual name: Mississippi SB 2577 (Wrongful Dissemination of Digitizations)
Short summary: Criminalizes dissemination of AI-generated deepfakes within 90 days of an election, without consent, intending to harm candidates or influence elections. Imposes penalties up to five years imprisonment or $10,000 fine. Exempts satire, news, and security-related activities. Effective July 1, 2024.
Long summary:
Establishes criminal penalties for wrongful dissemination of deepfakes, defined as realistic digital alterations of images or audio using AI or other technologies, particularly if used to influence elections.

Criminalizes dissemination within 90 days of an election without consent and with intent to harm a candidate or election outcome.

Imposes penalties of up to five years imprisonment or $10,000 fines for violations intended to incite violence or deter voting, with lesser penalties for other cases.

Allows injunctive relief actions by various parties, including the Attorney General and affected candidates, to prevent violations and remove disseminated deepfakes.

Exempts certain broadcasters and publishers, including those involved in bona fide news reporting or satire, from liability.

Requires clear labeling of deepfakes as a defense to prosecution.

Takes effect on July 1, 2024.

Official name: Iowa S 2243 (Sexual Exploitation of Minors, Use of Falsely Created Images)
Casual name: Iowa S 2243 (Sexual Exploitation of Minors, Use of Falsely Created Images)
Short summary: Amends Iowa law to prohibit knowingly possessing AI-created depictions of identifiable minors in prohibited sexual acts. Treats images of different minors as separate offenses; repeated violations escalate to a class "C" felony.
Long summary:
Amends existing law to make it unlawful to knowingly purchase or possess visual depictions of a minor engaging in a prohibited sexual act or its simulation.

Requires separate prosecution and punishment for each different minor depicted in such visual content, though multiple depictions of the same minor are treated as a single offense.

Classifies a first offense as a class "D" felony and a second or subsequent offense as a class "C" felony.

Establishes that prior convictions or deferred judgments under this law, or similar laws in other jurisdictions, constitute second or subsequent offenses.

Official name: Michigan House Bill 5141
Casual name: Michigan HB 5141 (Campaign Practices)
Short summary: Requires political advertisements generated substantially by AI to include disclaimers indicating AI involvement. Mandates specific formatting for AI disclaimers in graphic, audio, and video ads. Establishes fines for non-compliance and outlines exemptions for certain media and platforms.
Long summary:
Amends campaign finance law to require that political advertisements generated substantially by artificial intelligence include a clear and conspicuous statement indicating AI involvement.

Mandates that AI-generated political advertisements in graphic form display the statement in large text, while audio communications must audibly disclose AI use at the beginning or end.

Requires video advertisements to display the statement for at least four seconds and include an audible disclosure.

Imposes fines for violations, with $250 for the first offense and up to $1,000 for subsequent offenses.

Exempts bona fide news coverage and platforms with clear policies on AI content disclosure from these requirements.

Defines "qualified political advertisement" as including various advertising formats related to elections or ballot questions using AI-generated content.

Conditions the act’s effectiveness on the enactment of House Bill No. 5143.

Official name: West Virginia House Bill 3214
Casual name: West Virginia H 3214 (Road Optimization Assessment Data Pilot Program)
Short summary: Creates the "Road Optimization and Assessment Data Road Pilot Program" using AI and ML for road condition assessment. Requires data collection via GPS, laser systems, and video imagery in Monongalia and Preston Counties. Aims for predictive road maintenance over three years.
Long summary:
Creates the "Road Optimization and Assessment Data Road Pilot Program" to study alternative methods for assessing and financing road maintenance. 

Mandates the use of Machine Learning (ML) and Artificial Intelligence (AI) to evaluate road conditions in Monongalia and Preston Counties.

Requires the Commissioner of Highways to promulgate rules for legislative approval concerning data collection and road assessment.

Instructs the Division of Highways employees to drive all roads in the pilot counties for data capture.

Utilizes GPS, geotagged positioning, and laser measuring systems to assess road geometry and conditions.

Employs video documentation and reflectometer systems for comprehensive road condition analysis.

Requires biannual data capture and processing over three years to track road condition changes and provide predictive maintenance analysis.

Official name: Indiana Senate Bill 2024-150
Casual name: Indiana Senate Bill 2024-150 (AI and Cybersecurity)
Short summary: Establishes an Artificial Intelligence Task Force to study AI use and impact, requiring state agencies to inventory all artificial intelligence technologies in use, being developed or considered by the agency.
Long summary:
Establishes an Artificial Intelligence Task Force to study AI technologies used by Indiana state agencies, assessing their benefits, risks, and impacts on privacy, civil rights, and legal protections.

Requires the Task Force to produce annual reports with findings and recommendations for policies or legislation to govern AI use by state agencies.

Mandates state agencies compile and submit inventories of AI technologies in use or development, detailing vendors, intended use, data security measures, benefits, risks, and financial implications.

Official name: Indiana House Bill 1047
Casual name: Indiana HB 1047 (Sexual Offenses)
Short summary: Defines "computer generated image" as those created or modified using AI or software. Criminalizes distributing non-consensual intimate images, including AI-generated ones, with enhanced penalties for repeat offenders. Establishes new offenses for voyeurism involving AI or digital tools.
Long summary:
Amends Indiana law to define "computer generated image" as images created or modified through artificial intelligence or software.

Prohibits distribution of intimate images without consent, including AI-generated images, categorizing such distribution as a Class A misdemeanor or Level 6 felony for repeat offenders.

Criminalizes voyeurism involving AI or cameras, with enhanced penalties for repeat offenses or distribution of recorded images.

Establishes that using unmanned aerial vehicles to capture private images without consent constitutes remote aerial voyeurism, punishable as a Class A misdemeanor or Level 6 felony for repeat offenses or distribution.

Exempts certain distributions of images for criminal investigations or under court orders.

Requires commencement of actions for child sexual abuse before certain deadlines or conditions, with exceptions for specific bankruptcy proceedings.

Declares an emergency for the enactment of these provisions.

Official name: Michigan House Bill 5144 of 2023 (Election Law)
Casual name: Michigan HB 2023-5144 (Election Law)
Short summary: Prohibits distributing AI-generated deceptive media within 90 days before elections if intended to harm a candidate or deceive voters. Requires disclaimers for exceptions. Penalizes violations with fines and imprisonment. Allows injunctive relief by affected parties or the attorney general.
Long summary:
Prohibits the distribution of materially deceptive media produced by artificial intelligence within 90 days of an election if intended to harm a candidate's reputation or alter voting behavior.

Exempts media with a clear disclaimer that it has been manipulated and includes a citation to the original source.

Classifies violations as a misdemeanor for the first offense and a felony for subsequent offenses within five years.

Allows the attorney general, affected individuals, candidates, or voter organizations to seek permanent injunctive relief in certain courts.

Requires that injunctive relief complaints be non-frivolous and supported by clear and convincing evidence.

Defines "materially deceptive media" as AI-generated content that falsely depicts individuals in misleading ways.

Conditions the act's effectiveness on the enactment of House Bill No. 5143.

Official name: Illinois House Bill 4875
Casual name: Illinois HB 4875 (Publicity Act)
Short summary: Amends the Right of Publicity Act to regulate AI-generated digital replicas, prohibiting unauthorized commercial use without consent. Establishes liability for knowingly distributing such replicas. Exempts certain uses, like news or satire. Applies protections for 50 years posthumously. Effective January 1, 2025.
Long summary:
Amends the Right of Publicity Act in Illinois to define "artificial intelligence" as systems that generate outputs influencing environments, including generative AI.

Defines "digital replica" as an electronic representation of an individual created using AI without their actual participation.

Prohibits the use of an individual's identity or digital replica for commercial purposes without consent during their lifetime and for 50 years posthumously.

Restricts the distribution of works containing unauthorized digital replicas without consent.

Exempts certain uses, such as in news, political campaigns, or works of public interest, unless they falsely create the impression of authenticity.

Incorporates elements from federal law to exempt certain service providers from liability if they act to remove unauthorized replicas upon gaining knowledge.

Applies to acts occurring after January 1, 2025, excluding ongoing or prior legal actions.

Official name: Indiana 2024 House Bill 1133
Casual name: Indiana HB 1133 (Digitally Altered Media in Elections)
Short summary: Adds Chapter 8 to Indiana Code, regulating election campaign communications with digitally altered media. Requires disclaimers for such media, specifying format and timing. Allows candidates depicted in undisclosed fabricated media to sue for damages, injunctive relief, and legal costs.
Long summary:
Amends the Indiana Code to add regulations on the use of digitally altered media in elections.

Defines "campaign communication" to include media advocating for or against a candidate, with fabricated media depicting candidates requiring specific disclaimers.

Requires disclaimers for campaign communications containing fabricated media to state that elements have been digitally altered or artificially generated.

Mandates disclaimers in printed, audio, and video formats to meet specific visibility, audibility, and frequency standards.

Allows candidates depicted in fabricated media without the required disclaimer to sue the person who financed, sponsored, or disseminated the communication.

Entitles prevailing plaintiffs to recover actual damages, injunctive relief, and associated legal costs.

Places the burden of proof on the plaintiff to demonstrate depiction in fabricated media by clear and convincing evidence.

Expedites court hearings for related civil actions.

Declares an emergency for the immediate enactment of this regulation.

Official name: Oregon House Bill 4153
Casual name: Oregon HB 4153 (AI Task Force)
Short summary: Establishes a 14-member Task Force on Artificial Intelligence in Oregon. Requires examination of AI-related terms for legislation, aligning with federal definitions. Directs submission of a report by December 1, 2024. Declares an emergency for immediate effect.
Long summary:
Establishes the Task Force on Artificial Intelligence in Oregon, comprising 14 members with diverse expertise.

Requires task force members to have proficiency in computer science, AI, technology industries, workforce development, or data privacy.

Mandates the task force to examine and define AI-related terms for potential legislation, aligning closely with federal definitions.

Requires the task force to gather input from a wide range of stakeholders, including academia, consumer groups, and businesses.

Directs the task force to report recommendations to an interim committee by December 1, 2024.

Provides that the Legislative Policy and Research Director offers staff support, and state agencies assist the task force.

Specifies that the task force ceases on January 1, 2025, with an emergency declared for the act's immediate implementation.

Official name: Oregon Senate Bill 1571
Casual name: Oregon SB 1571 (AI in Campaign Ads)
Short summary: Require campaign communications using synthetic media to disclose manipulation. Allow the Secretary of State and Attorney General to enforce compliance through legal actions and penalties. Exempt certain media and satire. Enable rule adoption for implementation. Declare an emergency for immediate effect.
Long summary:
Mandates that campaign communications using synthetic media include a disclosure stating the manipulation of images, audio, or video.

Allows the Secretary of State and the Attorney General to initiate legal proceedings to prevent violations of the requirement for disclosure in campaign communications using synthetic media.

Imposes a civil penalty of up to $10,000 for violations, with penalties directed to the State Treasury's General Fund.

Excludes certain media providers, broadcasters, and publications from the disclosure requirement, provided they acknowledge potential authenticity issues or involve satire or parody.

Adds the new provisions to Oregon Revised Statutes chapter 260 and amends ORS 260.345 to incorporate enforcement mechanisms for violations involving synthetic media in campaign communications.

Permits the Secretary of State to establish rules to implement these provisions.

Declares an emergency for immediate enactment to preserve public peace, health, and safety.

Official name: West Virginia HB 5690 (Artificial Intelligence Task Force)
Casual name: West Virginia HB 5690 (AI Task Force)
Short summary: Establishes the West Virginia Task Force on Artificial Intelligence to recommend AI definitions, policies, legislation, and best practices. Requires submission of a report by July 1, 2025, and terminates the Task Force upon report submission or on July 1, 2025.
Long summary:
Establishes the West Virginia Task Force on Artificial Intelligence within the Office of the Governor. 

Specifies membership including representatives from government, industry, education, and health sectors, appointed by various authorities. 

Charges the Task Force with responsibilities such as recommending AI definitions for legislation, identifying relevant state agencies for AI policy oversight, and developing best practices for public sector AI use. 

Mandates recommendations on AI's impact on employment, protecting individual rights and data, and addressing AI use in education. 

Requires the Task Force to assess current AI use in state agencies and consider extending its mandate to keep pace with AI advancements. 

Obligates submission of a comprehensive report by July 1, 2025, after which the Task Force terminates. 

Requires initial appointments within 90 days and quarterly meetings, with administrative support provided by the Office of Technology.
Tags: Strategies: New institution;Strategies: New institution;Strategies: Convening;Strategies: Evaluation;Strategies: Governance development;Harms: Violation of civil or human rights, including privacy;Strategies: Government study or report;Strategies: Government support;Strategies: New institution

Official name: Hawaii Senate Bill 2687
Casual name: Hawaii SB 2687 (Deceptive Media)
Short summary: Prohibits reckless distribution of materially deceptive media using AI during elections. Establishes criminal penalties and civil remedies for violations. Exempts certain broadcasters and services. Requires disclaimers for manipulated media. Defines AI and materially deceptive media.
Long summary:
Prohibits the reckless distribution of materially deceptive media, including deepfake and generative AI content, during election periods, to protect candidates' reputations and electoral prospects.

Establishes criminal penalties for violations, including petty misdemeanors, misdemeanors for repeat offenses, and class C felonies for intent to cause violence or harm.

Allows exceptions for broadcasters and online services that did not create the deceptive media, or if disclaimers indicate manipulation.

Mandates that disclaimers must be clearly visible and in the same language as the media, with specific requirements for video, image, and audio content.

Provides civil remedies for individuals or organizations harmed by deceptive media, including damages, attorney's fees, and injunctive relief.

Empowers the attorney general, campaign spending commission, and other authorities to enforce these provisions and seek injunctions.

Defines "artificial intelligence" and "materially deceptive media" for clarity and enforcement purposes.

Official name: New Jersey Senate Bill 3432
Casual name: New Jersey S 3432 (Next New Jersey Program Act)
Short summary: Establishes the "Next New Jersey Program Act" promoting AI development by granting tax credits to eligible AI businesses. Requires capital investments in AI facilities, job creation, and collaboration with NJ-based institutions. Mandates compliance with sustainability standards, prevailing wages, and regulatory requirements. Limits apply to tax credit value and duration.
Long summary:
Establishes the "Next New Jersey Program" to drive investment into New Jersey’s AI sector, creating jobs and economic opportunities.

Requires businesses to invest a minimum of $100 million in capital and create at least 100 new full-time jobs to be eligible for tax credits.

Mandates businesses or divisions engaged in AI or large-scale AI data centers, demonstrating over 50% of revenue or employment in AI activities.

Stipulates collaboration with New Jersey-based research entities or startups.

Enforces compliance with environmental and labor standards, including prevailing wage for construction workers.

Enables businesses meeting criteria to apply for tax credits valid until March 1, 2029.

Empowers the New Jersey Economic Development Authority to oversee eligibility and award decisions, ensuring businesses remain compliant to retain credits.

Allows tax credit transfer certificates, with conditions on their sale and application.

Caps overall tax credits under the act at $250 million annually from a broader $11.5 billion limit across various programs over nine years.

Official name: Tennessee House Bill 2325
Casual name: Tennessee Artificial Intelligence Advisory Council Act
Short summary: Establishes the Tennessee Artificial Intelligence Advisory Council to recommend AI action plans for state government, focusing on economic benefits, responsible use, labor market impacts, and transparency. Requires annual reports through 2028, with a final report due by December 31, 2028.
Long summary:
Establishes the Tennessee Artificial Intelligence Advisory Council to guide AI awareness, education, and usage in state government.

Requires the council to submit an action plan to the governor for leveraging AI to improve government service efficiency and capture economic benefits.

Mandates the council to report annually on AI's impact on the labor market, propose legal and policy changes, and recommend workforce development strategies.

Directs the council to submit a final report by December 31, 2028, outlining AI governance frameworks, risk analyses, and recommendations for AI deployment in government.

Requires all state departments to cooperate with the council and allows the council to consult with AI experts.

Specifies that the council members will not receive compensation but will be reimbursed for travel expenses.

Repeals the act on December 31, 2028, and takes effect upon becoming law.

Official name: California Assembly Bill 3030 (2024)
Casual name: California AB 3030 (AI Health Care Services)
Short summary: Require health facilities using generative AI for patient communications to include disclaimers and contact instructions for human providers but exempt communications reviewed by a human health care provider from these requirements. Impose enforcement mechanisms for violations by health facilities, clinics, and physicians.
Long summary:
Requires health facilities, clinics, and physician offices using generative AI for patient communications in a variety of modalities to include a disclaimer indicating AI generation.

Mandates the disclaimer's prominence at the beginning, end, and/or throughout written, audio, and video communications, with instructions for contacting a human provider.

Exempts communications reviewed by a licensed health care provider from disclaimer requirements.

Defines key terms, including "artificial intelligence," "generative artificial intelligence," and "patient clinical information."

Subjects licensed health facilities, clinics, and physicians violating these requirements to enforcement mechanisms.
Tags: Risk factors: Transparency;Strategies: Disclosure;Strategies: Disclosure: In standard form;Applications: Medicine, life sciences and public health;Applications: Medicine, life sciences and public health;Applications: Medicine, life sciences and public health

Official name: California Assembly Bill 2013 (2024)
Casual name: California AB 2013 (AI Training Data)
Short summary: Requires developers to disclose training data for generative AI systems publicly by January 1, 2026, and for each subsequent release. Exempts AI systems focused on security, aircraft operation, and national defense. Specifies detailed documentation requirements for datasets, including sources, content types, and modifications.
Long summary:
Defines key terms such as "artificial intelligence," "developer," "generative artificial intelligence," and "synthetic data generation" to clarify the scope and applicability of the regulations.

Mandates developers of generative AI systems, including parties that release a new system or make substantial modifications to a system, released on or after January 1, 2022 to publicly disclose training data documentation on their websites by January 1, 2026, and for each subsequent release.

Requires documentation to include a summary of datasets, sources, purposes, data points, intellectual property status, personal information, any data processing or modifications, and the use of synthetic data generation.

Exempts AI systems solely for security, aircraft operation in national airspace, or national security and defense provided they are only available to federal entities.
Tags: Strategies: Evaluation;Risk factors: Privacy;Risk factors: Transparency;Risk factors: Security: Dissemination;Risk factors: Security;Strategies: Disclosure;Strategies: Disclosure: About inputs;Strategies: Disclosure: In standard form;Risk factors: Security;Risk factors: Transparency;Strategies: Disclosure;Strategies: Disclosure: About inputs;Applications: Government: military and public safety;Applications: Transportation

Official name: California AB 2885 (2024)
Casual name: California (2024) AB 2885 (Artificial Intelligence)
Short summary: Directs the California Department of Technology to inventory high-risk automated decision systems by September 2024; requires the Secretary of Government Operations to evaluate deepfakes, digital content forgery technology, and digital content provenance by October 2024; and requires local agencies to annually report on AI's impact on jobs in warehouse distribution centers that have received economic development subsidies.
Long summary:
Defines "artificial intelligence" as systems that infer from inputs how to influence environments and "high-risk automated decision systems" as a computational process that issues output intended to replace human discretionary decisionmaking in areas such as housing, education, and employment.

Requires, by September 1, 2024, the California Department of Technology to inventory high-risk automated decision systems that currently are or may be used, developed, or procured by state agencies, detailing decision-making roles, data usage, and risk mitigation measures.

Requires the Department to submit an annual report on the inventory of high-risk systems to legislative committees until 2029.

Defines terms related to the creation and tracking of misleading AI-generated material, including "deepfake," "digital content forgery," and "digital content provenance."

Requires the Secretary of Government Operations to evaluate the impact of deepfakes, digital content forgery technologies, and digital content provenance on state governance and residents and create a plan to develop digital content provenance mechanisms, with a report to the Legislature due by October 1, 2024.

Requires local agencies to annually report on automation impacts, including AI, on jobs in warehouse distribution centers that are beneficiaries of economic development subsidies and submit the report to the Governor’s Office of Business and Economic Development.

Official name: California Assembly Bill 2905 (2024)
Casual name: California Assembly Bill 2905 (Telecommunications: automatic dialing-announcing devices: artificial voices) (2024)
Short summary: Requires a person placing a call through the use of an automatic dialing-announcing device to make an unrecorded, natural voice announcement before using AI-generated voices in calls. Requires person calling to disconnect device upon termination of the call by either the person calling or the person called and defines "artificial intelligence" and "artificial voice."
Long summary:
Amends the Public Utilities Code to regulate the use of automatic dialing-announcing devices in California.

Requires person calling to make a natural voice announcement before using an automatic dialing-announcing device, which includes stating the nature of the call and the business or organization details; inquiring whether the called person consents to hear a prerecorded message; and informing the called person if the message uses an artificial voice.

Requires person calling to disconnect the device after call termination by either party.

Defines the terms “artificial intelligence” and “artificial voice."
Tags: Risk factors: Transparency;Strategies: Disclosure;Strategies: Disclosure: In deployment;Applications: Networking and telecommunications

Official name: California Senate Bill 1288 (2024)
Casual name: California SB 1288 (Public schools: artificial intelligence working group) (2024)
Short summary: Requires the Superintendent to convene a working group to develop guidance and a model policy on AI's safe use in education by 2026. Mandates reporting findings by 2027 and repeals provisions by 2031.
Long summary:
Requires the Superintendent of Public Instruction to form a working group to address artificial intelligence in public schools.

Requires the working group to develop guidance and a model policy for safe AI use in education by January 1, 2026.

Instructs the working group to create a model policy by July 1, 2026, for the safe and beneficial use of AI that does not negatively impact students or educators.

Mandates that the working group consist of certain stakeholders, including public school teachers and schoolsite administrators.

Calls the working group to assess AI use in education, including technologies in use, costs, and impacts on educators, and to detail current uses of artificial intelligence in educational settings.

Requires the working group to conduct at least three public meetings for stakeholder feedback.

Requires the working group to identify other ways in which the state can promote effective practices that minimize risk and maximize benefits to pupils and educators, including establishing communities of practice on the use of artificial intelligence in education.

Obliges the group to report findings and recommendations to the Legislature by January 1, 2027.

Dissolves the working group upon report submission and repeals provisions by January 1, 2031.

Official name: California Senate Bill 896 Generative Artificial Intelligence Accountability Act (2024)
Casual name: California SB 896 Generative Artificial Intelligence Accountability Act (2024)
Short summary: Promotes generative AI workforce training by the state. Directs the Department of Technology to update a report to the Governor about generative AI risks and directs the Office of Emergency Services to perform a risk analysis of potential threats posed by the use of generative AI to California’s critical infrastructure. Instructs state agencies to explore potential effective generative AI use cases and implement disclaimers in communications produced by generative AI.
Long summary:
States that no individual or group should be discriminated against by AI systems, encourages agency collaboration and the creation of public-private partnerships to train the AI workforce, and directs state agencies to protect people from generative AI risks, including through rulemaking or clarification of the applicability of existing rules to AI

Defines artificial intelligence and generative artificial intelligence

Directs the Department of Technology to update a report to the Governor about generative AI risks

Directs the Office of Emergency Services to perform a risk analysis of potential threats posed by the use of generative AI to California’s critical infrastructure and summarize the analysis for the Legislature annually

Instructs state agencies to explore potential effective generative AI use cases while ensuring compliance with state procurement policies.

Directs state agencies to implement disclaimers and human contact information in communications generated by generative AI

Official name: California Assembly Bill 2876 (2024)
Casual name: California AB 2876 (Pupil instruction: media literacy: artificial intelligence literacy: curriculum frameworks: instructional materials) (2024)
Short summary: Require the California Instructional Quality Commission to incorporate AI literacy in curriculum frameworks for mathematics, science, and history-social science and consider AI literacy in its criteria for evaluating instructional materials when they are next adopted by the state board after January 1, 2025.
Long summary:
Defines "AI literacy" as the knowledge, skills, and attitudes related to how artificial intelligence works, including its principles, concepts, applications, limitations, implications, and ethical considerations.

Mandates the commission to consider incorporating AI literacy content into the mathematics, science, and history-social science curriculum frameworks when those frameworks are next revised after January 1, 2025.

Requires the commission to consider including AI literacy in the criteria for evaluating mathematics, science, and history-social science instructional materials adopted by the state board after January 1, 2025.

Official name: California AB 1836
Casual name: California AB 1836 (Use of likeness: digital replica)
Short summary: Amends California Civil Code to require consent for using deceased personalities' likenesses in AI-generated replicas. Exempts certain expressive works. Imposes liability for unauthorized use with fines up to $10,000. Specifies consent requirements and transferrable rights for digital likenesses.
Long summary:
Amends the California Civil Code to impose liability on individuals using a deceased personality's likeness without consent, including AI-generated digital replicas. Requires violators to compensate for unauthorized use, with statutory minimums and profit disgorgement. Excludes certain uses such as news, satire, and fleeting references from consent requirements. 

Recognizes posthumous rights as transferable property interests, extending rights to heirs or designated persons. Limits rights duration to 70 years post-death and outlines specific inheritance rules. Mandates registration with the Secretary of State for enforcing claims, making these registrations public records.

Clarifies that certain artistic and informational works are exempt unless they serve commercial product promotion. Outlines exceptions for media owners unless knowingly complicit in unauthorized use. Enforces consistency with existing law by rendering remedies cumulative. Establishes retroactive application of rights back to those deceased before January 1, 1985.

Official name: California Assembly Bill 1831 (2024)
Casual name: California AB 1831 (Child Pornography Crimes)
Short summary: Prohibits the creation, distribution, possession, and exhibition of AI-generated child sexual assault material (CSAM) in California and imposes severe penalties.
Long summary:
Clarifies that child sexual assault material (CSAM) generated by artificial intelligence (AI) is obscene and prohibits the creation, distribution, and possession of this material in California.

Defines AI as a machine-based system that can infer from inputs how to generate outputs that influence environments.

Punishes the distribution or possession of AI-generated CSAM depicting minors under 18 engaging in sexual conduct with imprisonment and fines and classifies this conduct as a felony in certain cases 

Exempts law enforcement; medical, scientific, educational activities; lawful conduct between spouses; and legally emancipated children under 18 from these provisions.

Specifies enhanced penalties for possessing certain material and for repeat offenders

Specifies enhanced penalties for using government-owned computers, computer networks, or property for CSAM-related offenses.

Requires revenue from fines to support sexual assault investigator training and services for human trafficking victims.

Becomes operative only if Senate Bill 1381 is enacted by January 1, 2025.

Official name: California Senate Bill 942 (2024)
Casual name: California AI Transparency Act (2024)
Short summary: Enacts the California AI Transparency Act, requiring AI providers with over 1 million users to offer free AI detection tools and include clear disclosures in AI-generated content. Establishes enforcement through civil penalties and mandates preservation of disclosure features in licensed products.
Long summary:
Enacts the California AI Transparency Act, requiring covered providers of generative AI systems with significant user bases to offer free AI detection tools that allow users to assess whether image, video, or audio content was created or altered by the provider’s system.

Requires AI-generated content to include both manifest and latent disclosures, identifying it as AI-generated, with details about the provider and system.

Requires ongoing evaluation of AI-detection tools while prohibiting the collection or retention of personal information from detection tool users, except in specific opt-in scenarios.

Mandates disclosure capabilities to be clear and permanent, and demands that licenses for GenAI systems include obligations for content disclosures.

Imposes civil penalties of $5,000 per violation for non-compliance, with each day constituting a separate violation.

Excludes specific entertainment products from applicability and sets the operative date as January 1, 2026.

Official name: California AB 2839 (2024)
Casual name: California AB 2839 (Election Advertisements)
Short summary: Prohibits malicious distribution of materially deceptive AI-generated election content. Requires disclosures for manipulated media. Allows injunctive relief and damages for violations. Excludes bona fide news broadcasts. Applies 120 days before and after elections. Defines "deepfake" and "materially deceptive content." Enacts immediately.
Long summary:
Prohibits the distribution of materially deceptive AI-generated content related to elections, such as deepfakes, during specified periods before and after elections in California. 

Requires disclosures for manipulated content used by candidates, stating "This [media type] has been manipulated," ensuring transparency.

Allows recipients of deceptive content, candidates, or election officials to seek injunctive relief and damages against violators.

Exempts broadcasting stations from liability under certain conditions, such as bona fide news coverage or compliance with existing federal laws.

Defines key terms like "deepfake," "materially deceptive content," and "malice" to establish clear guidelines.

Applies provisions to all languages and mandates that disclosures match the language of the advertisement.

Includes an urgency clause, making the act effective immediately to protect upcoming elections from AI-driven disinformation.

Official name: California Senate Bill 1381 (2024)
Casual name: California SB 1381 (Crimes: child pornography) (2024)
Short summary: Expands legal prohibitions to include child pornography generated or altered by artificial intelligence. Subjects such AI-generated content to penalties. Allows forfeiture and destruction of such content without a criminal conviction. Becomes operative with AB 1831 enactment.
Long summary:
Expands existing child pornography laws to include materials digitally altered or generated by artificial intelligence.

Prohibits the production, distribution, or possession of AI-generated depictions involving minors engaging in sexual conduct.

Imposes penalties including fines and imprisonment for violations involving AI-generated content depicting minors, including recruiting minors to create such content.

Specifies exemptions for legally emancipated minors; law enforcement; legitimate medical, educational, or scientific activities; and lawful conduct between spouses.

Allows forfeiture of AI-generated depictions involving minors engaging in sexual conduct that is in the possession of city, county, or state officials or agencies.

Allows destruction of AI-generated materials regardless of criminal conviction.

Mandates that the act becomes operative only if AB 1831 is enacted by January 1, 2025.
