Official name: California Senate Bill 1120 (2024)
Casual name: California SB 1120 (Health care coverage: utilization review) (2024)
Short summary: Requires health care service plans and disability insurers using AI for utilization review to ensure compliance with clinical criteria, non-discrimination, transparency, and auditability. Prohibits AI from replacing provider decision-making or denying services based on medical necessity.
Long summary:
Requires health plans and disability insurers that use AI for utilization review or work with an entity that uses AI for utilization review to ensure that the AI is lawful; nondiscriminatory; based on clinical information; open to review; responsibly uses patient data; does not supplant health care provider decisionmaking; and does not cause harm to the enrollee.

Prohibits health plans and disability insurers that use AI for utilization review from denying, delaying, or modifying health care services based on medical necessity and mandates that only licensed health care professionals make determinations of medical necessity by evaluating clinical issues on a case-by-case basis; clarifies that state departments may issue guidance to help implement federal rules regarding AI in healthcare; and requires that health plans have a medical director review and report on the health care service plan's compliance with requirements
Tags: Strategies: Evaluation;Strategies: Evaluation: Post-market monitoring;Risk factors: Bias;Risk factors: Reliability;Risk factors: Interpretability and explainability;Risk factors: Privacy;Risk factors: Safety;Harms: Harm to health/safety;Harms: Violation of civil or human rights, including privacy;Applications: Medicine, life sciences and public health;Risk factors: Transparency;Strategies: Disclosure;Strategies: Disclosure: In deployment;Applications: Government: benefits and welfare;Harms: Discrimination;Strategies: Performance requirements;Strategies: Evaluation: Conformity assessment;Strategies: Evaluation: External auditing;Strategies: Input controls: Data use;Applications: Medicine, life sciences and public health;Harms: Harm to health/safety;Strategies: Performance requirements;Strategies: Government study or report;Applications: Government: benefits and welfare;Strategies: Governance development;Strategies: Evaluation;Strategies: Evaluation: Conformity assessment;Strategies: Evaluation;Strategies: Evaluation: Post-market monitoring;Risk factors: Bias;Risk factors: Reliability;Risk factors: Interpretability and explainability;Risk factors: Privacy;Risk factors: Safety;Harms: Harm to health/safety;Harms: Violation of civil or human rights, including privacy;Applications: Medicine, life sciences and public health;Risk factors: Transparency;Strategies: Disclosure;Strategies: Disclosure: In deployment;Applications: Government: benefits and welfare;Harms: Discrimination;Strategies: Performance requirements;Strategies: Evaluation: Conformity assessment;Strategies: Evaluation: External auditing;Strategies: Input controls: Data use;Applications: Medicine, life sciences and public health;Harms: Harm to health/safety;Strategies: Performance requirements;Applications: Government: benefits and welfare;Strategies: Governance development

Official name: Next Generation Military Education Act
Casual name: Next Generation Military Education Act
Short summary: Requires the Department of Defense to develop AI and machine learning courses for military personnel, expand the Digital On-Demand Program to all members and employees, and incorporate AI risks into annual cybersecurity training.
Long summary:
Amends the National Defense Authorization Act to require the Department of Defense to develop and provide distance education courses on artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning for Armed Forces personnel.

Mandates these courses to cover AI literacy, ethical use, data protection, and awareness of AI-generated content.

Requires military departments to ensure access and notify personnel about these courses.

Obligates the Secretary of Defense to report to Congress on the progress of these activities.

Expands participation in the Digital On-Demand Program to all Department of Defense entities and personnel, promoting AI education.

Incorporates AI risks and threats into annual cybersecurity training for Armed Forces and civilian Department of Defense employees.

Requires annual reporting to Congress on the expansion and promotion of the Digital On-Demand Program. 

Defines key terms, including "artificial intelligence" and "Digital On-Demand Program."

Official name: Improving Diagnosis in Medicine Act of 2024
Casual name: Improving Diagnosis in Medicine Act of 2024
Short summary: Establishes a research program to improve diagnostic safety and quality, including integrating AI-enabled tools. Requires expert panel consultation for data needs, involving the Chief AI Officer. Forms an interagency council to enhance research, policy, and collaboration in diagnosis improvement.
Long summary:
Amends the Public Health Service Act to establish a comprehensive research program on diagnostic safety and quality, focusing on reducing diagnostic errors and integrating AI tools.

Directs funding for new and existing research centers and mandates stakeholder engagement to enhance diagnostic quality and safety.

Requires the convening of an expert panel, including the Chief Artificial Intelligence Officer, to recommend data types and sources for research improvement.

Establishes an Interagency Council on Improving Diagnosis in Health Care, including members from AI-related departments, to enhance diagnosis quality and identify research gaps.

Mandates the creation of a strategic federal plan to improve diagnosis, with updates and progress reports submitted biennially to Congress.

Authorizes appropriations for these initiatives from 2025 to 2029, ensuring sustained financial support for improving diagnostic practices and integrating AI solutions.

Official name: Modernizing Data Practices to Improve Government Act
Casual name: Modernizing Data Practices to Improve Government Act
Short summary: Defines artificial intelligence using existing legislation and includes AI in data governance. Requires the Chief Data Officer Council to identify AI opportunities and enhance data practices. Mandates biennial reports on AI use and data governance improvements in agencies.
Long summary:
Amends title 44 of the United States Code to define "artificial intelligence" and "data governance."

Requires Chief Data Officers to focus on data management and governance, including AI-related data standards.

Mandates the Chief Data Officer Council to enhance data governance, evaluate new technologies, and ensure AI use improves government operations.

Directs the Council to report on data governance progress and provide recommendations for AI and emerging technology adoption, including data transparency and privacy.

Obliges the Director to issue guidance on implementing Council recommendations and maintain a public inventory of Chief Data Officers.

Sets reporting requirements for the Comptroller General on the Council's effectiveness and challenges.

Establishes a sunset clause, rendering the section ineffective after December 14, 2031.

Prohibits the allocation of additional funds to implement the Act.

Official name: AI Fraud Deterrence Act
Casual name: AI Fraud Deterrence Act
Short summary: Increases penalties for financial crimes like mail fraud, wire fraud, bank fraud, and money laundering when committed with the assistance of artificial intelligence, including higher fines (up to $2 million) and longer prison sentences (up to 30 years), and define "artificial intelligence" as per the National Artificial Intelligence Initiative Act of 2020.
Long summary:
Amends penalties for financial crimes committed with the assistance of artificial intelligence, increasing fines to $1,000,000 and imprisonment terms.

Mandates fines for AI-assisted money laundering to be not more than $1,000,000 or thrice the value of the funds involved, with imprisonment terms up to 20 years.

Defines "artificial intelligence" according to the National Artificial Intelligence Initiative Act of 2020 definition.

Codifies increased penalties and definitions within the United States Code to deter AI-assisted financial crimes.
Tags: Incentives: Criminal liability;Incentives: Civil liability;Applications: Government: judicial and law enforcement;Incentives: Criminal liability;Applications: Government: judicial and law enforcement

Official name: Eliminating Bias in Algorithmic Systems Act of 2024
Casual name: Eliminating Bias in Algorithmic Systems Act of 2024
Short summary: Requires agencies using or overseeing algorithms to establish civil rights offices addressing bias and discrimination. Mandates biennial reports on algorithmic harms. Establishes an interagency working group on algorithms and civil rights. Authorizes necessary appropriations for implementation.
Long summary:
Require each agency that uses, funds, or oversees algorithms to establish an office of civil rights focused on bias, discrimination, and other algorithmic harms.

Mandate these civil rights offices to employ experts and technologists to address issues related to covered algorithms.

Obligate each office of civil rights to submit biennial reports to congressional committees detailing the state of algorithms, risks of bias and discrimination, mitigation steps taken, stakeholder engagement, and recommendations for further actions.

Establish an interagency working group on algorithms and civil rights, led by the Assistant Attorney General of the Civil Rights Division, with membership from each agency's civil rights office.

Authorize necessary appropriations for agencies to implement the Act's provisions.

Official name: Economic Security and Diplomacy Act of 2024
Casual name: Economic Security and Diplomacy Act of 2024
Short summary: Authorize $5 million annually to acquire AI and machine learning tools for data analysis in the Office of Economic Analysis and Targeting. Establish and fund initiatives for robust economic security and technological policy, including AI-driven analysis and strategic foreign assistance.
Long summary:
Amends the State Department Basic Authorities Act to establish a Deputy Secretary of State for Economic Security, responsible for U.S. economic security matters including sanctions, trade, and supply chains.

Transfers assets, personnel, and duties from existing offices to this new Deputy Secretary role.

Redesignates and relocates various offices and positions, including creating an Assistant Secretary for Technology and Innovation Security, responsible for policy on emerging technologies and research security.

Establishes an Office of Economic Analysis and Targeting to conduct economic analysis, prepare sanctions targeting packages, and manage a federal database on economic security data.

Authorizes $5 million annually for artificial intelligence and machine learning tools to support data analysis.

Allows hiring of additional personnel without standard competitive service requirements to support these new roles and offices.

Designates Foreign Service officers to represent the Deputy Secretary's mission at strategic posts worldwide.

Official name: Data Protection Act of 2024
Casual name: Data Protection Act of 2024
Short summary: Establishes the Data Protection Agency to regulate high-risk data practices, requiring assessments and evaluations of AI systems. Oversees compliance with Federal privacy laws, emphasizing non-discrimination, privacy harm prevention, and transparency in automated decision-making. Conducts audits, enforces laws, supervises large data aggregators, and collaborates with other agencies for consistent regulation.
Long summary:
Establishes the Data Protection Agency (DPA) as an independent entity responsible for regulating high-risk data practices and the collection, processing, and sharing of personal data. 

Defines high-risk data practices, which include the use of automated decision systems and processes involving biometric information, and mandates high-risk data practice risk assessments and impact evaluations.

Empowers the DPA to issue rules, conduct investigations, enforce federal privacy law, and impose penalties on violators.

Requires large data aggregators, those with significant revenue or who process data of many individuals, to report compliance and undergo regular examinations.

Transfers certain rulemaking and enforcement powers from the Federal Trade Commission to the DPA.

Mandates coordination with other federal and state agencies to ensure consistent privacy regulation.

Allows states to enforce protections, provided they do not conflict with this act. 

Authorizes appropriations for the DPA's operations and activities.

Official name: Mississippi Senate Bill 3000
Casual name: Mississippi S 3000 (Eight Universities Fund), Section 42
Short summary: Allocate $15 million to the University of Mississippi for Data Science and Artificial Intelligence expenses as part of reauthorized Education Enhancement Funds for fiscal year 2023-2024.
Long summary:
Reappropriates $53,478,000 from the Education Enhancement Fund for fiscal year 2023-2024 to Institutions of Higher Learning, maintaining original funding purposes.

Allocates $15,000,000 to the University of Mississippi for expenses in Data Science and Artificial Intelligence, Healthcare Innovation, and related infrastructure.

Limits expenditure to the unexpended balance of funds as of June 30, 2023, and maintains the original purpose of the funds.

Allocates additional funds to Delta State University for flight simulators, Mississippi State University for computing infrastructure, and Alcorn State University for STEM programs, among other specified projects.

Ensures funds support technology transfer and entrepreneurial programs potentially linked to the Department of Defense and related federal research in Mississippi.

Requires consistency with the original authorization in Senate Bill 3002 from the 2022 Regular Session.

Official name: Iowa Senate Bill 2435
Casual name: Iowa S 2435 (Appropriations to the Education System), Division III, Section 6. ("GENERAL FUND APPROPRIATIONS")
Short summary: Allocates $2.8 million to Iowa State University for expanding AI, cybersecurity, computer science, and related technology programs to address workforce needs and foster innovation in digital agriculture, manufacturing, and biosciences.
Long summary:
Allocate $2.8 million to Iowa State University of Science and Technology to address workforce needs in science, technology, engineering, and mathematics (STEM) by expanding degree and certificate programs in artificial intelligence, cybersecurity, computer science, and related fields.

Promote innovation in areas such as digital agriculture, manufacturing, water quality, vaccine delivery technologies, and biosciences with the allocated funds.

Ensure that the state board of regents and the institutions of higher learning governed by them do not reduce budgeted funds for their police departments for the fiscal year 2024-2025.

Require the state board of regents to submit a quarterly financial report to the general assembly, including details on five-year graduation rates for the regents universities in the report for the quarter ending December 31, 2024.

Official name: Nebraska L 1284 (Teacher Incentive Programs), Sec. 2.
Casual name: Nebraska L 1284 (Teacher Incentive Programs), Sec. 2.
Short summary: Creates the Dyslexia Research Grant Program for Nebraska businesses researching AI-based writing assistance for dyslexia. Limits total grants to $500,000 from the Education Future Fund. Requires compliance; misuse triggers legal action to recover funds. Allows State Board rulemaking.
Long summary:
Create the Dyslexia Research Grant Program, administered by the State Department of Education, to support research on AI-based writing assistance for individuals with dyslexia.

Allow privately owned businesses in Nebraska researching AI writing tools for dyslexia to apply for grants, using a prescribed form.

Limit the total grant awards to $500,000, intended to be funded by the Education Future Fund.

Require all grant funds to be used specifically for researching AI tools that use aggregate writing analytics to help develop literacy plans for dyslexic students.

Mandate notification of the Attorney General if grant funds are misused, prompting a civil action to recover funds, which are then credited back to the Education Future Fund.

Authorize the State Board of Education to adopt rules and regulations to implement the program.

Official name: Idaho House Bill 2472
Casual name: Idaho HB 2472 (Managed Care Reform and Patient Rights Act), Sec. 30
Short summary: Requires utilization review programs using algorithmic processes to ensure clinical peers make adverse determinations, comply with accreditation standards, and include policies for algorithm verification. Establishes registration every two years with proof of compliance. Sets appeal procedures for adverse determinations.
Long summary:
Amends the Managed Care Reform and Patient Rights Act to require utilization review programs using algorithmic automated processes to ensure that only clinical peers make adverse determinations based on medical necessity.

Mandates these programs to register with the Department of Insurance every two years and provide proof of compliance with Health Utilization Management Standards or equivalent standards.

Requires licensed physicians with relevant board certifications to establish criteria for these automated processes and verify their accuracy.

Obliges health care plans to have appeals procedures, allowing appeals to be filed orally or in writing, with decisions rendered within specified timeframes.

Ensures confidentiality of patient-specific information obtained during utilization reviews, restricting access to authorized individuals.

Permits adverse determination appeals to an external independent review, with potential further appeals to the Department if found arbitrary or capricious.

Authorizes the Department to issue corrective actions or cease and desist orders for non-compliance.

Official name: Budget Act of 2024
Casual name: California AB 107 (Budget Act 2024), Sec. 2.00, Item 0511-001-0001 (For support of Secretary of Government Operations)
Short summary: Establishes monthly AI meetings with legislative staff to discuss Executive Order N-12-23. Defines AI and GenAI. Requires GenAI pilot data as personal information. Prohibits projects with sensitive data. Mandates compliance with GenAI guidelines. Allows de-identified data for research.
Long summary:
Establishes monthly Generative AI meetings with legislative staff to discuss Executive Order N-12-23 implementation.

Requires updates on deliverable completion, proof of concept implementation, and funding source identification.

Supports budget requests for generative AI technology solutions if additional resources are needed.

Defines "artificial intelligence" and "generative artificial intelligence" within the provisions.

Treats data collected in GenAI pilot projects as personal information per Civil Code Section 1798.3.

Prohibits GenAI pilot projects from including sensitive personal information, data on minors, educational records, or medical information.

Mandates compliance with California's GenAI Guidelines for departments in GenAI pilot projects.

Permits the use of de-identified data for research to justify resource requests if expanding pilot program scope.

Official name: Utah Senate Bill 149, Chapter 70
Casual name: Utah SB 149, Chapter 70 (Artificial Intelligence Policy Act)
Short summary: Establishes the Office of Artificial Intelligence Policy to oversee AI development. Creates an AI Learning Laboratory for research and regulatory evaluation. Permits regulatory mitigation agreements for AI testing. Criminalizes offenses committed with AI aid. Repeals the Act on May 1, 2025.
Long summary:
Establishes the Office of Artificial Intelligence Policy to oversee the AI Learning Laboratory Program, which analyzes AI technologies' risks, benefits, and regulatory implications.

Mandates the creation of regulatory mitigation agreements, allowing participants to operate under modified regulations while testing AI technologies.

Sets eligibility requirements for regulatory mitigation, including technical expertise, financial resources, and risk management plans.

Requires participants to report consumer harm, privacy breaches, or unauthorized data usage, with potential removal from the program for violations.

Allows an initial 12-month regulatory mitigation period, with a possible 12-month extension.

Defines using generative AI to commit offenses as a punishable act.

Specifies the program's repeal on May 1, 2025.

Takes effect on May 1, 2024.

Official name: Utah Senate Bill 149, Section 13-2-12
Casual name: Utah SB 149, Section 13-2-12 (Impact of generative AI on liability for violation of consumer protection law)
Short summary: Prohibits using generative AI as a defense for consumer protection violations. Requires disclosure of AI interactions in regulated occupations. Imposes fines up to $2,500 per violation. Authorizes courts to issue injunctions, order disgorgements, and impose additional penalties.
Long summary:
Establishes that using generative artificial intelligence (AI) is not a defense against violations of consumer protection laws.

Mandates disclosure when generative AI interacts with a person, requiring clear communication that the interaction is with AI, not a human.

Requires providers of regulated occupations to disclose when generative AI is used in service provision.

Prohibits service provision of regulated occupations via generative AI without meeting legal requirements.

Stipulates that disclosures be made verbally at the start of oral interactions and electronically before written exchanges.

Empowers the Division of Consumer Protection to enforce these provisions, including imposing fines up to $2,500 per violation.

Allows the division to seek court actions, which may result in injunctions, disgorgement of funds, or additional fines.

Permits civil penalties up to $5,000 for violating administrative or court orders, enforceable by the attorney general.

Official name: Utah Senate Bill 84, Part 13
Casual name: Utah S 84 (Governor’s Office of Economic Opportunity), Part 13. Innovation in Artificial Intelligence Grant Pilot Program
Short summary: Creates the Innovation in Artificial Intelligence Grant Pilot Program to fund business entities developing AI courses for K-12 students. Requires partnerships with schools, application evaluations, and written agreements. Permits rulemaking and mandates annual reporting on the program's progress.
Long summary:
Create the Innovation in Artificial Intelligence Grant Pilot Program to award grants to business entities.

Require the development of AI courses and curricula for K-12 students, emphasizing practical training and career preparation.

Mandate business entities to partner with public or private schools when awarded grants.

Specify application requirements, including program details, school partnerships, and other collaborations.

Evaluate applications based on their potential to achieve program goals and overall viability.

Obligate business entities to enter a written agreement with the office detailing grant specifics before receiving funds.

Authorize the office to establish rules for application procedures, grant awarding, and post-award reporting.

Include an annual written update on the program in the report outlined in Section 63N-1a-306.

Official name: Massachusetts House Bill 4885
Casual name: Massachusetts H 4885 (Firearm Laws), Section 148
Short summary: Establishes a special legislative commission to investigate emerging firearm technologies, including the risks and impacts of using digital firearm manufacturing code for machine learning and AI. Requires a report with findings and recommendations by March 1, 2025.
Long summary:
Establishes a special legislative commission to study emerging firearm technology, including personalized and microstamp technologies.

Requires the commission to evaluate the risks associated with using digital firearm manufacturing codes for machine learning and artificial intelligence.

Mandates the investigation of the accuracy, effectiveness, and utility of personalized firearm and microstamp technologies.

Instructs the commission to review any legal or constitutional issues relating to these technologies.

Tasks the commission with evaluating the commercial availability of these technologies for new and existing firearms.

Directs the commission to assess the feasibility and utility of a tax incentive program for personalized firearm technology.

Obliges the commission to submit its study and recommendations, including legislative suggestions, by March 1, 2025.

Official name: An Act Authorizing And Adjusting Bonds Of The State And Concerning Provisions Related To State And Municipal Tax Administration, General Government And School Building Projects
Casual name: Connecticut H 5524 (General Government And School Building Projects), Sec. 143-144
Short summary: Establishes an AI education tool pilot program for selected school districts, requiring compliance with privacy laws. Provides professional development for educators on using AI tools in classrooms, focusing on safety, benefits, and legal compliance.
Long summary:
Administers an artificial intelligence education tool pilot program by the Department of Education for the fiscal year ending June 30, 2025.

Awards a grant by the Commissioner of Education for implementing an AI tool in classrooms, involving five selected school districts.

Requires the inclusion of at least one rural, suburban, and urban district, reflecting state diversity.

Mandates compliance of the AI tool with laws on AI use and student data privacy, including the Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act.

Defines "artificial intelligence" as technology using data to train algorithms for tasks usually associated with human intelligence.

Provides professional development for educators in the pilot program, covering AI tool utilization and privacy laws.

Trains educators on AI benefits for instruction, student learning, academic achievement, and workforce development.

Official name: California AB 2355 (2024), Section 8
Casual name: California AB 2355 (AI in political advertisements), Sec 8
Short summary: Requires political advertisements generated or altered by AI to disclose “Ad generated or substantially altered using artificial intelligence.” Permits enforcement actions for non-compliance. Defines AI-related terms and specifies disclosure methods. Exempts service providers' rights under Section 230.
Long summary:
Requires committees creating or distributing qualified political advertisements to include a disclosure stating, “Ad generated or substantially altered using artificial intelligence,” in a clear and conspicuous manner.

Mandates that the disclosure be displayed or spoken as specified in existing sections of the Government Code.

Excludes alteration or negation of rights under Section 230 of Title 47 of the U.S. Code for interactive service providers.

Authorizes the Commission to seek injunctive relief or pursue administrative or civil remedies if committees fail to comply with disclosure requirements.

Defines "artificial intelligence" and specifies conditions under which media is considered "generated or substantially altered using artificial intelligence.”

Clarifies that immaterial alterations, such as cosmetic adjustments or resizing, do not qualify as being substantially altered by artificial intelligence.

Specifies that non-compliance does not constitute a misdemeanor.

Official name: Michigan House Bill 5507
Casual name: Michigan H 5507 (School Aid)
Short summary: Allocates $500,000 for a statewide AI research hub, enhancing expertise on responsible AI use in schools. Supports virtual education, best practices, and policy recommendations. Fosters innovation in instructional technology and virtual learning models for Michigan's education system.
Long summary:
Allocates up to $500,000 to enhance awareness and responsible use of artificial intelligence in schools.

Requires Michigan Virtual University to integrate AI principles in its training programs for educational personnel.

Mandates the Michigan Virtual Learning Research Institute to research and recommend virtual education delivery models leveraging AI.

Instructs the institute to analyze and report on AI-enhanced virtual learning effectiveness by reviewing data from virtual course providers.

Encourages partnerships with districts to study AI-driven competency-based virtual learning models.

Tasks the institute with policy recommendations to the governor on AI and virtual learning implementation.

Designates funds for AI-related professional development programs for educators and stakeholders.

Requires ongoing reports on the implementation of AI in virtual education, including its effectiveness, with a focus on elevating education delivery models in Michigan.

Supports the dissemination of best practices and standards for AI-integrated virtual learning across the state.

Official name: Maryland House Bill 1390
Casual name: Maryland H 1390 (Public School Construction Funding and Administration), Sec. 5.
Short summary: Requires the Interagency Commission on School Construction to report on the eligibility for school construction funding for AI weapon detection systems by December 15, 2024.
Long summary:
Directs the Interagency Commission on School Construction to report to the General Assembly by December 15, 2024.

Requires the report to assess the eligibility for school construction funding for artificial intelligence weapon detection systems.

Official name: New Mexico Senate Bill 192
Casual name: New Mexico S 192 (Appropriations and Legislative Confidentiality), Section 9.
Short summary: Funds the purchase of artificial intelligence equipment and consultancy services for the ASK academy charter school with $290,000.
Long summary: Appropriates $290,000 for the purchase of artificial intelligence equipment and to contract with consultants at the ASK Academy Charter School in Sandoval County.

Official name: Minnesota House Bill 4772
Casual name: Minnesota H 4772 (Elections policy), Sec. 76-77
Short summary: Criminalizes disseminating deep fakes to influence elections within specified periods. Imposes penalties, including imprisonment and fines, based on offender history and intent. Disqualifies convicted candidates from holding office. Effective July 1, 2024, for crimes thereafter.
Long summary:
Amends Minnesota law to criminalize the dissemination of deep fakes intended to influence elections.

Prohibits the distribution of deep fakes within 90 days before elections or political conventions without consent if intended to harm a candidate or influence election outcomes.

Excludes broadcasters following federal law from these prohibitions.

Imposes penalties for violations, including imprisonment up to five years and fines up to $10,000 based on prior offenses or intent to cause harm.

Requires that candidates convicted under this law forfeit their nomination or office.

Disqualifies individuals convicted under this law from holding certain public offices.

Sets the effective date for July 1, 2024, applicable to crimes committed thereafter.

Official name: Executive Order on Advancing United States Leadership in Artificial Intelligence Infrastructure (EO 14141)
Casual name: Executive Order on Advancing United States Leadership in Artificial Intelligence Infrastructure (EO 14141)
Short summary: Requires federal agencies to identify, solicit proposals for, and lease federal lands suitable for AI infrastructure and related clean energy projects. Requires the Secretary of State to develop a plan to coordinate AI infrastructure development with U.S. allies and partners, including workforce development.
Long summary:
Requires federal agencies to identify, solicit proposals for, and lease federal lands suitable for AI data centers and related clean energy projects, with the aim of full-capacity operation by the end of 2027.

Emphasizes high labor standards and community collaboration, among other factors, in the selection of sites for projects to account for community health, public safety, and ecological risks and harms.

Mandates cybersecurity, AI model evaluation, supply-chain protection, and other requirements for AI infrastructure developed pursuant to the executive order.  

Directs the Secretary of Energy to facilitate grid interconnection for AI infrastructure developed pursuant to the executive order. 

Calls for an expedited permitting processing for federal sites hosting AI infrastructure and related clean energy projects.  

Directs relevant agencies to take additional enumerated measures to expedite permitting and power procurement for AI infrastructure nationwide.

Requires the Secretary of State to develop a plan for robust engagement with allies on global AI infrastructure development, which includes exchanging best practices for workforce development and ensuring security and resilience in international supply chains.

Official name: Minnesota Senate Bill 2909
Casual name: Minnesota S 2909 (State Government)
Short summary: Require the Minnesota Fusion Center (MNFC) to annually report on the use of surveillance technologies, including artificial intelligence, types of activities monitored, and inter-agency data sharing. Involves AI governance and data practices oversight.
Long summary:
Requires the superintendent to prepare an annual report on the Minnesota Fusion Center (MNFC), detailing its general activities and monitored types, including AI or social media analysis tools.

Mandates inclusion of the MNFC's budget, staffing, and the technological tools utilized, specifically AI and data analysis technologies.

Obliges reporting on audit findings, data requests, suspicious activity reports (SARs), and the impact of AI tools in processing these reports.

Specifies the report must contain information on the MNFC's collaborations with local, state, and federal agencies, including data sharing and investigations aided by SARs and requests for information (RFIs).

Ensures the report's availability to relevant legislative committees and online by February 15 each year, starting in 2024.

Official name: Wyoming House Bill 1
Casual name: Wyoming H 1 (General Government Appropriations), Section 067
Short summary: Allocates $2.5 million to build AI expertise at the University of Wyoming, conditioned on equal matching funds from non-public entities. Excludes funds from future budgets to encourage temporary, targeted development efforts in AI infrastructure and capability.
Long summary:
Appropriates $2.5 million for building artificial intelligence expertise at the University of Wyoming, requiring a matching ratio of 1:1 from non-public sources.

Specifies that the AI-related funding should not be included in the university's standard budget for the next fiscal biennium.

Conditions the use of AI funds upon securing matched contributions from individuals, partnerships, corporations, or other entities.

Prohibits the transfer or expenditure of AI appropriation for any purpose other than building AI expertise.

Includes intent for developing AI expertise funding to be temporary and contingent on nonstate matching funds.

States that the appropriation's intent is not to support any office or program related to diversity, equity, and inclusion.

Overall, emphasizes the development of AI expertise through matched funding, with specific conditions and limitations on its use and future budget inclusion.

Official name: New York Assembly Bill A8808A
Casual name: New York A 8808, Part MM (Artificial Intelligence Deceptive Practices Act)
Short summary: Enacts the "Artificial Intelligence Deceptive Practices Act." Expands privacy rights against unauthorized use of likenesses through AI. Establishes liability for creating or distributing deceptive AI-altered media, particularly in election contexts. Defines penalties and enforcement mechanisms, including damages and injunctions.
Long summary:
Enacts laws addressing artificial intelligence (AI) deceptive practices to protect privacy and combat unauthorized digital alterations. 

Defines "digitization" as using AI and other technologies to alter realistic images or audio. 

Amends civil rights laws to establish a private right of action for false light invasion of privacy and unauthorized use of likeness or voice, including digitally altered depictions. 

Requires platforms and individuals to obtain consent before disseminating digitized images or audio depicting individuals in sensitive contexts without consent.

Mandates disclosures for political communications produced with digitized deceptive media within 60 days of an election, excluding satire, parody, or news reporting.

Prohibits coercion or impersonation using digitization, with heightened penalties for identity theft involving AI-altered representations. 

Allows legal actions against those intentionally causing emotional, financial, or social harm via AI-fabricated media. 

Incorporates these provisions into criminal and election laws, with penalties for violations and the possibility for injunctive relief.

Official name: South Carolina 2023 H 4754 (Section 4)
Casual name: South Carolina 2023 H 4754 (Sec 4)
Short summary: Holds licensees accountable for work produced using AI or machine learning, treating violations by these programs as direct violations by the licensee.
Long summary:
Amends Article 7, Chapter 57, Title 40 of the S.C. Code by adding Section 40-57-820.

Holds a licensee accountable for all work produced with the assistance of artificial intelligence, machine learning, or similar programs.

Treats violations committed through the use of these technologies as if committed directly by the licensee.

Official name: Oregon Senate Bill 5701
Casual name: Oregon S 5701 (State Financial Administration), Section 488.
Short summary: Increases funding by $100,000 for the University of Oregon Labor Education and Research Center to research AI and automation's potential workforce impacts across Oregon's key economic industries.
Long summary:
Increases the General Fund appropriation to the Higher Education Coordinating Commission for public university programs by $100,000.

Allocates this increased funding to the University of Oregon Labor Education and Research Center.

Directs the Center to research the potential workforce impacts of automation and artificial intelligence across Oregon’s key economic industries.

Official name: G20 Ministerial Statement on Trade and Digital Economy (Section I, Subsection 3 "Human-centered Artificial Intelligence")
Casual name: G20 Ministerial Statement on Trade and Digital Economy (Sec I, Subsec 3 "Human-centered Artificial Intelligence")
Short summary: Endeavors to provide an enabling environment for human-centered AI promoting innovation and investment. Recognizes AI's potential for inclusive growth. Commits to a human-centered approach guided by AI principles. Promotes privacy protection, international cooperation, and AI capacity building.
Long summary:
Endeavors to create an enabling environment for human-centered AI that fosters innovation, investment, digital entrepreneurship, and adoption of AI by MSMEs.

Recognizes AI's potential to promote inclusive economic growth and societal benefits, advancing sustainable and inclusive societies while empowering individuals.

Commits to addressing societal challenges posed by AI, such as transitions in the labor market, privacy, security, and ethical issues, through a human-centered approach guided by the G20 AI Principles.

Stipulates principles of inclusive growth, human-centered values, transparency, robustness, and accountability to maximize AI benefits while minimizing risks, with a focus on international cooperation and inclusion of developing countries.

Affirms the need to promote privacy protection, AI capacity building, and skills development.

Commits to international cooperation in research, policy development, and information sharing through the G20 Repository of Digital Policies and collaborative efforts.

Official name: G20 Ministerial Statement on Trade and Digital Economy (Annex "G20 AI Principles")
Casual name: G20 Ministerial Statement on Trade and Digital Economy (Annex "G20 AI Principles")
Short summary: Encourages stakeholders to engage in responsible AI development for positive societal outcomes. Requires AI systems to be transparent, robust, secure, and accountable. Promotes public and private investment in AI R&D. Supports international cooperation and the development of global standards.
Long summary:
Support the responsible stewardship of trustworthy AI by engaging stakeholders to achieve inclusive growth, sustainable development, and well-being.

Require AI actors to respect human-centered values, including the rule of law, human rights, and democratic values, throughout the AI lifecycle, supported by appropriate safeguards.

Commit AI actors to transparency and explainability, providing clear information to foster AI understanding and accountability.

Ensure robustness, security, and safety of AI systems, with traceability and systematic risk management throughout their lifecycle.

Hold AI actors accountable for aligning with the principles based on roles and context.

Encourage governments to invest in AI R&D, supporting open, unbiased datasets, and fostering a digital AI ecosystem.

Promote an enabling policy environment for AI, ensuring policy adaptation, assessment mechanisms, and readiness for labor market transformation.

Engage in international cooperation to advance AI principles, share AI knowledge, and develop global technical standards.

Encourage multi-stakeholder collaboration to monitor AI developments and ensure adherence to these principles.

Official name: Utah House Bill 534
Casual name: Utah H 534 (Boards and Commissions Modifications), Section 55-56
Short summary: Amends the law to oversee a deep technology initiative, including funding expanded educational programs in areas like artificial intelligence. Requires collaboration with employers and evaluation by an advisory council. Mandates institutions report annually on program outcomes and funding effectiveness.
Long summary:
Amends legislation to define "deep technology" to include artificial intelligence and other emerging technologies.

Mandates the development and oversight of a deep technology talent initiative by the board, effective May 1, 2024.

Requires institutions of higher education to submit proposals for partnering with employers to create programs in deep technology fields.

Stipulates proposal criteria must include program responsiveness to state needs, partnerships, and employer involvement.

Obligates the advisory council to review and prioritize program proposals based on capacity expansion, integration, and cost-effectiveness.

Directs the board to fund approved programs by evaluating recommendations from the advisory council and considering legislative appropriations.

Requires funded institutions to report annually on program activities and outcomes, including student enrollment and employment metrics.

Compels the board to provide an annual report on program successes and challenges to legislative committees.

Official name: Washington Senate Bill 5950
Casual name: Washington S 5950, Sec. 129, Subsection 50
Short summary: Provide $300,000 in 2024 and $500,000 in 2025 for Seattle to lease space for technology startups, focusing on AI, and to develop curricula for worker skills in AI as a business resource.
Long summary:
Allocate $300,000 from the general fund for fiscal year 2024 to the city of Seattle for leasing space dedicated to nonprofit and academic institutions.

Provide $500,000 for fiscal year 2025 for the same purpose.

Focus efforts on incubating technology business startups with an emphasis on artificial intelligence.

Support the development and teaching of curricula aimed at equipping workers with skills to utilize AI as a business resource.

Official name: North Carolina House Bill 259
Casual name: North Carolina Current Operations Appropriations Act of 2023, SECTION 7.36.(h)
Short summary: Allocates $3.2 million to New Hanover and $2 million to Davidson County Schools for AI-powered school safety systems. Requires reporting by January 15, 2025, on participant schools, spending, safety outcomes, AI capabilities, and additional relevant information.
Long summary:
Allocates $3.2 million to New Hanover County Schools and $2 million to Davidson County Schools for an AI School Safety Pilot Program.

Requires use of funds to implement AI technology into existing school safety systems, including access controls, alerting protocols, and intercom systems.

Mandates participating public school units to report to the Joint Legislative Education Oversight Committee by January 15, 2025.

Directs reports to include details on school participation, fund usage, impact on safety outcomes, AI system capabilities compared to traditional safety measures, and any additional relevant information.

Official name: Maryland Senate Bill 360
Casual name: Maryland S 360 (Budget Bill Fiscal Year 2025), Section 21., Subsection (50)
Short summary: Allocates $500,000 to Morgan State University's Center for Equitable AI and Machine Learning Systems, with funds restricted from transfer to other purposes and reverting to the General Fund if unspent.
Long summary:
Adds $500,000 in general funds to support the Center for Equitable Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Systems at Morgan State University.

Prohibits the transfer of unexpended funds to any other purpose.

Requires unspent funds to revert to the General Fund.

Official name: Illinois HB 4844 (2024), Section 90 ("Generative AI and Natural Language Processing Task Force")
Casual name: Illinois HB 4844, Sec 90 ("Generative AI and Natural Language Processing Task Force")
Short summary: Establishes the Generative AI and Natural Language Processing Task Force to recommend legislation, assess public service impacts, protect rights, and address cybersecurity challenges of generative AI. Requires a report to the Governor and General Assembly by December 31, 2024.
Long summary:
Amends the Department of Innovation and Technology Act by establishing the Generative AI and Natural Language Processing Task Force.

Mandates the Task Force to investigate and report on generative AI and natural language processing software.

Comprises the Task Force of government officials, educators, cybersecurity experts, AI experts, business and labor association members, and other stakeholders.

Requires the Task Force to hold at least five public meetings across various Illinois regions in a hybrid format.

Obligates the Task Force to recommend legislation to protect consumer information, suggest model policies for AI use in schools, and assess AI's impact on public services, civil rights, the workforce, and cybersecurity.

Instructs the Department to provide administrative and technical support to the Task Force.

Requires the Task Force to file a report by December 31, 2024, with the Governor and the General Assembly summarizing its findings and recommendations.

Official name: Virginia 2025-HB2094
Casual name: Virginia HB2094 (High-Risk Artificial Intelligence Developer And Deployer Act)
Collections: Editors' Picks;U.S. state and local documents
Short summary: Establishes operating standards for developers and deployers of high-risk AI systems, including mandatory risk management and impact assessments. Requires disclosures of potential algorithmic discrimination risks.
Long summary:
Enacts stringent frameworks for the development and deployment of high-risk AI systems in Virginia.

Defines consequential decisions as those which may affect access to the justice system, education, employment, and financial or other such services.

Defines high-risk AI systems as those used in a consequential decision, excluding systems restricted to narrows tasks, pattern detection, and human assistance.

Mandates developers to disclose intended uses, data inputs, potential risks of algorithmic discrimination, and performance evaluations to deployers.

Requires deployers to implement risk management policies, conduct pre and post deployment impact assessments, and disclose AI system interactions to consumers, especially when making consequential decisions.

Establishes a duty of care for developers and deployers to protect against algorithmic discrimination.

Outlines civil penalties for violations, including a fine up to $1,000, with enforcement authority granted exclusively to the Attorney General.

Provides exemptions for various sectors and situations, including federal compliance and actions in the public interest.

Allows for updates post-modification of AI systems and sets guidelines for consumer notifications and appeal processes in case of adverse decisions due to AI use.

Official name: Artificial Intelligence Safety Commitments (China AI Industry Alliance)
Casual name: Artificial Intelligence Safety Commitments (China AI Industry Alliance)
Short summary: Encourages industry self-regulation through the establishment of AI safety and risk management teams, rigorous model testing, data protection measures, infrastructure security, enhanced transparency, and safety research. Signed by major companies, including Alibaba, Baidu, Huawei, Tencent, and others, to uphold AI for good principles.
Long summary:
Calls for the establishment of safety teams and risk management mechanisms for AI safety and security.

Mandates safety and security testing for AI models, prioritizing evaluations in critical domains.

Implements data security measures to protect training and operational data, ensuring privacy and accuracy.

Enhances infrastructure security through regular penetration tests and incident response protocols.

Requires transparency in AI model capabilities, limitations, and risk disclosures.

Advances research in AI safety and security, addressing potential biases and risks in frontier applications.

Signatories include Alibaba, Baidu, Huawei, Tencent, and other major Chinese tech companies.

Official name: Framework for Artificial Intelligence Diffusion
Casual name: AI Diffusion Rule (US BIS)
Collections: Editors' Picks;U.S. regulations, executive orders, and agency policies
Short summary: Controls the export of AI chips and models outside the United States. Establishes a country-based tiering system to determine applicable restrictions, exceptions, and conditions, including security and reporting safeguards. Creates validated end-user programs to govern exports of AI chips to most countries. Defines an adjustable compute-based threshold for export-controlled AI models.
Long summary:
Imposes export controls on AI models, excluding publicly available models, and expands licensing requirements for AI chips to additional countries.

Establishes three tiers of countries for purposes of determining applicable export restrictions, including eighteen countries able to import AI chips without restrictions (tier 1); countries able to import AI chips through validated end-user (VEU) programs, limited-volume exceptions, and licensing, but otherwise subject to restrictions (tier 2); and countries subject to ongoing restrictions, including arms-embargoed countries (tier 3).

Establishes a Data Center Validated End User (DC VEU) program to govern compute exports related to major AI infrastructure development in second-tier countries, with different pathways and restrictions depending on data center operator nationality. Imposes cybersecurity, physical and personnel security, and sourcing mandates on DC VEU participants, as well as reporting requirements.

Imposes specific computing power deployment restrictions on companies operating in second-tier countries, including relative ratios and numerical caps.

Controls the export of AI models developed using compute above a given threshold, to be adjusted over time based on assessments of publicly available models. Requires cloud computing providers to implement security measures preventing the unauthorized training of models otherwise subject to export controls.

Official name: Freedom Online Coalition Joint Statement on Responsible Government Practices for AI Technologies
Casual name: Freedom Online Coalition Joint Statement on Responsible Government Practices for AI Technologies
Short summary: Urges governments to align AI practices with human rights laws, assess and monitor high-risk AI systems for impacts on equity, fairness, and safety. Requires public disclosure for risks, remedies for harms, support for personnel training, and secure AI procurement processes.
Long summary:
Endorses the responsible use and regulation of AI to enhance public service delivery while safeguarding human rights, as reiterated by the Freedom Online Coalition (FOC).

Acknowledges the potential risks of AI misuse, including bias, privacy violations, and exacerbation of inequalities, especially for marginalized populations.

Urges governments to adhere to international legal obligations, including the Universal Declaration of Human Rights, when employing AI systems.

Mandates assessment and management of AI risks in high-risk contexts.

Advocates for continuous monitoring and external audits of AI systems to maintain fairness and rights protection.

Mandates support for adequate training for personnel involved in AI to comprehend its impact on human rights.

Demands transparency, public consultation on AI uses, and mechanisms for public feedback and opting out of AI-enabled decisions.

Calls for effective remedies and redress for individuals harmed by AI systems.

Incorporates these practices into government procurement to ensure AI trustworthiness and conformity with human rights standards.

Defines high-risk AI as those that may impact human rights and safety with likely applications in healthcare, law enforcement and justice, and provision of public benefits.

Official name: Guidance on Algorithmic Discrimination and the New Jersey Law Against Discrimination
Casual name: Guidance on Algorithmic Discrimination and the New Jersey Law Against Discrimination
Short summary: Prohibits algorithmic discrimination under New Jersey's Law Against Discrimination (LAD) by covered entities. Holds entities liable for discriminatory outcomes from automated tools. Addresses disparate treatment, disparate impact, and failure to provide reasonable accommodations related to AI use.
Long summary:
Clarifies that the New Jersey Law Against Discrimination (LAD) applies to algorithmic discrimination through automated decision-making tools similarly to traditional discrimination.

Prohibits any form of discrimination facilitated by automated tools, regardless of intent or third-party involvement in tool development.

Requires covered entities, like employers and housing providers, to avoid disparate treatment and disparate impact discrimination in using these tools.

Mandates that entities provide reasonable accommodations to avoid discriminatory impacts, even if automated tools fail to recognize accommodations.

Holds entities liable for discrimination resulting from their use of third-party developed tools, emphasizing the importance of evaluating these tools for bias.

Encourages developers and vendors to design and test automated tools to minimize discriminatory outcomes, thus reducing liability risk under the LAD.

Reiterates the LAD's comprehensive protections against discrimination, regardless of the technology used, and provides resources for understanding these protections.

Official name: AMBITIOUS, SAFE, RESPONSIBLE: Our approach to the delivery of AI-enabled capability in Defence
Casual name: AMBITIOUS, SAFE, RESPONSIBLE: Our approach to the delivery of AI-enabled capability in Defence
Short summary: Commits the Ministry of Defense to exploiting AI comprehensively; requires consideration of algorithmic bias, responsibility and accountability, unpredictability, unintended consequences and incentives, people implications, and human control for AI; mandates following the five AI ethical principles.
Long summary:
Adopts an ambitious approach to AI-enabled capabilities for Defense, aiming for comprehensive exploitation.

Addresses key challenges such as algorithmic bias, responsibility and accountability, and people implications that teams at Defense must take into consideration. 

Emphasizes the safe, legal, and ethical development and use of AI for Defense. 

Creates a governance framework involving senior defense officials and specialized units to ensure regulatory compliance, policy development, and oversight. 

Requires an implementation approach that is outward-facing, applied across the entire AI system lifecycle, and context-specific.

Establishes five ethical principles for AI in Defense, including human-centricity, responsibility, and understanding.

Explains the role of the AI Ethics Advisory Panel, which is responsible for advising the 2nd Permanent Secretary for Defense. 

Reaffirms the UK's commitment against developing fully autonomous weapon systems, advocating for international cooperation to create norms, positive obligations, and global governance related to autonomy in weapons systems.

Official name: New York 2025-A768
Casual name: New York 2025-A768 (NY AI Consumer Protection Act)
Short summary: Establishes AI consumer protection with documentation, audits, and risk management requirements for high-risk AI decision systems. Mandates developer disclosures, consumer notifications, and opportunities to contest AI-based decisions.
Long summary:
Enacts the New York Artificial Intelligence Consumer Protection Act to manage risks of  AI decision systems. 

Requires developers to ensure systems do not cause algorithmic discrimination through regular audits, starting January 2027. 

Mandates developers provide comprehensive technical documentation and risk assessments to deployers. 

Obligates deployers to notify consumers when high-risk AI systems make significant decisions affecting them and to offer explanation and recourse for adverse decisions. 

Imposes a risk management program for deployers to identify and mitigate potential discrimination. 

Allows the attorney general to enforce compliance through audits and penalties, with the possibility of corrective actions to avoid litigation. 

Establishes exceptions for preempting federal law compliance and protects trade secrets from disclosure.

Official name: JSP 936 V1.1 Dependable Artificial Intelligence (AI) in Defence (part 1: directive)
Casual name: JSP 936 V1.1 Dependable Artificial Intelligence (AI) in Defence (UK MoD)
Short summary: Requires the Ministry of Defense’s development and use of AI to be governed by national and international law and to align with the principles of human centricity, responsibility, understanding, bias and harm mitigation, and reliability; provides guidance on AI ethics governance, AI lifecycle agnostic requirements, quality, safety, and security, and AI assurance.
Long summary:
Explains the Ministry of Defense’s adopted definition of AI, the scope of the document, and the delegation of responsibilities for TLB Holders and Chief Executives, and requires the JSP to be applied by all MOD staff in all phases of the AI lifecycle.

Requires the MOD’s development and use of AI to be governed by national and international law and to align with the principles of human centricity, responsibility, understanding, bias and harm mitigation, and reliability. 

Provides requirements related to AI ethics governance, including that each TLB appoint a RAISO and that large AI projects with significant ethical risks have an ethics manager and an independent ethics assurance mechanism.

Addresses the teaming of humans with AI by mandating the adoption of a Human Centered Design approach across AI systems’ lifecycles, encouraging the identification of AI systems’ workforce implications, and providing guidance on the training implications of AI.

Explains AI development and use lifecycle concepts, such as DevOps, and provides guidance on lifecycle agnostic requirements, including those related to planning, data collection and control, and AI verification and validation.

Requires safety risk analysis, security risk analysis, and other measures to ensure  requirements related to AI quality, safety, and security. 

Obliges AI suppliers to deliver products at a level of confidence that is commensurate with their use, and provides guidance on AI assurance.

Official name: Provisions Pertaining to U.S. Investments in Certain National Security Technologies and Products in Countries of Concern
Casual name: Outbound Investment Security Rule (U.S. Treasury)
Collections: Editors' Picks;U.S. regulations, executive orders, and agency policies
Short summary: Defines AI systems and outlines U.S. regulations for investments in national security technologies with countries of concern. Requires notification of certain AI-related transactions, classifies them as notifiable or prohibited and mandates compliance oversight, reporting, and potential penalties for violations.
Long summary:
Implements Executive Order 14105 by requiring U.S. persons to notify the Department of the Treasury about transactions involving national security technologies in "countries of concern," and prohibits certain transactions that pose acute national security threats.

Defines AI systems as machine-based systems capable of making predictions, recommendations, or decisions that affect environments, thereby impacting transactions involving AI development and deployment.

Specifies which transactions are notifiable or prohibited, including those involving AI systems intended for military, intelligence, or surveillance uses as well as those of a specified computing power.

Mandates U.S. persons to file notifications and prohibits knowingly directing others to undertake prohibited transactions.

Details civil and criminal penalties for violations, including fines up to $250,000 or imprisonment up to 20 years.

Outlines exceptions, including certain public securities transactions and specific national interest exemptions.

Permits the Secretary of the Treasury to compel divestment of any prohibited transaction, with the aid of the Attorney General.

Official name: Massachusetts HD 396 (2025-2026)
Casual name: Massachusetts HD396 (An Act to ensure accountability and transparency in artificial intelligence systems)
Short summary: Establishes AI accountability and consumer protection laws in Massachusetts. Requires developers to mitigate algorithmic discrimination risks and disclose them. Mandates deployers implement risk management for high-risk AI. Enforces corporate AI disclosure and consumer notification. Grants authority to Attorney General for enforcement.
Long summary:
Establishes Chapter 93M regarding AI accountability and consumer protection in Massachusetts, defining key terms such as algorithmic discrimination and high-risk AI systems. 

Mandates developers to identify, mitigate, and disclose risks of algorithmic discrimination, and requires public summaries of AI systems and biases.

Compels deployers of high-risk AI systems to implement risk management programs and conduct annual impact assessments, with mandatory consumer notifications on consequential AI decisions.

Requires corporations to disclose their use of AI in consumer targeting and behavior influence, ensuring transparency and consumer rights protection.

Exempts small businesses, low-risk systems, and federally compliant entities from certain provisions.

Empowers the Attorney General to enforce regulations, categorizing violations under unfair trade practices, while providing a defense for proactive compliance.

Permits rulemaking authority for the Attorney General to set compliance and notification standards and mandates a public education campaign on AI consumer rights. 

Specifies varying effective dates for sections within 180 days to one year post-passage.

Official name: New Mexico 2025 HB 60
Casual name: New Mexico AI Act
Short summary: Enacts the Artificial Intelligence Act, requiring developers and deployers of high-risk AI systems to prevent algorithmic discrimination, conduct impact assessments, and disclose risks. Implements risk management and consumer notification protocols. Grants enforcement authority to the state department of justice. Effective July 1, 2026.
Long summary:
Enacts the Artificial Intelligence Act to regulate the use of AI in New Mexico, focusing on high-risk AI systems that make consequential decisions.  

Requires developers to use reasonable care to prevent algorithmic discrimination and disclose known risks to recipients and the state.  

Mandates impact assessments for deployers, updating them annually or upon modification of AI systems.  

Obliges deployers to notify consumers of AI's role in consequential decisions and provide appeal opportunities for adverse outcomes.  

Exempts trade secrets from mandatory disclosure, but requires consumer notification when information is withheld.  

Confers enforcement authority to the state department of justice, allowing consumer civil actions for violations.  

Sets the effective date for July 1, 2026, with rulemaking by the state department of justice due by January 1, 2027.

Official name: Joint Statement on Risk Assessment of Advanced AI Systems (International Network of AI Safety Institutes)
Casual name: Joint Statement on Risk Assessment of Advanced AI Systems (International Network of AI Safety Institutes)
Short summary: Commits to establish a shared scientific basis for AI risk assessments. Requires risk assessments to be actionable, transparent, comprehensive, multistakeholder, iterative, and reproducible. Encourages collaboration among stakeholders and adapting methodologies as AI systems evolve.
Long summary:
Highlights six key aspects for risk assessment of advanced AI systems: actionable, transparent, comprehensive, multistakeholder, iterative, and reproducible.

Commits to developing a shared scientific basis for risk assessments via joint evaluations and cooperative research, allowing flexibility for members to adapt assessments according to international and domestic frameworks.

Emphasizes actionable assessments that inform effective mitigation based on specified criteria, and transparency to ensure evidence-based outcomes and broader understanding.

Advocates for comprehensiveness by linking potential risks to real-world harms and benefits, considering impacts from large-scale AI adoption, and aligning assessments with Sustainable Development Goals.

Stresses a multistakeholder approach, incorporating diverse perspectives from industry, academia, civil society, government, and multiple disciplines.

Calls for iterative assessments, adapting to AI advancements, ongoing safety research, and post-deployment monitoring.

Encourages reproducibility to enhance verification, validation, and interoperability among methods and actors.

Official name: AI Safety Governance Framework v1.0 (National Technical Committee 260 on Cybersecurity of SAC)
Casual name: AI Safety Governance Framework v1.0 (TC260)
Short summary: Establishes principles for AI safety governance and promotes a people-centered approach to AI governance; emphasizes stakeholder cooperation to prevent AI safety risks and outlines safety guidelines for developers, service providers, and users.
Long summary:
Establishes principles for AI safety governance, emphasizing a balance between development and security and involving all stakeholders in a coordinated approach.

Explains AI safety risks, including risks from algorithms, risks from  data, and cybersecurity risks.

Supports the formulation and refinement of comprehensive AI safety and security risk governance mechanisms, including the adoption of a tiered, category-based management and a traceability management system for AI.

Outlines safety guidelines for developers, service providers, users in key sectors, and general users, focusing on ethics, data protection, and risk assessment and mitigation strategies.

Official name: Texas Responsible Artificial Intelligence Governance Act (HB 1709)
Casual name: Texas Responsible AI Governance Act
Short summary: Regulates AI systems in Texas through the Texas Responsible Artificial Intelligence Governance Act. Imposes duties on developers, distributors, and deployers to prevent algorithmic discrimination. Requires high-risk AI systems impact assessments and consumer disclosures. Establishes penalties for noncompliance, prohibits specific AI uses, and introduces an AI sandbox program for controlled testing.
Long summary:
Establishes the Texas Responsible Artificial Intelligence Governance Act to regulate high-risk AI systems and prohibit certain harmful AI uses.

Requires developers, distributors, and deployers of high-risk AI systems to protect consumers from algorithmic discrimination and complete impact assessments.

Mandates the disclosure to consumers when interacting with high-risk AI systems and provides consumer rights to contest decisions made by these systems.

Prohibits AI systems from manipulating behavior, social scoring, using biometric identifiers without consent, inferring sensitive attributes, causing harm, and producing unlawful content.

Creates an Artificial Intelligence Council to guide ethical AI use, issue advisory opinions, and suggest legislative reforms.

Initiates an AI Regulatory Sandbox Program for controlled testing without full compliance.

Implements AI workforce development grants to address workforce gaps and support AI-related education.

Allows enforcement by the Attorney General with civil penalties for non-compliance.

Effective from September 1, 2025.

Official name: Common Intelligence Community Interim Guidance Regarding the Acquisition and Use of Foundation AI Models
Casual name: Common Intelligence Community Interim Guidance Regarding the Acquisition and Use of Foundation AI Models
Collections: U.S. regulations, executive orders, and agency policies
Short summary: Provides guidance for the Intelligence Community on acquiring, modifying, and using foundation AI models while ensuring compliance with Attorney General guidelines. Requires lawful acquisition, safeguards for U.S. person information, and regular reviews to address privacy implications.
Long summary:
Provides interim guidance for the Intelligence Community (IC) on acquiring and using foundation AI models, focusing on model acquisition, modification, and outputs.

Requires model acquisition to comply with legal standards, ensuring alignment with established IC frameworks.

Mandates prompts and outputs be used for authorized purposes and treated as collected data when applicable, ensuring compliance with existing guidelines on retention and dissemination.

Emphasizes the need for oversight mechanisms to track U.S. person information used or retrieved from AI models.
