Official name: The City of Tulsa Municipal Court Rules 2024
Casual name: The City of Tulsa Municipal Court Rules 2024, Rule 25. "Generative AI"
Short summary: Require parties using generative AI for court documents to disclose its use and name the specific tool. Include examples like ChatGPT, Bing AI, or Google Bard. Ensure compliance with existing filing standards under 12 O.S. §2011.
Long summary:
Require any attorney, pro se defendant, victim, or other party using generative AI in drafting documents for the Municipal Criminal Court to disclose the use of such AI.

Specify the name of the generative AI tool used in the filing.

Include ChatGPT, Bing AI, and Google Bard under the definition of generative AI.

Maintain the applicability of 12 O.S. §2011 to all filings, ensuring compliance is certified by the person signing the document.

Official name: Los Angeles AI Roadmap 2024, Best Practices and Roadmap
Casual name: Los Angeles AI Roadmap 2024, Best Practices and Roadmap
Short summary: Emphasizes AI training for employees and IT developers. Requires departments to set clear objectives before AI deployment and conduct proofs of concept. Advises AI tools remain secure, ethical, and data privacy is protected. Encourages transparency and human-centric AI use.
Long summary:
Establishes two distinct user segments for AI tools: City Employees and IT Professionals, requiring tailored training for each group.

Instructs non-technical department leadership to set clear goals before AI tool deployment.

Encourages piloting AI tools to understand their strengths and weaknesses before widespread use.

Anticipates most AI tools will be accessed through existing software platforms.

Emphasizes the need for adaptable AI tools that align with evolving data and requirements.

Advises staying current on AI tools and frameworks for maximized effectiveness.

Requires comprehensive AI training for both users and developers, highlighting security and ethical considerations.

Insists on cybersecurity and data privacy for AI implementation.

Highlights AI as a long-term investment needing high-quality data and data transparency.

Asserts City data ownership and the need for ethical, transparent, and human-centered AI tools.

Outlines a roadmap for training, deploying AI tools, and developing ethics guidelines for the City.

Official name: Kendall County Generative AI Chatbot Usage Policy
Casual name: Kendall County Generative AI Chatbot Usage Policy
Short summary: Regulates use of generative AI chatbots for county work. Prohibits sharing proprietary or confidential data. Requires Executive approval and accuracy checks on AI-generated content. Mandates training for employees. Enforces ethical use, with potential disciplinary action for violations.
Long summary:
Outlines the use of generative AI chatbots, like ChatGPT and Bard, for Kendall County employees. 

Prohibits using county email addresses and submitting proprietary or confidential data into these platforms. 

Requires employees to consult with Executives regarding chatbot use, who may approve, deny, or modify usage parameters. 

Mandates proper citation and accuracy checks for AI-generated content. 

Enforces disciplinary actions, including termination, for policy violations or inappropriate use, ensuring ethical compliance with conduct and antidiscrimination policies. 

Instructs employees to undergo training on proper chatbot use. 

Applies Technology and Record Retention Policies when using AI with County equipment. 

Addresses training queries with Human Resources or the Information, Communication and Technology Department Director.

Official name: Miami Dade County Artificial Intelligence Report: Policies, Principles & Guidelines
Casual name: Miami Dade County Artificial Intelligence Report, County Administrative Order (EMPLOYEE USE OF AI)
Short summary: Requires Miami-Dade County employees under the Mayor to use artificial intelligence (AI) tools ethically and responsibly. Instructs the Human Resources (HR) and Information Technology Departments (ITD) to provide necessary training and oversight. Directs ITD to oversee AI procurement, deployment, and data security. Establishes guidelines for AI training, risk assessments, data handling, and community engagement, ensuring compliance with privacy and ethical standards.
Long summary:
Guides Miami-Dade County employees in the responsible and ethical use of AI, focusing on transparency, data security, and privacy.

Requires adherence to public records laws and ethical guidelines.

Instructs the Human Resources and Information Technology Departments to provide AI training, emphasizing ethical use, bias recognition, and data management.

Mandates risk assessments prior to AI implementation.

Tasks the Information Technology Department with overseeing AI procurement, deployment, and system security.

Establishes protocols for data governance, encryption, and privacy.

Defines roles for directors, supervisors, and employees in AI project management, ethical leadership, and compliance.

Implements regular audits, assessments, and vendor evaluations to maintain AI system integrity.

Encourages community engagement and stakeholder discussions on AI deployment.

Commits to continuous improvement with annual assessments and updates by the IT Department, involving multidisciplinary teams and beta testing initiatives.

Provides resources through the IT Department’s website.

Official name: City of San Jose Artificial Intelligence Policy 1.7.12
Casual name: San Jose AI Policy 1.7.12
Short summary: Defines roles for AI governance, requiring San José to align AI usage with security and privacy policies. Prohibits real-time biometric identification, emotion analysis, and social scoring. Requires an AI review, technical documentation, and immediate reporting of harmful incidents. Enforces policy compliance with potential disciplinary actions.
Long summary:
Establishes a governance framework for AI use in San José, aligning with the City’s data security, privacy, and existing policies.

Directs the Chief Information Officer (CIO) to manage the AI Handbook, with the City Information Security Officer (CISO) and City Digital Privacy Officer (CDPO) ensuring system security, resilience, privacy, and policy compliance.

Requires users to uphold guiding principles such as effectiveness, transparency, equity, accountability, privacy, and security.

Prohibits AI applications involving real-time biometric identification, emotion analysis, fully automated impactful decisions, social scoring, and cognitive manipulation.

Mandates reporting AI-caused harm within 24 hours.

Implements sunset procedures for outdated AI systems.

Obliges compliance with public records laws and City’s transparency policies.

Enforces policy adherence, with violations subject to disciplinary action.

Requires Finance to obtain AI usage disclosures in procurement.

Defines AI and related system terms within the policy context.

Official name: State of Ohio Administrative Policy on AI
Casual name: Ohio State Administrative Policy on AI
Short summary: Establishes statewide governance for AI use by Ohio state agencies, including AI solution development, procurement, security, and privacy requirements. Mandates AI Council oversight, training for workforce use, vendor compliance, and ongoing monitoring for data quality and bias.
Long summary:
Establishes statewide governance for AI implementation, emphasizing data protection and quality in Ohio.

Requires state agencies to document and approve AI use cases and submit them to the AI Council for approval.

Mandates testing and ongoing monitoring to prevent bias and errors in AI outputs.

Requires human oversight to verify AI decisions, especially those impacting legal or regulatory areas.

Dictates AI procurement procedures, ensuring security, privacy, and proper data licensing.

Charges the Ohio Department of Administrative Services with developing AI training for state workers, emphasizing ethical considerations and the responsible use of generative AI.

Mandates security and privacy controls, prohibiting the input of sensitive data into generative AI without consent.

Instructs the Chief Data Officer Council to establish data governance for AI, focusing on data integrity and regulation.

Assigns the multi-agency AI Council with overseeing AI use, evaluating societal impacts, and maintaining AI solution repositories.

Official name: Rhode Island Executive Order 24-06
Casual name: Rhode Island EO 24-06 (AI and Data Centers of Excellence)
Short summary: Establishes a Chief Data Officer within the Department of Administration to oversee a Data Center of Excellence. Requires the creation of an AI Center of Excellence to develop AI policies, ethics, and training. Forms an AI Task Force to assess AI risks and opportunities.
Long summary:
Establishes a single state data governance structure, creating a federated data platform to manage data effectively across Rhode Island state agencies.

Mandates the Department of Administration (DOA) to appoint a Chief Data Officer (CDO) to oversee the Data Center of Excellence, enhancing data operations and readiness for AI integration.

Creates an AI Center of Excellence to formulate AI policies and ethics, minimizing bias and guiding AI implementation in state operations.

Directs all state agencies to participate in the federated data system, sharing data for policy analysis and exploring AI applications to improve operations.

Forms an AI Task Force to assess AI risks and opportunities, providing advice to policymakers.

Instructs the Department of Labor and Training (DLT), Rhode Island Department of Education (RIDE), and Office of Post Secondary Commissioner (OPC) to develop AI training programs for state and private-sector workers, integrating AI topics into existing curricula.

Official name: New Jersey Executive Order No. 346
Casual name: New Jersey EO 346
Short summary: Establishes an Artificial Intelligence Task Force to study AI technologies, assess societal impacts, and recommend government actions for ethical use. Involves Executive Branch officials, allows public and expert consultation, and requires a report to the Governor within 12 months.
Long summary:
Establishes the Artificial Intelligence Task Force responsible for studying AI technologies' societal impacts and recommending government actions for ethical use.

Includes the Chief Technology Officer, Chief Innovation Officer, Chief Executive Officer of the Economic Development Authority, and other key officials as members.

Authorizes the Task Force to gather data from state departments and agencies.

Requires executive branch cooperation and assistance to fulfill the Task Force's objectives.

Directs the Task Force to consult with academics, industry professionals, and consumer advocates.

Issues a reporting requirement to the Governor within 12 months of the Order’s enactment, to be shared with the Legislature and public.

Permits additional appointments by the Governor as necessary.

Clarifies the Task Force’s advisory nature in providing advice to the Governor and executive branches. 

Takes effect immediately upon issuance.

Official name: State of North Dakota Artificial Intelligence Policy
Casual name: North Dakota Artificial Intelligence Policy
Short summary: Establishes policy for North Dakota's AI use to ensure safety, privacy, and ethical standards. Requires AI reliability, transparency, and accountability. Mandates risk management, privacy assessments, and compliance with legal frameworks. Directs NDIT to provide training and evaluate new AI technologies.
Long summary:
Requires entities in North Dakota’s executive branch to ensure AI technologies are reliable, valid, and transparent, with clear explanations of AI use and marked AI decision-making involving medium to high-risk data.

Mandates accountability for secure AI development, compliance with laws and standards, and prohibits using state-managed passwords for third-party apps.

Incorporates NDIT’s Security Risk Management Program in AI operations, mandates regular risk assessments, and restricts inputting moderate/high-risk data into public AI/ML services.

Requires NDIT to offer AI training and workshops, maintain an AI technology inventory, and ensure entities use vetted technologies.

Demands compliance with data protection laws, ethical AI use, and fairness without bias.

Stipulates AI must adhere to legal standards, including copyright, with review of licensing agreements and recognition that AI-generated content lacks copyright protection.

Official name: California Executive Order N-12-23
Casual name: California Executive Order N-12-23 (State use of GenAI)
Short summary: Directs California agencies to draft reports and guidelines on GenAI use, focusing on risks and equitable outcomes. Establishes GenAI pilot projects and partnerships with universities. Promotes workforce training and risk analysis, particularly for infrastructure and regulatory impacts.
Long summary:
Orders California's Government Operations Agency and related entities to draft a report for the Governor within 60 days on beneficial uses and risks of Generative AI (GenAI) by the state.

Requires the California Cybersecurity Integration Center and related bodies to perform a risk analysis by March 2024 on GenAI threats to energy infrastructure, brief the Governor on findings, and make public recommendations for actions to guard against GenAI threats when appropriate. 

Directs various California departments to issue procurement and training guidelines for GenAI by January 2024.

Instructs relevant agencies to develop guidelines by July 2024 for using GenAI tools with vulnerable communities.

Mandates establishment of GenAI pilot project infrastructure by March 2024.

Urges the Governor's Office to partner with UC Berkeley and Stanford to evaluate GenAI's impact.

Tasks legal counsel to assess GenAI's regulatory impact and recommend updates.

Emphasizes collaboration with civil society, academia, and industry experts in drafting guidelines and conducting analyses.

Official name: Maryland State Executive Order 01.01.2024.02
Casual name: Maryland State Executive Order 01.01.2024.02 (Use of AI in Maryland State Government)
Short summary: Establishes an AI Subcabinet to ensure responsible AI use by Maryland state agencies, promoting principles of fairness, transparency, and innovation, while safeguarding privacy and security. Requires development of an AI Action Plan and training programs for state employees.
Long summary:
Establishes an AI Subcabinet to oversee AI use in Maryland state government, fostering principles such as fairness, innovation, privacy, safety, and transparency.

Directs the AI Subcabinet to create an AI Action Plan incorporating NIST’s AI Risk Management Framework to ensure adherence to AI principles.

Instructs the Subcabinet to upskill the state workforce in AI and explore partnerships with external AI talent for targeted projects.

Mandates the study of AI's impact on critical domains, including workforce and economic development, and the provision of recommendations to the Governor and legislature.

Requires the Department of Information Technology to assess infrastructure for safely testing AI prototypes and collaborate with the Department of General Services to create procedures for AI pilots.

Ensures AI initiatives align with state and federal laws and does not impair existing authorities, remaining severable if parts are invalidated.

Official name: Vermont Agency of Digital Services Report regarding Inventory of Artificial Intelligence Usage, pursuant to 3 V.S.A. § 3305
Casual name: Vermont Agency of Digital Services AI Inventory report
Short summary: Requires the Agency of Digital Services to maintain and annually review Vermont's AI inventory. Necessitates identification of problematic AI through bias testing and recommends varied remediation measures. Suggests focusing inventory on AI posing potential risks and monitoring outcomes.
Long summary:
Creates an inventory of automated decision systems used by the government, emphasizing AI's role within human processes, to be maintained by the Division of Artificial Intelligence under the Agency of Digital Services (ADS).

Requires annual updates and reviews for the inventory's accuracy and completeness.

Recommends various remediation measures for systems showing problematic behavior, including process changes upstream or downstream, modifications to the AI, or decommissioning problematic systems.

Advises narrowing the inventory’s scope to focus on AI systems that pose potential risks rather than encompassing all apps and tools.

Highlights the importance of ongoing outcome monitoring over third-party bias testing, advising additional tracking elements for monitoring results and applied remediation.

Proposes focusing on AI system autonomy rather than their decision-making capability, urging identification of agencies directly utilizing these systems.

Official name: North Carolina State Government Responsible Use of Artificial Intelligence Framework
Casual name: North Carolina Government Responsible Use of AI Framework
Short summary: Require state agencies to follow the AI Framework, which emphasizes principles such as human oversight, transparency, security, data privacy, diversity, auditing, and workforce empowerment. Obligate agencies to maintain AI inventories and conduct risk assessments using the NIST AI Risk Management Framework.
Long summary:
Establishes the North Carolina State Government Responsible Use of Artificial Intelligence Framework to ensure ethical AI application across state agencies.

Requires state agencies to follow guidelines which emphasize human-centered AI with oversight ensuring AI use benefits citizens without negatively impacting rights or access to services.

Stipulates transparency, requiring agencies to notify individuals of AI use and its impacts in accessible language.

Mandates robust security measures, ongoing monitoring, and risk assessments to ensure the safety and effectiveness of AI systems.

Demands strict data privacy controls throughout AI lifecycle stages and promotes equitable AI development free from biases.

Insists on accountability where agencies must audit AI use and provide necessary training to personnel.

Empowers staff through AI training to maximize public service effectiveness.

Requires agencies to maintain an AI inventory, report it for transparency, and conduct risk assessments using NIST AI RMF guided by state offices for comprehensive risk management.

Official name: State of New Hampshire Use of Artificial Intelligence Technologies Policy
Casual name: New Hampshire State Use of AI Policy
Short summary: Guides New Hampshire State Employees on ethical AI use, emphasizing human oversight, transparency, and data protection. Requires agency heads to ensure compliance and report AI use. Employs AI for public services under strict ethical guidelines and accountability measures. Maps to NIST standards.
Long summary:
Requires all New Hampshire State Employees to adhere to ethical guidelines for AI use, emphasizing protection of Personal Identifying Information (PII) and sensitive data.

Dictates a human-centric approach, transparency, fairness, privacy, and continuous monitoring in AI deployment for government services.

Allows AI for decision support, public engagement, predictive analytics, image and video analysis, natural language processing, generative content, healthcare, environmental monitoring, and transportation, subject to stringent data protection and human oversight.

Mandates AI systems to explain decisions and actions and undergo ethical reviews when high-risk.

Instructs employees to ensure data protection and adhere to cybersecurity measures per the Statewide Information Security Manual and NIST-800-53 standard.

Requires informing users about AI interactions and data handling.

Assigns Agency heads responsibility for policy adherence, with AI technologies reported to the Business Relationship Management Division.

References New Hampshire AI ethics code and laws related to deepfakes and AI use.

Official name: Alabama Executive Order No. 738
Casual name: Alabama Executive Order No. 738 (PROVIDING FOR THE RESPONSIBLE AND PRODUCTIVE USE OF GENERATIVE AI IN STATE GOV)
Short summary: Establishes a Task Force on Generative AI to encourage responsible use in state agencies. Requires reports on GenAI use by May 2024 and policy recommendations by November 2024. Directs the Office of Information Technology to facilitate GenAI pilot projects and development.
Long summary:
Establishes the Governor’s Task Force on Generative Artificial Intelligence to evaluate and promote the responsible use of GenAI in Alabama's executive-branch agencies.

Requires the Task Force to submit a report by November 30, 2024, detailing GenAI use, risks, and recommendations for its governance framework within state government, including data management, model monitoring, and human oversight.

Instructs each state executive-branch agency to submit an inventory of their GenAI use by May 1, 2024, detailing vendor information, capabilities, and safety measures.

Directs the Office of Information Technology to establish a secure cloud infrastructure for GenAI pilot projects, encouraging state agencies to test and utilize GenAI responsibly.

Encourages state executive-branch agencies to explore GenAI applications in collaboration with experts for improving government services and employee performance.

Specifies that the order affects executive-branch entities and does not create private enforceable rights.

Official name: Wisconsin Executive Order #211
Casual name: Wisconsin Executive Order #211 (Governor’s Task Force on Workforce and Artificial Intelligence)
Short summary: Establishes the Governor’s Task Force on Workforce and Artificial Intelligence, chaired by the Secretary of the Department of Workforce Development. Directs the Task Force to analyze AI's labor market impact, advance equity, and recommend workforce and education policy solutions.
Long summary:
Creates the Governor’s Task Force on Workforce and Artificial Intelligence, chaired by the Secretary of the Department of Workforce Development or a designee.

Appoints additional members from key state departments, educational institutions, and other stakeholders, as determined by the Governor.

Instructs the Task Force to gather and analyze information to produce an advisory action plan for the Governor.

Requires the plan to assess generative AI’s impact on Wisconsin’s labor market, predict future impacts, and identify effects on key industries and skillsets.

Explores initiatives to advance equity and economic opportunity related to AI-driven changes.

Recommends solutions, including policy changes in workforce development and education, based on identified impacts.

Attaches the Task Force to the Department of Workforce Development.

Official name: Mississippi Senate Bill 2062
Casual name: Mississippi Artificial Intelligence in Education Task Force Act (SB 2062)
Short summary: Establishes an AI in Education Task Force to evaluate AI applications in K-12, assess ethical and data privacy implications, and develop policy recommendations. Appoints twelve members and requires reports by December 15, 2024. Dissolves the task force on January 1, 2025.
Long summary:
Establishes an Artificial Intelligence in Education Task Force in Mississippi to evaluate and recommend AI applications in K-12 education.

Requires the State Superintendent of Education to chair the task force, with administrative support provided by the Department of Education.

Comprises twelve members appointed by the Governor, Lieutenant Governor, and Speaker of the House, possessing expertise in education, technology, and data privacy.

Instructs the task force to assess AI's ethical, legal, and data privacy implications, develop policy recommendations, and propose guidelines for AI integration in education.

Tasks the force with identifying workforce needs and strategies for data protection, and fostering collaboration between educational sectors and industries.

Requires submission of interim and final reports detailing findings and recommendations to state officials and public posting of the final report.

Dissolves the task force on January 1, 2025, and takes effect on July 1, 2024.

Official name: Georgia Technology Authority Artificial Intelligence Responsible Use (SS-23-002)
Casual name: Georgia Technology Authority AI Responsible Use
Short summary: Regulates state agencies' ethical, transparent use of AI, prioritizing rights and minimizing harm. Requires GTA oversight in AI deployment, bias identification, privacy protection, training, and misuse reporting. Ensures collaboration transparency, rigorous testing, and contingency planning.
Long summary:
Outlines protocols for the responsible use of AI within Georgia state agencies, focusing on ethics, transparency, and accountability.

Requires agencies to align AI use with legal standards and agency objectives and to report AI tools to the Georgia Technology Authority (GTA).

Mandates testing and monitoring of AI solutions to ensure safety and accuracy, while addressing ethical, legal, and societal implications.

Specifies guidelines for contracting third-party AI services, emphasizing adherence to GTA and NIST standards and practices for responsible AI.

Instructs agencies to identify and mitigate biases and inaccuracies in AI-generated outputs.

Ensures data protection by adhering to Enterprise's Data Protection Policies and obtaining consent for data use.

Stipulates training and awareness for responsible AI use and a system for reporting AI misuse or unintended impacts.

Requires rigorous testing and ongoing monitoring for AI development and collaboration.

Allows State CIO or GTA OIS to terminate the use of AI tools posing risks.

Official name: Measures for Labeling of AI-Generated Synthetic Content
Casual name: Measures for Labeling of AI-Generated Synthetic Content
Short summary: Requires clear labeling of AI-generated synthetic content in China through both explicit user-facing indicators and implicit metadata, mandates service providers and platforms to verify, disclose, and preserve such labels, and ensures compliance through regulatory oversight.
Long summary:
Requires online information service providers to label AI-generated synthetic content with both explicit and implicit labels, ensuring that users can easily recognize AI-generated content.

Mandates explicit, user-visible labeling on text, image, audio, video, and virtual scene content, and requires that such labels remain when content is downloaded, reproduced, or exported.

Instructs service providers to embed implicit labels in digital file metadata, indicating the content’s origin and attributes, and encourages the use of digital watermarks.

Mandates content transmission platforms to verify metadata for implicit labels and add notifications if AI-origin is detected or suspected.

Obligates application distribution platforms to assess apps for compliance with generative AI services and labeling.

Requires service providers to include labeling methods and specifications in user agreements.

Permits exceptions for omitting explicit labels if users assume responsibility, with logs stored for a period of six months.

Requires service providers to comply with relevant laws, administrative regulations, and national standards, and to provide labeling materials during algorithm filings and safety assessments to help prevent illegal or criminal misuse.

Sets penalties for non-compliance to be enforced by the relevant regulatory authorities.

Official name: Texas Responsible Artificial Intelligence Governance Act (HB 149)
Casual name: Texas Responsible AI Governance Act (2025 Update) (HB 149)
Link to document: https://www.legis.state.tx.us/BillLookup/Text.aspx?LegSess=89R&Bill=HB149 - wait until docx version is available
Collections: Editors' Picks;U.S. state and local documents
Short summary: Establishes the Artificial Intelligence Council to regulate AI, preventing harm, discrimination, and privacy infringement, and requires disclosures of AI use to consumers. Establishes the AI Council and Sandbox Program for testing AI systems and authorizes the attorney general to enforce compliance and impose penalties.
Long summary:
Regulates AI systems in Texas under the Texas Responsible Artificial Intelligence Governance Act.

Prohibits AI systems from inciting harm, criminality, manipulation, or discrimination based on political viewpoints or protected classes.

Establishes the Artificial Intelligence Council and a regulatory sandbox program for controlled AI testing.

Requires government disclosure to consumers when AI is used in interactions.

Instructs the attorney general to enforce regulations, manage complaints, and impose fines for non-compliance.

Prohibits government use of AI for social scoring or capturing biometric identifiers in violation of rights.

Mandates that AI must not produce certain unlawful content and must adhere to consumer protections and data privacy.

Official name: Guiding Opinions on Strengthening the Governance of Science and Technology Ethics (Draft for Feedback)
Casual name: Guiding Opinions on Strengthening the Governance of Science and Technology Ethics (Draft for Feedback)
Short summary: Outlines guiding ideology and basic requirements to strengthen ethical S&T governance and self-reliance, emphasizes AI ethical risk assessment, transparency and compliance, and education. Requires universities and businesses to establish ethics review committees for high-risk AI activities.
Long summary:
Designates the National Science and Technology Ethics Commission as responsible for guiding and coordinating AI ethical governance at large, with the Ministry of Science and Technology handling day-to-day affairs.

Requires institutions engaged in AI activities and research to establish S&T ethics review committees, mandates comprehensive reviews for high-risk activities.

Instructs local governments and relevant departments to oversee S&T ethics, including risk assessment and emergency handling in high-risk fields.

Promotes legislative development for S&T ethics, mandates the formulation of specific norms and frameworks with accountability mechanisms for regulation violations. 

Supports educational initiatives in S&T ethics for students and professionals and urges transparent public communication.

Official name: (Trial) Measures for Science and Technology Ethics Reviews (Draft for Feedback)
Casual name: (Trial) Measures for Science and Technology Ethics Reviews (Draft for Feedback)
Short summary: Establishes ethics review committees for S&T activities, including AI, to assess ethical risks. Requires committees to follow principles of independence and transparency and create emergency review procedures and high-risk AI activities.
Long summary:
Establishes that work units engaged in sensitive areas, including AI, must form ethics committees to conduct thorough ethical reviews of S&T activities.

Requires these reviews to ensure AI activities comply with ethics principles, such as fairness and inclusivity.

Stipulates review committees to include diverse experts and provide training on ethics reviews.

Mandates reviews of AI systems and algorithms adhere to national data security and ethical norms, ensuring transparency and reliability.

Grants committees the authority to approve, revise, or reject AI projects based on ethical evaluations.

Introduces simplified and emergency review procedures for low-risk or urgent situations, with detailed conditions.

Calls for procedures to handle violations within AI ethics reviews, including potential penalties.

Insists on a national ethics management registry for committee activities and checklisted high-risk AI activities.

Implies that the Ministry of Science and Technology oversees interpretation and enforcement of these measures, which include significant provisions for governance of AI research and its ethical implications.

Official name: New York SB5959 (2020)
Casual name: New York (2020) SB 5959 (Dissemination of Digital Replica)
Short summary: Establishes liability for unauthorized use of deceased personalities' digital replicas in advertising or scripted works. Requires consent from specified parties and allows transferring rights posthumously. Regulates digitization of sexually explicit depictions without consent, allowing legal action.
Long summary:
Establishes the right of publicity, allowing individuals to control the use of their name, voice, signature, photograph, or likeness, especially for deceased performers and personalities. 

Prohibits unauthorized use of digital replicas in fictional works without clear disclaimers or consent from rights holders. 

Defines penalties for illicit use, including compensatory and punitive damages, and requires proof of unauthorized profits for damages calculation. 

Clarifies exceptions for newsworthy, educational, or political contexts where rights protection does not apply.

Allows a private right of action for individuals depicted in non-consensual, sexually explicit material, prohibiting dissemination.

Requires disclaimers be insufficient defense in unauthorized explicit material cases.

Enables registration of deceased personalities' rights with the Secretary of State.

Establishes a 40-year post-mortem protection limit for these rights.

Specifies severability and additional legal recourse without superseding existing legal protections.

Official name: California 2023 SCR 17 (Relative to AI)
Casual name: California 2023 SCR 17 (Relative to AI)
Short summary: Affirms California Legislature's commitment to President Biden's safe AI vision and "Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights" principles. Commits to examining and implementing these principles in state legislation and policies related to automated systems.
Long summary:
Affirms the California Legislature's commitment to President Biden’s vision for safe AI and the "Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights."

Commits the Legislature to examining and implementing principles, including Safe and Effective Systems, Algorithmic Discrimination Protections, Data Privacy, Notice and Explanation, and Human Alternatives in AI-related policies.

Directs the Secretary of the Senate to transmit copies of this resolution for distribution.

Official name: New York Senate Bill S3971B (2019)
Casual name: New York SB3971 (Temporary AI State Comission)
Short summary: Establishes a temporary commission to study AI, robotics, and automation regulation in New York, including law, liability, employment impact, confidentiality, restrictions, and sector applications. Requires a final report before the act's expiration on December 31, 2020.
Long summary:
Establishes the New York State Artificial Intelligence, Robotics and Automation Commission to study and determine regulations for AI, robotics, and automation.

Tasks the commission with examining current state laws, comparative policies, liability issues, employment impact, confidentiality, restrictions on AI weaponry use, regulatory impacts on the technology industry, and public sector AI applications.

Comprises thirteen members appointed by state government officials and university chancellors.

Authorizes the commission to conduct hearings and gather testimonies to thoroughly analyze relevant AI issues.

Requires the commission to publish a final report for state leadership at least thirty days before the act's expiration.

Sets the act's expiration and repeal date as December 31, 2020.

Official name: West Virginia House Resolution 3
Casual name: West Virginia HR 3 (2024)
Short summary: Creates a Select Committee on Artificial Intelligence within the West Virginia House of Delegates. Charges the committee with receiving testimony, considering legislation, and recommending actions on AI issues. Consists of up to eleven members appointed by the Speaker.
Long summary:
Establishes a Select Committee on Artificial Intelligence within the West Virginia House of Delegates.

Comprises up to eleven members appointed by the Speaker of the House.

Tasks the committee with receiving testimony, considering legislation, and recommending action on AI-related issues in the state.

Subjects the committee to the House rules governing Standing Committees, where applicable.

Official name: Hawaii House Concurrent Resolution 71
Casual name: Hawaii HCR 71 (2024)
Short summary: Request the Department of Health to convene a Stakeholder Working Group to assess language access in healthcare, including AI impacts, and to recommend enhancements. Require a report to the Legislature by 2025 detailing findings, recommendations, and proposed legislation.
Long summary:
Request the Department of Health to convene a Stakeholder Working Group to study language access in health care settings, focusing on the benefits and impacts of technologies such as artificial intelligence.

Instruct the Working Group to assess experiences of limited English proficiency (LEP) populations, existing language services, and disparities in health care access.

Advise the Group to make recommendations for enhancing meaningful access to health care, including options for utilizing AI-based solutions.

Require the Department of Health to invite stakeholders, including LEP individuals, health care providers, insurers, community-based organizations, and language service providers to participate in the Group.

Instruct the Department of Health to submit a report of the Group's findings and recommendations, along with proposed legislation, to the Legislature by a specified deadline.

Official name: New Jersey Assembly Resolution 141 (2024)
Casual name: New Jersey AR 141 (2024)
Short summary: Urges AI platforms to voluntarily prevent and remove deepfake and cheapfake content. Requires transmission of this resolution to AI company CEOs in New Jersey. Highlights harmful impacts of altered media and suggests responsible AI use offers societal benefits.
Long summary:
Urges platforms generating and disseminating deepfake and cheapfake media to voluntarily commit to preventing and removing harmful content.

Defines deepfakes as media that appears to depict authentic speech or conduct through substantial technical means, and cheapfakes as any software-generated audiovisual alteration.

Notes that generative AI platforms can anticipate and prevent the creation of harmful content, with social media and content-sharing forums capable of taking steps to remove such media.

Recognizes the potential negative impact, including impersonation, fraud, and political misinformation, while acknowledging the promise of AI if used responsibly.

Encourages the development of AI accountability and transparency standards, following examples set by the federal government and other states.

Calls for New Jersey to become a leader in responsible media technology through these voluntary commitments.

Official name: Louisiana SCR 49
Casual name: Louisiana (2023) SCR 49 (Study Impact of AI)
Short summary: Urges the Joint Legislative Committee on Technology and Cybersecurity to study AI's impact on operations, procurement, and policy. Requires a written report with recommendations submitted to relevant committees 60 days before Louisiana's 2024 Regular Session. Solicits input from several governmental and industry entities.
Long summary:
Urges the Joint Legislative Committee on Technology and Cybersecurity to study the impact of artificial intelligence on operations, procurement, and policy in Louisiana.

Requires the committee to submit a written report of its findings to the House Committee on Commerce and the Senate Committee on Commerce, Consumer Protection and International Affairs at least sixty days before the 2024 Regular Session.

Instructs the report to propose recommendations and legislative proposals to aid government and businesses in leveraging AI benefits while protecting consumers.

Encourages the committee to seek input from the Department of Economic Development, the office of technology services, Division of Administration, the Department of Justice, and industry partners.

Transmits a copy of the resolution to the Joint Legislative Committee on Technology and Cybersecurity.

Official name: Rhode Island HB 6654 (2022)
Casual name: Rhode Island (2022) HB 6654 (Consumer Protection in Eye Care)
Short summary: Regulates AI devices in eye assessments, requiring synchronous/asynchronous provider-patient interaction, medical history collection, and disclosure about assessment limits. Requires providers to verify patient details, maintain records, adhere to care standards, and not rely solely on assessment data. Authorizes penalties for violations.
Long summary:
Regulates the use of automated or virtual assessment mechanisms, including AI devices, for conducting eye assessments, requiring interaction between the patient and a licensed provider.

Requires the collection of medical history and previous prescriptions and mandates acceptance of certain disclosures about the limitations of such assessments.

Specifies that an assessment mechanism cannot issue initial prescriptions for contact lenses or their renewals without a prior in-person examination within 24 months.

Obliges providers to follow the standard of eye care, verify patient identity and age, and confirm recent in-person examinations before issuing prescriptions.

Imposes a civil penalty up to $10,000 for violations, enforceable by the department of health or attorney general, with rights to an administrative hearing and appeal.

Instructs the department of health to create necessary rules and regulations.

Ensures severability of the act’s provisions if any part is invalidated by a court.

Official name: Maryland 2020 HB49
Casual name: Maryland 2020 HB 49 (Pretrial Risk Scoring Instruments)
Short summary: Requires jurisdictions using pretrial risk scoring instruments for release eligibility to conduct independent validation studies every 3-5 years. Establishes a grant fund for eligible counties to set up or improve pretrial services and conduct these validations.
Long summary:
Require jurisdictions using pretrial risk scoring instruments for determining defendants' pretrial release eligibility to conduct independent validation studies of these tools at least once every three to five years.

Establish a Pretrial Services Program Grant Fund to provide grants to eligible counties for creating or improving pretrial services programs, or for conducting validations of pretrial risk scoring instruments as per § 5–103 of the Criminal Procedure Article.

Require eligible counties applying for grants to provide a description of how their programs meet requirements and information deemed necessary by the Executive Director of the Governor’s Office of Crime Control and Prevention.

Ensure pretrial services programs use validated, evidence-based, race-neutral risk scoring instruments following Maryland Rules, apply effective best practices, and incorporate multiple levels of supervision based on defendant risk scores, including technological and treatment provisions.

Official name: Delaware House Concurrent Resolution 7 (2019-2020)
Casual name: Delaware (2019-20) HCR 7 (Planning for the Impacts of AI on the Workforce)
Short summary: Urges Delaware State Agencies and Government branches to plan for the effects of automation, robotics, and AI. Recognizes the need for daily operational and long-term strategic preparations to limit negative impacts on Delawareans.
Long summary:
Recognizes the potential impact of robotics, automation, and AI on the workforce and the importance of planning for their effects.

Requires Delaware State Agencies and government branches to implement daily operational and long-term strategic planning to mitigate negative impacts of these technologies.

Encourages consideration of legal and ethical implications in AI development without hindering innovation.

Emphasizes the need for AI applications to prioritize security, ethics, dignity, autonomy, and self-determination.

Highlights the risk of technological displacement and its effects on public and private sectors.

Aims to prepare Delaware for changes in employment due to advancements in AI and automation.

Official name: New Hampshire H1688 (2024)
Casual name: New Hampshire H1688 (AI Use By State Agencies)
Short summary: Prohibits state agencies from using AI for unlawful discrimination, public surveillance without a warrant, and malicious deepfakes. Requires human review for irreversible AI decisions. Mandates disclosure for AI-generated content. Ensures compliance via audits and annual reporting to authorities.
Long summary:
Define "artificial intelligence," "generative AI," "deepfake," and "state agency" in relation to AI governance.

Apply provisions to all state agency computer systems, with exceptions for research and personal use systems.

Prohibit uses by state agencies for classifying individuals leading to discrimination, surveillance in public spaces without a warrant, and malicious use of deepfakes.

Allow AI use with human oversight on irreversible decisions, disclosure of AI-generated content, and informing users of AI interactions.

Require state agencies to review AI use, modify inconsistent procedures, and document compliance within nine months, reporting to the department of information technology.

Mandate annual reporting and updates on AI system compliance and procurement to key government officials and public posting on the department website.

Sever provisions to ensure remaining regulations remain in effect if parts are invalidated.

Enact the chapter on July 1, 2024.

Official name: Rhode Island SB 2083 (2022)
Casual name: Rhode Island (2022) SB 2083 (Consumer Protection in Eye Care)
Short summary: Regulates AI-based eye assessment mechanisms, requiring patient-provider interaction and verification of recent in-person exams. Forbids AI-generated prescription use alone and imposes penalties for violations. Directs the Department of Health to establish implementing rules and regulations.
Long summary:
Define "assessment mechanism" to include AI and technology used for remote or in-person eye assessments.

Require such mechanisms to offer patient-provider interaction, gather patient history, and acknowledge limitations, including not replacing in-person exams or issuing initial contact lens prescriptions.

Instruct providers using assessment mechanisms to adhere to traditional care standards, verify patient identity and age, ensure recent in-person exams, and maintain patient records.

Establish that using eye assessment data alone for prescriptions is prohibited, and providers must certify prescriptions with their signature and license.

Impose civil penalties up to $10,000 for violations, with rights to hearings and appeals.

Authorize the department of health to implement rules and regulations for enforcement.

Include severability to maintain chapter validity if parts are invalidated.

Official name: Rhode Island House Resolution 6423
Casual name: Rhode Island H6423 (2023)
Short summary: Requests the Department of Administration and RI Division of Information Technology to report on the use, validation, and evaluation of algorithmic decision-making, address biases in AI, and propose regulatory actions. Requires submission of findings to the House by September 29, 2023.
Long summary:
Requests the Department of Administration and the RI Division of Information Technology report on the extent of algorithmic decision-making used by the State of Rhode Island.

Tasks them with assessing the validation, testing, and evaluation of automated decision systems, especially regarding biases such as racial and gender biases.

Instructs them to provide recommendations on regulatory or legislative action regarding the use of automated decision systems by state offices, compliance with data protection laws, and timelines for the implementation of AI technologies.

Requires delivery of this information to the House of Representatives and the Chair of the House Committee on Innovation, Internet, and Technology by September 29, 2023.

Authorizes the Secretary of State to transmit certified copies of this resolution to relevant state officials.

Official name: Illinois House Bill 53 (2021)
Casual name: Illinois HB 53 (2021)
Short summary: Amends the Artificial Intelligence Video Interview Act to require employers using AI for video interviews to collect and report applicants' race and ethnicity data to the Department of Commerce and Economic Opportunity. Requires the Department to analyze and report potential racial bias annually.
Long summary:
Amends the Artificial Intelligence Video Interview Act by requiring employers using AI to analyze video interviews for in-person interview selection to collect demographic data.

Requires the collection of race and ethnicity data of applicants selected and not selected for interviews, as well as of those hired.

Obligates employers to report this demographic data annually to the Department of Commerce and Economic Opportunity by December 31.

Requires the Department to analyze this data and determine any racial bias in the AI use.

Instructs the Department to report findings of racial bias to the Governor and General Assembly by July 1 each year.

Official name: Colorado SB21-169
Casual name: Colorado (2021) SB 169 (Protecting Consumers From Unfair Discrimination)
Short summary: Prohibits insurers from unfairly discriminating using algorithms and predictive models based on personal attributes. Requires the insurance commissioner to establish rules, conduct investigations, and engage stakeholders. Obligates insurers to demonstrate non-discriminatory use of consumer data.
Long summary:
Prohibits insurers from using external consumer data, algorithms, and predictive models that result in unfair discrimination based on various protected characteristics such as race, gender, or sexual orientation.

Requires the Commissioner of Insurance to adopt rules ensuring insurers demonstrate their data and models do not result in unfair discrimination.

Engages a stakeholder process to gather input from insurers, consumers, and other parties before rule adoption.

Obliges insurers to provide detailed information about their data usage and implement risk management frameworks to minimize discrimination risks.

Allows the Commissioner to examine and investigate insurers' data practices.

Permits exemption for certain insurance types such as title insurance and high-premium commercial insurance policies.

Defines key terms like "algorithm" and "predictive model" as related to insurance practices, emphasizing consumer data's impact on discrimination.

Official name: Illinois House Bill 2557 (2019)
Casual name: Illinois HB 2557 (AI Video Interview Act)
Short summary: Requires employers in Illinois using AI for video interview analysis to notify applicants, explain AI functionality, and obtain consent. Prohibits sharing videos except with necessary evaluators. Instructs employers to delete videos upon applicant request within 30 days.
Long summary:
Requires employers in Illinois to notify job applicants if artificial intelligence will analyze their video interviews and to provide information on how the AI works, including characteristics it evaluates.

Requires employers to obtain applicants' consent before using AI to evaluate them.

Prohibits employers from using AI analysis on applicants who do not consent.

Permits sharing of applicant videos only with individuals necessary for evaluating the applicant's fitness for the position.

Obliges employers to delete video interviews and instruct any recipients to do the same, including backup copies, within 30 days if the applicant requests it.

Official name: Utah SB 96
Casual name: Utah SB 96 (Deep Technology Initiative)
Short summary: Establishes a Deep Technology Talent Initiative to fund programs incorporating AI, among other technologies, in education. Instructs the board to collaborate with institutions and employers, and the advisory council to recommend funding, focusing on meeting workforce needs and integrating disciplinary expertise.
Long summary:
Establishes a Deep Technology Talent Initiative to develop and oversee funding for programs related to deep technology, including artificial intelligence.

Requires institutions of higher education to collaborate with employers to create programs that address workforce needs in deep technology sectors.

Mandates submission and review of program proposals by institutions seeking funding, emphasizing workforce relevance, partnerships, and the integration of deep technology skills.

Instructs the Deep Technology Talent Advisory Council to prioritize and recommend proposals for funding based on specified criteria including workforce impact and cost-effectiveness.

Allocates $5 million from the Workforce Development Restricted Account to the Deep Technology Talent Initiative for fiscal year 2021.

Requires annual reports from funded programs on performance and outcomes, which the board must submit to relevant legislative committees.

Defines the composition and responsibilities of the Deep Technology Talent Advisory Council in supporting the initiatives.

Official name: Louisiana (2024) HCR 66
Casual name: Louisiana HCR 66
Short summary: Requests the Joint Legislative Committee on Technology and Cybersecurity to study and recommend AI use and regulation. Requires data and testimony solicitation. Instructs the committee to make annual policy recommendation reports to the Louisiana legislature.
Long summary:
Requests the Joint Legislative Committee on Technology and Cybersecurity to study and make recommendations concerning the use and regulation of artificial intelligence.

Instructs the joint committee to solicit data and testimony from relevant persons, entities, agencies, and institutions.

Requires the joint committee to make annual reports with policy recommendations to the legislature.

Recognizes the multifaceted potential threats of artificial intelligence in education, criminal justice, finance, health, welfare, and state fiscal health.

Acknowledges the potential for beneficial uses of artificial intelligence to improve government operations and service efficiency.

Emphasizes the necessity for more information before developing policies to maximize AI benefits and minimize its misuse in the state of Louisiana.

Official name: Alaska Senate Bill No. 177
Casual name: Alaska SB 177, Section 2. ("Use by State Agencies of Artificial Intelligence")
Short summary: Requires departments to inventory AI systems in state agencies biannually, publish findings online, and assess impacts. Prohibits using AI for biometric identification, emotion recognition, manipulation, or social scoring. Requires agency consent for sensitive data use.
Long summary:
Requires the Department of Administration to biennially inventory AI systems for consequential decisions used by state agencies, detailing system names, vendors, capabilities, and impact assessments, and publish results online.

Obligates state agency heads to conduct impact assessments every two years for AI systems, analyzing efficacy, oversight, accountability, risks, effects on individuals, and compliance with anti-discrimination measures, with results also published online.

Prohibits state agencies from using AI systems involving biometric identification, emotion recognition, cognitive manipulation, social scoring, and those using data hosted in specified foreign countries.

Restricts state agencies' data transfer without consent and requires multi-factor authentication for contracted AI systems.

Directs the department to establish regulations for AI system governance, require pre-implementation assessments, and prevent discriminatory impacts.

Allows individuals to sue for damages resulting from violations related to AI use.

Official name: Alaska Senate Bill No. 177
Casual name: Alaska SB 177, Section 1. (Deepfakes)
Short summary: Require disclosure for deepfakes intended to harm a candidate or deceive voters by stating, "This communication has been manipulated or generated by artificial intelligence." Ensure the statement is audible or visible throughout digital communications.
Long summary:
Require individuals who know or should know that a communication includes a deepfake depicting a candidate or political party, intended to deceive voters, to include a specific disclosure statement: "This communication has been manipulated or generated by artificial intelligence."

Mandate that the statement be audible in audio communications and visible throughout the entirety of print, video, or digital communications.

Define "deepfake" as an image, audio, or video created or manipulated with complex computational techniques to create a realistic yet false depiction of an individual, potentially altering a reasonable person's understanding of their appearance, conduct, or words compared to the original media.

Official name: Security Management Measures for the Application of Facial Recognition Technology
Casual name: Security Management Measures for the Application of Facial Recognition Technology
Short summary: Regulates facial recognition technology use in China, including through informed consent requirements, assessment requirements, storage and processing conditions, and certain outright prohibitions. Requires impact assessments and local filing for large volumes of facial recognition data. Delegates oversight and enforcement to cyberspace and public security authorities.
Long summary:
Regulates the use of facial recognition technology for processing facial information within China, ensuring compliance with relevant laws and ethical standards.

Exempts the use of facial recognition in research and development or algorithm training.

Requires a specific, necessary purpose for processing, with minimized impact on individual rights.

Obligates personal information processors to inform individuals transparently about processing activities, including purposes, methods, and rights.

Mandates obtaining consent separately for processing, with provision for withdrawal.

Requires parental consent for processing minors' facial information and formulating specific rules for handling such data.

Restricts storing facial information within devices unless consent or laws allow external transmission and limits retention to what is necessary.

Mandates conducting personal information protection impact assessments in advance and retaining assessment reports for three years.

Prohibits using facial recognition as the sole identity verification method if alternatives exist.

Requires filing procedures with local cyberspace administrations if information volume reaches 100,000 individuals.

Empowers cyberspace administrations and public security authorities to supervise, inspect, and handle complaints regarding facial recognition misuse.
Tags: Risk factors: Privacy;Harms: Violation of civil or human rights, including privacy;Risk factors: Transparency;Strategies: Disclosure;Risk factors: Privacy;Strategies: Disclosure;Strategies: Evaluation: Impact assessment;Harms: Violation of civil or human rights, including privacy;Risk factors: Safety;Risk factors: Privacy;Strategies: Evaluation: Impact assessment;Strategies: Disclosure

Official name: Massachusetts Executive Order 629
Casual name: Massachusetts EO 629 (AI Strategic Task Force)
Short summary: Establishes an AI Strategic Task Force in Massachusetts to study AI impacts and recommend strategies. Advises the Governor on AI-related development, investment, job creation, and responsible use policies. Requires regular meetings and submission of a comprehensive report.
Long summary:
Establishes an AI Strategic Task Force within the Office of the Governor of Massachusetts to study AI and Generative AI (GenAI) technologies and their impacts on the state.

Charges the Task Force with advising the Governor on making Massachusetts a leader in AI research and development.

Requires the Task Force to identify collaboration opportunities in key industries such as life sciences, healthcare, and finance, and explore public investments in AI.

Advises on AI-related job creation, assessing workforce impacts, and suggesting workforce training.

Recommends policies for responsible AI development, addressing ethical concerns like bias, privacy, and security.

Empowers the Task Force to convene subcommittees for addressing specific AI issues.

Obligates the Task Force to submit a report summarizing its work and recommendations to the Governor.

Specifies an effective date immediately after issuance until 30 days post-report submission.

Official name: Kansas Policy and Procedures Memorandum 8200.00
Casual name: Kansas PPM 8200.00 (Gen AI Policy)
Short summary: Outlines acceptable use of generative AI for Kansas's Executive Branch agencies. Requires human review of AI outputs for accuracy and prohibits reliance on AI for official statements. Bans input of Restricted Use Information and copyrighted material into AI.
Long summary:
Outlines the acceptable use of generative AI by Executive Branch Cabinet and Non-Cabinet Agencies in Kansas, emphasizing safety, privacy, and intellectual property protection.

Requires entities to review AI-generated content for accuracy, appropriateness, privacy, and security before dissemination.

Prohibits verbatim use of AI-generated content, reliance on it for final decisions or official statements, and impersonation of individuals or organizations.

Forbids input of Restricted Use Information, inappropriate, copyrighted, or proprietary material into AI systems.

Directs agencies to require contractors to disclose AI usage in contracts and prohibits use of state confidential data without approval.

Allows software code from AI only after risk mitigation and mandates annotation.

Tasks agency heads with procedural compliance and the Office of Information Technology Services (OITS) with policy maintenance.

Official name: Washington Executive Order 24-01
Casual name: Washington EO 24-01 (Artificial Intelligence)
Short summary: Instructs WaTech to report on generative AI initiatives and develop training plans. Requires WaTech and DES to provide procurement and usage guidelines. Mandates creating an accountability framework for generative AI's ethical use. Orders assessments of AI’s impact on communities and workforce by specific deadlines.
Long summary:
Orders WaTech, with cabinet agencies, to report on generative AI initiatives by September 2024, assessing feasibility, benefits, and challenges.

Directs WaTech and DES to issue guidelines for AI procurement focusing on safety, equity, and privacy by September 2024.

Requires DES and WaTech to develop an AI training plan for government workers by January 2025.

Instructs WaTech to create guidelines for AI's community impact, focusing on equity and algorithmic bias by December 2024.

Mandates DES and WaTech to update procurement templates to include equity and risk considerations by January 2025.

Tasks the Office of Equity to develop an accountability framework for AI equity and ethics by September 2024.

Ensures WaTech provides risk assessment guidance for high-risk AI systems by December 2024.

Commands the State Human Resources Division to assess AI's workforce impact and mitigate negative effects by December 2024.

Charges the Workforce Training Board with fostering AI education and research by January 2025.

Official name: DC Mayor's Orders 2024-028
Casual name: DC MO 2024-028 (AI Values and Strategic Benchmarks)
Short summary: Establishes an AI Advisory Group and AI Taskforce to guide responsible AI deployment in the District of Columbia. Requires agencies to align AI tools with values of benefit, safety, equity, accountability, transparency, sustainability, and privacy. Defines strategic AI benchmarks.
Long summary:
Establishes six core AI Values that District of Columbia agencies must assess before deploying AI tools, specifically focusing on benefits, safety, accountability, transparency, sustainability, and privacy.

Forms an AI Advisory Group and an AI Taskforce to align AI Values, conduct community engagement, support governance, and advise the Mayor on AI use.

Instructs the Office of the Chief Technology Officer, Department of Human Resources, and other agencies to develop privacy, cybersecurity, recruitment, and procurement benchmarks by specified deadlines.

Requires agencies to submit AI strategic plans addressing potential improvements, risks, and stakeholder engagement by designated dates.

Charges the AI Taskforce with providing technical advice, reporting on progress, and ensuring compliance with strategic benchmarks.

Mandates that the Advisory Group and AI Taskforce sunset on December 31, 2026. 

Requires these bodies to provide ongoing guidance, review AI tools, and align technology use with the District's stated AI Values.

Official name: Virginia Executive Directive 5 (2023)
Casual name: Virginia (2023) ED 5 (Recognizing the Risks and Seizing the Opportunities of Artificial Intelligence)
Short summary: Directs the Office of Regulatory Management to establish AI standards, review laws, ensure transparency, and make recommendations for AI use. Tasks coordination with education and workforce institutions for AI guidelines. Requires completion of actions by December 15, 2023.
Long summary:
Directs the Office of Regulatory Management, with the Chief Information Officer and relevant Secretariats, to establish AI usage standards for Virginia's state government.

Requires reviewing laws and regulations affecting AI, including privacy and intellectual property laws, to ensure public protection.

Ensures AI usage is transparent, secure, and impartial with adequate privacy safeguards.

Recommends uniform AI standards across state agencies, including education and IT guidelines.

Collaborates with educational institutions to integrate AI in curricula and promote responsible AI usage in learning environments.

Prepares students and the workforce for careers involving AI through partnerships with colleges and industry.

Identifies opportunities for AI to enhance state government efficiency and ethical decision-making.

Explores AI's economic impacts, encouraging industry innovation and supporting displaced workers.

Works with the Virginia Department of Energy to assess AI's impact on power requirements.

Requires completion of these actions and recommendations by December 15, 2023.

Official name: Opinions on Strengthening the Governance of Science and Technology Ethics
Casual name: Opinions on Strengthening the Governance of Science and Technology Ethics
Short summary: Requires work units engaged in S&T activities in AI and other areas to establish S&T ethics review committees and commits the government to formulating S&T ethics norms and guidelines for areas such as AI.
Long summary:
Explains that the National Science and Technology Ethics Commission is responsible for guiding and coordinating the S&T ethics governance system, with the Ministry of Science and Technology overseeing daily operations.

Instructs work units engaged in S&T activities in AI and other areas to form S&T ethics review committees to address ethical risks independently.

Commits the government to formulating S&T ethics norms and guidelines for AI and other areas. 

Calls for strengthening legislative research on S&T ethics in AI and other areas during the 14th Five-Year Plan.
Tags: Strategies: New institution;Strategies: Governance development

Official name: Washington SB 5092 (2021-22), Section 151
Casual name: Washington SB 5092 (2021-22), Sec 151 ("For the Consolidated Technology Services Agency")
Short summary: Requires the office of the chief information officer to convene a work group to examine review and audit of automated decision systems for fairness, transparency, and accountability. Develops recommendations for state law changes on AI-enabled system use and procurement.
Long summary:
Establishes a work group led by the Office of the Chief Information Officer (OCIO) to develop recommendations for automated decision systems, including AI-enabled profiling, focusing on fairness, transparency, and non-discrimination.

Requires the OCIO to oversee procurement and contracting for IT goods and services, ensuring cloud technology fee recovery and project data posting on the statewide IT dashboard.

Provides $12,000 for examining and auditing automated decision-making systems to ensure fairness and transparency.

Allocates $23,150,000 for Microsoft's 365 licenses with advanced security features, alongside distribution and annual license reporting requirements.

Designates $12,393,000 for cybersecurity efforts, $4,303,000 for small agency IT services, and $1,493,000 for cybersecurity implementation, pending bill enactment.

Involves various state departments in creating a health and human services enterprise coalition led by the Health Care Authority.

Directs collaborative data management efforts for a state-operated geospatial data portal related to natural hazards.

Official name: Pennsylvania Executive Order 2023-19
Casual name: Pennsylvania EO 2023-19 (Governing Use of Gen AI in Pennsylvania)
Short summary: Establishes the Generative AI Governing Board to oversee AI use, ensuring alignment with core values such as accuracy, equity, and privacy. Instructs the Office of Administration to develop policies and training for responsible AI use within Pennsylvania's agencies.
Long summary:
Creates the Generative AI Governing Board to oversee the ethical and effective use of Generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) by Commonwealth of Pennsylvania agencies.

Requires agencies to apply core values such as accuracy, adaptability, equity, innovation, mission alignment, privacy, proportionality, safety, security, and transparency in using Generative AI technologies.

Charges the Office of Administration (OA) with developing policies to ensure responsible AI use, including auditing outputs for accuracy and protecting user privacy.

Instructs the Board to recommend guidelines and assess agency requests to utilize Generative AI, ensuring adherence to core values, bias testing, and aligning applications with governmental missions.

Composes the Board of key government officials and mandates monthly meetings.

Encourages collaboration with external AI experts and stakeholders for feedback on AI usage.

Orders OA to provide training for employees on using Generative AI tools effectively.

Official name: West Virginia HB 2760 (2021), Sec 11-13Q "Credit allowed for specified high technology manufacturers."
Casual name: West Virginia HB 2760 (2021), Sec 11-13Q "Credit allowed for specified high technology manufacturers."
Short summary: Defines "high technology manufacturing business" to include AI-related manufacturing, like autonomous vehicles and robotic equipment. Specifies tax credits for such businesses that invest and create over 20 new jobs. Allows 100% tax credit over 20 years. Directs the commissioner to prescribe rules to determine credit allowances.
Long summary:
Defines "high technology manufacturing businesses" to include entities involved in manufacturing activities incorporating artificial intelligence, autonomous vehicles, drones, and related AI-operated machinery. 

Allows these businesses a tax credit against state taxes equal to 100% of the tax attributable to their qualified investment in expanding or establishing AI-related operations, providing they create at least 20 new jobs within 12 months.

Ensures that the median compensation for these new jobs exceeds $45,000, adjusted annually for inflation.

Precludes businesses claiming this credit from simultaneously claiming other tax credits for the same investment.

Empowers the commissioner to establish rules and require notices for monitoring the application and eligibility of the credit. 

Tasks the commissioner with overseeing compliance and verifying ongoing eligibility for the credit throughout the 20-year period.

Official name: (Trial) Measures for Science and Technology Ethics Reviews
Casual name: (Trial) Measures for Science and Technology Ethics Reviews
Short summary: Regulates the circumstances under which ethics reviews for AI-related research must be conducted and review processes. Requires organizations conducting research involving potentially ethically problematic scientific research to establish ethics review committees.
Long summary:
Requires organizations conducting research involving sensitive science and technology ethics areas, including life sciences and AI, to establish ethics review committees.

Obliges these committees to guide risk assessments, conduct ethics reviews, and manage interdisciplinary membership for unbiased decision-making.

Requires ethics reviews for activities involving data and algorithms, mandating that data handling activities comply with relevant data security and personal information laws.

Mandates expert reconsiderations for high-risk emerging technologies, including algorithms and automated decision-making systems.

Mandates that AI research organizations conduct comprehensive ethical risk assessments and develop emergency response plans.
