Official name: Mississippi House Bill 633 (2021)
Casual name: Mississippi HB 633 (Mississippi Computer Science and Cyber Education Equality Act), Section 4.
Short summary: Requires the State Department of Education to implement K-12 computer science curriculum, including AI and machine learning. Directs collaboration with Mississippi State University. Ensures all schools offer computer science instruction by the 2024-2025 school year.
Long summary:
Authorizes the State Department of Education to implement a K-12 computer science curriculum based on 2018 standards, including artificial intelligence and machine learning.

Instructs the State Department of Education to collaborate with the Center for Cyber Education at Mississippi State University for curriculum development.

Requires school districts, starting in the 2022-2023 school year, to ensure all middle schools offer computer science instruction and 50% of elementary schools provide a minimum of one hour weekly.

Mandates charter schools serving middle and high school students to offer computer science courses.

Starting in the 2023-2024 school year, directs 50% of high schools to provide computer science courses and all elementary schools to offer at least one hour of exploratory computer science weekly.

Ensures, beginning the 2024-2025 school year, computer science instruction is available in all schools within each district.

Official name: Vermont 2019 Act No.16, Section 20
Casual name: Vermont 2019 Act No.16, Sec 20 (AI Task Force)
Short summary: Require the Artificial Intelligence Task Force to submit an update by February 15, 2019, and a final report by January 15, 2020, to relevant Senate and House Committees. Allow up to 15 meetings, excluding public hearings. Cease existence by January 15, 2020.
Long summary:
Amends the meeting limitations of the Artificial Intelligence Task Force to permit no more than 15 meetings, excluding public hearings for obtaining public testimony on AI.

Requires the Task Force to submit an update by February 15, 2019, to the Senate Committee on Government Operations and the House Committee on Energy and Technology.

Requires the Task Force to submit a final report by January 15, 2020, to the same committees, detailing their findings and recommendations related to AI.

Specifies that the Task Force will cease to exist on January 15, 2020.

Official name: Massachusetts (2020) House Bill 5250, Section 92
Casual name: Massachusetts (2020) HB 5250 (An Act Enabling Partnerships For Growth), Sec 92
Short summary: Establishes a commission to study AI and automation's workforce impact, ensuring sustainable jobs and benefits. Directs development of policies and practices to support workers and a robust economy. Requires reporting on recommendations and legislation for future workforce challenges.
Long summary:
Establishes a special commission to study the impact of automation and artificial intelligence on the workforce, businesses, and the economy.

Charges the commission with ensuring sustainable jobs, fair benefits, and workplace safety standards.

Instructs the commission to study trends in industry transformation and impacts on workers, including access to healthcare and benefits.

Collaborates with the executive office of labor and workforce development to track industry trends and research best practices for the future of work.

Requires the commission to gather data from various sectors, work with training organizations, and hold public hearings across different regions.

Directs the commission to file a report by September 1, 2021, with analysis, recommendations, and potential legislation for securing worker benefits and updating workforce training.

Ensures recommendations include support for lifelong learning, skill development, and integration of new technologies in workforce training.

Official name: New York City Council No. 1806-A
Casual name: New York City Council No. 1806-A (Reporting on Algorithmic Tools Used By City Agencies)
Short summary: Amends the administrative code to require annual reporting by city agencies on algorithmic tools impacting public rights and services. Instructs agencies to detail tool names, purposes, data, usage, vendor involvement, and implementation dates. Prohibits disclosure violating laws or public safety.
Long summary:
Requires each New York City agency to report annually to the mayor’s office of operations or a designated entity by December 31 on all algorithmic tools used in the prior year.

Defines "algorithmic tool" as technologies derived from AI, machine learning, or predictive analytics that impact public rights, services, and resource allocation.

Instructs agencies to provide tool names, purposes, data types and sources, usage descriptions, vendor details, and start dates.

Directs the mayor’s office to compile this information and report it to the mayor and the council speaker by March 31, sorted by agency.

Prohibits disclosure of any information that violates laws, endangers safety, or interferes with investigations.

Implements the law with immediate effect.

Official name: Memorandum 25-21 (Accelerating Federal Use of AI through Innovation, Governance, and Public Trust)
Casual name: Memo 25-21 (Accelerating Federal Use of AI through Innovation, Governance, and Public Trust)
Collections: U.S. regulations, executive orders, and agency policies
Short summary: Instructs federal agencies to prioritize AI innovation, governance, and public trust while removing bureaucratic barriers. Requires development of an AI strategy, risk management practices for high-impact AI applications, and appointment of a Chief AI Officer.
Long summary:
Directs all Executive Branch departments and agencies to adopt AI by accelerating AI use and innovation while ensuring responsible deployment and governance.  

Requires removal of barriers to AI innovation, focusing on delivering value for taxpayers and improving public services.  

Instructs agencies to develop and share AI strategies, prioritize American AI products, and enhance workforce AI readiness.  

Directs agencies to designate a Chief AI Officer (CAIO) to oversee AI efforts and improve public accountability.  

Mandates implementation of minimum risk management practices for high-impact AI and provides guidance on AI governance, including creating AI impact assessments and ensuring human oversight.  

Encourages sharing and reusing of AI resources across federal agencies to prevent redundancy and foster collaboration.  

Implements public trust in AI use by requiring performance monitoring and mechanisms for public consultation and feedback.  

Excludes specific domains like national security systems from AI governance requirements.

Official name: Memorandum 25-22 (Driving Efficient Acquisition of Artificial Intelligence in Government)
Casual name: Memo 25-22  (Driving Efficient Acquisition of Artificial Intelligence in Government)
Collections: U.S. regulations, executive orders, and agency policies
Short summary: Directs Federal agencies to acquire AI systems in such a way that enhances efficiency, competitive procurement, and mitigates vendor lock-in risks at the lowest cost to the taxpayer. Requires policy updates for privacy, intellectual property, and data management while also mandating interagency collaboration.
Long summary:
Promotes the competitive American AI marketplace by mandating federal agencies to procure AI systems that enhance efficiency and mission effectiveness while avoiding dependence on single vendors.

Ensures taxpayer dollars are safeguarded by tracking AI performance and managing risks as outlined in Executive Order 13960.

Directs agencies to update internal procedures within 270 days to align with this memorandum and OMB Memorandum M-25-21, requiring cross-functional teams for AI acquisitions and addressing intellectual property rights.

Mandates the protection of privacy and intellectual property, emphasizing compliance with federal law and policy.

Specifies that future AI contracts comply with new guidelines by establishing a clear timeline for implementation.

Excludes certain AI acquisitions and activities, such as national security systems and incidental AI use by contractors.

Requires GSA to develop AI acquisition guides within 100 days and establish a shared repository of best practices within 200 days.

Official name: Next Generation Pipelines Research and Development Act
Casual name: H. R. 2613 Pipelines R&D Act
Short summary: Establishes a demonstration initiative and joint research program to advance AI-driven technologies for pipeline systems. Prioritizes AI models for monitoring and predicting pipeline integrity and lifecycle. Allocates funding for AI research in pipeline safety, efficiency, and resilience.
Long summary:
Establishes a demonstration initiative to fund advanced pipeline technologies via competitive merit reviews, focusing on AI-driven advancements in leak detection, sensor technologies, and machine learning for pipeline monitoring.

Directs funding of $45 million in 2026 and $50 million annually from 2027-2030 for relevant projects, with a sunset provision five years post-enactment.

Forms a Joint Research and Development Program involving the Department of Energy and other federal agencies to advance AI and machine learning models for pipeline systems, ensuring security and integrity.

Creates a National Pipeline Modernization Center to commercialize AI-assisted pipeline technologies, requiring competitive selection and location adjacent to existing infrastructure.

Mandates the National Institute of Standards and Technology to develop standards supporting AI in pipeline safety, with a $2.5 million annual allocation from 2026-2030.

Aims to leverage and complement existing energy and pipeline safety infrastructure and programs through collaborative interagency efforts and non-federal funding matching.

Official name: H.R.1569 CATCH Fentanyl Act
Casual name: H.R.1569 CATCH Fentanyl Act
Short summary: Establishes pilot projects to evaluate AI and other tech enhancements for cargo inspections at U.S. land ports. Requires assessment of technologies like AI, machine learning, and quantum sciences. Emphasizes privacy protection, cost-effectiveness, and performance. Prohibits additional appropriations.
Long summary:
Mandates the establishment of pilot projects by the Secretary of Homeland Security within one year to evaluate technologies for enhancing inspection processes at U.S. land ports of entry.

Requires testing of at least five types of nonintrusive inspection technology enhancements, including artificial intelligence and machine learning, to aid in detecting contraband and threats.

Ensures private sector involvement and emphasizes cost-effectiveness in technology selection.

Obligates safeguarding data privacy during inspections, with adherence to laws, data anonymization techniques, and regular audits.

Demands reports analyzing effectiveness, recommendations for widespread technology use, and cost breakdowns.

Includes necessary privacy and civil liberties impact assessments and reports before implementation and post-pilot.

Prohibits new appropriations, relying on existing resources for implementation.

Official name: Protect Victims of Digital Exploitation and Manipulation Act of 2025
Casual name: Protect Victims of Digital Exploitation and Manipulation Act of 2025
Short summary: Prohibits producing or distributing digital forgeries of intimate depictions using AI without consent, penalizing offenders with fines or imprisonment. Excludes certain good-faith distributions and applies extraterritorially if individuals involved are U.S. nationals.
Long summary:
Amends title 18, United States Code, to prohibit the production or distribution of digital forgeries of intimate visual depictions without the consent of the identifiable individual involved.

Defines digital forgery as an intimate visual depiction created or altered using software, machine learning, artificial intelligence, or other computer-generated means that appears authentic to a reasonable person.

Exempts actions taken in good faith during legal proceedings, medical applications, law enforcement, or investigations of unlawful content or conduct.

Excludes communications service providers from liability unless they recklessly distribute the content.

Applies regulations if either the offender or the victim is a U.S. national.

Defines terms such as consent, digital forgery, identifiable individual, intimate visual depiction, and communications service.

Provides severability to ensure the statute's provisions remain effective even if parts are judged unconstitutional.

Official name: H2444 Promoting Resilient Supply Chains Act of 2025
Casual name: H2444 Promoting Resilient Supply Chains Act of 2025
Short summary: Establishes responsibilities for promoting resilient supply chains and emerging technologies, including AI, to strengthen U.S. national security. Requires Assistant Secretary to assess and respond to supply chain vulnerabilities. Encourages reduced reliance on certain foreign goods. Forms Supply Chain Resilience Working Group.
Long summary:
Assigns the Assistant Secretary of Commerce new responsibilities to promote the resilience of critical supply chains and emerging technologies, including artificial intelligence. 

Establishes a Supply Chain Resilience Working Group to assess and address vulnerabilities in critical supply chains and technologies.

Mandates consultations with various stakeholders, including government entities and international allies, to improve supply chain readiness and response to disruptions.

Encourages domestic production and reduced reliance on countries posing national security risks for critical goods and technologies, including AI.

Protects voluntarily submitted critical supply chain information from public disclosure under the Freedom of Information Act.

Requires periodic assessments and strategy reports to Congress on supply chain resiliency and emerging technology impacts.

Sets a 10-year term for the act's provisions and requires a public comment period for certain designations and reports.

Official name: Promoting United States Leadership in Standards Act of 2025
Casual name: Promoting US Leadership in Standards Act of 2025
Short summary: Directs the National Institute of Standards and Technology and the Department of State to promote U.S. participation in AI standards development. Establishes a web portal and pilot program to support hosting domestic AI standards meetings, with grants for eligible organizations.
Long summary:
Mandates the Director of the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), in coordination with the Secretary of State, to brief Congress about opportunities for U.S. support in developing AI standards within one year.

Requires the establishment of a web portal to inform U.S. industry and federal agencies about international AI standards efforts and participation opportunities.

Initiates a pilot program to support AI standards meetings in the U.S. by awarding grants to eligible entities.

Specifies eligibility criteria for these grants and defines allowable costs.

Requires annual briefings to Congress on the pilot program's effectiveness.

Directs the development of recommendations for a permanent program if deemed feasible within two years.

Authorizes a budget of $5 million for the program from fiscal years 2024 through 2028. 

Ends the pilot program five years post-enactment, with consideration for permanent implementation.

Official name: S257 Promoting Resilient Supply Chains Act of 2025
Casual name: S257 Promoting Resilient Supply Chains Act of 2025
Short summary: Creates a Supply Chain Resilience Working Group to assess vulnerabilities in critical supply chains, emphasizing AI and emerging technologies. Tasks the Assistant Secretary of Commerce to lead initiatives enhancing supply chain resilience and promoting domestic AI technology production. Requires regular reporting to Congress.
Long summary:
Requires the Assistant Secretary of Commerce to promote the resilience of critical supply chains and emerging technologies vital for U.S. national security, in consultation with key government figures.

Mandates the creation of a Supply Chain Resilience Working Group within 120 days, comprising multiple federal agencies, to evaluate and model critical supply chains, including those for emerging technologies such as AI.

Directs the Assistant Secretary to identify vulnerabilities, develop response strategies, and assess domestic and allied manufacturing capacities within one year.

Prohibits disclosure of voluntarily submitted critical supply chain information under FOIA, providing protection against unauthorized use.

Necessitates annual reporting to Congress detailing strategies and evaluations of supply chain resiliency, including the role of technologies such as AI.

Limits the Act’s funding to existing resources and imposes a 10-year termination period for the Act’s provisions.

Official name: Nondiscrimination in Health Programs and Activities
Casual name: Nondiscrimination in Health Programs and Activities, § 92.210
Authority: Department of Health and Human Services
Collections: U.S. regulations, executive orders, and agency policies
Short summary: Prohibits covered entities from discriminating in health programs using patient care decision support tools based on race, color, national origin, sex, age, or disability. Requires identifying and mitigating discrimination risks in these tools.
Long summary:
Prohibits covered entities from discriminating based on race, color, national origin, sex, age, or disability through the use of patient care decision support tools in health programs or activities.

Requires covered entities to make reasonable efforts to identify uses of such tools that employ input variables or factors measuring race, color, national origin, sex, age, or disability.

Mandates covered entities to make reasonable efforts to mitigate discrimination risks from identified tools in their health programs or activities.

Official name: Emerging Innovative Border Technologies Act
Casual name: Emerging Innovative Border Technologies Act 2025
Short summary: Requires DHS to submit a plan for using emerging technologies, potentially incorporating AI, to enhance border security. Authorizes CBP Innovation Teams to research and adapt technologies. Mandates assessments of technology impact and coordination with the Science and Technology Directorate.
Long summary:
Mandates the Secretary of Homeland Security to submit a plan within 180 days to integrate and deploy emerging technologies, such as AI and machine learning, for border security.

Specifies the contents of the plan, including assessment of privacy impacts, coordination with private and public sectors, and scaling of current programs.

Authorizes CBP Innovation Teams to research and adapt commercial technologies for border security, setting strategic goals and operating procedures.

Requires annual reporting by CBP on Innovation Teams' activities, including descriptions of piloted technologies and their effectiveness.

Promotes identifying and phasing out obsolete technologies in favor of advanced systems, with cost assessments.

Encourages collaboration across sectors to leverage innovative technologies and incentivizes private sector participation. 

Obligates CBP to evaluate authorities and potential needs for new authorization to facilitate technology acquisition and integration, ensuring effective border security operations.

Official name: Leveraging Artificial Intelligence to Streamline the Code of Federal Regulations Act of 2025
Casual name: Leveraging AI to Streamline the Code of Federal Regulations Act of 2025
Short summary: Requires annual AI reviews of federal regulations for redundancy and obsolescence. Mandates OMB and NIST consultation to ensure AI system accuracy and security. Compels agencies to review, amend, or rescind regulations deemed outdated or redundant. Implements expedited regulatory changes.
Long summary:
Requires the Director of the Office of Management and Budget, in consultation with the National Institute of Standards and Technology, to annually implement a process using artificial intelligence to identify redundant or outdated federal regulations.

Mandates the use of an AI system that meets strict standards for accuracy, transparency, accountability, and national security risk.

Obliges agencies to review identified redundant or outdated regulations within 30 days and make a final determination of their status.

Commands the rescission or amendment of regulations found to be redundant or outdated within 30 days after determination.

Requires the public publication of any determination with a brief explanation, with an option for a classified annex to Congress if necessary.

Amends Title 5 of the United States Code to facilitate the expedited rescission and amendment of redundant or outdated regulations identified through AI review.

Official name: AI PLAN Act
Casual name: AI PLAN Act
Short summary: Requires the Secretaries of Treasury, Homeland Security, and Commerce to report to Congress on AI's financial crime risks. Considers risks like deepfakes and voice cloning. Suggests legislative recommendations and best practices for risk mitigation and incident response.
Long summary:
Require the Secretary of the Treasury, Secretary of Homeland Security, and Secretary of Commerce, in consultation with specified officials, to jointly submit annual reports to Congress on defending against financial crime risks posed by AI, including fraud and misinformation.

Describe interagency policies and procedures to protect against these risks, listing available and needed resources, technologies, and budget estimates.

Consider risks such as deepfakes, voice cloning, and foreign election interference.

List officials consulted as the U.S. Trade Representative, Attorney General, Federal Reserve Chairman, NIST Director, Undersecretary of Commerce for Industry and Security, and SEC Chairman.

Submit recommendations to Congress within 90 days post-report, including legislative suggestions and best practices for risk mitigation related to AI-driven financial crimes.

Official name: HEARTS Act of 2025
Casual name: HEARTS Act of 2025
Short summary: Prioritizes nonanimal methods in NIH research. Establishes the National Center for Alternatives to Animals in Research and Testing to develop and support humane scientific methods, including AI. Requires federally funded entities to report animal usage, encouraging reductions.
Long summary:
Establishes the National Center for Alternatives to Animals in Research and Testing to promote and fund nonanimal research methods, including artificial intelligence, within one year of the Act's enactment, under the National Institutes of Health (NIH).

Requires the NIH to prioritize nonanimal methods in research proposals, assess the feasibility of nonanimal methods before approving research, and ensure proposals are reviewed by experts in nonanimal techniques.

Instructs the Director of the National Center to support federally funded researchers in developing and adopting alternatives to animal testing, including AI, and report on animal usage publicly.

Mandates each federally funded research entity using animals to report biannually on the number and species of animals used and submit and update plans to reduce these numbers. 

Defines "animal" to include any live nonhuman vertebrate or cephalopod used in research or testing.

Official name: PATHS Act
Casual name: PATHS Act
Short summary: Extends the Department of Homeland Security's authority to use transaction mechanisms involving AI technology until 2028, requiring notification and briefing to congressional committees. Reduces the contract award threshold from $4,000,000 to $1,000,000.
Long summary:
Extends the use of other transaction authority for research and development acquisition under the Homeland Security Act of 2002 from September 30, 2024, to September 30, 2028.  
   
Mandates notification to congressional committees within 72 hours after the use or extension of transaction authority involving artificial intelligence (AI) technology.  
   
Requires the Secretary of Homeland Security to offer a briefing explaining the reason for using or extending AI-related transaction authority.  
   
Reduces the maximum amount for covered contract awards under the James M. Inhofe National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2023 from $4,000,000 to $1,000,000.

Official name: Supporting Innovation in Agriculture Act of 2025
Casual name: Supporting Innovation in Agriculture Act of 2025
Short summary: Establishes a 30% tax credit for investments in innovative agricultural technology, emphasizing AI, machine learning, and precision agriculture for specialty crops. Applies to technology placed in service by 2035, enhancing efficiency in production, processing, and storage.
Long summary:
Establishes a tax credit of 30% for investments in innovative agricultural technology projects under the Internal Revenue Code.

Defines "innovative agricultural technology project" as technology used to produce, store, process, and package specialty crops with precision agriculture or controlled environment agriculture, effective through December 31, 2035.

Specifies "qualified property" includes tangible personal property, software, and technology necessary for these projects and subject to depreciation rules.

Includes provisions for specific modern technologies, such as AI, machine learning, robotics, and various sensors, under both controlled environment and precision agriculture technologies.

Prohibits double benefit from using both this tax credit and specific government grants.

Amends sections of the Internal Revenue Code to incorporate the innovative agricultural technology investment credit.

Sets the amendment's applicability to projects starting construction after January 1, 2025.

Official name: Generative AI Terrorism Risk Assessment Act
Casual name: Generative AI Terrorism Risk Assessment Act
Short summary: Require the Secretary of Homeland Security to conduct annual assessments of terrorism threats involving generative AI. Task DHS with analyzing the use of generative AI by terrorists for radicalization and weapon development. Instruct to coordinate and share findings, protecting rights and privacy.
Long summary:
Requires the Secretary of Homeland Security to conduct annual assessments, in consultation with the Director of National Intelligence, on terrorism threats posed by terrorist organizations using generative AI applications.

Instructs inclusion of analysis on incidents involving extremist messaging, recruitment, and WMD capabilities linked to generative AI in these assessments, plus recommendations for countermeasures.

Ensures these assessments are coordinated with DHS legal and rights offices to protect privacy and civil liberties, and shared in unclassified form publicly, with classified annexes if necessary.

Mandates a briefing to congressional committees within 30 days of assessment submission.

Directs the Secretary to incorporate and disseminate terrorism threat information from fusion centers into DHS systems.

Defines "generative AI applications" as AI models generating synthetic content and clarifies terms for fusion centers and terrorist organizations.

Official name: Protecting Our Children in an AI World Act of 2025
Casual name: Protecting Our Children in an AI World Act of 2025
Short summary: Amends title 18, United States Code, to prohibit child pornography produced using artificial intelligence by eliminating certain affirmative defenses and expanding the definition of "sexually explicit conduct" to include actual or simulated obscene exhibitions of specific body parts.
Long summary:
Amends title 18 of the United States Code to prohibit child pornography produced using artificial intelligence.

Eliminates the affirmative defense in prosecutions involving AI-generated child pornography by removing certain clauses in Section 2252A(c).

Expands the definition of sexually explicit conduct in Section 2256(2)(B) to include actual or simulated obscene exhibition of the clothed or unclothed genitals, pubic area, buttocks, or female nipple.

Ensures that if any provision of the Act is held unconstitutional, the remaining provisions continue to have effect.
Tags: Harms: Detrimental content;Risk factors: Safety;Strategies: Government support;Strategies: Government support

Official name: Artificial Intelligence and Critical Technology Workforce Framework Act of 2025
Casual name: AI and Critical Technology Workforce Framework Act of 2025
Short summary: Expands NIST's functions to develop AI workforce frameworks. Requires periodic updates and reports to Congress. Mandates AI-specific framework development within 540 days. Includes professional and nontraditional skill considerations. Engages industry and government in framework updates.
Long summary:
Expands the functions of the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) to include the development of workforce frameworks for critical and emerging technologies, including artificial intelligence.

Mandates the Director of NIST to develop, publish, and periodically update a workforce framework specifically for artificial intelligence within 540 days of the Act's enactment.

Requires the inclusion of competencies, workforce categories, and work roles relevant to AI in the workforce framework, highlighting career pathways for individuals with nontraditional backgrounds.

Obligates consultation with federal agencies, industry, and educational institutions to develop these frameworks.

Demands reports to Congress on the updates and practical applications of the frameworks, with a focus on increasing employer and educational adoption.

Promotes global adoption by producing resources in multiple languages.

Incorporates professional and employability skills relevant to AI workforce frameworks and assesses additional framework needs for emerging technologies, such as quantum information science.

Official name: Health Tech Investment Act
Casual name: Health Tech Investment Act
Short summary: Amends the Social Security Act to require that certain algorithm-based healthcare services receive appropriate Medicare payments. Defines standards for classifying these services, including costs, eligibility criteria, and duration under specific payment classifications, emphasizing the role of AI-driven technologies. Codifies payment policy for AI-related software services.
Long summary:
Amends the Social Security Act to classify algorithm-based healthcare services under Medicare’s new technology ambulatory payment classification.

Requires the Secretary of Health and Human Services to assign payment classifications based on cost data submitted by manufacturers, including diverse factors like invoice prices and overhead.

Specifies that algorithm-based services cannot be removed from their payment classification until adequate claims data is available, and mandates a minimum tenure of five years within the initial classification.

Adjusts the criteria for eligibility in new technology classifications to include distinct procedures that use AI, machine learning, or other software when used adjunctively with underlying services.

Defines algorithm-based healthcare services as those using FDA-approved AI or software for clinical guidance.

Mandates the payment system for software services as described in a 2022 Medicare rule, effective from January 2023.

Official name: Healthy Technology Act of 2025
Casual name: Healthy Technology Act of 2025
Short summary: Amends the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act to include AI and machine learning technologies as eligible drug prescribers if they are state-authorized and FDA-approved, cleared, or authorized by the FDA and other purposes.
Long summary:
Revises the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act to include artificial intelligence and machine learning technologies can qualify as a practitioner eligible to prescribe drugs if authorized by the state involved and approved, cleared, or authorized by the Food and Drug Administration, and for other purposes.

Requires section 503(b) of the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (21 U.S.C. 353(b)) to be amended by adding the following at the end.

Requires that any artificial intelligence or machine learning technology included as a “practitioner licensed by law to administer such drug” be authorized pursuant to a statute of the State involved to prescribe the drug involved.

Mandates that the artificial intelligence or machine learning technology be approved, cleared, or authorized by the Food and Drug Administration under section 510(k), 513, 515, or 564.

Official name: H.R.1851 Fighter Force Preservation and Recapitalization Act of 2025
Casual name: H.R.1851 Fighter Force Preservation and Recapitalization Act of 2025
Short summary: Amends title 10 to increase the Air Force's fighter aircraft inventory, allows temporary reductions during recapitalization, mandates quarterly reports on inventory, requires delivery prioritization to existing squadrons, and implements a recapitalization plan for the Air National Guard through 2030.
Long summary:
Amends U.S. Code to increase the required minimum number of fighter aircraft in the Air Force to 1,900, extending compliance to 2030, but allows temporary reductions for transitions, provided levels don't fall below 1,800.

Mandates annual reports by the Secretary of the Air Force to Congress on the status of fighter aircraft inventory, including new acquisitions, unit assignments, and any legacy retirements, with penalties for late submission.

Requires prioritization of recapitalized fighter allocations to existing Air Force squadrons, allowing one-for-one legacy aircraft retirement.

Obligates the Air National Guard to maintain 25 fighter squadrons and restricts retiring legacy or fifth-generation aircraft unless unserviceable.

Directs an annual recapitalization plan for Air National Guard squadrons, assessing financial and readiness impacts, with a focus on acquiring F–16 Block 70 aircraft.

Defines terms such as "advanced capability fighter aircraft," "fifth-generation fighter aircraft," and "next-generation air dominance fighter aircraft."

Official name: Promoting Precision Agriculture Act
Casual name: Promoting Precision Agriculture Act
Short summary: Requires the Secretary of Agriculture to develop interconnectivity standards for precision agriculture, considering the impact of AI. Instructs the Comptroller General to assess these standards' effectiveness biennially, considering AI's influence on adoption and industry coordination.
Long summary:
Require the Secretary of Agriculture, in consultation with the Director of the National Institute of Standards and Technology and the Federal Communications Commission, to develop voluntary, consensus-based interconnectivity standards for precision agriculture within two years.

Direct the Secretary to coordinate with relevant stakeholders, including public, private, and industry organizations, while considering factors such as the evolving demands and connectivity needs of precision agriculture, cybersecurity challenges, and the impact of AI and advanced wireless communications technology.

Instruct the Comptroller General of the United States to conduct a study one year after the standards' development and every two years thereafter for eight years, assessing these standards for their voluntariness and effectiveness in fostering precision agriculture.

Require submission of a report to relevant Senate and House Committees summarizing the findings from each study.

Official name: Fair Grocery Pricing Act
Casual name: Fair Grocery Pricing Act
Short summary: Prohibits food producers from using algorithmic systems to manipulate prices or supply. Declares such actions as violations of antitrust laws. Assigns enforcement to the Federal Trade Commission, Attorney General, and state attorneys general. Allows civil actions by aggrieved individuals.
Long summary:
Prohibits food producers from using algorithmic systems to artificially inflate prices or reduce supply through coordinating functions or tacit agreements.

Deems any such use as a per se violation of the Sherman Act.

Mandates the Federal Trade Commission and the Attorney General to enforce this prohibition using applicable antitrust laws.

Authorizes civil actions by the FTC, state attorneys general, and aggrieved persons to recover damages and seek penalties.

Invalidates pre-dispute arbitration agreements and joint action waivers related to violations of this Act at the plaintiff’s election.

Ensures that this Act does not modify existing antitrust laws or preempt state or local laws.

Includes a severability clause to uphold remaining provisions if any part is deemed unconstitutional.

Official name: China Technology Transfer Control Act of 2025
Casual name: China Technology Transfer Control Act of 2025
Short summary: Regulates the export of technology related to AI and other fields to China by requiring the President to control such exports for national security. Requires the U.S. Trade Representative to annually list AI-related products supported by the Chinese government.
Long summary:
Control the export, re-export, and transfer of certain national interest technology and intellectual property, including AI, to China, starting 180 days after the Act's enactment.

Require the Secretary of State and the Secretary of Commerce to report within 90 days on the necessity of controls under specific export regulations.

Prescribe regulations within 180 days to enforce these controls.

Impose sanctions under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act on entities engaging in the unlawful transfer or use of controlled technologies with China, excluding goods importation.

Empower the President to waive sanctions if deemed in U.S. national security interests.

Instruct the United States Trade Representative to annually list Chinese products, including AI, that receive government support or assist in human rights violations.

Include AI technologies in products receiving Chinese support under its industrial policy, "Made in China 2025."

Official name: Department of Homeland Security Vehicular Terrorism Prevention and Mitigation Act of 2025
Casual name: Department of Homeland Security Vehicular Terrorism Prevention and Mitigation Act of 2025
Short summary: Requires the Department of Homeland Security to report on vehicular terrorism threats, including potential misuse of AI in automotive technologies, and countermeasures. Evaluates threat assessments, technological innovations, and collaborative efforts with stakeholders while ensuring respect for individual rights.
Long summary:
Requires the Secretary of Homeland Security, alongside TSA and CISA, to submit a report on the prevention, deterrence, and response to vehicular terrorism.

Mandates the report to assess threats related to connected and autonomous vehicles and ADAS technologies, including AI-driven cybersecurity vulnerabilities.

Instructs inclusion of evaluations regarding DHS's engagement with public and private stakeholders, focusing on vehicle rental and ride-sharing platforms to prevent misuse.

Calls for recommendations on AI-enabled predictive analytics and vehicle immobilization technologies to detect and mitigate threats.

Demands coordination with enforcement agencies to address vehicular terrorism through intelligence sharing and joint training.

Ensures countermeasures and AI use respect privacy and civil liberties.

Obliges publication of an unclassified report summary for public access and annual briefings to Congress on developments and recommendations.

Defines “vehicular terrorism” as the use of vehicles to commit acts of terrorism.

Official name: IRONDOME Act of 2025
Casual name: IRONDOME Act of 2025
Short summary: Accelerates the development of autonomous agents to defend the U.S. homeland and armed forces against cruise missiles and drone raids. Uses all available authorities to ensure a cost-effective approach.
Long summary:
Accelerates the development of autonomous agents to defend against cruise missiles and drones.

Requires the Secretary to use all available authorities to achieve this goal.

Aims to enhance cost-effective defense of the United States homeland and forward-deployed armed forces.

Official name: TAME Extreme Weather and Wildfires Act
Casual name: TAME Extreme Weather and Wildfires Act
Short summary: Directs the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration to develop AI-based weather models, improve forecasts, and analyze fire environments. Encourages public-private partnerships for AI innovation and workforce development. Ensures open access to non-sensitive data and models.
Long summary:
Directs the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) to use artificial intelligence (AI) for adapting to extreme weather and wildfires.

Mandates the development of comprehensive weather forecasting training datasets and AI weather models.

Requires NOAA to explore advanced AI applications to enhance weather forecasts and improve information delivery.

Instructs NOAA to provide technical assistance for AI weather model usage and establish best practices.

Directs the creation of an AI-driven program to predict, detect, and analyze wildfires.

Advocates for partnerships with private and academic entities for innovation in environmental forecasting.

Obligates NOAA to strengthen its workforce expertise in AI applications for weather forecasting.

Allows public access to data and code developed under the Act, with considerations for national security and intellectual property.

Provides guidance on minimizing environmental impacts when using AI technologies in these initiatives.

Official name: Foreign Investment Guardrails to Help Thwart (FIGHT) China Act
Casual name: H.R.2246 Foreign Investment Guardrails to Help Thwart (FIGHT) China Act
Short summary: Imposes sanctions on Chinese entities involved in defense, surveillance sectors. Prohibits U.S. investments in covered national security transactions and prohibits holding securities of listed Chinese companies. Requires U.S. persons to notify certain investments involving AI-development-related technologies in China.
Long summary:
Imposes sanctions on certain Chinese entities and individuals engaged in defense or surveillance sectors through the International Emergency Economic Powers Act.

Requires notification for U.S. investments in prohibited technologies and establishes penalties for violations, reflecting stringent oversight on national security transactions involving China.

Mandates divestment of securities by U.S. persons from entities on the Non-SDN Chinese Military-Industrial Complex Companies List, prohibiting holding after a specified period.

Outlines procedures for approving exceptions to these prohibitions through waivers, subject to congressional notification and review.

Establishes a framework for using collaborative reports and a public database for covered foreign persons while protecting sensitive information.

Outlines regulations and processes to enforce prohibitions, commands an annual report to assess developments, and encourage multilateral cooperation to prevent China’s acquisition of sensitive technologies. 

Designates Commerce and Treasury authorities to oversee implementation and allows Presidential delegation of some powers.

Official name: Decoupling America's Artificial Intelligence Capabilities from China Act of 2025
Casual name: Decoupling America's Artificial Intelligence Capabilities from China Act of 2025
Short summary: Prohibits U.S. persons from engaging in AI-related activities with Chinese entities of concern, including research, development, and transfer of AI technology. Restricts financial interests and imposes penalties for violations. Requires the Secretary of Commerce and other officials to implement these prohibitions.
Long summary:
Prohibits United States persons from importing AI technology from China and exporting AI technology to China after 180 days of enactment.

Establishes penalties for individuals and entities violating these prohibitions, including fines and forfeiture of federal contracts.

Requires the Secretary of Commerce to issue regulations implementing these prohibitions within 90 days of enactment.

Prohibits United States persons from conducting AI research and development for or with Chinese entities or nationals of concern.

Enforces penalties for these violations, including fines and federal benefits forfeiture.

Emphasizes penalties disqualifying violators from federal financial assistance for five years.

Restricts U.S. persons from having financial interests in Chinese AI research entities involved in military or surveillance activities.

Permits the President to use emergency economic powers for enforcing these prohibitions.

Involves multiple federal departments in enforcing these regulations.

Official name: H.R.1142 - To amend the Public Health Service Act to direct the Secretary of Health and Human Services to establish drug adherence guidelines, and for other purposes.
Casual name: H.R.1142 Amending the Public Health Service Act for Drug Adherence Guidelines
Short summary: Requires the Secretary of Health and Human Services to establish drug adherence guidelines using artificial intelligence and machine learning, aiming for 90% adherence for Medicare Part B and D drugs. Promotes usage of generic and biosimilar drugs.
Long summary:
Amends the Public Health Service Act to mandate the Secretary of Health and Human Services to establish drug adherence guidelines aimed at 90 percent adherence for Medicare part B and D drugs.

Requires incorporation of artificial intelligence and machine learning technologies in these guidelines.

Promotes, to the greatest extent possible, the use of generic and biosimilar drugs.

Official name: Transformational Artificial intelligence to Modernize the Economy against Extreme Weather and Wildfires Act
Casual name: TAME Extreme Weather and Wildfires Act 2025
Short summary: Requires development of AI weather models for accurate forecasts and disaster response. Promotes public-private partnerships and access to forecasting data. Authorizes funding and technical support while protecting national security and intellectual property. Encourages research and personnel retention strategies.
Long summary:
Mandates the improvement of weather, water, and wildfire forecast accuracy through artificial intelligence (AI) by federal agencies.

Promotes public-private partnerships to expedite AI adoption for mitigating environmental hazards.

Directs the development and curation of comprehensive weather forecasting training datasets in consultation with various federal and research entities within four years.

Permits exploration of AI weather models for global, regional, and local forecasting.

Encourages the use of AI to enhance communication and public understanding of weather risks.

Mandates regular reporting to Congress on activities and potential risks of foreign access to U.S. weather data.

Allows the Under Secretary to enter partnerships, including co-investment strategies, for AI and environmental forecasting innovation.

Provides for public access to operational AI weather models, subject to national security and intellectual property considerations.

Authorizes appropriations to support these initiatives through fiscal years 2026 to 2030.

Official name: S.305 Small Business Technological Act of 2025
Casual name: S.305 Small Business Technological Act of 2025
Short summary: Amends the Small Business Act to permit loans for business software, including AI-utilizing tools. Specifies that loans for specified software purposes before enactment are permissible. Prohibits loan use for research and development. Does not affect working capital definitions.
Long summary:
Authorize the Small Business Administration to provide loans for business software, cloud computing services, and technology that utilize artificial intelligence.

Permit these loans to support business operations, including the processing, payment, or tracking of payroll expenses, human resources, sales and billing functions, and accounting or tracking of supplies, inventory, records, and expenses.

Clarify that this provision does not invalidate prior loans made for similar purposes under the Small Business Act.

Prohibit the use of these loans for research and development activities.

Ensure the amendment does not alter the definition of working capital in the context of the Small Business Act.

Official name: Promoting Precision Agriculture Act of 2025
Casual name: Promoting Precision Agriculture Act of 2025
Short summary: Requires development of voluntary interconnectivity standards for precision agriculture, considering AI’s impact. Involves consultation with relevant stakeholders. Mandates a GAO assessment of these standards every two years to evaluate their voluntary nature and adoption success in precision agriculture.
Long summary:
Mandates the Secretary of Agriculture, in consultation with the National Institute of Standards and Technology and the Federal Communications Commission, to develop voluntary interconnectivity standards for precision agriculture within two years of enactment.

Requires coordination with public and private stakeholders, including industry organizations and voluntary standards bodies, to create these standards.

Includes the consideration of evolving demands, connectivity needs, cybersecurity challenges, and the impacts of advanced wireless technology and AI on precision agriculture.

Directs the Comptroller General to conduct studies assessing these standards' development, voluntary nature, industry coordination, and adoption success, with reports due to Senate and House committees.

Aims to enhance precision agriculture participation in the U.S. and promote U.S. leadership in setting standards for precision agriculture through voluntary consensus standards development organizations.

Official name: Create AI Act of 2025
Casual name: Create AI Act of 2025
Short summary: Establishes the National Artificial Intelligence Research Resource (NAIRR) to democratize AI research by providing resources. Directs the NAIRR Steering Subcommittee under the Office of Science and Technology Policy to oversee operations. Sets procedures for privacy, ethics, security, and user eligibility. Defines funding mechanisms and governance structures.
Long summary:
Establishes a National Artificial Intelligence Research Resource (NAIRR) to enhance AI research accessibility and spur innovation.

Requires the Director of the National Science Foundation, in coordination with a NAIRR Steering Subcommittee, to oversee NAIRR’s establishment and governance.

Forms a Program Management Office to manage NAIRR’s daily operations, select an Operating Entity, and oversee asset allocation.

Defines user eligibility criteria for accessing NAIRR, including U.S.-based researchers and institutions.

Mandates policies for privacy, ethics, scientific integrity, and system security, with input from various advisory committees.

Authorizes the establishment of a fee schedule for NAIRR access, ensuring a free tier for research purposes.

Permits acceptance of donations to support NAIRR’s operation from the private sector.

Coordinates with relevant federal entities to integrate AI testbeds and educational resources.

Emphasizes research security compliance, aligning with national security guidelines on research integrity.

Official name: Bipartisan Health Care Act
Casual name: Bipartisan Health Care Act, Sec. 217. Report on wearable medical devices.
Short summary: Require the Comptroller General to assess AI in wearable medical devices for clinical decision-making, focusing on treatment accuracy, AI's benefits and challenges, and policy options to enhance benefits and mitigate challenges. Submit findings to Congress within 18 months.
Long summary:
Instructs the Comptroller General of the United States to conduct a technology assessment and submit a report to Congress on wearable medical devices.

Requires the report to assess the capabilities and limitations of these devices in supporting clinical decision-making.

Includes an evaluation of the potential for such devices to accurately prescribe treatments.

Examines the benefits and challenges of integrating artificial intelligence to enhance these devices.

Provides policy options to maximize benefits and mitigate challenges associated with the development or use of wearable medical devices.

Official name: CORE Act of 2025
Casual name: CORE Act of 2025, Section 2(b) ("Report on transboundary hydrocarbon reservoirs")
Short summary: Require the Secretaries to utilize artificial intelligence for modeling and analysis of transboundary hydrocarbon resources, aiming to reduce uncertainty in resource estimates. Prioritize advanced data technologies and collaborative efforts with relevant federal agencies for comprehensive assessment.
Long summary:
Leverages artificial intelligence and other emerging technologies to approximate the resources in transboundary hydrocarbon reservoirs.

Requires the application of data analysis tools, AI, quantum computing, and modeling to refine resource estimates and reduce uncertainty.

Instructs the Secretaries to prioritize advanced geophysical, geological, and geotechnical data in the report.

Encourages partnerships with federal agencies such as the National Science Foundation and the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration to utilize existing maritime technologies.

Tasks the report preparation with addressing uncertainties using AI and other technologies for informed decision-making on transboundary hydrocarbon reservoirs.

Official name: Air National Guard Squadron Preservation Act of 2025
Casual name: Air National Guard Squadron Preservation Act of 2025, sec. 2., subsection (c)
Short summary: Study requires evaluation of unmanned/autonomous capabilities in Air Force fighters and potential cost savings from foreign sales of F-16 variants. Identifies eligible units for advanced fighters and suitability for missions, and assesses multiyear contract acquisition benefits.
Long summary:
Mandates the Secretary, with the Air National Guard Director, to study the necessity of acquiring advanced capability fighter aircraft for the Air Force.

Requires consideration of plans to implement unmanned or autonomous capabilities in fighter aircraft.

Includes evaluation of cost savings linked to foreign sales of F-16 variants.

Identifies eligible Air Force units for recapitalization with advanced fighter aircraft.

Assesses how these aircraft meet current and contingency mission needs of combatant commanders.

Determines whether a multiyear contract is the optimal acquisition method.

Obligates the Secretary to report findings to congressional defense committees within 180 days of enactment.

Official name: Duplicative Grant Consolidation Act
Casual name: Duplicative Grant Consolidation Act, Sec. 4. "Report on feasibility of leveraging artificial intelligence to identify duplicative Federal grant applications."
Short summary: Requires the Director of the Office of Management and Budget to report on using AI to identify duplicative federal grant applications and detect waste, fraud, and abuse, in collaboration with designated federal agencies, submitted to relevant Congressional committees.
Long summary:
Mandates the Director of the Office of Management and Budget to submit a report to Congressional committees.

Requires consultation with the Secretary of Energy, the Director of the National Science Foundation, and the Director of the National Institute of Standards and Technology.

Focuses on assessing the feasibility of using AI to identify duplicative Federal grant applications.

Seeks to determine whether an applicant has received or submitted another grant application for the same purpose.

Aims to identify waste, fraud, and abuse in the grant application process through AI technology.

Official name: Western Wildfire Support Act of 2025, Section 205 "Study on modernizing wildfire response technologies"
Casual name: Western Wildfire Support Act of 2025 (Sec. 205 "Study on modernizing wildfire response technologies.")
Short summary: Requires study on AI feasibility and data needs for wildland fire predictive modeling. Analyzes computing requirements to enhance modeling accuracy and speed. Reviews AI's potential use in predictive models. Submits results to Congress and public within one year.
Long summary:
Require the Secretaries to conduct a study on modernizing wildfire response technologies.

Include in the study necessary improvements to radio communications systems and infrastructure during wildland fire or prescribed fire operations, assessing the quality, reliability, failure impacts, and improvement options.

Assess real-time or near-real-time situational awareness tools for operational firefighters, ensuring interoperability and evaluating additional remote sensing and mapping capabilities.

Conduct a cost analysis of commercially available systems versus internally developed systems.

Analyze wildland fire predictive modeling, focusing on data requirements, computing needs, and the feasibility of using artificial intelligence for more accurate predictions.

Require the submission and public availability of the study results within one year of the Act's enactment to congressional committees and the public.

Official name: Wildfire Prevention Act of 2025, Section 302 "Public-private wildfire technology deployment and testbed partnership."
Casual name: Wildfire Prevention Act of 2025 (Sec. 302 "Public-private wildfire technology deployment and testbed partnership.")
Short summary: Establishes a pilot program for new wildfire technology, prioritizing AI, that connects private entities with federal agencies for testing. Requires annual reports on technology tested, costs, and outreach. Encourages emerging technologies for wildfire mitigation. Expires in seven years.
Long summary:
Establishes a deployment and testbed pilot program for new and innovative wildfire technologies within 60 days of enactment.

Requires coordination among the Secretaries of Agriculture and Interior and covered agencies to identify technology priority areas like AI, quantum sensing, and augmented reality for wildfire prevention and mitigation.

Prioritizes covered entities with emerging technologies that address identified issues for participation in the pilot program.

Mandates real-time testing and on-the-ground coordination between selected entities and covered agencies.

Requires public outreach to invite applications from entities for technology testing and demonstration in key priority areas.

Obligates annual reporting to appropriate committees, detailing participating entities, tested technologies, cost estimates, and outreach efforts.

Sets the program's expiration at 7 years after enactment, with annual assessments and recommendations to foster the adoption of promising technologies.

Official name: Leading Ethical AI Development (LEAD) for Kids Act
Casual name: Leading Ethical AI Development (LEAD) for Kids Act April 2025
Collections: Editors' Picks;U.S. state and local documents
Short summary: Establishes the LEAD for Kids Act, mandating AI systems intended for children to assess risk levels and adhere to regulations. Forms a board to oversee compliance, issue guidelines, and ensure AI doesn't adversely impact children. Imposes penalties for noncompliance.
Long summary:
Establishes the LEAD for Kids Standards Board to govern AI systems affecting children in California.

Requires AI developers to classify systems as "covered products" with risk assessments ranging from prohibited to low risk.

Mandates developers to register covered products and submit risk assessments by July 1, 2028.

Prohibits AI use that adversely impacts children's health, safety, or privacy without informed consent.

Requires companion chatbots and systems that assess emotional states or collect biometric data to receive additional scrutiny.

Mandates independent audits based on risk level and public incident reporting for adverse impacts.

Obligates deployers to prevent unauthorized child access and adhere to usage requirements that do not increase system risk.

Prohibits retaliation against employees reporting violations.

Allows the Attorney General to enforce violations with penalties, and provides a private right of action for harmed children.

Creates the LEAD for Kids AI Fund to support these regulatory activities.

Official name: Advancing Artificial Intelligence Education for American Youth
Casual name: Advancing AI Education for American Youth EO
Collections: Editors' Picks;U.S. regulations, executive orders, and agency policies
Short summary: Establishes a Task Force to implement AI education policy, promoting AI literacy and training for educators. Launches a Presidential AI Challenge and prioritizes AI in K-12 instruction through partnerships. Enhances teacher training and apprenticeships related to AI across various sectors.
Long summary:
Establishes a White House Task Force on Artificial Intelligence Education to promote AI literacy and proficiency in the United States, chaired by the Director of the Office of Science and Technology Policy.

Requires the Task Force to coordinate Federal efforts on AI education, including organizing the Presidential Artificial Intelligence Challenge to encourage collaboration and innovation in AI.

Mandates public-private partnerships to provide K-12 AI education resources and calls for increased funding and resources to develop AI literacy.

Prioritizes AI-focused training for educators and the integration of AI tools in classrooms through grants and public-private partnerships.

Instructs the Secretary of Education and other Federal entities to support the use of AI in educational settings to improve outcomes.

Directs the Secretary of Labor to increase AI-related Registered Apprenticeships and promote AI skill development through the Workforce Innovation and Opportunity Act (WIOA).

Encourages agencies to prioritize AI in educational grant programs.

Official name: United States-Israel Defense Partnership Act of 2025
Casual name: United States-Israel Defense Partnership Act of 2025, Sec. 6. "United States-Israel Emerging Technology Capabilities Cooperation."
Short summary: Supports U.S.-Israel defense collaboration on AI, cybersecurity, robotics, and more. Authorizes joint R&D, requires protective measures for sensitive data, establishes cost-sharing agreements, assigns the Irregular Warfare Technology Support Directorate as lead, and provides $50 million annually from 2026-2030.
Long summary:
Supports and encourages defense collaboration between the United States and Israel in emerging technologies, specifically artificial intelligence, cybersecurity, robotics, quantum, and automation. 

Authorizes the Secretary of Defense, in coordination with the Secretary of State and the Director of National Intelligence, to jointly conduct research, development, testing, and evaluation with Israel in these areas. 

Requires protection of sensitive information and national security interests during any activities carried out under this authority. 

Prohibits activities from commencing until a report is submitted detailing a memorandum of agreement for cost-sharing and intellectual property rights, and ensuring semiannual expenditure reporting by Israel.

Designates the Irregular Warfare Technology Support Directorate as the lead agency once the report is submitted. 

Mandates semiannual reports to Congress on program developments. 

Authorizes $50,000,000 annually from 2026 to 2030 for this program.

Official name: Department of Energy Quantum Leadership Act of 2025, Section 2 "Department of Energy quantum information science research program"
Casual name: Department of Energy Quantum Leadership Act of 2025, Sec 2 ("Department of Energy quantum information science research program")
Short summary: Amends the National Quantum Initiative Act to require quantum science, engineering, and technology research programs. Establishes quantum computing strategic and supply chain plans. Engages industry and academia for commercialization. Restricts funding tied to foreign entities of concern and Confucius Institutes.
Long summary:
Amends the National Quantum Initiative Act to enhance quantum information science, engineering, and technology research led by the Secretary of Energy.

Mandates the establishment of National Quantum Information Science Research Centers to drive breakthroughs and collaborate with industries and other entities.

Directs cooperative research and commercialization efforts to advance various quantum fields, including AI, quantum computing, communications, and security.

Requires the development of a strategic 10-year plan for high-performance computing systems integrating quantum and AI.

Funds a new $20 million per year program for quantum high-performance computing R&D from 2026 to 2030.

Establishes a university-led traineeship program and prohibits funding to entities affiliated with Confucius Institute or certain foreign entities.

Prioritizes coordination with other federal agencies and entities.

Allocates $175 million annually for these initiatives from 2026 to 2030 with specific restrictions.

Official name: Coast Guard Authorization Act of 2025, Subtitle C--"Matters Involving Uncrewed Systems"
Casual name: Coast Guard Authorization Act of 2025, (Subtitle C - "Matters Involving Uncrewed Systems")
Short summary: Establishes a National Advisory Committee on Autonomous Maritime Systems to advise on regulation. Launches a pilot program exempting NOAA-operated uncrewed maritime systems from certain regulations. Mandates Coast Guard training on these systems. Requires a Coast Guard report on uncrewed systems and technology development.
Long summary:
Establishes a National Advisory Committee on Autonomous Maritime Systems to advise the Secretary on regulation and use within U.S. territorial waters, comprising 15 expert members representing diverse maritime sectors.

Implements a two-year pilot program exempting small uncrewed maritime systems from certain regulatory requirements when operated by the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration, unless immediate safety concerns arise.

Mandates the Coast Guard to develop a training course on small uncrewed maritime systems, addressing operations, safety, and communication aspects over three years.

Incorporates National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration representation into the Automated and Autonomous Vessel Policy Council.

Requires the Coast Guard to submit a report within one year establishing an uncrewed systems capabilities office, detailing strategies for technology acquisition, coordination, collaboration, and expanding capabilities using AI and machine learning.

Defines relevant terms, including "counter-uncrewed system" and "small uncrewed maritime systems."

Official name: Copyright and Artificial Intelligence Part 3: Generative AI Training Report Pre-Publication Version 2025
Casual name: Copyright Office Part 3 Generative AI Training Pre-Publication Version 2025
Short summary: Recommends allowing AI licensing markets to develop without government intervention; concludes that voluntary market-based solutions are preferable to compulsory licensing. Suggests considering extended collective licensing (ECL) for unworkable sectors.
Long summary:
Suggests that voluntary licensing agreements for AI training are preferable to compulsory licensing solutions, as market-based solutions can reduce transaction complexity and cost and avoid policy inertia.

Proposes extended collective licensing (ECL) as a less intrusive approach for sectors where voluntary licensing proves unworkable.

Discourages opt-out mechanisms but emphasizing consent for uses within statutory rights and acknowledges that Congress may consider exceptions allowing opt-outs.

Addresses antitrust concerns and encourages the Department of Justice to provide guidance.
Tags: Strategies: Licensing, registration, and certification;Strategies: Governance development;Harms: Harm to property;Harms: Violation of civil or human rights, including privacy

Official name: Copyright and Artificial Intelligence Part 1: Digital Replicas July 2024
Casual name: Copyright Office Part 1 Digital Replicas 2024
Short summary: Recommends Congress establish a federal right to protect individuals from unauthorized digital replicas, make the right licensable but not assignable with civil remedies, apply secondary liability with conditioned safe harbor for OSPs, include First Amendment accommodations, avoid full preemption of state publicity rights, and rely on the Copyright Office as a resource for Congress and other branches in considering these recommendations and future developments
Long summary:
Recommends that Congress establish a federal right to protect individuals from the distribution of unauthorized digital replicas during their lifetimes. 

Proposes that this right be licensable and subject to guardrails but not assignable, with remedies such as monetary damages and injunctive relief available. 

Recommends applying traditional rules of secondary liability, with a safe harbor for online service providers suitably conditioned.

Suggests explicit First Amendment accommodations in the law.

Advises against full preemption of state laws, recognizing well-developed state rights of publicity. 

Offers the Copyright Office as a resource for Congress, the courts, and the executive branch in implementing these recommendations and addressing future developments.

Official name: One Big Beautiful Bill Act 2025, Section 43201 ("Artificial intelligence and information technology modernization initiative")
Casual name: One Big Beautiful Bill Act 2025, Section 43201 ("Artificial intelligence and information technology modernization initiative")
Short summary: Appropriates $500 million to the Department of Commerce for AI-driven system modernization and cybersecurity improvements for Federal information technology systems.
Long summary:
Appropriates $500 million to the Department of Commerce for FY 2025 to modernize federal IT systems using AI and automation technologies.

Authorizes the Secretary of Commerce to use funds to replace legacy systems, adopt efficient AI models, and enhance cybersecurity with AI solutions.

Enacts a 10-year moratorium preventing state and local governments from regulating AI models or systems.

Exempts laws facilitating AI deployment or removing legal barriers, provided they do not impose additional requirements unless federally mandated or generally applicable.
Tags: Strategies: Government support;Risk factors: Security;Risk factors: Security: Cybersecurity;Risk factors: Reliability;Applications: Government: other applications/unspecified;Strategies: Tiering

Official name: Responsible AI safety and education act
Casual name: New York A6453 (RAISE Act)
Collections: Editors' Picks;U.S. state and local documents
Short summary: Amends the general business law to establish the Responsible AI Safety and Education Act (RAISE Act), compelling transparency, safety protocols, and mandatory disclosures for frontier AI models. Enables state enforcement through penalties, prohibiting deployments that pose critical harm risks. Applies to frontier models in New York.
Long summary:
Amends New York's general business law to introduce the Responsible AI Safety and Education (RAISE) Act, implementing transparency requirements for deploying AI frontier models.

Requires large AI developers to implement and retain a written safety and security protocol that must be published with redactions and submitted to the Attorney General and Homeland Security.

Stipulates annual reviews of the protocol to accommodate any advancements.

Requires prompt reporting of safety incidents to authorities within 72 hours.

Prohibits knowingly making false or misleading statements concerning AI documentation.

Empowers the Attorney General to bring civil actions for violations, with penalties up to $30 million for repeated offenses.

Confirms that the Act applies exclusively to frontier AI models developed or operating in New York State.

Clarifies that this Act doesn't establish a private right of action and is cumulative with other legal obligations.

Short summary: Appropriates $1.076 billion for AI and machine learning to enhance non-intrusive inspection equipment and combat narcotics entry. Restricts funds for untested autonomous surveillance towers. Defines "autonomous" systems as AI-integrated tools identifying items of interest.

Official name: H.R. 3447 Chip Security Act
Casual name: Chip Security Act 2025
Short summary: Requires the Secretary of Commerce to mandate chip security mechanisms for location verification before exporting integrated circuit products. Obligates reporting of tampering or diversion. Mandates the assessment and development of additional security mechanisms, considering national security and export compliance objectives.
Long summary:
Mandates the Secretary of Commerce to issue standards for chip security mechanisms on integrated circuit products within 180 days of the Act's enactment.

Requires covered integrated circuit products to include location verification and other security features before export, re-export, or in-country transfer.

Obligates exporters to report any unauthorized change in product location, user, or tampering incidents to the Under Secretary of Industry and Security.

Calls for an assessment within one year to identify additional security mechanisms needed to enhance Export Control Reform Act compliance and prevent unauthorized use.

Directs the Secretary to develop secondary security requirements based on assessment findings and report to Congress with a roadmap for implementation.

Empowers the Secretary to verify ownership and location of exported products and maintain records.

Requires annual assessments of new chip security mechanisms and recommends export control modifications to expand permissible destinations for secure products.

Official name: California Senate Bill 53
Casual name: California SB 53 September 2025 (Artificial intelligence models: large developers)
Collections: Editors' Picks;U.S. state and local documents
Short summary: Requires large frontier developers to implement and publish frontier AI frameworks, assess catastrophic risks, and publish transparency reports; requires the Office of Emergency Services to establish reporting mechanisms for critical safety incidents and catastrophic risk assessments; establishes a consortium to develop a framework for the creation of CalCompute; creates civil penalties for violations of this chapter.
Long summary:
Defines terms for the purposes of this chapter, including catastrophic risk and critical safety incident.

Requires large frontier developers to implement and publish frontier AI frameworks, publish transparency reports, and disclose assessments of catastrophic risks to the Office of Emergency Services.

Instructs the Office of Emergency Services to establish a critical safety incident reporting mechanism and a mechanism for frontier developers to submit summaries of catastrophic risk assessments; mandates the disclosure of critical safety incidents that pose an imminent risk of death or serious injury within 24 hours. 

Tasks the Office of Emergency Services, beginning in 2027, to annually produce and submit a report with anonymized and aggregated information regarding critical safety incidents to the Legislature and Governor.

Creates a civil penalty of up to $1,000,000 for violations of this chapter, specifically the failure of a frontier developer to report a safety incident or comply with their own frontier AI framework.

Creates a 14-member consortium to develop a framework for the creation of CalCompute, a public cloud computing cluster that will advance the development and deployment of safe and equitable AI. 

Prohibits frontier developers from preventing, or retaliating against, covered employees who disclose information about catastrophic risks or violations of the Business and Professions Code.

Provides remedies for violations of this section, and explains how courts should handle cases regarding disclosures made by covered employees.

Allows Attorney General and individuals to seek penalties for violations.
Long summary:
Prohibits operators from making companion chatbots available to children unless they prevent harms such as encouraging self-harm, violence, substance abuse, illegal activity, or engaging in erotic interactions.

Requires operators to ensure chatbots do not prioritize user validation over factual accuracy or safety.

Mandates that chatbots do not offer unsupervised mental health therapy or discourage professional help.

Allows the Attorney General to impose civil penalties, injunctive relief, and recover attorney’s fees for violations.

Permits children or their guardians to sue operators for actual and punitive damages, as well as attorney’s fees and injunctive relief.

Defines “companion chatbots” and sets specific criteria for their operation regarding children under 18.

Applies severability to maintain the validity of remaining provisions if any are invalidated.

Official name: General Purpose AI Code of Practice, Transparency Chapter
Casual name: General Purpose AI Code of Practice, Transparency Chapter
Short summary: Requires general-purpose AI model providers to comply with AI Act obligations, particularly Articles 53 and 55. Obligates providers to maintain, disclose, and update model documentation for compliance assessment. Tasks the AI Office with overseeing adherence to these obligations.
Long summary:
Provides a Code of Practice to enhance compliance with Articles 53 and 55 of the AI Act, focusing on human-centric and trustworthy AI while ensuring protection against AI's harmful effects.

Guides providers of general-purpose AI models in fulfilling documentation obligations per the AI Act, ensuring updated Model Documentation is accessible to the AI Office and downstream providers.

Requires disclosures of contact information to facilitate information requests from the AI Office and downstream providers, ensuring compliance assessment for high-risk AI systems through timely information provision.

Advises on maintaining the quality, security, and integrity of documented information, suggesting adherence to technical standards and protocols.

Encourages voluntary public transparency, allowing public access to non-confidential information, and providing training content summaries as required by AI Act provisions in line with Article 53(1)(d).

Exempts open-source AI models without systemic risk from these requirements, aligning obligations with the proportionality principle.
