Official name: White Paper on Artificial Intelligence Regulation in Ukraine: Vision of the Ministry of Digital Transformation of Ukraine 2024
Casual name: White Paper on AI Regulation in Ukraine 2024 (Stages 1 and 2)
Short summary: Supports the development of tools to help businesses and the state prepare for future regulation, including a methodology to assess AI's impact on human rights; proposes creating general and sectoral AI recommendations and voluntary codes of conduct; calls for the gradual implementation of EU regulation and the creation of an AI supervisory body before EU accession.
Long summary:
Proposes the development of tools to help businesses and the state prepare for future regulation, including a methodology for assessing AI's impact on human rights and a regulatory sandbox.

Promotes the voluntary labeling of AI systems to foster transparency, trust, and accountability.

Calls for the creation of general and sectoral recommendations, voluntary codes of conduct, and a web portal for the Responsible AI center. 

Explains how the proposed soft tolls and training tools are expected to positively impact businesses, citizens, and the state.

Advocates for drafting Ukrainian AI regulation law immediately after the adoption of EU AI regulation; supports the gradual implementation of EU AI regulation and the deferral of certain provisions in order to provide more preparation time.

Emphasizes the need for Ukraine to transpose EU AI Regulation and establish an AI supervisory body before EU accession.

Encourages partnerships with leading AI platforms to address potential human rights violations; proposes applying the Trusted Flagger concept, which would involve Ukrainian NGOs acting as Trusted Flaggers that monitor and filter complaints about human rights violations related to AI use.

Allows stakeholders, such as government bodies and academia, to provide feedback on the White Paper within 3 months of its publication.

Official name: Federal Senate Bill of Law No. 21, of 2020
Casual name: Brazil Federal Senate Bill of Law No. 21, of 2020
Short summary: Establishes a framework of aims, foundation, and principles for AI governance in Brazil, defines guidelines for future regulations, including risk-proportionate regulation. Prioritising human rights, environmental protection, transparency, non-discrimination, competitiveness, and the balancing of innovation and self-regulation with safety requirements for high-risk systems.
Long summary:
Establishes foundations and principles for AI development and applications in Brazil and guidelines for the promotion and performance of public authorities. 

Defines AI systems as computational processes that, from goals defined by humans, can learn from data to perceive, interpret, and interact with the external environment, excluding automation solely guided by predefined parameters without learning capability.

Declares that AI implementation in Brazil is aimed at technological development, sustainable and inclusive economic development, societal well-being, competitiveness, increased participation in the value chain, improved public services, innovation, and environmental protection.

Declares that the foundations for AI development and implementation in Brazil are scientific and technological development, free enterprise and competition, human rights and democratic values, freedom of expression and communication, non-discrimination, recognition of the dynamic nature, the encouragement of self-regulation, protection of privacy, information security, national defence and sovereignty, freedom of business models, preservation of AI systems' security, stability, and functionality, free competition, and freedom from abusive market practices.

Declares that the principles for AI development and application in Brazil are beneficial purpose, human centrality, non-discrimination, neutrality, transparency, security and prevention, responsible innovation, and data availability.

Requires the Brazilian government to observe guidelines on necessity, sector context, specific risks, proportionality, evidence-based decision-making, public consultations, and regulatory impact analysis, as well as subjective liability rules when regulating the implementation of AI.

Establishing a tiering of the risk-based management guidelines, encouraging flexible regulatory techniques and responsible innovation in low-risk AI, and granting legal government power to inquire into high-risk AI's security and prevention, and mandating liability for AI-harm in consumer relations and for public service providing legal entities.

Established guidelines for the Brazilian government's performance regarding the usage and promotion of AI, including promotion of trust in AI, investment in research and development, promotion of interoperability, incentives for adoption, incentives for job market restructuring, educational innovation, stimulus for innovation-promoting regulation, stimulus for multi-stakeholder governance collaboration, promotion of international cooperation, as well as transparency and ethical usage. 

Mandates sectoral bodies and entities to apply the law and to monitor AI risk management, establish rights, duties, and responsibilities, and recognize self-regulatory institutions.

Grants exclusive authority to the federal Union for legislating on AI

Official name: Federal Senate Bill of Law No. 5051, of 2019
Casual name: Brazil Federal Senate Bill of Law No. 5051, of 2019
Short summary: Requires human supervision in AI systems and assigns civil liability for damages from AI usage to the supervisor.  Requires state entities to train workers on AI and create specific workers' protection policies.
Long summary:
Establishes nonexhaustive principles for AI usage in Brazil rooted in human dignity, transparency, and the protection of privacy and personal data.

Requires AI decision-making systems to be auxiliary to humans, with supervision compatible with the type and severity of decisions.

Attributes civil liability for AI-induced damages to the system’s human supervisor.

Directs government entities to promote AI education in the workforce, create worker protection policies, and ensure gradual AI adoption.

Requires government AI applications to enhance service quality and efficiency for the population.

Effective 45 days after publication.
Tags: Strategies: Government support: AI workforce-related;Strategies: Governance development

Official name: Federal Senate Bill of Law No. 2338, of 2023
Casual name: Brazil Federal Senate Bill of Law No. 2338, of 2023
Short summary: Establishes regulations for developing, implementing, and using AI systems in Brazil, emphasizing human rights, privacy, non-discrimination, and transparency. Requires risk assessment for AI applications, prohibiting excessive-risk systems. Defines governance and accountability for high-risk AI, stipulating rights to contest automated decisions. Designates a competent authority for oversight and enforcement, and prescribes penalties for non-compliance. Encourages innovation through regulatory sandboxes while ensuring data protection and ethical AI usage.
Long summary:
Sets general rules for AI development, implementation, and use in Brazil, prioritizing human rights, democratic values, and innovation.

Establishes principles such as transparency, non-discrimination, and human oversight for AI systems.

Defines 'artificial intelligence system' and 'artificial intelligence agents,' and assigns regulation and oversight to a designated competent authority.

Mandates that AI systems undergo preliminary risk assessment, with high-risk systems requiring algorithmic impact assessments.

Prohibits AI systems that exploit vulnerabilities or use subliminal techniques to harm individuals.

Requires transparency and accountability in AI systems, including the right to contest automated decisions and request human intervention.

Stipulates civil liability provisions, holding AI providers or operators accountable for damages, with stricter liability for high-risk systems.

Encourages innovation through regulatory sandboxes and considers AI impact on copyright and intellectual property.

Designates a competent authority for enforcement, inspection, and issuance of sanctions for non-compliance.

Official name: National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2026, Section 224 ("National Security and Defense Artificial Intelligence Institute")
Casual name: FY2026 NDAA, Section 224 ("National Security and Defense Artificial Intelligence Institute")
Short summary: Authorize the Secretary of Defense to establish AI Institutes focused on national security. Directs support for interdisciplinary AI research, partnership, innovation ecosystems, and workforce development.
Long summary:
Allows the Secretary of Defense to establish one or more artificial intelligence research institutes focused on national security and defense.

Requires each institute to address foundational or cross-cutting AI challenges relevant to defense and national security.

Directs institutes to form partnerships across federal agencies, academia, nonprofit research organizations, state and local governments, and industry.

Requires institutes to foster innovation ecosystems that translate research into applications and products that enhance national security and defense capabilities.

Requires support for research and development that spans multiple institutions of higher education and organizations.

Requires institutes to support interdisciplinary research and workforce development in AI and related disciplines within the United States.
Tags: Strategies: New institution;Strategies: Government support: For R&D;Strategies: Government support: AI workforce-related

Official name: National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2026, Section 3117 ("Appropriate scoping of artificial intelligence research within the National Nuclear Security Administration")
Casual name: FY2026 NDAA, Section 3117 ("Appropriate scoping of artificial intelligence research within the National Nuclear Security Administration")
Short summary: Limits funding for AI research within the National Nuclear Security Administration to nuclear security missions. Allows AI research programs elsewhere within the Department of Energy or other Federal agencies, provided it doesn't interfere with nuclear security missions or facilities.
Long summary:
Amends the Atomic Energy Defense Act to specify that funds appropriated for AI research and development by the National Nuclear Security Administration (NNSA) can only support the Administration's nuclear security missions.

States that this funding limitation does not prohibit the establishment of national security AI research programs within other areas of the Department of Energy or other federal agencies.

Allows the use of NNSA resources for an enduring national security AI program by external entities, provided support is given on a full cost recovery basis and does not interfere with the nuclear security missions of involved facilities.
Tags: Strategies: Government support;Strategies: Government support: For R&D;Applications: Government: military and public safety

Official name: National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2026, Section 347 ("Integration of commercially available artificial intelligence capabilities into logistics operations")
Casual name: FY2026 NDAA, Section 347 ("Integration of commercially available artificial intelligence capabilities into logistics operations")
Short summary: Facilitates integration of commercial AI for logistics into two Department of Defense exercises in 2026. Directs the Secretary of Defense to brief Congress on exercise specifics and AI integration impact on readiness and operations.
Long summary:
Facilitates the integration of commercially available AI capabilities into logistics operations of the Department of Defense (DoD) during fiscal year 2026 exercises.

Requires the Secretary of Defense to identify and choose AI products designed for logistics that comply with DoD data security protocols.

Ensures the selected AI products can adapt to software changes rapidly and prioritizes products from small or nontraditional firms.

Expedites approvals for secure data use and ensures compliance with DoD cybersecurity policies for these exercises.

Mandates an interim briefing by March 1, 2026, detailing the exercises, participating commanders, AI capabilities used, timelines, resource needs, and assessment metrics.

Requires a post-exercise briefing by the commander on the integration, effectiveness, and recommendations for further AI development in DoD operations.

Official name: National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2026, Section 1007 ("Use of technology using artificial intelligence to facilitate audit of the financial statements of the Department of Defense for fiscal year 2026")
Casual name: FY2026 NDAA, Section 1007 ("Use of technology using artificial intelligence to facilitate audit of the financial statements of the Department of Defense for fiscal year 2026")
Short summary: Encourages the Secretaries of Defense, Army, Navy, and Air Force to use AI for auditing financial statements. Requires the Director of the Chief Digital and AI Office to oversee AI adoption for financial management, coordinating with relevant defense officials.
Long summary:
Encourages the Secretaries of Defense, Army, Navy, and Air Force to use AI and machine learning technologies to facilitate audits of the Department of Defense's financial statements for fiscal year 2026.

Oversees the Director of the Chief Digital and Artificial Intelligence Office, in coordination with the Under Secretary of Defense for Research and Engineering and the Inspector General, in adopting AI and machine learning technologies to support financial management and enterprise business operations.
Tags: Applications: Business services and analytics;Strategies: Convening;Applications: Business services and analytics

Official name: National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2026, Section 6601 ("Artificial Intelligence security guidance")
Casual name: FY2026 NDAA, Section 6601 ("Artificial Intelligence security guidance")
Short summary: Requires National Security Agency Director to develop AI security guidance defending against nation-state theft or sabotage, identify vulnerabilities, and collaborate with external entities. Requires publishing and updating the guidance at classified or unclassified levels.
Long summary:
Amends the Intelligence Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2025 to require the National Security Agency Director to develop security guidance to defend AI technologies from theft by nation-state adversaries.

Requires identification of vulnerabilities in AI technologies, focusing on cybersecurity risks and security challenges unique to protecting such technologies from theft or sabotage by nation-state adversaries.

Requires identification of AI supply chain elements that could benefit adversaries if accessed.

Requires identification of supply chain, development, or product lifecycle elements that, if accessed by adversaries, would either accelerate their AI progress or provide opportunities to compromise the confidentiality, integrity, or availability of AI systems or associated supply chains.

Requires identification of strategies for AI technologies to identify, protect, detect, respond to, and recover from cyber threats.

Permits the NSA Director to collaborate, on a voluntary basis, with other U.S. government departments and agencies, research entities, and private sector entities on AI model safety and security.

Permits the NSA Director to provide computing resources the Director deems appropriate in support of such collaboration.

Requires the NSA Director to publish, and may update from time to time, AI security guidance to be shared with relevant public and private sector entities at unclassified or classified levels.
Tags: Risk factors: Security: Cybersecurity;Strategies: Governance development;Risk factors: Security

Official name: National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2026, Section 245 ("Biological data for artificial intelligence")
Casual name: FY2026 NDAA, Section 245 ("Biological data for artificial intelligence")
Short summary: Requires the Secretary of Defense to develop requirements ensuring DoD-funded biological data resources facilitate AI use. Defines "qualified biological data," includes metrics for data quality, cybersecurity safeguards, privacy protections, and allows national security exceptions. Requires the Secretary to consult relevant sectors about the feasibility of new requirements and review existing frameworks.
Long summary:
Requires the Secretary of Defense to develop and implement requirements for storing biological data from Department of Defense-funded research to facilitate the use of such data for advanced computational methods, including AI, within one year of the act's enactment.

Defines "qualified biological data resource" using criteria such as data type, dataset size, federal funding amount, data sensitivity, and other factors deemed appropriate by the Secretary of Defense.

Imposes tiered cybersecurity safeguards and access controls for data storage.

Establishes exceptions for national security and requirements for individual privacy protection.

Consults with military and research officials, as well as private and academic stakeholders, to prevent overly burdensome requirements.

Incorporates existing federal frameworks and standards as appropriate.
Tags: Strategies: Governance development;Risk factors: Security;Strategies: Governance development

Official name: National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2026, Section 1512 ("Artificial intelligence and machine learning security in the Department of Defense")
Casual name: FY2026 NDAA, Section 1512 ("Artificial intelligence and machine learning security in the Department of Defense")
Short summary: Requires the Secretary of Defense to develop a cybersecurity policy for AI/ML systems no later than 180 days after the act is passed.  Develop a comprehensive review of the effectiveness of the AI/ML policies. Addresses potential security risks, implements methods to mitigate those risks, and establishes standard policy.  Requires a comprehensive report of the threats and cybersecurity measures by August 31, 2026.
Long summary:
Requires the Secretary of Defense to develop a cybersecurity policy for AI/ML systems no later than 180 days after the act is passed.  

Addresses potential security risks specific to AI, such as model serialization attacks, model tampering, data leakage, adversarial prompt injection, model extraction, model jailbreaks, and supply chain attacks.

Implements methods to mitigate those risks using industry-recognized frameworks to ensure AI/ML systems are trustworthy.

Requires training for the workforce of the Department of Defense to ensure personnel are prepared to identify and mitigate vulnerabilities that are specific to AI/ML.

Establishes standard policy for governance, testing, and auditing of AI systems.

Requires the Secretary of Defense to submit a comprehensive review and report of the effectiveness of the AI/ML cybersecurity practices, including an identification of gaps in the existing security measures, by August 31, 2026.

Official name: National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2026, Section 1534 ("Digital sandbox environments for artificial intelligence")
Casual name: FY2026 NDAA, Section 1534 ("Digital sandbox environments for artificial intelligence")
Short summary: Requires the Secretary of Defense, via the Chief Digital and Artificial Intelligence Officer, to establish an AI sandbox task force by April 2026 to facilitate AI experimentation and deployment. Identifies members and duties, with termination by January 2030.
Long summary:
Establishes a Task Force on artificial intelligence sandbox environments by April 1, 2026, under the Secretary of Defense through the Chief Digital and AI Officer.

Tasks the Task Force to develop and deploy AI sandbox environments for experimentation, training, and deployment across the Department of Defense.

Requires AI sandbox environments to be designed for use by personnel with varying technical proficiencies, facilitate familiarization with AI capabilities, and accelerate responsible AI adoption.

Chairs the Task Force with the Chief Digital and AI Officer and includes key officials from the Department of Defense, military departments, and combatant commands.

Requires the Task Force to standardize AI sandbox requirements across the Department of Defense, catalog and align existing solutions, improve efficiency and approvals, and provide clear guidance and resources to support users of all technical skill levels.

Requires a briefing to congressional defense committees by August 1, 2026.

Terminates the Task Force on January 1, 2030.

Defines key terms including "artificial intelligence" and "artificial intelligence sandbox environment."

Official name: National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2026, Section 547 ("Pilot program for generative artificial intelligence and spatial computing for performance training and proficiency assessment")
Casual name: FY2026 NDAA, Section 547 ("Pilot program for generative artificial intelligence and spatial computing for performance training and proficiency assessment")
Short summary: Instructs the Secretary of the Navy to develop a pilot program for generative AI and spatial computing in training. Requires assessment of feasibility compared to other methods. Directs a report on program results 90 days post-termination.
Long summary:
Instructs the Secretary of the Navy to develop and implement a pilot program using generative AI and spatial computing for immersive training and proficiency assessment within 90 days of enactment.

Requires the program to develop content for at least five occupational specialties and compare the feasibility and effectiveness of these methods with traditional training, especially regarding cost and time efficiency.

Terminates the program one year after the date of establishment.

Requires the Secretary of the Navy to submit a report to congressional defense committees within 90 days after program termination, detailing program results, effectiveness analysis, and any cost or time savings achieved.
Tags: Applications: Government: military and public safety;Strategies: Evaluation;Strategies: Pilots and testbeds;Strategies: Government study or report;Applications: Government: military and public safety

Official name: National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2026, Section 1515 ("Incorporation of artificial intelligence considerations into cybersecurity training")
Casual name: FY2026 NDAA, Section 1515 ("Incorporation of artificial intelligence considerations into cybersecurity training")
Short summary: Requires the Secretary of Defense, via the Chief Information Officer, to revise cybersecurity training for Armed Forces and civilian employees to include AI-related cybersecurity challenges within one year of the Act’s enactment.
Long summary:
Requires the Secretary of Defense, through the Department of Defense's Chief Information Officer, to revise mandatory cybersecurity training.

Includes content addressing unique cybersecurity challenges posed by artificial intelligence.

Sets a deadline of one year from the enactment date of this Act for these revisions to be implemented.

Applies to both members of the Armed Forces and civilian employees within the Department of Defense.
Tags: Strategies: Government support;Strategies: Government support: AI workforce-related;Risk factors: Security;Risk factors: Security: Cybersecurity

Official name: National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2026, Section 1535 ("Artificial Intelligence Futures Steering Committee")
Casual name: FY2026 NDAA, Section 1535 ("Artificial Intelligence Futures Steering Committee")
Short summary: Establishes the Artificial Intelligence Futures Steering Committee by April 1, 2026, under the Secretary of Defense. Directs it to develop policies for AI adoption, assess AI trajectories, and analyze AI risks and adversary developments. Requires quarterly meetings and a report to U.S. Congress by January 31, 2027.
Long summary:
Establishes the Artificial Intelligence Futures Steering Committee by April 1, 2026, under the Secretary of Defense.

Comprises key defense leaders, such as the Deputy Secretary of Defense and the Vice Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff.

Holds responsibility for formulating policies for the governance, adoption, and risk mitigation of advanced AI systems, including those approaching artificial general intelligence.

Analyzes the trajectory of emerging AI technologies, such as neuromorphic computing and agentic algorithms.

Assesses adversaries' AI capabilities and potential operational effects of integrating AI into Department of Defense systems.

Develops strategies for ethical and technical AI integration, outlining resource requirements and goals.

Requires meetings at least quarterly.

Instructs the Deputy Secretary of Defense to report findings by January 31, 2027, and mandates public release of the unclassified portion.

Terminates the committee's responsibilities on December 31, 2027.

Official name: National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2026, Section 350 ("Pilot program for data-enabled ground vehicle maintenance")
Casual name: FY2026 NDAA, Section 350 ("Pilot program for data-enabled ground vehicle maintenance")
Short summary: Establishes a pilot program for the Armed Forces to apply AI in ground vehicle maintenance. Requires assessment of AI feasibility, effectiveness, cost savings, and cybersecurity risks. Requires a report to Congress. Terminates authority by January 1, 2029.
Long summary:
Establishes a pilot program using commercially available AI technologies to improve ground vehicle maintenance in the Army, Navy, or Air Force.

Requires the Secretary concerned, in consultation with the Chief Digital and Artificial Intelligence Officer of the Department of Defense, to implement the program within 90 days of the Act's enactment.

Directs the Secretary to assess the feasibility, effectiveness, and cost savings of AI-driven maintenance, and to identify cybersecurity and integration challenges.

Requires a report to Congress on the pilot program's activities within one year of enactment.

Terminates the pilot program on January 1, 2029.

Official name: National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2026, Section 341 ("Maintenance inspection capabilities and requirements")
Casual name: FY2026 NDAA, Section 341 ("Maintenance inspection capabilities and requirements")
Short summary: Requires the Secretary of Defense to ensure maintenance of aviation safety items and parts includes technical data requirements or diagnostic tools, leveraging AI and machine learning for predictive maintenance when necessary. Ensures acquisition planning for such tools is integrated into system sustainment.
Long summary:
Require the Secretary of Defense to ensure maintenance of aviation critical safety items and mission critical parts includes using technical data requirements or diagnostic tools when available.

Prohibit reliance solely on visual inspection unless no other tools are available or visual inspection suffices based on technical data.

Mandate that the acquisition of necessary technical data requirements and diagnostic tools is planned for system sustainment, including those leveraging AI and machine learning to enhance predictive maintenance capabilities.

Define a diagnostic tool as one capable of detecting issues like corrosion and damage, further specifying the use of AI for predictive maintenance databases.

Official name: National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2026, Section 907 ("Defense science board study on optimal organizational structure for digital solution and software delivery")
Casual name: FY2026 NDAA, Section 907 ("Defense science board study on optimal organizational structure for digital solution and software delivery")
Short summary: Requires the Secretary of Defense to direct a Defense Science Board study on optimizing digital solutions and software delivery in the Department of Defense, including AI model management and governance. Submits a report to Congress by February 2027.
Long summary:
Directs the Secretary of Defense to have the Defense Science Board study the optimal organizational structure for digital solutions engineering and software delivery within the Department of Defense.

Instructs the study to assess current organizational structures, challenges, and opportunities for integrating digital solutions, including artificial intelligence.

Requires evaluation of potential organizational approaches, such as establishing a new agency, consolidating functions, or creating hybrid models.

Includes a mandate to analyze advantages, cost-effectiveness, and governance requirements, with particular attention to artificial intelligence model management.

Demands recommendations for optimal organizational structures and unique acquisition authorities to enhance rapid digital solution deployment.

Necessitates transition planning embodying timelines, resource needs, and necessary legislative or regulatory adjustments.

Specifies delivery of the final report to the Secretary of Defense by February 1, 2027, followed by its submission to Congress with comments.

Official name: National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2026, Section 7335 ("Uncrewed systems capabilities report")
Casual name: FY2026 NDAA, Section 7335 ("Uncrewed systems capabilities report")
Short summary: Requires the Commandant to establish an uncrewed systems capabilities office within the Coast Guard, focusing on the acquisition, development, and integration of AI-enhanced uncrewed and counter-uncrewed technologies for improved maritime operations and interagency collaboration.
Long summary:
Requires the Commandant to submit a report within one year outlining a plan for establishing an uncrewed systems capabilities office within the Coast Guard.

Instructs the report to include a management strategy for the development and deployment of uncrewed systems, a service-wide coordination strategy, and contracting authorities for technology development.

Mandates a list of useful commercially available uncrewed technologies and a cross-agency collaboration plan with the Department of Defense and other agencies.

Calls for a strategy for obtaining and sharing data to improve maritime domain awareness.

Directs the development of a concept for a data system integrating AI and machine learning tools.

Obligates the Commandant, in coordination with various science and technology agencies, to brief relevant congressional committees on small uncrewed maritime systems annually for three years.

Official name: National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2026, Section 5303 ("Reports on technology transformation projects at the department")
Casual name: FY2026 NDAA, Section 5303 ("Reports on technology transformation projects at the department")
Short summary: Requires the Secretary to report annually on technology transformation projects, including those using AI, to congressional committees. Directs the publication of a public summary. Instructs the GAO to evaluate and report on project implementation, metrics, and oversight.
Long summary:
Require the Secretary, starting 180 days after the Act's enactment, to annually report on technology transformation projects, including those involving artificial intelligence, completed in the past two fiscal years to appropriate congressional committees.

Detail each report with objectives, scopes, technologies used, performance metrics, and any challenges encountered.

Include specific elements related to AI, such as efficiency, user impact, security, costs, and adoption rates.

Publish a public summary of the report on the Department's website within 60 days of submission, considering national security interests.

Direct the Government Accountability Office (GAO) to evaluate, 18 months post-enactment and biennially, the Department's implementation of technology projects, focusing on efficiency, adoption, and AI oversight.

Recommend the GAO provide suggestions for improving management and evaluation of these technology efforts.

Official name: National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2026, Section 1086 ("Framework for reforming technology transfer and foreign disclosure policies")
Casual name: FY2026 NDAA, Section 1086 ("Framework for reforming technology transfer and foreign disclosure policies")
Short summary: Requires the Secretary of Defense to create a framework for revising technology transfer and foreign disclosure policies, accommodating advanced technologies like artificial intelligence. Instructs updates to the National Disclosure Policy and mandates annual audits of license denials.
Long summary:
Directs the Secretary of Defense to produce a framework to revise technology transfer and foreign disclosure policies, emphasizing emerging and advanced technologies like AI.

Requires guidelines to balance technology protection with information sharing.

Includes recommendations for updating the National Disclosure Policy to incorporate AI and other advanced technologies.

Requires mechanisms to streamline approval processes and enhance transparency.

Mandates the consolidation of technology security and foreign disclosure approvals.

Requires updated directives detailing responsibilities in technology transfer.

Establishes metrics for evaluating policy effectiveness and mandates annual audits of denied license applications.

Instructs regular engagement with defense industry stakeholders, including nontraditional contractors.

Requires submission of the framework and annual reports to congressional defense committees, detailing improvements and industry feedback incorporation.

Allows reports to be unclassified but may include a classified annex.

Official name: National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2026, Section 1061 ("Notification of waivers under Department of Defense Directive 3000.09")
Casual name: FY2026 NDAA, Section 1061 ("Notification of waivers under Department of Defense Directive 3000.09")
Short summary: Amends Chapter 3 of title 10, United States Code, to require the Secretary of Defense to notify congressional defense committees of waivers related to autonomy in weapon systems, including rationale, description, and duration, within 30 days. Allows a classified annex.
Long summary:
Amends Chapter 3 of title 10, United States Code, to require the Secretary of Defense to notify congressional defense committees of any waivers issued under Department of Defense Directive 3000.09, which pertains to autonomy in weapon systems.

Requires this notification within 30 days of issuing any such waiver.

Specifies that each notification must include the rationale for the waiver, a description of the autonomous weapon system or technology involved, and the waiver's anticipated duration.

Allows these notifications to be submitted in unclassified form but permits a classified annex if necessary.

Amends Section 1066(b) of the National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2025 to include provisions for legal reviews in the context of approving and deploying lethal autonomous weapon systems.

Official name: National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2026, Section 1048 ("Authorization of Eastern Regional Range Complex for multi-domain operations and robotic autonomous systems training, testing, and experimentation")
Casual name: FY2026 NDAA, Section 1048 ("Authorization of Eastern Regional Range Complex for multi-domain operations and robotic autonomous systems training, testing, and experimentation")
Short summary: Authorize the Secretary of Defense to develop the Eastern Regional Range Complex for multi-domain operations, robotic autonomous systems training, and testing, including AI-related unmanned aircraft systems. Coordinate with various defense and research entities; consult on spectrum access requirements.
Long summary:
Authorize the Secretary of Defense, through military department Secretaries, to establish an Eastern Regional Range Complex as a hub for multi-domain operations and robotic autonomous systems, including unmanned aircraft systems.

Require the complex to facilitate joint non-kinetic, electromagnetic warfare, cyber, and information operations training using a range of environments and networks.

Support integrated multi-domain operations involving air, land, sea, cyber, and space components.

Conduct training, experimentation, and testing of robotic autonomous systems, and develop tactics and procedures for unmanned systems.

Evaluate emerging technologies and systems in contested cyber and electromagnetic environments.

Facilitate integration of prototype experimentation and exercises for rapid capability fielding.

Coordinate activities with the Joint Counter-small Unmanned Aircraft Systems Office, Joint Staff, and Defense Research Offices, among others.

Authorize consultation with the FCC and NTIA for spectrum access to support operations.

Official name: National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2026, Section 914 ("Small-UAS industrial base working group")
Casual name: FY2026 NDAA, Section 914 ("Small-UAS industrial base working group")
Short summary: Requires the Deputy Secretary of Defense to establish the Small-UAS Industrial Base Working Group by January 2026 to analyze and enhance the supplier base for small-UAS systems. Directs the Group to assess investments, partnerships, and qualification processes to strengthen the sUAS industrial base.
Long summary:
Establishes the Small-UAS Industrial Base Working Group, led by the Director of the Defense Autonomous Warfare Group and coordinated by the Deputy Secretary of Defense, no later than January 15, 2026.

Analyzes the sUAS industrial base, including system manufacturers and component suppliers, to strengthen the supplier base.

Identifies investments and actions to address fragile supply chains, and recommends partnerships between the sUAS industrial base and the Federal Government.

Reports findings and recommendations by April 1, 2026, with biannual updates thereafter, to both the Deputy Secretary of Defense and congressional defense committees.

Authorizes the Secretary of the Army to establish the SkyFoundry program if specific conditions are met.

Defines "small-UAS" and outlines its components, such as motors, batteries, and sensors, that the Working Group will focus on in its analyses and development plans.

Official name: National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2026, Section 233 ("Limitation on use of funds for certain navy software")
Casual name: FY2026 NDAA, Section 233 ("Limitation on use of funds for certain navy software")
Short summary: Prohibits expending funds on Navy's unmanned vessel software unless the Secretary of the Navy reports program schedules, cost estimates, performance assessments, and capability delivery assessments to congressional defense committees, and the Chief of Naval Operations validates operational user needs.
Long summary:
Prohibits the use of funds for the Navy's autonomy baseline manager or common control system for unmanned surface vessel programs unless specified conditions are met.

Requires the Secretary of the Navy to submit to congressional defense committees the original and current schedule of key deliverables, cost estimates, test and experimentation reports, and a market assessment from the Defense Innovation Unit.

Requires an assessment that program capabilities meet or exceed industry standards.

Requires the Chief of Naval Operations to validate that the program meets the operational needs of the Navy to congressional defense committees.

Official name: National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2026, Section 7337 ("National Academy of Sciences report on uncrewed systems and use of data")
Casual name: FY2026 NDAA, Section 7337 ("National Academy of Sciences report on uncrewed systems and use of data")
Short summary: Require the Commandant to arrange for the National Academy of Sciences to assess uncrewed maritime domain technologies for the Coast Guard, focusing on benefits, costs, operational impacts, and necessary adjustments. Instruct report submission to Congress within one year.
Long summary:
Require the National Academy of Sciences to assess uncrewed, autonomous, or remotely-controlled maritime technologies for Coast Guard use.

Describe benefits and limitations of such systems for ocean observation, vessel monitoring, weather observation, search and rescue, intelligence, and communications.

Assess how these technologies can guide Federal investment priorities by considering costs, reliability, versatility, and lifecycle.

Analyze how new and emerging technologies can lower costs and manpower while expanding Coast Guard capabilities and efficiency.

Evaluate progress from research to effective operations since the 2020 study on unmanned systems.

Identify necessary adjustments in Coast Guard authorities and procedures to incorporate uncrewed technologies.

Submit the assessment to Congressional committees within one year of agreement.

Utilize existing Coast Guard reports for cost formulation in the assessment.

Official name: National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2026, Section 345 ("Technology enhancement for surface ship maintenance")
Casual name: FY2026 NDAA, Section 345 ("Technology enhancement for surface ship maintenance")
Short summary: Requires the Secretary of the Navy to adopt advanced technologies, such as AI-driven predictive maintenance, for ship maintenance. Establishes a process for evaluating new proposals. Involves third-party review of disqualified proposals and mandates updates to related policies.
Long summary:
Instructs the Secretary of the Navy to investigate and adopt advanced technologies for surface ship maintenance to enhance readiness, reduce costs, and address delays.

Prioritizes technologies like automated weld inspection, real-time monitoring, and AI-driven predictive maintenance.

Establishes a process allowing non-government entities to propose additional advanced technologies for evaluation and potential adoption.

Directs the evaluation of new proposals within 90 days and requires consideration of technical merit and Navy needs.

Requires third-party review of decisions not to adopt proposed technologies, with findings reported to Congress.

Allows prioritization based on operational needs and budget constraints, with justifications included in reports.

Mandates updates to policies and contracts upon adoption of new technologies.

Obligates the Secretary to report to Congress within 180 days detailing timelines for technology adoption and explanations for any non-pursuit.

Official name: National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2026, Section 225 ("Advanced robotic automation for munitions manufacturing")
Casual name: FY2026 NDAA, Section 225 ("Advanced robotic automation for munitions manufacturing")
Short summary: Requires the Secretary of the Army to support robotic automation in munitions manufacturing. Includes objectives like safe system design, increased production, cyber security, and workforce training. Coordinates with relevant departments and briefs Congress by March 1, 2026.
Long summary:
Require the Secretary of the Army to implement a program to enhance robotic automation in munitions manufacturing at government-owned, contractor-operated facilities.

Include objectives such as designing safe, scalable robotic systems for munitions production, increasing production capacity, developing secure cyber infrastructure for integrating factory operations, and supporting workforce training in robotics and advanced manufacturing.

Coordinate with the Joint Program Executive Office Armaments and Ammunition and other relevant Army components during program execution.

Require a briefing to congressional defense committees by March 1, 2026, detailing program progress, lessons learned, and recommendations for broader adoption of robotic automation technologies in the defense sector.

Official name: SAFE from PRC Investments Act
Casual name: SAFE from PRC Investments Act
Short summary: Amends the Securities Exchange Act to require public companies and exchange-traded funds to disclose ties to the Chinese government or Communist Party, including board memberships, financial operations, military involvements, and data management, focusing on governance and security implications.
Long summary:
Requires public companies and exchange-traded funds (ETFs) investing in companies with ties to the People’s Republic of China (PRC) to disclose whether senior officials of the PRC government or Communist Party of China (CPC) sit on their boards or have executive roles.

Mandates disclosure of cash held in PRC operations, restrictions on cash repatriation, and details of assets, liabilities, and technology transfers.

Obligates reporting on interactions with CPC officials, expenditures on PRC operations, and any lobbying activities in the United States.

Compels ETFs to reveal ownership, CPC branches, contractual agreements, and involvement in PRC industrial policies or military contracts of invested PRC companies.

Requires disclosure of PRC companies’ products and services, U.S. defense contracts, and involvement in sectors like AI, telecommunications, and semiconductors.
Tags: Strategies: Disclosure

Official name: To require a briefing on the national security threats associated with Chinese autonomous ground vehicles operating in the United States.
Casual name: HR6896, 2023, Chinese autonomous ground vehicles briefing requirement.
Short summary: Requires the Secretary of Defense to brief Congress by March 30, 2024, on national security threats from Chinese autonomous ground vehicles, including data types collected, dual-use technology implications, and potential CCP or PLA use for military planning.
Long summary:
Requires the Secretary of Defense to provide a briefing by March 30, 2024, on national security threats from Chinese autonomous ground vehicles in the U.S., especially vehicles near Department of Defense bases and sensitive U.S. Government facilities.

Requires the briefing to include information on data types collected, dual-use implications of their technologies, and how the CCP or People’s Liberation Army could potentially use collected data for military planning.

Official name: New Jersey S 2723
Casual name: New Jersey S 2723 ("Integrated Digital Experience Act")
Short summary: Authorizes the Chief Technology Officer to evaluate AI, machine learning, and cloud services use by state agencies for public services. Requires agencies to submit IT modernization plans leveraging data analytics and enhancing digital services, ensuring mobile-friendly and accessible websites.
Long summary:
Requires each state agency to develop and submit an information technology modernization and improvement plan to the Chief Technology Officer within one year of request.

Mandates the plan to include strategies for improving digital services using data analytics, cloud computing, electronic forms, and digitized workflows, ensuring mobile-friendly and accessible websites.

Authorizes the Chief Technology Officer to review these plans in collaboration with the Chief Innovation Officer.

Mandates the Chief Technology Officer to annually evaluate the feasibility of using AI, machine learning, and cloud services for public services, and to develop data analytics capabilities for policy development.

Requires the development of metrics to assess each agency's implementation of a modern digital experience, updated at least every two years and reevaluated every three years.
Tags: Strategies: Evaluation

Official name: An Act Establishing the Alabama Commission on Artificial Intelligence and Associated Technologies.
Casual name: Alabama AI Commission Act
Short summary: Establishes the Alabama Commission on AI to review and advise on AI in various sectors. Requires qualified membership including expertise in Artificial Intelligence and mandates the disclosure of a report of the findings available to the public.
Long summary:
Establishes the Alabama Commission on Artificial Intelligence to review and advise on AI growth and use in governance, healthcare, education, transportation, and future industries.

Requires the commission to consider the establishment of a permanent AI commission.

Comprises members appointed by the Governor, Lieutenant Governor, House Speaker, and Senate President Pro Tempore, including experts in AI, workforce development, technology, ethics, privacy, or computer science.

Requires the commission to submit a report by May 1, 2020, with findings and recommendations for administrative or policy actions related to AI, available on the website of the Office of the Governor.

Official name: Countering Adversarial and Malicious Partnerships at Universities and Schools Act of 2023
Casual name: CAMPUS Act
Short summary: Prohibits Department of Defense funding to entities contracting with Chinese institutions supporting the People's Liberation Army. Denies visas to individuals from these institutions. Limits educational funding and strengthens U.S.-Taiwan partnerships for Mandarin instruction. Lowers foreign gift disclosure threshold.
Long summary:
Requires the Director of National Intelligence to create and annually update a list of China-based universities that support the People’s Liberation Army, with the first submission to Congress due within 180 days of enactment.

Requires the Department of Defense to restrict research, development, testing, and evaluation funding from being provided to U.S. entities that maintain contracts with institutions on that list or on the Commerce Department’s Entity List.

Rars entities from being cleared to host or store classified information unless they certify they have no active research partnerships with listed institutions.

Prohibits federal K-12 education funding from going to schools that maintain contracts with China-domiciled entities.

Authorizes grants to U.S. schools to support Mandarin language instruction and Chinese cultural programming in partnership with Taiwan.

Prohibits federal research funds for entities contracting with listed Chinese entities.

Lowers the reporting threshold for foreign gifts to institutions of higher education from $250,000 to $50,000.

Official name: PAID Act, Sec. 3 (Requirements for Private Passenger Automobile Insurers)
Casual name: PAID Act, Sec. 3 (Requirements for Private Passenger Automobile Insurers)
Short summary: Prohibits insurers from using specific personal factors, including gender and credit score, to determine car insurance rates. Requires submission to FTC to ensure no disparate impacts from algorithms. Mandates public access to underwriting rules.
Long summary:
Recognize that auto insurance is required in all states except New Hampshire, and that driving uninsured can lead to serious penalties.

Recognize that many insurers use variables to determine what consumers pay based on individual details (income proxies), not actual crash risk, which can block customers from preferred rates.

Prohibit auto insurers from using certain personal factors to decide whether a consumer can get coverage or what a consumer pays.

Prohibit insurers from considering gender, education level, job or occupation, employment status, homeownership status, ZIP code, census tract, marital status, credit score, consumer reports, previous insurer, or prior purchase of insurance from that insurer when determining eligibility or price.

Require insurers to submit information to the FTC within one year of enactment and every two years thereafter.

Require insurers to provide the FTC information showing that their marketing, underwriting, pricing, claims handling, fraud investigations, and any algorithms or models used in these activities do not create a disparate impact.

Require insurers to make underwriting rules and rate filings available for public inspection.

Clarify what counts as a violation and prohibit any use of banned factors that raises costs, restricts coverage, or blocks discounts.

Preserve the authority of federal and state agencies to enforce prohibitions against unfair or deceptive practices.

Recognize that state regulations still apply and that federal law overrides state law only to the extent a state rule conflicts with this Act.
Tags: Risk factors: Bias;Risk factors: Bias;Risk factors: Bias;Harms: Discrimination

Official name: Alabama SB 78
Casual name: Alabama SB 78 ("Council on Advanced Technology")
Short summary: Establishes the Alabama Council on Advanced Technology and Artificial Intelligence to review and advise on AI use and development. Requires annual reports with recommendations. Comprises appointed experts and legislative members. Emphasizes diversity in appointments. Effective one year after approval.
Long summary:
Establishes the Alabama Council on Advanced Technology and Artificial Intelligence.

Requires the council to review and advise the Governor and Legislature on the use and development of advanced technology and artificial intelligence.

Composes the council of 22 members, including appointees from the Governor, Lieutenant Governor, Speaker of the House, and Senate President Pro Tempore, and the Secretaries of Commerce and Information Technology, or their designees.

Mandates that non-legislative appointees have expertise in AI, technology, ethics, privacy, workforce development, or computer science.

Stipulates that the council meets at least twice a year and allows virtual participation.

Obliges the council to submit an annual report with policy recommendations on AI and advanced technology to the Governor and Legislature.

Enforces diversity in appointments regarding gender, race, and geography.

Prohibits salary for non-legislative members but allows expense reimbursement; provides legislative members with regular compensation for meeting attendance.

Sets the act’s effective date to one year post-approval.
Tags: Strategies: New institution;Strategies: Government study or report;Strategies: Convening

Official name: New Jersey SB 3876
Casual name: New Jersey SB 3876 ("Artificial Intelligence Officer")
Short summary: Establishes an Artificial Intelligence Officer and Implementation Officer to oversee AI systems in New Jersey's state agencies. Mandates development of procedures for using AI in critical decisions, ensuring compliance with national standards, and mitigating risks. Creates an AI Advisory Board for oversight and recommendations.
Long summary:
Establishes the position of Artificial Intelligence Officer to develop and update procedures regulating automated systems used by state agencies for critical decisions.

Mandates state agencies to comply with these procedures and submit inventories of their automated systems, which will be published online.

Creates the New Jersey Artificial Intelligence Advisory Board to advise state agencies, hold public hearings on preliminary procedures, and issue reports and recommendations.

Establishes the position of Artificial Intelligence Implementation Officer to review and approve or deny new automated systems based on compliance with procedures, and conduct periodic reevaluations.

Forms a task force to study AI and create an AI Bill of Rights, with a report due within one year.

Subjects officers and the board to open public records laws while protecting trade secrets and work product protections.

Official name: REGULATION (EU) 2024/1689 OF THE EUROPEAN PARLIAMENT AND OF THE COUNCIL of 13 June 2024 laying down harmonised rules on artificial intelligence and amending Regulations (EC) No 300/2008, (EU) No 167/2013, (EU) No 168/2013, (EU) 2018/858, (EU) 2018/1139 and (EU) 2019/2144 and Directives 2014/90/EU, (EU) 2016/797 and (EU) 2020/1828 (Artificial Intelligence Act)
Casual name: EU AI Act
Short summary: Implements a risk-based regulatory framework for AI, with specific attention to single-purpose AI systems used in risky contexts and general-purpose AI systems with significant capability. Imposes risk-dependent requirements related to accuracy, cybersecurity, evaluation, monitoring, transparency, and registration. Defines exceptions, penalties, and governance structures.
Long summary:
Classifies AI systems into unacceptable, high, limited, and minimal risk categories, defining specific regulatory requirements for each category. Requires strict monitoring and quality management for high-risk systems. 

Establishes transparency and cooperation requirements for general-purpose AI models. Imposes additional risk management and evaluation obligations for general-purpose models with systemic risks.

Applies to AI developers and deployers whose systems are used in the EU market, regardless of their physical location, with certain exceptions.

Defines regulatory exemptions for free and open-source AI systems, subject to transparency and licensing requirements.

Establishes the European Commission’s AI Office to lead implementation efforts and enforce compliance. Allows significant penalties for violations. Defines additional AI regulatory roles for advisory boards and national-level authorities.

Official name: Legal Advisory on the Application of Existing California Law to Artificial Intelligence in Healthcare (California Attorney General)
Casual name: Legal Advisory on the Application of Existing Law to AI in Healthcare (California Attorney General)
Short summary: Provides guidance on how California’s consumer protection, civil rights, competition, and data privacy laws apply to AI use in healthcare; explains California law regarding the use of AI to override doctors’ decisions, discriminate, or infringe on patient privacy.
Long summary:
Encourages healthcare entities in California using AI to comply with existing state laws related to consumer protection, civil rights, competition, and data privacy.

Provides guidance on non-discrimination laws and explains that AI developers, vendors, and users must ensure their systems do not have a discriminatory impact.

Explains the application of California patient privacy and autonomy laws to AI, including the Confidentiality of Medical Information Act (CMIA).

Emphasizes the need for compliance with both state and federal regulations with no exceptions for AI-related practices.

Official name: AI in America: OpenAI's Economic Blueprint
Casual name: AI in America: OpenAI's Economic Blueprint
Short summary: Calls on the federal government to promote US leadership in AI development with nationwide rules to boost innovation, secure AI frontier models, and align AI with democratic values. Urges common export policies, national security prioritization, infrastructure investment, and AI education enhancement.
Long summary:
Defines a US strategy emphasizing national competitiveness, model safeguards, and national security for AI, with the federal government leading and consulting with industry.

Promotes export policies to securely share frontier AI models with allies while limiting exports to adversary nations.

Encourages federal support for AI companies, including sharing national security insights and reducing bureaucratic obstacles for collaboration.

Advocates infrastructure development to support AI, emphasizing chips, data, energy, and talent to maintain US leadership.

Proposes common-sense rules for AI safeguarding public safety while fostering innovation, emphasizing provenance data in AI-generated content, and AI user personalization.

Suggests creating AI Economic Zones and a nationwide AI education strategy to enhance AI ecosystem support and competitiveness.

Envisions a Compact for AI among allies for capital and supply chain access, contrasting Chinese-backed infrastructure projects.

Engages in developing training programs for future AI talent, promoting collaboration between government and industry.

Official name: Promoting Digital Privacy Technologies Act
Casual name: Promoting Digital Privacy Technologies Act
Short summary: Defines privacy-enhancing technologies (PETs) and instructs the National Science Foundation to support research on them. Directs the Office of Science and Technology Policy to coordinate with NSF, NIST, and the FTC to accelerate PET development and use, and to report to Congress on implementation.
Long summary:
Defines “privacy-enhancing technologies” (PETs) to include methods such as differential privacy, synthetic data, and federated learning.

Instructs the National Science Foundation (NSF) to support PET research by awarding research projects related to privacy preserving technologies including algorithms, machine learning, and other computational processes used to protect privacy when handling data.

Amends (D) of 4(a)(1) of the Cybersecurity Research and Development Act.

Directs the Office of Science and Technology Policy (OSTP) to coordinate with NSF, the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), and the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) to accelerate the development, deployment, and use of PETs.

Requires OSTP to report to Congress on progress made in advancing PET research and policy recommendations to improve interagency coordination using PETs.

Official name: William M. (Mac) Thornberry National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2021, Division A, Title VII, Section 808 ("Acquisition authority of the Director of the Joint Artificial Intelligence Center")
Casual name: FY2021 NDAA, Division A, Title VII, Section 808 ("Acquisition authority of the Director of the Joint Artificial Intelligence Center")
Short summary: Instructs the Secretary of Defense to delegate acquisition authority to the Director of the Joint Artificial Intelligence Center; allocates personnel and funding to support outlined responsibilities of the Center, including overseeing the transition of artificial intelligence capabilities into appropriate acquisition programs.
Long summary:
Instructs the Secretary of Defense to delegate acquisition authority to the Director of the Joint Artificial Intelligence Center, and outlines responsibilities of an acquisition executive in the execution, supervision, discussion, and coordination of relevant acquisition activities.

Allocates personnel and funding to support Center missions and the transition of artificial intelligence capabilities into appropriate acquisition programs or into operational use. 

Requires the Secretary of Defense to provide to congressional defense committees a demonstration of operational capability and an implementation plan that describes relevant acquisition activities, plans the oversight of an acquisition executive, and assesses the acquisition workforce, tools, and infrastructure needs of the Center.

Official name: William M. (Mac) Thornberry National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2021, Division A, Title XVII, Section 1751 ("Guidance and direction on use of direct hiring processes for artificial intelligence professionals and other data science and software development personnel")
Casual name: FY2021 NDAA, Division A, Title XVII, Section 1751 ("Guidance and direction on use of direct hiring processes for artificial intelligence professionals and other data science and software development personnel")
Short summary: Instructs the Secretary of Defense to review existing guidance and create new guidance on the direct hiring processes for AI professionals and to submit a report on the guidance issued; lists the minimum requirements for the guidance.
Long summary:
Instructs the Secretary of Defense to review existing guidance and create new guidance for secretaries of military departments and heads of defense on the direct hiring processes for AI professionals and other software and data science personnel.

Requires that the guidance instructs HR to utilize direct hiring authorities.

Instructs hiring authorities to use a panel of subject matter experts to review qualifications, authorizes the use of ePortfolio reviews, and encourages the use of referral bonuses for recruitment.

Instructs the Secretary of Defense to submit a report of the guidance issues to Congress within one year of the issuance of the guidance and highlights the requirements of the report.
Tags: Strategies: Government study or report;Strategies: Government support: AI workforce-related;Applications: Government: military and public safety

Official name: William M. (Mac) Thornberry National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2021, Division A, Title II, Subtitle C ("Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technology")
Casual name: FY2021 NDAA, Division A, Title II, Subtitle C ("Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technology")
Short summary: Addresses the research and development of artificial intelligence technologies in the Department of Defense. Establishes a board of advisors for the Joint Artificial Intelligence Center to provide strategic advice and technical expertise on matters relating to the development and use of artificial intelligence. Supports the implementation of ethical and responsible artificial intelligence systems and standards for artificial intelligence.
Long summary:
Instructs the Secretary of Defense to assign roles and responsibilities relating to the research, development, testing, procurement of, requirements for, and operational use of artificial intelligence technologies to appropriate officials within the Department of Defense.

Supports the incorporation of standards for artificial intelligence into the strategies and doctrine of the Department.

Stipulates the roles of the Director of the joint Artificial Intelligence center.

Encourages the systematic evaluation of AI systems.

Instructs the Secretary of Defense to establish a board of advisors for the Joint Artificial Intelligence Center with responsibilities like providing strategic advice and expertise to the Secretary and the Director on matters relating to the development and use of artificial intelligence.

Instructs the Secretary of Defense to identify a set of no fewer than five use cases of the application of existing artificial intelligence enabled systems to support improved management of enterprise acquisition, personnel, audit, or financial management functions.

Instructs the Secretary of Defense to conduct an assessment to determine how the Department can most effectively implement ethically and responsibly developed artificial intelligence.

Requires the secretary to pilot technology development and prototyping activities that leverage commercially available technologies and systems to demonstrate new artificial intelligence enabled capabilities to support the use cases.

Permits the secretary of Defense to establish a steering committee on emerging technology and national security threats, which committee shall develop a strategy for the organizational change, capability development, and technology investments in emerging technologies needed to maintain the technological superiority of the United States military.

Official name: William M. (Mac) Thornberry National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2021, Division A, Title I, Section 152 ("Transfer of responsibilities and functions relating to electromagnetic spectrum operations")
Casual name: FY2021 NDAA, Division A, Title I, Section 152 ("Transfer of responsibilities and functions relating to electromagnetic spectrum operations")
Short summary: Instructs the Secretary of Defense to transfer all the responsibilities of the Commander of the US Strategic Command that are germane to electromagnetic spectrum operations (EMSO) including advocacy for joint electronic warfare capabilities; providing contingency electronic warfare support to other combatant commands; and supporting combatant command joint training and planning related to electromagnetic spectrum operations; requires an evaluation of the need for automated and machine learning- or artificial intelligence-assisted electronic warfare capabilities.
Long summary:
Instructs the Secretary of Defense to transfer all the responsibilities of the Commander of the US Strategic Command that are germane to electromagnetic spectrum operations (EMSO).

Instructs the Secretary to develop a transfer plan with consideration of all appropriate entities, while considering whether the receiving electromagnetic spectrum operations organization should have a unitary structure or hybrid structure.

Requires the Secretary to develop the transfer plan while considering the resources required by the receiving electromagnetic spectrum operations organization to fulfill the responsibilities and functions as well as the results of the evaluations carried out.

Requires an evaluation and assessment of the need for automated and machine learning- or artificial intelligence-assisted electronic warfare capabilities.
Tags: Strategies: Evaluation;Strategies: Government study or report;Applications: Government: military and public safety

Official name: William M. (Mac) Thornberry National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2021, Division A, Title II, Section 247 ("Pilot program on the use of electronic portfolios to evaluate certain applicants for technical positions")
Casual name: FY2021 NDAA, Division A, Title II, Section 247 ("Pilot program on the use of electronic portfolios to evaluate certain applicants for technical positions")
Short summary: Instructs the Secretary of Defense to carry out a pilot program where certain applicants for technical positions within the Department of Defense will be evaluated based partly on each applicant’s electronic portfolio.
Long summary:
Defines the term "technical position" as a Department of Defense position that (1) requires expertise in artificial intelligence, data science, or software development and (2) is eligible for direct hire authority under section 9905 of title 5, United States Code, or section 2358a of title 10, United States Code. 

Instructs the human resources manager of each Department of Defense participating organization to identify a subset of technical positions for which electronic portfolios should be evaluated during the hiring process.

Requires the Human Resources manager to evaluate applicants for such positions by reviewing their best electronic portfolios, as chosen by the applicant.

Directs the Secretary of Defense to submit a report to the congressional defense committee on the results of the program that describes how electronic portfolios affected the timeliness of technical position hiring, the level of satisfaction with new hires based on electronic portfolio evaluations, and recommends expanding the use of electronic portfolios in the hiring process or making it permanent.
Tags: Strategies: Evaluation;Strategies: Government study or report;Strategies: Pilots and testbeds;Applications: Government: military and public safety

Official name: William M. (Mac) Thornberry National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2021, Division A, Title II, Section 241 ("Measuring and incentivizing programming proficiency")
Casual name: FY2021 NDAA, Division A, Title II, Section 241 ("Measuring and incentivizing programming proficiency")
Short summary: Instructs the Secretary of Defense to measure, track and reward programming, coding and artificial intelligence proficiency amongst armed forces and civilian employees of the Department of Defense.
Long summary:
Authorizes resources for managing government support for AI-related education and proficiency

Instructs the Secretary of Defense to leverage existing civilian software development and architecture certification programmes to implement DOD coding language proficiency and AI competency tests.

Requires the Secretary of Defense to update record keeping systems to track artificial intelligence and programming certification testing results just like the foreign language competency system.

Instructs the secretary of Defense to share information with the heads of other intelligence agencies about the coding language proficiency and artificial intelligence competency tests and encourages them to implement tracking and reward systems. 

Requires the Secretary of Defense to implement a system of rewards for Department of Defense employees who perform successfully on specific language coding proficiency and artificial intelligence competency tests.

Advocates for special pay for a Department of Defense employee who is certified and proficient in a computer or digital programming language but may not exceed 20% of basic pay.
Tags: Strategies: Government support;Strategies: Government support: For R&D;Strategies: Government support: AI workforce-related;Applications: Education;Applications: Government: military and public safety

Official name: William M. (Mac) Thornberry National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2021, Division A, Title II, Section 249 ("Part-time and term employment of university faculty and students in the Defense science and technology enterprise")
Casual name: FY2021 NDAA, Division A, Title II, Section 249 ("Part-time and term employment of university faculty and students in the Defense science and technology enterprise")
Short summary: Instructs the Secretary of Defense to establish a program to offer Defense science and technology research employment opportunities for faculty and students of higher education institutions.
Long summary:
Instructs the Secretary of Defense to establish a program to offer Defense science and technology employment opportunities to selected faculty and students of higher education institutions to carry out research projects. 

Specifies that no fewer than five positions must be given to faculty who conduct research in artificial intelligence and machine learning.

Requires the Secretary to carry out the program within 180 days of enactment and submit three annual reports of program results to Congress.
Tags: Strategies: Government study or report;Strategies: Government support;Strategies: Government support: For R&D;Strategies: Government support: AI workforce-related;Applications: Government: military and public safety

Official name: William M. (Mac) Thornberry National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2021, Division A, Title II, Section 248 ("Pilot program on self-directed training in advanced technologies")
Casual name: FY2021 NDAA, Division A, Title II, Section 248 ("Pilot program on self-directed training in advanced technologies")
Short summary: Instructs the Secretary of Defense to offer online courses related to advanced technologies for civilian employees of the Department of Defense and members of the Armed Forces.
Long summary:
Instructs the Secretary of Defense to execute a pilot program that offers online courses related to advanced technologies for civilian employees of the Department of Defense and members of the Armed Forces.

Requires the Secretary to develop procedures to document course completion, incentivize course completion, and evaluate course quality and effectiveness.

Requires the Secretary to carry out the program within a year after enactment, and decrees that the program terminate on October 1, 2024.

Official name: William M. (Mac) Thornberry National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2021, Division A, Title II, Section 513 ("Grants to support STEM education in the Junior Reserve Officers’ Training Corps")
Casual name: FY2021 NDAA, Division A, Title II, Section 513 ("Grants to support STEM education in the Junior Reserve Officers’ Training Corps")
Short summary:
Instructs the Secretary along with the Secretary of Education to create and support grants to fund STEM education programs to students in the Junior Reserve Corps.
Outlines specific disciplines and STEM subjects which the grants will apply to.
Long summary:
Instructs the Secretary and Secretary of Education to create and support grants to fund STEM education programs for students specifically in the Junior Reserve Corps. 
Instructs different departments of the government, such as the National Science foundation, as well as local governments to carry out programs that are supported by said grants.
Outlines what activities are allowed under the grants, and what specific area of studies and subjects that they would work under. 
Details subjects that are eligible for said grants, which include science, technology, engineering, mathematics, computer science, and more.
Allows for the Secretary of Defense to determine which additional subjects and areas of studies could be applicable to the grant.
Tags: Strategies: Government support;Strategies: Government support: AI workforce-related;Applications: Education

Official name: William M. (Mac) Thornberry National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2021, Division A, Title XV, Section 6505 ("GAO studies on trafficking")
Casual name: FY2021 NDAA, Division A, Title XV, Section 6505 ("GAO studies on trafficking")
Short summary: Instructs the Comptroller General of the United States to conduct a study on trafficking routes employed by transnational criminals. This emcompasses examining methods for laundering proceeds from trafficking, with a focus on the role of emerging technologies, including Artificial Intelligence, in facilitating the laundering of proceedings from trafficking.
Long summary:
Instructs the Comptroller General of the United States to carry out a study in consultation with law enforcement, relevant federal agencies, private sector stakeholders (such as financial institutions, data and technology companies), to investigate major trafficking routes used by transnational criminal organizations and to what extent the trafficking routes are related.

Directs the study to include commonly used methods to launder and move the proceeds of trafficking; the types of suspicious financial activity that are associated with illicit trafficking networks, and how financial institutions identify and report such activity.

Directs the study to include steps financial institutions are taking to detect and prevent bad actors who are laundering the proceeds of illicit trafficking, including data analysis, policies, training procedures, rules, and guidance,

Necessitates the study to include the role those emerging technologies, including artificial intelligence, digital identity technologies, distributed ledger technologies, virtual assets and online marketplaces can play in enabling the laundering of proceeds from trafficking. 

Requires the Comptroller General of the United States to submit a report to the congres, summarizing the results of the study and any recommendations for regulatory action.

Official name: William M. (Mac) Thornberry National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2021, Division A, Title XI, Section 1102 ("Enhancement of public-private talent exchange programs in the Department of Defense")
Casual name: FY2021 NDAA, Division A, Title XI, Section 1102 ("Enhancement of public-private talent exchange programs in the Department of Defense")

Long summary:

Including exchanges in Research and Engineering, the Chief Information Officer, and each Armed Force under the jurisdiction of a military department.
