Official name: “Managing Active and Reserve Tech Talent Effectively Act of 2023”
Casual name: MARTTE Act of 2023
Short summary: Establishes a policy to build human capital infrastructure for AI, coding, and computer programming skills across the Armed Forces. Requires alignment of career fields with AI-related work roles, assessment and tracking of skilled personnel, and use of reserve component members’ civilian AI expertise. Mandates implementation plans and ongoing reporting to Congress.
Long summary:
Establishes a comprehensive human capital infrastructure within the U.S. Armed Forces to develop, track, and manage computer programming, coding, and artificial intelligence skills.

Requires the military departments to create career field occupational codes and special skill designators aligned with AI, machine learning, software engineering, and related work roles under the Department of Defense Cyber Workforce Framework.

Mandates assessment and ongoing tracking of personnel who possess AI and coding skills or who have completed relevant qualification processes.

Directs the services to implement a unified strategy and digital platforms to support the identification, assignment, and presentation of AI and coding capabilities to combatant commands.

Requires each military department, under the Secretary’s direction and in consultation with senior digital, information, and manpower officials, to implement the policy and submit implementation plans within 180 days.

Extends similar requirements to reserve components, enabling members to earn occupational designations based on civilian AI and programming experience and requiring processes to track and employ these skills.

Requires annual updates in budget justification materials until all implementation milestones are met.

Official name: Wildfire Technology Demonstration, Evaluation, Modernization, and Optimization Act
Casual name: Wildfire Technology DEMO Act
Short summary: Establishes a wildfire technology testbed pilot program prioritizing emerging technologies including AI. Requires collaboration with federal agencies and private entities to test and report on new technologies for wildfire prevention, detection, and mitigation over four years.
Long summary:
Establishes a wildfire technology testbed pilot program within 60 days of the Act's enactment, coordinated by the Secretaries of Agriculture and the Interior.

Incorporates the program into an existing interagency coordinating group on wildfires.

Identifies key technology priority areas, including hazardous fuels reduction, dispatch communications, remote sensing, safety equipment, and operational dashboards.

Partners selected private entities, nonprofits, and higher education institutions with federal agencies for real-time testing during wildfire mitigation.

Prioritizes emerging technologies such as artificial intelligence, quantum sensing, augmented reality, and advanced communications for wildfire mitigation.

Requires covered entities to submit applications, including technology testing proposals.

Mandates annual reports to Congress on participating entities, tested technologies, costs, outreach efforts, and technology assessments.

Terminates the program four years after establishment.

Official name: DOE and NASA Interagency Research Coordination Act
Casual name: DOE and NASA Research Coordination Act
Short summary: Authorize collaborative research between the Department of Energy and the National Aeronautics and Space Administration, including on machine learning, and collaborative data and infrastructure usage. Requires the DoE and NASA to report to Congress, within two years, on interagency research and development conducted.
Long summary:
Authorizes the Secretary of Energy and the National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) to coordinate research and development activities through a Memorandum of Understanding, or other appropriate agreements, and authorizes competitive awards to carry out activities.

Proposes collaborative activities to include research of focus areas, including machine learning; the development of methods for accommodating large data sets; data sharing between the DoE, NASA, the National Laboratories, and other appropriate entities; and the enabling of NASA to access DoE infrastructure and capabilities. 

Requires the DoE and NASA to report to Congress, within two years, on interagency research and development conducted, results, and on potential for future areas of collaboration.

Official name: HOME Act of 2024, Sec. 7 (Identification of Unfair Screening Practices)
Casual name: HOME Act of 2024, Sec. 7 (Identification of Unfair Screening Practices)
Short summary: Establishes a program to identify unfair rental housing screening practices using algorithms. Requires annual reporting to Congress on collected information regarding these practices, including background checks and tenant income evaluations.
Long summary:
Mandates the Secretary, the Federal Trade Commission, and the Bureau of Consumer Financial Protection to jointly collect information on unfair practices in tenant and applicant screenings, including the use of algorithms.

Requires the identification of practices that prevent access to or retention of rental housing, focusing on tenant background checks, adverse action notices, and tenant income sources.

Obliges the submission of an annual report to Congress detailing the information collected under the program.
Tags: Strategies: Convening;Strategies: Government study or report

Official name: FAA Research and Development Act of 2023, Sec. 215 ("Air Traffic Control Training")
Casual name: FAA R&D Act, Sec. 215 ("Air Traffic Control Training")
Short summary: Requires the assessment of AI, machine learning, and other technologies to improve air traffic control training. Develops a deployment timeline for these technologies. Involves collaboration with stakeholders. Obligates a report to Congress within one year.
Long summary:
Requires the FAA to carry out a research program to modernize and streamline air traffic control training, subject to available appropriations.

Mandates the assessment of advanced technologies, including AI, machine learning, and virtual/augmented reality, to enhance knowledge retention and training effectiveness.

Develops a timeline for deploying proven advanced technologies and processes for accreditation in training programs and facilities.

Includes collaboration with labor organizations and other stakeholders in the research process.

Obligates the Administrator to submit a report to Congress on the research findings within one year of the Act's enactment.
Tags: Applications: Transportation;Strategies: Evaluation;Strategies: Government study or report

Official name: FAA Research and Development Act of 2023, Sec. 210 ("Technology review of artificial intelligence and machine learning technologies")
Casual name: FAA R&D Act, Sec. 210 ("Review of AI and ML")
Short summary: Requires the Administrator to review AI and machine learning technologies for airport efficiency and safety. Includes examination of jet bridges, airport service vehicles, aircraft taxi, and other areas. Mandates a report to Congress within one year, including China's AI applications.
Long summary:
Requires the FAA to conduct a review of current and planned AI and machine learning technologies to enhance airport efficiency and safety.

Mandates the review to cover applications of AI and machine learning specifically in jet bridges, airport service vehicles, and aircraft taxiing.

Allows the Administrator to include other relevant areas that can improve airport efficiency and safety.

Obligates the Administrator to submit a report to Congress within one year, detailing the review's findings.

Includes a required examination of China's domestic application of AI and machine learning technologies in the report.
Tags: Strategies: Government study or report;Applications: Transportation;Strategies: Government study or report

Official name: Small Business Artificial Intelligence Advancement Act
Casual name: Small Business Artificial Intelligence Advancement Act
Short summary: Amends the National Institute of Standards and Technology Act to develop, update, and disseminate AI resources for small businesses, ensuring applicability, promoting understanding, including case studies, and aligning with international standards. Requires biennial reviews, voluntary usage, and reporting to Congress.
Long summary:
Amends the National Institute of Standards and Technology Act to mandate the development and dissemination of AI resources for small businesses.

Requires these resources to include technical standards, best practices, benchmarks, methodologies, and case studies, ensuring they are widely applicable and technology-neutral.

Mandates that resources promote understanding and adoption of AI, adhere to international standards, and incorporate federal educational resources.

Obligates the Director to review and update these resources every two years.

Coordinates with the Small Business Administration for the distribution and utilization of AI resources through its partners, emphasizing voluntary use.

Compels a report to Congress within four years, detailing resources, feedback, and recommendations for enhancing AI utilization by small businesses.

Official name: Bipartisan Framework for U.S. AI Act
Casual name: Bipartisan Framework for U.S. AI Act
Short summary: Establishes a licensing regime for AI developers, requiring risk management and compliance with oversight audits. Holds AI companies accountable for harms, including privacy violations and deepfake creation. Limits AI technology exports to adversaries. Demands transparency and consumer protection, especially for children.
Long summary:
Establishes a licensing regime requiring companies developing sophisticated A.I. models to register with an independent oversight body. 

Requires developers to maintain risk management, pre-deployment testing, and reporting programs as part of licensing conditions.

Grants the oversight body authority to audit companies and cooperate with state Attorneys General.

Mandates Congress to ensure A.I. companies are legally accountable for harms caused by their models, including privacy breaches and civil rights violations.

Urges Congress to use export controls and sanctions to limit A.I. technology transfers to adversary nations.

Promotes transparency by requiring companies to disclose A.I. model information and ensure users know they are interacting with A.I.

Requires A.I. system providers to label deepfakes and maintain a public database of A.I. information.

Obliges companies to implement consumer protections, including safety brakes and data usage control, especially concerning children.

Official name: Mississippi Executive Order No. 1584
Casual name: Mississippi EO 1584 (Foster Stakeholder Collaboration and Harness AI)
Short summary: Directs Mississippi's Department of Information Technology Services to inventory AI use in state agencies, evaluate existing AI processes, and develop AI policy recommendations. Aims to safeguard principles of fairness, privacy, reliability, and accountability.
Long summary:
Directs the Mississippi Department of Information Technology Services (ITS) to inventory all AI technologies across state agencies by providing a standardized form.

Mandates ITS to evaluate current AI processes and guidelines at state agencies.

Instructs ITS to develop policy recommendations for the responsible use of AI, emphasizing fairness, privacy, security, transparency, and accountability.

Encourages statewide coordination on AI use, enabling ITS to engage with government stakeholders and industry experts for best practices and strategies.

Requires state agencies to cooperate with ITS by completing AI inventories and designating a staff member for AI coordination.

Implements the Executive Order immediately, with provisions to remain effective until amended, superseded, or rescinded.

Official name: Preventing PLA Acquisition of United States Technology Act of 2023
Casual name: Preventing PLA Tech Acquisition
Short summary: Prohibits federal entities from partnering or collaborating with specific Chinese institutions (that engage in on technologies, including AI. Requires creating a website listing these technologies and entities. Demands annual reports and audits on research relationships with said Chinese entities.
Long summary:
Prohibits covered U.S. entities from engaging in scientific research or technical exchanges with Chinese entities of concern, particularly in technologies like AI, identified as priorities in China's military-civil fusion strategy.

Requires the Secretary of Defense, with other officials, to maintain a website listing prohibited scientific research areas and Chinese entities of concern.

Mandates covered entities to report annually on any research relationships with Chinese entities of concern and allows audits to ensure compliance.

Precludes entities violating prohibitions from receiving federal financial assistance.

Permits exceptions for fields not substantially contributing to military-civil fusion.

Directs the Secretary of Defense to issue regulations to enforce these provisions and coordinate enforcement across federal agencies.
Tags: Applications: Government: military and public safety;Strategies: Governance development;Strategies: Input controls;Strategies: Disclosure;Incentives: Subsidies

Official name: FAIRR Act
Casual name: FAIRR Act
Short summary: Amends the Financial Stability Act to require the Financial Stability Oversight Council to assess AI risks in financial systems, identify regulatory gaps, and recommend improvements. Enhances oversight of AI service providers, imposes treble penalties for machine-manipulated media, and establishes liability for AI deployment.
Long summary:
Amends the Financial Stability Act of 2010 to assign the Financial Stability Oversight Council duties regarding AI in the financial sector.

Requires the Council to coordinate with member agencies on AI-related financial system risks and direct the Office of Financial Research to study AI use by financial institutions.

Obligates the Council to identify AI-related threats to financial stability, including market manipulation and disruption by malign actors.

Mandates submission of a report within 180 days identifying regulatory gaps and recommending solutions, including referencing the NSA's September 2023 Cybersecurity Information Sheet.

Calls for scenario-based exercises to test AI-related financial disruption defenses.

Expands authority to oversee third-party AI service providers to financial institutions.

Increases penalties for securities violations involving machine-manipulated media to three times the standard amount.

Imposes liability on developers for AI model outcomes under Federal securities laws, with limited potential for waivers.

Official name: Pandemic and All-Hazards Preparedness and Response Act, Sec. 407 ("Assessment of Artificial Intelligence Threats to Health Security")
Casual name: Pandemic Preparedness and Response Act, Sec. 407 ("AI Threats to Health Security")
Short summary: Requires the Secretary of Health and Human Services to contract the National Academies to assess AI vulnerabilities, including open-source and large language models, to health security. Includes roles, responsibilities, and risk mitigation recommendations. Mandates initial and follow-up implementation reports from the National Academies to Congress on findings and subsequent health security actions.
Long summary:
Requires the Secretary of Health and Human Services to seek a contract with the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine to conduct a study on AI-related health security vulnerabilities within 45 days of enactment.

Mandates the study to assess risks posed by AI advancements, including those related to chemical, biological, radiological, and nuclear (CBRN) threats, and the ways AI may enable, enhance, or protect against such threats.

Describes roles and capabilities of HHS agencies and other Federal entities in identifying, preparing for, and mitigating these vulnerabilities.

Summarizes ongoing Federal activities related to the identification, understanding, and mitigation of these risks.

Identifies current or anticipated gaps in roles, responsibilities, and capabilities regarding AI-related health security.

Provides recommendations for improving Federal efforts to identify, prepare for, and mitigate these vulnerabilities.

Requires the National Academies to submit a report on the study to the Senate HELP Committee and the House Energy and Commerce Committee within 2 years.

Mandates the Secretary of HHS to submit a follow-up report on actions taken to mitigate AI-related health security risks within 1 year after the National Academies’ report.

Official name: Novel, Advanced Spectrum and Communications Technology Networks Promotion Act
Casual name: Advanced Communications Technology Networks Act
Short summary: Requires the Assistant Secretary of Commerce for Communications and Information to support the establishment and implementation of technologies for electromagnetic spectrum management, including artificial intelligence systems.
Long summary: Requires the Assistant Secretary of Commerce for Communications and Information to support the establishment, prototyping, and implementation of technologies for electromagnetic spectrum management, such as artificial intelligence systems for operating advanced antenna arrays and artificial intelligence systems to enable dynamic spectrum access.
Tags: Strategies: Government support;Applications: Networking and telecommunications;Strategies: Government support: For R&D

Official name: Advanced Aviation Integration Act
Casual name: Advanced Aviation Integration Act
Short summary: Designates the FAA Deputy Administrator to lead the integration of advanced aviation systems, establishes the Office of Advanced Integration, and creates the Advanced Aviation Coordination Unit to strengthen oversight, certification, and collaboration on unmanned and emerging aviation technologies within the national airspace.
Long summary:
Designates the Deputy Administrator of the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) as the executive responsible for leading advanced aviation integration across the national airspace.

Requires coordination among FAA offices to manage policy, rulemaking, research, and workforce planning related to advanced aviation systems.

Mandates the Deputy Administrator to submit biannual reports on workforce requirements, staffing, and budget resources for advanced aviation oversight.

Establishes an Office of Advanced Integration, led by a Director, to manage coordination and leadership functions for advanced aviation system research, rulemaking, and certification.

Creates an Advanced Aviation Coordination Unit composed of representatives from FAA offices to promote safe integration, streamline certification, and develop consistent standards for advanced aviation systems.

Directs the formation of working groups on unmanned aircraft systems, advanced air mobility, and safety technology integration under the Coordination Unit.

Defines “advanced aviation system” to include unmanned, powered-lift, autonomous, electric, and hydrogen-powered aircraft, as well as digital and information-centric aviation systems.

Authorizes the Secretary of Transportation to facilitate interagency coordination to support the FAA’s integration and standardization efforts.

Official name: National Quantum Initiative Reauthorization Act, Sec. 16 ("Department of Energy Quantum Information Science Research Program")
Casual name: National Quantum Initiative Reauthorization Act, Sec. 16 ("DOE Quantum Information Science Research Program")
Short summary: Amends the National Quantum Initiative Act to require the Department of Energy to enhance quantum information science research, including AI integration, through new research centers, strategic planning, and industry outreach, with up to $130 million annually authorized through 2027.
Long summary:
Amends the National Quantum Initiative Act to mandate the Secretary of Energy to implement a quantum information science, engineering, and technology research program.

Establishes National Quantum Information Science Research Centers to advance quantum technology, networking, sensing, and communications infrastructure.

Requires cooperative research initiatives with industry, National Laboratories, and academic institutions, covering artificial intelligence, machine learning, quantum communications, sensing, networking, and other quantum technologies.

Mandates providing research experiences and training for students in quantum-related fields.

Directs the creation of a 10-year strategic plan for quantum high-performance computing that integrates artificial intelligence and machine learning, to be submitted to Congress.

Supports outreach to industry to promote commercialization and energy-sector applications of quantum technology.

Authorizes up to $130 million annually from fiscal years 2024 through 2027 to carry out these activities.

Official name: Advancing Nuclear Regulatory Oversight Act
Casual name: Advancing Nuclear Regulatory Oversight Act
Short summary: Directs the Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC) to evaluate and integrate advanced technologies, including AI and machine learning, into nuclear oversight and inspection processes. Requires reports to Congress and stakeholder consultation to enhance risk-informed, performance-based regulation and operational efficiency.
Long summary:
Requires the Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC) to report to Congress within 180 days on regulatory changes made during the COVID-19 emergency, examining their impact on licensing and safety.

Mandates the NRC to develop and submit a report within one year identifying specific improvements in oversight and inspection programs, emphasizing risk-informed, performance-based procedures and the use of advanced technologies, including AI and machine learning.

Directs the NRC to consult with the Department of Energy, National Laboratories, industry, and nongovernmental stakeholders in developing these reports.

Requires assessment of oversight processes, event reporting, staff training, and digital modernization to improve regulatory efficiency and safety without compromising public health or security.

Obligates the NRC to identify and implement actions based on assessments and recommendations to improve training, inspection, oversight, and licensing activities without compromising its mission. 

Instructs the Government Accountability Office to review the NRC’s facility and office space use to reduce costs for taxpayers and licensees.
Tags: Applications: Energy and utilities;Strategies: Disclosure;Risk factors: Reliability;Strategies: Government study or report;Strategies: Government study or report

Official name: Stop Spying Bosses Act
Casual name: Stop Spying Bosses Act
Short summary: Requires employers to disclose AI-driven workplace surveillance details and prohibits using AI to predict non-work behavior. Establishes a Privacy and Technology Division for oversight. Enables private rights of action and whistleblower protections. Mandates coordination with federal and state agencies.
Long summary:
Prohibits employers from using workplace surveillance to monitor labor activities, ascertain political or religious views, or determine health or immigration status unrelated to job duties.

Requires employers to disclose details about workplace surveillance practices, including data collection methods, frequency, storage locations, business purposes, and third-party transfers, to employees and applicants.

Establishes a Privacy and Technology Division within the Department of Labor to oversee compliance and issue regulations.

Allows employees to access, correct, and request data collected about them and prohibits data transfer to third parties without disclosure and cybersecurity measures.

Grants employees and labor organizations the right to bring civil actions against violations, with potential damages and injunctive relief.

Prohibits predispute arbitration agreements regarding surveillance-related disputes.

Mandates annual reports to Congress on workplace surveillance impacts and recommendations for mitigating harms.

Coordinates with federal and state agencies to ensure consistent regulatory treatment of workplace surveillance.

Official name: Next Generation Pipelines Research and Development Act, Section 4 ("Advanced pipeline materials and technologies demonstration initiative")
Casual name: H. R. 7073 Pipelines R&D Act, Section 4 ("Advanced pipeline materials and technologies demonstration initiative")
Short summary: Requires the Secretary of Energy to establish a demonstration initiative to advance pipeline and related infrastructure technologies, such as autonomous sensor systems; advanced computational, data analytics, and machine learning models; and autonomous robotic and patch technologies. Requires the Secretary of Energy to evaluate projects' technical performance and economic feasibility during the selection process.
Long summary:
Requires the Secretary of Energy to establish a demonstration initiative awarding financial assistance for demonstration projects, of low- to mid-technology readiness, for pipelines and associated infrastructure technologies.

Declares advanced sensor technologies such as autonomous sensor systems, including uncrewed aircraft, eligible for financial assistance through the initiative.

Declares advanced computational, data analytics, and machine learning models, as well as autonomous robotic and patch technologies, eligible for financial assistance through the initiative.

Requires the Secretary of Energy to evaluate projects' technical performance and economic feasibility, for return on investment impact, in the selection process for the initiative. 

Authorizes $45 million for fiscal year 2025 and $50 million annually for fiscal years 2026-2029 from designated offices to implement the initiative.

Terminates the initiative five years after enactment.

Official name: Diversify Tech Act
Casual name: Diversify Tech Act
Short summary: Establishes a task force within the Department of Commerce to promote diversity, equity, inclusion, and accessibility (DEIA) in the tech industry. Evaluates AI's role in DEIA, monitors minority treatment, and reports on layoffs and DEIA progress.
Long summary:
Establishes a Tech Industry Diversity, Equity, Inclusion, and Accessibility (DEIA) Task Force within the Department of Commerce.

Mandates the Task Force to conduct audits on minority representation, hiring policies, and layoff trends in the tech industry.

Requires submission of an initial audit report to Congress within 60 days of the Task Force’s establishment.

Obliges the Task Force to promote DEIA, offer guidance to tech entities, and consult with stakeholders on DEIA issues.

Tasks the Task Force with advising on outreach programs and encouraging venture capital investment in minority-owned startups.

Mandates annual reports evaluating tech industry progress on DEIA goals, the impact of AI on discrimination, and proposing policy solutions for accountability.

Requires a special report on how 2023 tech layoffs affected DEIA, including potential violations of federal laws.

Official name: Small Business Broadband and Emerging Information Technology Enhancement Act of 2023
Casual name: S. 2677 Small Business Technology Enhancement Act
Short summary: Designates a coordinator to assist small businesses in adopting AI and emerging technologies, requiring collaboration with federal agencies. Mandates biennial reports on broadband's impact on small businesses, and updates training for SBA employees on AI technologies.
Long summary:
Designates a senior employee of the Office of Investment and Innovation as the broadband and emerging information technology coordinator to assist small businesses in adopting broadband and emerging technologies.

Requires the coordinator to work with the SBA's chief information officer, chief technology officer, and other relevant federal agencies.

Mandates the coordinator to identify best practices and catalog tools and training for small businesses on broadband and emerging technologies.

Obligates the coordinator to provide training to SBA employees on these technologies and relevant federal programs.

Requires biennial reports on the SBA's activities related to broadband and emerging technologies.

Instructs the Chief Counsel for Advocacy to study and report on the impact of broadband availability, speed, and price, and the deployment of emerging technologies on small businesses.

Amends the Small Business Act to include provisions for increasing small business access to broadband and emerging technologies.
Tags: Strategies: Governance development;Strategies: Convening;Strategies: Governance development;Strategies: Government support;Strategies: Government study or report

Official name: Algorithmic Accountability Act of 2023
Casual name: Algorithmic Accountability Act of 2023
Short summary: Directs the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) to issue rules requiring “covered entities” to conduct, document, and submit impact assessments for automated decision systems used in critical decisions (e.g., jobs, credit, housing, health, education). Requires initial and annual summary reports, stakeholder consultation, testing for performance and fairness, and mitigation of likely material harms resulting from automated decision systems. Creates an FTC Bureau of Technology, builds a public repository of summaries, redefines violations under the FTC Act, and authorizes state attorney general enforcement.
Long summary:
Directs the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) to issue regulations requiring covered entities to conduct and document impact assessments for automated decision systems (ADS) and augmented critical decision processes that materially affect consumers in areas such as employment, education, housing, healthcare, and finance.

Requires covered entities to perform pre-deployment and ongoing evaluations addressing privacy, security, reliability, fairness, and potential discriminatory or harmful impacts, and to mitigate any material negative effects identified.

Mandates stakeholder consultation with internal ethics and technology teams and external experts, advocates, and impacted communities during system design, testing, and deployment.

Requires covered entities to maintain detailed documentation and submit both initial and annual summary reports to the FTC in standardized, machine-readable formats describing system purpose, data sources, performance metrics, fairness testing, and mitigation measures.

Directs the FTC to establish a public, searchable repository containing non-confidential information from summary reports and to publish annual aggregate findings on the use and oversight of automated decision systems.

Establishes a Bureau of Technology within the FTC to oversee implementation, technical analysis, and enforcement, supported by additional staff and resources.

Treats violations as unfair or deceptive acts or practices (UDAP) under the FTC Act, authorizes state attorneys general to bring civil enforcement actions, and encourages coordination with other federal and state regulators.

Preserves existing state, tribal, and local laws while promoting consistent national oversight of algorithmic decision-making systems.

Official name: Measures for the Management of Generative Artificial Intelligence Services (Draft for Comment)
Casual name: Measures for the Management of Generative Artificial Intelligence Services (Draft for Comment)
Short summary: Requires AI-generated content in China to meet a series of criteria, including accuracy, intellectual property protection, and respect for individual privacy; also requires AI developers to perform security assessments on their generative AI systems and submit those assessments to regulators.
Long summary:
Requires AI-generated content in China to meet a series of criteria, including accuracy, non-discrimination with regard to an array of protected characteristics, intellectual property protection, exclusion of violent content or content that subverts state power, and respect for individual privacy.

Requires AI developers to conduct a security assessment on generative AI systems that evaluates the cybersecurity of the system and its ability to comply with regulations; the assessment must be submitted to regulators before the system can be released for public use. 

Requires AI developers to prevent their generative AI systems from being used for various purposes, including engaging in unfair economic competition, and requires generative AI systems to make themselves inaccessible to individuals who use the systems for such purposes. 

Describes the legal liability that developers hold for the content generated by their AI systems and the punishments, including fines, they may face for violating these rules.

Official name: Artificial Intelligence Allied Collaboration for Crucial Operations, Research, and Development Act of 2024
Casual name: AI ACCORD Act of 2024
Short summary: Requires the Secretary of Defense to submit a strategy for enhancing AI-driven defense collaboration with Middle Eastern allies. Establishes a forum for combatant commands to coordinate AI tools and training. Mandates reporting to Congress on progress and recommendations for legislative action.
Long summary:
Mandates the Secretary of Defense to submit a strategy to Congress within 180 days to enhance cooperation with Middle Eastern allies using partner-sharing network capabilities for joint defense.

Requires the strategy to include a summary of ongoing efforts, challenges, and recommended actions for implementing joint partner-sharing networks.

Directs the Chief Data and Artificial Intelligence Officer of the Department of Defense to establish a forum for warfighters to coordinate on AI tools, methodologies, and training within 180 days.

Specifies that the forum should identify AI use cases, categorize risks, and prioritize AI tools while considering training shortfalls and opportunities for cooperation with allies.

Obligates the Secretary of Defense to report progress on the forum's establishment and recommend legislative actions to Congress within one year.

Official name: Strengthening Consumer Protections and Medical Debt Transparency Act
Casual name: Medical Debt Transparency Act
Short summary: Establishes a public database for medical debt collection, requiring health care entities to report debt collection practices. Prohibits extraordinary collection actions during pending insurance appeals or before determining patient eligibility for assistance. Requires detailed patient billing information and imposes penalties for non-compliance.
Long summary:
Amends the Public Health Service Act to prohibit health care entities and their debt collectors from initiating extraordinary collection actions if a health insurance appeal is pending.

Mandates health care entities to determine patients' eligibility for assistance programs before commencing extraordinary collection actions.

Prohibits extraordinary collection actions until 180 days after the initial bill is sent to the patient.

Requires health care entities to provide patients with itemized statements and detailed payment receipts, and information on language assistance services.

Establishes a public database for medical debt collection, requiring health care entities to submit detailed annual reports, including data on debt collection practices and patient demographics.

Imposes penalties for non-compliance, including actual damages and additional court-permitted amounts.

Requires the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau to report on medical debt collections, incorporating data from the public database and other sources.

Official name: Deploying American Blockchains Act of 2023
Casual name: Deploying American Blockchains Act of 2023
Short summary: Designates the Secretary of Commerce as principal advisor on blockchain and AI policy. Requires development of policies addressing AI-related risks in blockchain. Promotes U.S. leadership and competitiveness in blockchain and AI integration. Establishes advisory committees and reports to Congress.
Long summary:
Designates the Secretary of Commerce as the principal advisor to the President on blockchain technology and related applications.

Requires the Secretary to develop policies and recommendations addressing issues such as decentralized identity, cybersecurity, AI, regulatory compliance, and fraud reduction.

Instructs the Secretary to promote U.S. leadership and competitiveness in blockchain technology through a Blockchain Deployment Program.

Mandates the establishment of advisory committees with diverse stakeholders to support blockchain adoption.

Directs the Secretary to facilitate the development and dissemination of best practices for blockchain technology.

Obligates the Secretary to consult with private sector stakeholders and disseminate research on blockchain technology.

Requires annual reports to Congress on activities, recommendations for legislation, and emerging risks and trends in blockchain technology.

Terminates the Blockchain Deployment Program seven years after enactment.

Official name: Artificial Intelligence Public Awareness and Education Campaign Act
Casual name: Artificial Intelligence Public Awareness and Education Campaign Act
Short summary: Requires the Secretary of Commerce to launch a nationwide public education campaign on artificial intelligence. On the campaign, they must explain its benefits, risks, and everyday uses. They must also promote public understanding of AI-generated media and fraud risks, and call for reporting key performance indicators to Congress.
Long summary:
Requires the Secretary of Commerce to create and lead a nationwide public awareness and education campaign about artificial intelligence, focusing on explaining what AI is, how it affects people’s daily lives, and the benefits and risks that come with using it.

Directs the Secretary to work with other federal agencies, including the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) and the National Telecommunications and Information Administration (NTIA), to make sure the information shared with the public is accurate and up to date.

Instructs the Secretary to set clear goals and measurable results, known as key performance indicators, to track the campaign's effectiveness and gather data that demonstrates its impact.

It also encourages targeted education for groups more likely to be victims of AI scams or fraud, such as older adults, helping them recognize and avoid AI-driven deception. and it promotes public understanding of how AI is used in everyday technology, such as predictive text, navigation apps, and voice assistants, as well as in business applications like fraud detection and financial trading.

Requires the Secretary to consult experts from universities, research groups, private companies, and community organizations to guide the campaign and ensure it reflects real-world AI developments. Lastly, it mandates that the Secretary report to Congress one year after starting the campaign, including data on its success and recommendations for improvement or future AI-related outreach.

Official name: Department of Peacebuilding Act of 2023, Sec. 107 ("Office of Technology for Peace")
Casual name: Department of Peacebuilding Act, Sec. 107 ("Office of Technology for Peace")
Short summary: Directs the Department of State to give grants to researchers and developers of AI for the public good as well as researchers who prioritize safety and fair use of AI
Long summary:
There shall be in the Department an Office of Technology for Peace,

The Department of State has a vested interest in ensuring Artificial Intelligence is used for the benefit of society. 

Offers grants to researchers and developers who find ways to ensure that AI is beneficial to society.

Emphasizes the Artificial Intelligence for non-malignant purposes in public health, transportation, and sustainable energy.

Official name: New Jersey Bill A537
Casual name: New Jersey Bill A537 ("Discrimination in Automobile Insurance")
Short summary: Requires automobile insurers using artificial intelligence or other automated underwriting systems to annually submit documentation to New Jersey’s Department of Banking and Insurance proving their pricing practices are nondiscriminatory and balanced across policyholders. Authorizes the Commissioner to issue rules to enforce compliance.
Long summary:
Requires automobile insurers using automated or predictive underwriting systems to annually provide the Department of Banking and Insurance with documentation demonstrating no discriminatory outcomes in pricing based on race, ethnicity, sexual orientation, or religion.

Mandates insurers to show that each pricing segment is balanced and not disproportionate to the overall policyholder population.

Defines automated or predictive underwriting system as a computer-generated process, including robotic process automation, artificial intelligence, or other specialized technology, used to evaluate risk and determine insurance rates.

Authorizes the Commissioner of Banking and Insurance to adopt rules and regulations to implement the act.

Specifies that the act takes effect six months after enactment and applies to all automobile insurance policies issued or renewed thereafter.

Official name: Computer Science for All Act of 2023
Casual name: Computer Science for All Act of 2023
Short summary: Expands access to computer science (CS) education nationwide, including instruction in AI and computational thinking. Authorizes the Department of Education to fund grants supporting AI-related curriculum, teaching training, and equitable participation in CS programs.
Long summary:
Requires the Secretary of Education to award grants to states, local educational agencies, and eligible Tribal schools to expand computer science education, including instruction in artificial intelligence and computational thinking.

Mandates grant recipients to provide every high school student with access to computer science education within five years, ensure a progression of computer science education from prekindergarten through middle school, minimize enrollment and academic achievement gaps, and monitor and evaluate program activities.

Requires the use of grant funds for teacher training, expanding access to learning materials, and reducing equity gaps in STEAM education for underrepresented groups.

Permits grant funds to be used for building regional collaborations, hiring instructional personnel, and disseminating effective practices.

Limits use of grant funds for equipment purchases to 15%. Directs the Secretary to reserve at most 2.5 percent of funds available for grants under this section for national activities.

Authorizes $250 million for fiscal year 2024 and the following four fiscal years.

Requires grant recipients to report biannually on the use of funds and disclose student demographics.

Mandates the Secretary to report to Congress within five years on program expansion recommendations.

Official name: CREATE AI Act of 2023
Casual name: CREATE AI Act of 2023
Short summary: Establishes the National Artificial Intelligence Research Resource (NAIRR) to democratize access to AI resources, enhance U.S. AI research capacity, and foster diverse talent. Creates a steering subcommittee and program office to oversee governance, resource allocation, and compliance with privacy and ethical standards.
Long summary:
Establishes the National Artificial Intelligence Research Resource (NAIRR) to democratize access to AI research resources and spur innovation.

Creates a NAIRR Steering governance structure chaired by the Director of the Office of Science and Technology Policy to oversee governance and resource allocation.

Mandates the National Science Foundation to establish a Program Management Office to maintain day-to-day operations and ensure compliance.

Requires development of an Operating Entity through competitive processes to manage the NAIRR.

Ensures resources include computational resources, data repositories, educational tools, and AI testbeds.

Specifies criteria for user eligibility, emphasizing U.S.-based researchers and entities.

Imposes requirements and audits for privacy, ethics, civil rights, and security in AI research using NAIRR.

Authorizes a fee schedule with a free access tier to expand research participation.

Ensures adherence to national security, research integrity, and open-source principles.

Official name: Do Not Disturb Act
Casual name: Do Not Disturb Act
Short summary: This bill requires robocalls and text messages to clearly disclose at the start when artifical intelligence is being used to mimic a human voice so recipients know when they are intereacting with AI. the bill also gives double the penalties for scams that rely on AI impersonation.
Long summary:
The bill requires robocallers and telemarketers to clearly disclose artificial intelligence at the beginning of any call or text message when it is being use to mimic a human voice. This ensures that consumers are being informed and aware that they are interacting with AI rather than a real person. The bill also raises penalties for violations involving AI impersonations, the fines however are doubled if used AI to commit fraud, cause harm, or wrongfully obtain money. 

On the other hand, internet-based phone providers such as VoIP services are responsible for verifying the identities of their customers, monitoring call and texts that indicate any AI-driven ronocall ro telemarketing activity. Within this it must conduct investigations into suspecious behavior and reporting evidence of violation to the FCC to enforce the law. The bill then requires the FTC to revise existing telemarketing regulations to mandate clear discolure whenever AI is used in calls or texts.

Official name: Medicare Transaction Fraud Prevention Act
Casual name: Medicare Transaction Fraud Prevention Act
Short summary: Establishes a two-year pilot program using predictive risk-scoring algorithms for oversight of Medicare payments. Requires testing, evaluation, and review considering AI ethics guidelines. Authorizes human review of transactions flagged by algorithms. Permits intervention in high-risk transactions, including notifications and potential Medicare card terminations.
Long summary:
Establishes a pilot program under the Medicare program to test the use of a predictive risk-scoring algorithm for overseeing payments related to durable medical equipment and clinical diagnostic laboratory tests.

Requires the pilot to run for two years, starting no later than January 1, 2025, with voluntary beneficiary participation.

Mandates algorithm development in collaboration with industry representatives and modeled after real-time consumer feedback mechanisms, with scores ranging from 1 (least risky) to 99 (most risky).

Conditions use of the algorithm on prior testing and evaluation considering the AI Bill of Rights and requires notifying affected beneficiaries about data processing and implications.

Allows transaction review and possible suspension based on high-risk scores, offering beneficiaries the chance to dispute inaccuracies.

Requires that alerts for identified fraud risks be communicated to beneficiaries, along with guidance on reporting suspected fraud to law enforcement, and authorizes determination of Medicare card termination to prevent fraud.

Official name: Seizing the opportunities of safe, secure and trustworthy artificial intelligence systems for sustainable development
Casual name: UN General Assembly Resolution on AI (2024)

Official name: Recommendation of the Council on Artificial Intelligence (5/2/2024)
Casual name: OECD AI Principles (May 2024 update)
Short summary: Recommends principles for trustworthy AI including inclusivity, transparency, robustness, and accountability. Encourages investment in AI R&D, the creation of inclusive digital ecosystems, and the establishment of adaptable policy frameworks; advocates for international cooperation and capacity-building to advance AI governance and labor market transformation.
Long summary:
Recommends that AI actors and stakeholders promote principles for responsible AI, including inclusive growth, respect for human rights, transparency, robustness, and accountability.

Calls on governments and AI actors to invest in AI research and development, focusing on ethics, privacy, and reducing bias.

Advises governments to develop flexible governance environments and adapt policies to facilitate the deployment of AI systems.

Promotes building human capacity and preparing for labor market changes through skills training and fair transitions for workers as AI is deployed.

Urges governments to cooperate internationally to share AI knowledge, establish global technical standards, and promote use of internationally comparable indicators for AI progress.

Instructs the Digital Policy Committee to continue advancing AI governance by developing practical guidance and reporting on the implementation of these recommendations.

Official name: Pennsylvania HB 49
Casual name: Pennsylvania HB 49 ("AI Business Registry")
Short summary: Establishes a registry for businesses operating AI systems in Pennsylvania. Requires submission of business details, including code type and contact information. Prohibits commercial use of collected data. Mandates annual reporting to legislative committees. Defines "artificial intelligence."
Long summary:
Establishes a registry of businesses operating artificial intelligence (AI) systems in Pennsylvania.

Requires the Department of State to coordinate with other state agencies to create paper and online registry forms.

Mandates the collection of specific information from businesses, including business name, IP address, type of AI code, software intent, and contact details.

Prohibits the sale or commercial use of collected information and exempts it from Right-to-Know Law disclosure.

Maintains a searchable online database for businesses operating AI systems.

Obligates the Department of State to submit an annual report on the registry to legislative committees, detailing the number of registered businesses, registrant distribution by agency, and incurred expenses.

Defines "artificial intelligence" as computer systems performing tasks requiring human intelligence.

Sets the act to take effect 60 days post-enactment.

Official name: No AI FRAUD Act
Casual name: No AI FRAUD Act
Short summary: Establishes property rights in likeness and voice, transferable post-mortem for ten years. Prohibits unauthorized cloning and replicas, imposing liabilities. Specifies damages per violation. Allows punitive damages and attorney fees. Provides First Amendment defense. Exempts negligible harm. Sets four-year limitations period.
Long summary:
Establishes property rights for individuals in their likeness and voice, treating them as intellectual property that is transferable and descendible for ten years post-mortem.

Prohibits unauthorized distribution, transmission, or availability of personalized cloning services, digital voice replicas, or digital depictions without the individual’s consent, imposing liabilities for violations.

Specifies that unauthorized use incurs damages of $50,000 per violation for cloning services and $5,000 per violation for voice replicas or depictions, plus actual damages and profits.

Allows for punitive damages and attorney fees for injured parties and invalidates disclaimers as a defense.

Provides a First Amendment defense, balancing public interest with intellectual property rights, and considers commercial use and impact on the value of the original work.

Exempts negligible harm from liability and defines harm to include financial, physical, and severe emotional distress.

Sets a four-year limitations period for civil actions and ensures no preemption of other rights or laws.

Official name: DOE and NSF Interagency Research Act
Casual name: DOE and NSF Interagency Research Act
Short summary: Facilitates collaborative AI research between the Department of Energy and National Science Foundation, focusing on machine learning, data analytics, and computational advancements. Requires interagency agreements and promotes data sharing, infrastructure support, and STEM education initiatives.
Long summary:
Requires the Department of Energy (DOE) and the National Science Foundation (NSF) to coordinate research and development activities.

Mandates the establishment of a memorandum of understanding between the DOE and NSF, utilizing a competitive, merit-reviewed process for project applications.

Authorizes collaborative research in key areas including artificial intelligence, machine learning, data analytics, quantum computing, and computational sciences.

Promotes interagency collaboration and data sharing among Federal agencies, National Laboratories, and other institutions.

Supports research infrastructure development and STEM education initiatives, including internships and professional development.

Allows reimbursable agreements between the DOE and NSF to enhance research effectiveness.

Obligates the DOE and NSF to report on interagency coordination, research achievements, and future opportunities within two years of enactment.

Ensures activities comply with research security provisions under existing law.

Official name: Combating Cartels on Social Media Act of 2023
Casual name: Combating Cartels on Social Media Act of 2023
Short summary: Requires the Secretary of Homeland Security to assess and counter cartel recruitment on social media by developing a national strategy, consulting with operators and communities, creating reporting mechanisms, and designating an official within CBP to manage and share intelligence
Long summary:
Requires the Secretary of Homeland Security to assess within 180 days how transnational criminal organizations use covered services, including social media and other online platforms, for recruitment and other illicit activities, and to evaluate existing government and operator responses

Mandates submission of a National Strategy to combat illicit recruitment activity by Transnational Criminal Organizations on social media and online platforms within one year

Proposes improvements in cooperation between government entities and social media operators, enhanced intelligence analysis, and proactive law enforcement efforts, international partnerships, and targeted outreach to youth in border communities

Requires establishment of a centralized mechanism for reporting recruitment efforts by transnational criminal organizations to the Department of Homeland Security

Excludes any legislative recommendations not enacted by strategy submission date, including encryption policies and Section 230 reforms

Instructs the Secretary to consult with various federal, state, and local entities, as well as social media companies and experts during strategy development and implementation

Mandates semiannual reporting on the implementation of the strategy and its progress to appropriate congressional committees for five years

Designates a specific official within U.S. Customs and Border Protection to manage intelligence related to recruitment activities and to facilitate voluntary information reporting from social media operators

Official name: Countering Antisemitism Act, Sec. 8 (Online Antisemitism, Holocaust Denial, and Distortion)
Casual name: Countering Antisemitism Act, Sec. 8 (Online Antisemitism, Holocaust Denial, and Distortion)
Short summary: Requires the National Coordinator to conduct annual studies on online antisemitism, including Holocaust denial and distortion. Recommends transparency in algorithms, content moderation, and platform accountability. Submits reports to specific Senate and House committees for 10 years.
Long summary:
Mandates the National Coordinator to Counter Antisemitism to conduct a study on online antisemitism, including Holocaust denial and distortion, within 180 days of the Act's enactment and annually for 10 years.

Requires the study to analyze the prevalence of antisemitic content online.

Recommends to Congress measures to counter online antisemitism, focusing on transparency in algorithmic systems, content moderation, enforcement of community standards, and accountability for individuals and platforms.

Obligates the National Coordinator to submit the report to specific Senate and House committees.

Official name: Advanced Aviation Act
Casual name: Advanced Aviation Act
Short summary: Establishes the Office of Advanced Aviation, headed by an Associate Administrator, to oversee rulemaking, certification, and integration of advanced aviation systems, including AI-driven technologies, into national airspace. Requires interagency coordination and stakeholder engagement for safe and timely integration.
Long summary:
Redesignates the Office of NextGen as the Office of Advanced Aviation within 1 year.

Appoints an Associate Administrator to oversee the Office of Advanced Aviation.

Coordinates rulemaking and approval processes for advanced aviation systems' standardization, certification, safety, and integration into national airspace.

Establishes an Office of Advanced Integration to manage responsibilities related to advanced aviation systems.

Forms an Advanced Aviation Coordination Unit to promote safe integration and certification of advanced aviation systems.

Redesignates the William J. Hughes Technical Center as the William J. Hughes Technical Center for Advanced Aviation, responsible for research, development, and integration of new aviation technologies.

Establishes an Aviation Innovation Program to evaluate and integrate new aviation-related technologies into the national airspace system.

Defines "advanced aviation systems" to include unmanned aircraft systems, counter-UAS systems, powered lift aircraft, and related technologies.

Official name: Small Business Artificial Intelligence Training Act of 2024
Casual name: Small Business Artificial Intelligence Training Act of 2024
Short summary: Requires the Secretary of Commerce to develop AI training resources for small businesses, including those in rural and underserved areas. Mandates annual review and updates of resources. Allows grants for AI training. Terminates the program after three years.
Long summary:
Mandates the Secretary of Commerce, in coordination with various agencies, to develop AI training resources for small businesses, including those in rural, Tribal, or underserved areas.

Requires these resources to cover topics such as prompt engineering, financial management, cybersecurity, and government contracting.

Obligates the review and update of these resources every year to ensure relevance and effectiveness.

Coordinates with the Small Business Administration for the distribution of these resources through its network, including development centers and business opportunity centers.

Allows for a grants program to fund training providers using the developed resources.

Demands initial and annual reports to Congress on the progress and impact of the training resources.

Establishes a sunset clause terminating the program three years after the Act's enactment.

Official name: Pennsylvania HB 1380
Casual name: Pennsylvania HB 1380 ("Administration of Assistance Programs")
Short summary: Utilizes artificial intelligence for eligibility verification and fraud detection in public assistance programs. Authorizes data access from state agencies to verify residency and eligibility. Prioritizes job training programs to increase employment and self-reliance.
Long summary:
Authorizes the department to utilize data analytics and artificial intelligence for eligibility verification and fraud detection in public assistance programs.

Permits the department to access data from other state agencies to verify residency and eligibility of applicants.

Empowers the department to establish rules, regulations, and standards for assistance program administration, including eligibility criteria and benefit modifications.

Requires the department to ensure expenditures do not exceed appropriations and to seek necessary federal waivers or approvals.

Allows the establishment of health savings accounts and medical assistance premiums to aid residents transitioning out of assistance programs.

Mandates physical health managed care organizations to share medical assistance claims data with providers.

Official name: Federal Information Security Modernization Act of 2023, Sec. 14 ("Automation and Artificial Intelligence")
Casual name: Federal Information Security Modernization Act, Sec. 14 ("Automation and AI")
Short summary: Requires the Director to issue AI guidance for agency cybersecurity improvement. Mandates annual AI cybersecurity reports to Congress. Orders the Comptroller General to report on AI-related privacy and cybersecurity risks, and study AI's role in federal cybersecurity automation.
Long summary:
Define "information system" as per section 3502 of title 44, United States Code.

Mandates the Director to issue guidance on AI use by agencies to enhance cybersecurity of information systems.

Requires the Director and agency heads to consider AI systems where automation aids cybersecurity.

Obligates the Director to report annually for 5 years to Congress on AI use in enhancing cybersecurity.

Instructs the Comptroller General to report to Congress on AI-associated privacy and cybersecurity risks within 2 years.

Directs the Comptroller General to study and report within 2 years on AI and automation use across the federal government for cybersecurity, including automated updates to cybersecurity tools and processes.

Official name: Protect Working Musicians Act of 2023
Casual name: Protect Working Musicians Act of 2023
Short summary: Limits liability under antitrust laws for Individual Music Creator Owners who collectively negotiate or refuse licensing to Dominant Online Music Distribution Platforms or companies developing or deploying generative AI. Specifies conditions for collective negotiations, ensuring consistency with antitrust laws.
Long summary:
Grants Independent Music Creator Owners immunity from antitrust liability for collectively negotiating music licensing terms with Dominant Online Music Distribution Platforms or developing or deploying generative AI.

Defines "generative artificial intelligence" as AI systems generating novel text, video, images, audio, or other media based on prompts.

Defines “Individual Music Creator Owner” as an individual or group of musicians, producers, mixers, or sound engineers who own their copyrights and either earn less than $1 million annually in licensing revenue or qualify as a small business.

Defines “Dominant Online Music Distribution Platform” as an online service for listening to sound recordings with over $100 million in annual music-related revenue, excluding entities eligible for certain statutory licenses.

Requires that collective negotiations among Independent Music Creator Owners be nondiscriminatory, not limited to price, and directly related to negotiations with Dominant Online Music Distribution Platforms or generative AI companies.

Ensures that collective negotiations do not involve parties other than Independent Music Creator Owners, Dominant Online Music Distribution Platforms, or generative AI companies.

Stipulates that the Act does not modify, impair, or supersede existing antitrust laws beyond the specified provisions.
Tags: Applications: Broadcasting and media production;Strategies: Convening;Strategies: Tiering

Official name: New York artificial intelligence act (New York AI act)
Casual name: New York S1169 (New York AI Act)
Short summary: Promotes the safe and responsible development, deployment, and use of AI by regulating high-risk AI systems to prevent algorithmic discrimination. Requires independent audits of high-risk AI systems and end-user notification of their use, and establishes reporting requirements for the developers and deployers of high-risk AI systems.
Long summary:
Establishes the "New York AI Act" to ensure AI development and use in New York State is safe, responsible, and non-discriminatory.

Creates whistleblower protections for employees disclosing AI-related non-compliance.

Requires deployers of high-risk AI systems to notify end-users whenever such systems are used to make consequential decisions.

Requires developers and deployers of high-risk AI systems to undergo independent audits before and after deployment for algorithmic discrimination.

Mandates detailed reporting by developers and deployers of high-risk AI systems to the Attorney General, covering system capabilities and potential risks.

Prohibits the development, deployment, or use of social scoring AI systems that evaluate individuals based on behavioral data, resulting in unfair treatment or rights infringement.

Allows the Attorney General to enforce compliance and provides a private right of action for those harmed by AI non-compliance.

Official name: California Attorney General’s Legal Advisory on the Application of Existing California Laws to Artificial Intelligence
Casual name: California AG’s Legal Advisory on the Application of Existing Laws to AI
Short summary: Guides AI developers and users in California on compliance with existing laws governing consumer protections, data protections, civil rights protections, competition laws, and new AI-specific laws.
Long summary:
Guides AI developers, sellers, and users in California on legal obligations under state consumer protection, civil rights, competition, and data privacy laws.

Requires that AI systems comply with laws against unfair practices, deceptive advertising, false advertising, and anticompetitive trusts.

Explains that under California law, it is unlawful to use AI for impersonation, false representation, and the creation of deceptive political content.

Addresses risks of bias and discrimination in AI systems by explaining state civil rights laws and protections. 

Explains how the California Consumer Privacy Act and other state privacy laws apply to the use of AI systems and protect Californians' data.

Details new AI legislation, effective January 2025, mandating disclosures and detection tools for AI-generated content, and explaining unauthorized AI uses in entertainment and election contexts.

Explains new laws expanding prohibitions on exploitative AI uses, including specific laws addressing child pornography and material produced by deepfake technologies.

Describes a new law requiring health insurers to ensure licensed physicians monitor AI decision-making tools.

Official name: National Standard of the People’s Republic of China: Cybersecurity Technology – Basic Safety Requirements for Generative Artificial Intelligence Services (Draft for Feedback)
Casual name: Basic Safety Requirements for Generative Artificial Intelligence Services (National Standard - Draft for Feedback)
Short summary: Establishes safety and security requirements for generative AI services and service providers. Requires data filtering, intellectual property management, personal information protection, and secure model output with an emphasis on preventing AI systems from generating detrimental content.
Long summary:
Establishes basic safety requirements for generative AI services, focusing on training data safety, model safety, and safety measures.

Requires service providers to conduct safety assessments and manage training data sources, ensuring data does not contain over 5% illegal content.

Mandates the verification of intellectual property rights and personal information consent before using data for training.

Stipulates regular security audits and monitoring of models to prevent malicious attacks and ensure content accuracy.

Obliges disclosure of service limitations, models, and personal data usage to users.

Demands the implementation of mechanisms for complaints and a backup strategy for service continuity.

Sets specific guidelines for minors, requiring parental controls and restrictions on unsuitable content.

Requires transparency in service provision, detailing applicable scenarios and user consent for data use in training.

Mandates service providers to construct banks of unsafe key words and questions which AI models should and should not refuse to answer.

Official name: California (2023-2024) AB 96
Casual name: California AB 96 (Public Employment: Automated Vehicle Tech)
Short summary: Requires public transit employers to notify employee representatives 10 months before procurement of autonomous transit vehicle technology affecting jobs. Allows collective bargaining on technology development, implementation, workforce transition, and training plans. Grants Public Employment Relations Board jurisdiction over unfair practice charges.
Long summary:
Require public transit employers in California to notify the exclusive employee representative at least 10 months before starting a procurement process for autonomous transit vehicle technology that could eliminate jobs.

Mandate collective bargaining within 30 days upon notification or within 10 days of the public transit employer receiving a written request from the exclusive employee representative, focusing on developing and implementing the autonomous transit technology, creating transition plans for affected workers, and preparing them for new roles.

Ensure that this chapter does not impose any labor requirements that are less protective than existing statutory or contractual obligations.

Grant the Public Employment Relations Board jurisdiction to handle unfair practice charges related to this chapter.

Authorize the board to establish, amend, or repeal regulations necessary to implement the chapter, including emergency regulations for the immediate preservation of public welfare.
Tags: Applications: Transportation;Strategies: Disclosure: In deployment;Risk factors: Transparency;Strategies: Disclosure;Applications: Transportation;Strategies: Government support: AI workforce-related;Strategies: Government support

Official name: Illinois HB 0645
Casual name: Illinois HB 0645 ("Future of Work Act")
Short summary: Creates the Illinois Future of Work Task Force to plan for future labor needs. Requires assessment of emerging technologies like AI. Mandates job standards development. Instructs identification of future job opportunities. Proposes workforce training programs. Submits report by May 1, 2022.
Long summary:
Creates the Illinois Future of Work Task Force to proactively plan for the future of work and address current labor issues.

Requires the Task Force to identify and assess emerging technologies, particularly automation and AI, that significantly impact employment, wages, and skills.

Mandates the development of job standards and working conditions to build a vibrant middle class.

Instructs the Task Force to identify future job opportunities and shape them to improve life in Illinois.

Compiles research and best practices from other regions on deploying technology for the public good.

Develops tools to evaluate the impact of proposed technologies on workers, employers, and the state.

Proposes workforce development and training programs for future jobs.

Requires the Task Force to submit a report with findings to the Governor and General Assembly by May 1, 2022, and dissolves upon report submission.
Tags: Strategies: New institution;Strategies: Government support: For R&D;Strategies: Government support;Strategies: Government support: AI workforce-related;Strategies: Disclosure

Official name: Securing Growth and Robust Leadership in American Aviation Act, Sec. 1024 ("Technology Review of Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Technologies")
Casual name: American Aviation Act, Sec. 1024 ("Review of AI and ML Technologies")
Short summary: Requires the Administrator of the Federal Aviation Administration to conduct a review of current and planned AI and machine learning technologies to enhance airport efficiency and safety. Asks the administrator to consider best practices from previous applications of AI and Chinese use cases in enhancing airport operations.
Long summary:
Requires the Administrator to conduct a review of current and planned AI and machine learning technologies to enhance airport efficiency and safety.

Asks the Administrator to consider best practices and lessons learned from domestic and international AI and machine learning applications.

Permits coordination with other Federal agencies to identify China's use of AI and machine learning in airport operations.

Mandates the review to include AI and machine learning applications in jet bridges, airport service vehicles, aircraft taxi, air traffic control operations, and other areas deemed necessary by the Administrator.

Obligates the Administrator to submit a report to Congress within one year detailing the review's findings.
Tags: Applications: Transportation;Strategies: Evaluation;Applications: Transportation;Strategies: Convening;Strategies: Evaluation;Applications: Transportation;Strategies: Government study or report

Official name: Securing Growth and Robust Leadership in American Aviation Act, Sec. 1028 ("Air Traffic Control Training")
Casual name: American Aviation Act, Sec. 1028 ("Air Traffic Control Training")
Short summary: Requires the Administrator of the Federal Aviation Administration to assess the benefits of deploying AI and other advanced technologies in enhancing air traffic controller training. Mandates the Administrator to submit a report to Congress on research findings within one year.
Long summary:
Mandates the Administrator to carry out a research program assessing the benefits of deploying advanced technologies such as AI to modernize, enhance, and streamline on-the-job training for FAA air traffic controllers.

Requires the Administrator to include collaboration with labor organizations and other stakeholders in the research program.

Mandates the Administrator to report findings to Congress within 1 year of the Act's enactment.
Tags: Applications: Transportation;Strategies: Evaluation;Strategies: Government study or report;Strategies: Convening;Strategies: Government study or report

Official name: REAL Political Advertisements Act
Casual name: REAL Political Advertisements Act
Short summary: Requires political advertisements containing images or video generated with artificial intelligence to include clear disclaimers. Directs the Federal Election Commission (FEC) to define AI-generated content, issue implementing regulations, and report on compliance and enforcement.
Long summary:
Amends the Federal Election Campaign Act of 1971 to require any political advertisement containing images or video footage generated in whole or in part using artificial intelligence to include a clear and conspicuous disclaimer, with specific formatting requirements for text, audio, video, and other media to ensure visibility and audibility.

Directs the Federal Election Commission to promulgate regulations within 120 days of enactment to implement the disclaimer requirement and define "content generated by artificial intelligence" to reflect both current and emerging uses of generative AI.

Requires the FEC to submit biennial reports to Congress assessing compliance and enforcement, recommending modifications, and identifying further measures to enhance transparency and accountability in political advertising.

Official name: Directive on Automated Decision-Making (Canada)
Casual name: Directive on Automated Decision-Making (Canada)
Short summary: Mandates federal institutions to assess and mitigate risks of automated decision systems through Algorithmic Impact Assessments. Requires transparency, bias testing, data governance, peer review, and recourse mechanisms to ensure responsible, fair, and auditable AI use in administrative decisions.
Long summary:
Requires Algorithmic Impact Assessments before production of any automated decision system, with regular updates and public disclosure of results in accessible formats.

Mandate transparency by notifying individuals when decisions are made in whole or in part by automated systems, and by providing plain-language explanations of how and why decisions were made.

Direct rigorous testing and continuous monitoring of automated decision systems to detect and mitigate unintended biases and ensure compliance with laws. 

Obligates data governance and quality assurance, including peer reviews and Gender-based Analysis Plus.

Ensures the possibility of human intervention in automated decisions and provides clients with recourse options to challenge decisions.

Specifies consequences for non-compliance under the Financial Administration Act.

Designates the Chief Information Officer to provide guidance and maintain the Algorithmic Impact Assessment.
