Official name: Regulations of Shenzhen Special Economic Zone on Promoting the Artificial Intelligence Industry
Casual name: Regulations of Shenzhen Special Economic Zone on Promoting the Artificial Intelligence Industry
Short summary: Establishes a coordination mechanism to balance AI development and safety, promotes standardized industry development and public data sharing, encourages innovation through international cooperation, and mandates ethical AI usage principles. Implements tiered AI risk assessment and supervision strategies.
Long summary:
Establishes mechanisms for promoting the AI industry in Shenzhen, emphasizing high-quality AI development and integration across social and economic sectors.

Mandates the Shenzhen Municipal Government to coordinate AI development and safety, incorporating AI development into national plans and setting clear industry standards.

Encourages international collaboration in AI research and development and emphasizes ethical safety norms.

Requires construction of AI infrastructure, such as data centers, public data platforms, and open-source systems.

Encourages application of AI technologies in societal areas, such as healthcare, social governance, and education, and supports AI projects strategically.

Calls for an AI ethics committee to oversee ethical norms and governance structures, ensuring compliance with safety standards.

Implements differentiated supervision based on AI application risk levels, with high-risk applications requiring pre-evaluation.

Prohibits AI use for unlawful activities or discrimination and mandates consumer protection and privacy safeguards.

Regulations effective November 1, 2022.

Official name: Washington (2018) HB 2970
Casual name: Washington HB 2970 (Autonomous Vehicle Working Group)
Short summary: Establishes a work group to develop policy recommendations for autonomous vehicle operation on Washington roads. Requires following industry developments, exploring legal changes, and engaging stakeholders. Mandates annual progress reports and recommendations for state laws and policies. Expires December 31, 2023.
Long summary:
Mandates the Washington State Transportation Commission to establish a work group to develop policy recommendations for autonomous vehicle operation on public roadways.

Requires executive branch membership from various state departments and branches, alongside legislative members and additional stakeholders from sectors including technology and motor vehicle manufacturing.

Directs the work group to monitor advancements in autonomous vehicle technology, including federal and state regulatory frameworks, and to prepare state policies for autonomous commercial and passenger vehicles.

Necessitates consideration of diverse policy areas, such as vehicle testing, registration, driver's license requirements, traffic laws, cybersecurity, and impacts on labor and businesses.

Instructs the commission to report annually to the governor and legislature on progress and propose legal modifications.

Sets an expiration date of December 31, 2023, for sections detailing the establishment and function of the work group.
Tags: Strategies: New institution;Strategies: Disclosure;Strategies: Governance development;Strategies: Convening;Strategies: Government study or report

Official name: Washington (2022) SB 5460
Casual name: Washington SB 5460 (Recommendations of Autonomous Vehicle Work Group)
Short summary: Amends autonomous vehicle testing regulations, requiring self-certifying entities to provide contact information, testing locations, vehicle identification, insurance proof, accident reports, and notify law enforcement. Permits public access to testing information and annual legislative reports. Effectively defines "autonomous" vehicles. Effective October 1, 2022.
Long summary:
Amends requirements for testing autonomous vehicles on public roads in Washington State.

Mandates that self-certifying entities provide contact information, test locations, vehicle identification numbers, and proof of insurance for testing.

Requires assignment of a unique identification number for autonomous vehicles lacking a vehicle ID as mandated by law.

Obliges reporting of collisions and moving violations involving autonomous vehicles on public roads, including whether the autonomous system was active during incidents.

Demands advance written notice to relevant law enforcement agencies before testing, detailing the expected testing period, vehicle description, and contact information.

Allows the Department of Licensing to charge a fee to offset administrative costs of the self-certification testing program.

Ensures public access to information submitted by testing entities, with annual legislative reporting.

Defines "autonomous" vehicle levels per Society of Automotive Engineers standards J3016.

Prohibits autonomous vehicle testing without fulfilling initial information requirements.
Tags: Strategies: Licensing, registration, and certification;Strategies: Disclosure;Strategies: Disclosure: About incidents;Strategies: Pilots and testbeds;Strategies: Disclosure;Strategies: Licensing, registration, and certification;Strategies: Government study or report

Official name: Facial Recognition Act of 2023
Casual name: Facial Recognition Act
Short summary: Regulates law enforcement use of facial recognition by requiring court orders, protecting civil rights, and mandating transparency. Prohibits reliance on facial recognition alone for arrests, requires accuracy and bias testing, and establishes reporting, auditing, and public notice requirements.
Long summary:
Prohibits law enforcement from using facial recognition without a court order, except in defined emergency circumstances. 

Mandates regular removal of photos from databases used for facial recognition, including minors and individuals not charged or acquitted.

Requires detailed applications from prosecutors and approval from courts for authorized use of facial recognition.

Prohibits the use of facial recognition with body cameras, for face surveillance, or for immigration enforcement.

Enforces annual accuracy and bias testing by independent entities and prohibits systems that fail to meet minimum standards. 

Requires public reporting and logging of facial recognition use, including demographic data.

Mandates audits of law enforcement agencies' use of facial recognition to ensure compliance and prevent misuse.

Official name: AI Incident Reporting and Security Enhancement Act
Casual name: AI Incident Reporting and Security Enhancement Act
Short summary: Directs the National Institute of Standards and Technology to update the National Vulnerability Database for AI vulnerabilities and assess voluntary reporting of significant AI security and safety incidents. Requires multi-stakeholder collaboration to evaluate and develop definitions, standards, guidelines, and reporting mechanisms for incident tracking.
Long summary:
Directs the Director of the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) to update the National Vulnerability Database to include AI security vulnerabilities.

Requires NIST to establish common definitions for AI security vulnerabilities and develop standards for managing these vulnerabilities.

Mandates a multi-stakeholder process to assess voluntary reporting and tracking of substantial AI security and safety incidents, and to establish guidelines, best practices, and standardized mechanisms for reporting AI incidents.

Obligates NIST to submit a report to Congress within three years, detailing the findings and recommendations from the multi-stakeholder process on AI incident reporting and tracking.

Official name: Artificial Intelligence Literacy Act of 2023
Casual name: H. R. 6791 (AI Literacy Act of 2023)
Short summary: Amends the Digital Equity Act to promote AI literacy by establishing grants for AI education in schools, community colleges, and higher education. Supports teacher training, lab development, and virtual learning platforms and requires annual Congressional reports on AI education initiatives.
Long summary:
Amends the Digital Equity Act of 2021 to include artificial intelligence (AI) literacy within the scope of digital literacy.

Defines "artificial intelligence literacy" as understanding basic AI principles, applications, implications, limitations, and ethical considerations.

Requires grants to support AI literacy initiatives in public elementary and secondary schools, including teacher training, professional development, and resource development.

Supports higher education institutions to develop AI learning labs, virtual platforms, and educational tools for teachers and community educators.

Includes grants to community anchor institutions for AI education and training programs.

Mandates an annual report to Congress summarizing and analyzing the progress and impact of AI literacy initiatives, with recommendations for expansion or modification.

Official name: AI Transparency in Elections Act of 2024
Casual name: AI Transparency in Elections Act of 2024
Short summary: Amends the Federal Election Campaign Act to require clear disclaimers on political ads using AI-generated content. Imposes penalties for non-compliance and mandates the Federal Election Commission to issue regulations and report compliance.
Long summary:
Requires the inclusion of disclaimers on political advertisements containing content substantially generated by AI.

Mandates that disclaimers must clearly state the use of AI in creating or materially altering the content.

Establishes safe harbor guidelines for what constitutes a "clear and conspicuous manner" in disclaimers for image, audio, and video communications.

Amends the Federal Election Campaign Act of 1971 to include penalties for violations of the disclaimer requirements, with fines up to $50,000.

Directs the Federal Election Commission (FEC) to issue regulations outlining criteria for determining substantially AI-generated content and requirements for the disclosure statement. 

Requires the FEC to report on compliance and enforcement within two years and biannually thereafter to Congress.

Official name: Advancing Automation Research and Development in Agriculture Act
Casual name: Advancing Automation Research and Development in Agriculture Act
Short summary: Establishes a competitive grant program to advance mechanization and automation in specialty crops. Prioritizes projects enhancing labor efficiency, technology adoption, and human capital development. Requires non-Federal matching funds and scientific peer review. Allocates $20 million annually from 2024 onwards.
Long summary:
Establishes a stand-alone competitive research and extension grant program under the Department of Agriculture to advance mechanization and automation for specialty crops.

Mandates projects to create or improve cost-effective technologies that reduce manual labor and increase efficiency in crop production, resource management, and processing.

Requires projects to promote the adoption of mechanization and automation technologies by emphasizing factors such as connectivity, autonomy, and cost-effectiveness.

Specifies projects to accelerate automation through prototype development, in-field trials, and rapid commercialization.

Allows grants to be awarded to federal agencies, universities, research institutions, private organizations, and other eligible entities.

Mandates a competitive grant process based on scientific peer review and industry relevance.

Requires annual consultations with the specialty crops committee to review procedures, objectives, and grant awards.

Allocates $20 million annually from the Commodity Credit Corporation to fund the program starting fiscal year 2024.

Official name: International Artificial Intelligence Research Partnership Act of 2024
Casual name: International Artificial Intelligence Research Partnership Act of 2024
Short summary: Establishes international AI research partnerships, encouraging U.S. cities to collaborate with international counterparts while aligning with U.S. research priorities. Prohibits partnerships with cities in countries of concern. Limits research to non-national security areas. Allocates $20 million annually for implementation.
Long summary:
Establishes international artificial intelligence research partnerships between U.S. cities and their international counterparts to promote AI leadership, economic cooperation, and workforce development.

Encourages public-private partnerships, including nonprofits and academic institutions, to support these AI research collaborations.

Requires coordination with the National Science Foundation to align activities with U.S. AI research priorities and leverage existing resources.

Prohibits partnerships with cities in countries deemed a national security concern or detrimental to U.S. foreign policy.

Limits research to non-national security AI areas and mandates adherence to export control regulations.

Authorizes the Secretary of State to enter contracts to facilitate partnerships and allocates $20 million annually from 2025 to 2028 for implementation.

Mandates annual reporting on partnerships, research scope, city participation, coordination efforts, and implementation challenges to relevant congressional committees.

Official name: Expanding AI Voices Act
Casual name: Expanding AI Voices Act
Short summary: Amends the National AI Initiative Act to require the National Science Foundation to award grants to broaden AI research, education, and workforce development. Targets underrepresented institutions, fosters collaborations, and emphasizes ethical practices in AI education.
Long summary:
Amends the National AI Initiative Act by introducing a new subsection focused on expanding AI capacity.

Mandates the Director of the National Science Foundation to competitively award eligible higher education institutions and nonprofit organizations to enhance AI research, education, and workforce development.

Specifies eligibility for institutions not among the top 100 in federal R&D spending, historically Black colleges, minority-serving institutions, Tribal colleges, and their consortia.

Encourages collaborations with various stakeholders, including AI research institutes and private sector entities.

Allows use of funds for AI research programs, faculty development, resource access, community building, workshops, and ethical AI education.

Requires outreach to eligible entities, emphasizing underserved and underrepresented communities.

Ensures awarded projects complement existing programs without duplication.

Considers diversity, geographic distribution, and resource constraints in award decisions.

Official name: Canada Artificial Intelligence and Data Act (Companion document)
Casual name: Canada Artificial Intelligence and Data Act (Companion document)
Short summary: Proposes AIDA to regulate high-impact AI systems, ensuring safety, accountability, and human rights protection. Empowers the Minister to enforce compliance and prohibits harmful AI uses. Establishes an AI and Data Commissioner. Aligns with international standards. Consults stakeholders for regulation development.
Long summary:
Proposes the Artificial Intelligence and Data Act (AIDA) to regulate AI in Canada, focusing on high-impact AI systems.

Empowers the Minister of Innovation, Science, and Industry to enforce AIDA, supported by a new AI and Data Commissioner.

Prohibits the reckless and malicious use of AI that causes harm, introducing new criminal law provisions.

Aligns with international norms, such as the EU AI Act, and integrates with existing Canadian laws.

Mandates businesses to identify, assess, and mitigate risks associated with high-impact AI systems.

Requires transparency, fairness, safety, and accountability in AI systems.

Introduces administrative penalties and criminal offences for non-compliance.

Plans a consultation process for the development of regulations, seeking input from stakeholders.

Establishes mechanisms for human oversight and monitoring of AI systems.

Targets AI systems impacting services, biometric identification, behavior influence, and health and safety.

Official name: Advancing the Responsible Acquisition of Artificial Intelligence in Government
Casual name: OMB Requirements for AI Acquisition in Government
Collections: U.S. regulations, executive orders, and agency policies;Editors' Picks
Short summary: Directs federal agencies to enhance AI acquisition policies by promoting a competitive AI market. Requires interagency collaboration, risk management for AI impacting rights and safety, innovation in procurement, and implement structures to govern and manage business processes.
Long summary:
Directs agencies to improve responsible AI acquisition, emphasizing cross-functional and interagency collaboration, risk management, and market competitiveness.

Requires agencies to update acquisition policies to reflect AI governance responsibilities and share AI acquisition information across the executive branch.

Mandates compliance with risk management practices for AI impacting rights or safety, including managing privacy, security, and discrimination risks.

Urges agencies to prevent vendor lock-in by prioritizing interoperability, transparency, and innovative acquisition practices.

Encourages the acquisition of generative AI for experimentation, while emphasizing transparency, risk mitigation, and environmental considerations.

Applies to all federal agencies, excluding the Intelligence Community and National Security Systems, and sets specific deadlines for compliance and reporting. 

Fosters a diverse AI marketplace by minimizing vendor lock-in and promoting competition through strategic contractual requirements.

Official name: Workforce for AI Trust Act
Casual name: Workforce for AI Trust Act
Short summary: Amends the National Artificial Intelligence Initiative Act to support NSF fellowships for interdisciplinary AI research, emphasizing ethics and responsible practices. Enhances NIST's role in AI governance workforce development and risk management, focusing on education, technical standards, and professional certifications.
Long summary:
Amends the National Artificial Intelligence Initiative Act of 2020 to authorize the National Science Foundation (NSF) to support interdisciplinary AI fellowships, encouraging ethical AI practices.

Mandates the NSF to conduct outreach to diverse institutions and groups underrepresented in STEM fields for fellowship applicants.

Directs the NSF to promote AI-based training and workshops across STEM fields to accelerate scientific discovery and technology development.

Requires the integration of diverse disciplinary perspectives in AI research peer review panels.

Instructs the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) to support education and workforce development to expand the AI governance workforce, focusing on managing AI-related risks.

Mandates NIST to develop technical standards and guidance for AI risk management careers, including mapping, measuring, and validating AI systems.

Official name: Google DeepMind Frontier Safety Framework Version 2.0
Casual name: Google DeepMind Frontier Safety Framework Version 2.0
Short summary: Establishes Critical Capability Levels (CCLs) to assess AI risks from misuse and deceptive alignment and requires evaluations for CCL proximity and applies mitigations. Addresses detection and monitoring strategies for deceptive alignment and deployment procedures for misuse risks.
Long summary:
Establishes the "Frontier Safety Framework" for managing risks of advanced AI, focusing on Critical Capability Levels (CCLs) related to misuse and deceptive alignment.

Outlines CCLs for high-risk domains, including chemical, biological, radiological, nuclear attacks (CBRN), cyber threats, and machine learning R&D, needing security and deployment mitigations.

Mandates regular evaluations of frontier AI models to assess approaching CCLs, employing "early warning evaluations" for proactive risk management.

Specifies mitigations when models near CCLs, using internal and external expertise to develop response plans, emphasizing automated monitoring for deceptive alignment risks.

Describes governance protocols, involving review and approval by corporate bodies like the Google DeepMind AGI Safety Council, and outlines disclosure criteria to government authorities and external organizations.

Plans continual updates to risk modeling, capability assessments, and risk-appropriate actions as AI capabilities evolve.

Official name: An Act to amend Tennessee Code Annotated, Title 39, Chapter 14, Part 1 and Title 47, relative to the protection of personal rights.
Casual name: ELVIS Act (Tennessee)
Collections: Editors' Picks;U.S. state and local documents
Short summary: Amends Tennessee law to protect individuals' rights to their name, photograph, voice, or likeness, including simulated versions, against unauthorized commercial use. Imposes liability for unauthorized AI-generated likenesses. Allows fair use exceptions and extends rights posthumously. Effective July 1, 2024.
Long summary:
Expands the "Personal Rights Protection Act of 1984," renaming it the "Ensuring Likeness, Voice, and Image Security Act of 2024."

Grants individuals property rights over their name, photograph, voice, or likeness across all mediums.

Defines "voice" to include both actual and simulated sounds identifiable to an individual.

Terminates exclusive commercial rights after two years of non-use, beginning ten years after death.

Imposes civil liability for the unauthorized commercial use of an individual's name, photograph, voice, or likeness.

Prohibits distribution of technology primarily used to produce unauthorized likenesses. 

Allows individuals or parties with exclusive contracts for personal services or sound recordings to enforce rights. 

Exempts certain uses protected by the First Amendment as fair use, including news, commentary, and satire. 

Mandates that employees or owners of any medium used for advertising be liable if they publish or disseminate an advertisement in violation of this act, and if they should have known about the violation.

Official name: Political Declaration on Responsible Military Use of Artificial Intelligence and Autonomy
Casual name: Political Declaration on Responsible Military Use of AI and Autonomy
Short summary: Requires endorsing States to ensure military AI use aligns with international law. Mandates oversight, transparency, and rigorous testing for military AI systems. Urges minimizing bias, implementing safeguards, clear system functions, and staff training. Promotes public commitment and international cooperation for responsible AI deployment.
Long summary:
Require States to ensure military AI use complies with international law, particularly international humanitarian law.

Mandate accountability through a responsible human command chain.

Impose legal reviews to align military AI with international obligations and enhance civilian protection.

Demand oversight by senior officials for high-consequence AI applications.

Advocate minimizing unintended bias in military AI.

Insist on transparent, auditable methodologies and documentation for military AI development.

Oblige training for personnel to understand AI capabilities and limitations, preventing automation bias.

Prescribe explicit, well-defined uses for military AI, with rigorous testing for safety and effectiveness.

Implement safeguards to detect and mitigate AI failures, with the ability to deactivate systems if needed.

Encourage States to publicly commit to these measures, support responsible AI use, and engage internationally to refine and promote these standards.

Official name: Hiroshima Process International Guiding Principles for Advanced AI Systems
Casual name: Hiroshima Process International Guiding Principles for Advanced AI Systems
Short summary: Establishes international guiding principles on AI development and deployment, which include proactive risk identification and mitigation, public disclosure of AI systems' capabilities, information sharing among government, industry, and civil society stakeholders, data privacy protection measures, and content authentication and provenance mechanisms.
Long summary:
Establishes international guiding principles around the safe, secure, and trustworthy development and deployment of artificial intelligence worldwide.

Recommends proactive mitigation of risks across the AI lifecycle and investment in physical, cyber, and insider threat security controls.

Recommends the public disclosure of advanced AI systems' capabilities, limitations, and appropriate or inappropriate uses.

Recommends information sharing and reporting of incidents among relevant industry, government, and civil society stakeholders

Recommends the deployment of content authentication and provenance mechanisms to enable users to identify AI-generated content.

Encourages development of advanced AI systems foremost to address key global challenge in alignment with the UN SDG goals.

Recommends the development and adoption of international technical standards.

Recommends the implementation of personal data privacy and IP protection measures.

Official name: Hiroshima Process International Code of Conduct for Organizations Developing Advanced AI Systems
Casual name: Hiroshima Process International Code of Conduct for Organizations Developing Advanced AI Systems
Short summary: Promotes safe, secure AI through voluntary guidance for developing advanced AI systems. Emphasizes risk assessment, transparency, security controls, and responsible AI governance. Encourages research, international standards, and societal benefits. Prohibits harmful AI applications undermining democratic values or human rights.
Long summary:
Promotes the safe, secure, and trustworthy development of AI by providing voluntary guidance for organizations developing advanced AI systems.

Encourages organizations to identify, evaluate, and mitigate risks across the AI lifecycle, employing diverse testing methods and ensuring traceability through documented development practices.

Urges ongoing monitoring of AI systems post-deployment to identify vulnerabilities and misuse, through collaboration with stakeholders to mitigate risks, while advocating for public reporting and enhanced transparency regarding identified limitations.

Encourages information sharing and incident reporting, as well as the development and disclosure of AI governance and risk management policies among stakeholders to promote safety and trustworthiness.

Recommends robust security controls, including cybersecurity measures and insider threat safeguards, to protect AI system components from unauthorized access.

Promotes the development of content authentication mechanisms to identify AI-generated content and the creation of international technical standards for AI development.

Official name: Framework to Mitigate AI-Enabled Extreme Risks
Casual name: Framework to Mitigate AI-Enabled Extreme Risks
Short summary: Establishes a framework for federal oversight of frontier AI models to mitigate extreme risks. Proposes potential oversight entities and mandates expert involvement to address biosecurity, chemical, cybersecurity, and nuclear threats.
Long summary:
Establishes a framework for federal oversight of advanced AI models, termed "frontier models," to mitigate extreme AI-enabled risks like biological, chemical, cyber, and nuclear threats.

Defines frontier models as those requiring computing power exceeding 10^26 operations, and capable of diverse tasks or intended for specific high-risk areas.

Mandates entities selling or renting large computing resources to report usage and exercise due diligence, particularly concerning foreign clients.

Requires developers to notify an oversight entity before training frontier models and integrate safeguards against extreme risks.

Obligates developers to adhere to cybersecurity standards and report risk mitigation steps.

Imposes an evaluation and licensing process before deploying frontier models, using a tiered structure based on risk level to determine deployment scope.

Suggests options for an oversight entity, including an interagency body, new agency, or existing federal agencies, emphasizing expert involvement.

Tasks the oversight entity to report on emerging challenges and risks, ensuring framework adaptability.

Official name: Oversee Emerging Technology Act
Casual name: Oversee Emerging Technology Act
Short summary: Requires federal agencies substantially engaged with emerging technologies to appoint a senior official as an emerging technology lead. Tasks them with advising on AI use, policy expertise, interagency collaboration, and procurement input. Mandates informing Congress of appointments and responsibilities.
Long summary:
Requires the head of each covered federal agency engaged in emerging technologies to appoint or designate a Senior Executive Service individual or equivalent as an emerging technology lead.

Mandates that these leads advise agencies on the responsible use of emerging technologies, including artificial intelligence.

Obliges these leads to provide expertise on responsible policies and practices, collaborate with interagency bodies, and input on procurement policies.

Instructs the President to inform Congress within 180 days of enactment about the appointed leads and their authorities and responsibilities.

Official name: To establish in the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration a program to improve precipitation forecasts, and for other purposes.
Casual name: H. R. 4913 NOAA Precipitation Forecasts
Short summary: Supports AI and machine learning research to enhance precipitation prediction accuracy and reliability. Encourages collaboration with academic and private sectors to evaluate emerging technologies. Uses high-performance computing to advance Earth System Models' predictive skills for extreme precipitation phenomena.
Long summary:
Establishes a program within the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) to improve precipitation forecasts.

Requires the use of fully coupled Earth System Models to enhance precipitation prediction.

Mandates the utilization of high performance computing and other technologies, including machine learning and artificial intelligence, to advance precipitation forecasts.

Supports research and development in collaboration with academic and private sector partners to test and evaluate emerging technologies.

Improves the accuracy, reliability, and timeliness of precipitation predictions across various timescales.

Incorporates social and behavioral sciences to enhance precipitation forecast products and communication.

Coordinates efforts with Federal, State, local, Tribal, and academic entities.

Authorizes appropriations of $15 million annually from fiscal year 2024 through fiscal year 2028 for the program.

Official name: AI Grand Challenges Act of 2024
Casual name: AI Grand Challenges Act of 2024
Short summary: Establishes the AI Grand Challenges Program to award competitive prizes for AI research in identified grand challenge areas like health, cybersecurity, and national security.
Long summary:
Establishes the AI Grand Challenges Program to stimulate AI research, development, and commercialization through prize competitions addressing grand challenges in various sectors like national security, health, and environment.

Requires the Director of the National Science Foundation, in coordination with public and private entities, to identify AI grand challenges and develop problem statements.

Mandates the creation of a specific grand challenge for AI-enabled cancer breakthroughs.

Sets eligibility criteria for participants, requiring private entities to be U.S.-based and individuals to be U.S. citizens or permanent residents.

Requires submission of a report within 60 days of awarding a prize, detailing the winning submission and its benefits, as well as biennial reports to Congress on program activities and public outreach, to be made publicly accessible.

Official name: To provide for Federal civilian agency laboratory development for testing and certification of artificial intelligence for civilian agency use, and for other purposes.
Casual name: Federal Civilian Agency AI Testing and Certification Act
Short summary: Requires Homeland Security, via FEMA, to develop AI testing centers for civilian agencies, ensuring rights protection and democratic principles. Establishes an AI Incident Reporting Office. Mandates biannual reports to Congress, and authorizes $20 billion for implementation.
Long summary:
Establishes federal civilian agency laboratories for AI testing and certification under the Secretary of Homeland Security through FEMA.

Mandates development of AI training and testing centers to evaluate AI systems for federal use, prioritizing privacy, transparency, accountability, and protection of individual rights.

Prohibits automated decision-making that threatens due process and constitutional rights.

Requires real-world use cases to inform AI system outcomes in federal settings, ensuring no personal data is used.

Creates a digital repository for use cases, ensuring Federal agencies can train and test AI systems appropriately.

Establishes an Office of Artificial Intelligence Incident Reporting within the Department of Homeland Security for agency collaboration and reporting on AI experiences.

Requires biannual reports from DHS to Congress on implementation, challenges, and resource needs, and agency reports on adverse AI experiences.

Authorizes $20 billion in appropriations to implement these measures.

Official name: Singapore National AI Strategy 2.0
Casual name: Singapore National AI Strategy 2.0
Short summary: Seeks to establish AI as a public good in Singapore; aiming to build excellence and empowerment through industry, government, and research collaboration. Prioritizes infrastructure, talent, and trusted AI governance.
Long summary:
Aims to harness AI for public good and position Singapore as a leader in AI innovation. 

Sets two primary goals: achieving excellence in AI to maximize value creation and empowering communities to use AI with confidence. 

Outlines a focus on three systems: Activity Drivers, People & Communities, and Infrastructure & Environment, supported by 10 enablers. 

Plans to establish AI Centers of Excellence, strengthen the AI start-up ecosystem, and drive public sector AI adoption. 

Aims to attract global AI talent and boost its AI practitioner pool to support AI demand. 

Emphasizes building a trusted AI environment through updated regulatory frameworks, security measures, and international collaboration. 

Seeks to enhance data management and privacy technologies for responsible AI development and position Singapore as a global AI governance leader.

Encourages making more public sector data available for AI development that serves the Public Good.

Official name: Securing Growth and Robust Leadership in American Aviation Act, Sec. 908 ("Part 107 Waiver Improvements")
Casual name: American Aviation Act, Sec. 908 ("Part 107 Waiver Improvements")
Short summary: Requires the Administrator of the Federal Aviation Administration to leverage big data analytics and machine learning in reviewing requests for certificates of waiver under section 107.200 of title 14, Code of Federal Regulations.Requires the Administrator of the Federal Aviation Administration to improve waiver request processes by leveraging big data analytics and machine learning.Adopts a performance- and risk-based approach for waiver requests. Prohibits open-ended prompts unless necessary. Utilizes big data and machine learning for waiver data. Publishes waivers online, protecting proprietary info. Expedites similar waiver modifications and renewals. Considers property access control.
Long summary:
Requires the Administrator of the Federal Aviation Administration to leverage big data analytics and machine learning in reviewing requests for certificates of waiver under section 107.200 of title 14, Code of Federal Regulations.Adopts a performance- and risk-based approach for reviewing waiver requests under section 107.200 of title 14, CFR.

Improves the waiver application process and prohibits the use of open-ended descriptive prompts, except for unusual or irregular operations.

Leverages data, including big data analytics and machine learning, from previous waiver requests.

Requires consideration of control over access to real property within the operation area and recognizes safety enhancements from such control.

Publishes all issued certificates of waiver on the FAA website, including terms, conditions, limitations, and airspace class, while protecting proprietary information.

Allows streamlined approval for waiver applications substantially similar to previously approved ones.

Establishes an expedited review process for modifying or renewing previously issued waivers.

Official name: Canadian Guardrails for Generative AI – Code of Practice
Casual name: Canadian Guardrails for Generative AI – Code of Practice
Short summary: Proposes a voluntary code of practice for generative AI in Canada to avoid harmful impacts and build trust in AI systems. Requires developers, deployers, and operators to prevent misuse, assess bias, ensure human oversight, and conduct rigorous testing.
Long summary:
Proposes a voluntary code of practice for generative AI systems in Canada to ensure safety, security, and trust ahead of binding regulation.

Requires developers, deployers, and operators to identify and prevent malicious and inappropriate uses, mitigating safety risks of AI systems throughout their lifecycle.

Mandates assessment and curation of datasets to avoid biases and ensure fairness.

Emphasizes transparency through methods like watermarking AI-generated content and explaining system development processes.

Encourages human oversight and monitoring, including mechanisms to report adverse impacts and routine model updates.

Calls for accountability with internal governance, independent audits, and defined roles and responsibilities.

Official name: To direct the Secretary of Defense to establish a working group to develop and coordinate an artificial intelligence initiative among the Five Eyes countries, and for other purposes.
Casual name: H. R. 6425 (Five Eyes countries AI initiative)
Short summary: Directs the Secretary of Defense to establish the Five AIs Strategic AI Working Group to develop and coordinate AI initiatives among Five Eyes countries. Requires comparison of AI systems, testing interoperability, ethical frameworks, and expanding innovation efforts.
Long summary:
Directs the Secretary of Defense to establish the Five AIs Strategic Artificial Intelligence Working Group to develop and coordinate AI initiatives among Five Eyes countries. 

Outlines the organization of the working group, designating a senior civilian or military officer as head, and encouraging participation of Five Eyes Intelligence Oversight and Review Council members. 

Tasks the working group with comparing AI systems, identifying interoperability solutions, and testing AI model redundancy.

Instructs the development of shared strategies for AI research, development, and data management.

Requires the submission of a plan for the establishment and activities of the working group within 60 days and biannual reports to congressional committees.

Official name: New York A7838
Casual name: New York A7838 ("Long-term impact of AI on workforce")
Short summary: Requires the New York Department of Labor to study AI's long-term impact on the workforce, issuing interim reports every five years and a final report by 2035. Prohibits state entities from using AI to displace employees until the final report.
Long summary:
Mandates the Department of Labor to begin a study on the long-term impact of artificial intelligence on the state workforce within six months.

Requires consultation with the Department of Civil Service and the Office of Information Technology Services.

Calls for examination of job performance, productivity, training, education requirements, privacy, and security.

Obligates the Department of Labor to issue an interim report every five years and a final report by January 1, 2035, with recommendations for legislative action.

Prohibits all state departments and governmental entities from using AI to displace any natural person from their employment until the final report and recommendations are received.

Ensures immediate effect upon enactment.

Official name: Rhode Island SB 146
Casual name: Rhode Island SB 146 ("Consumer Protection Gaming Act")
Short summary: Prohibits the use of facial recognition, biometric recognition, and certain automated decision systems in video-lottery terminals and online betting applications. Restricts AI use for influencing gaming outcomes. Excludes standardized rewards programs from these prohibitions.
Long summary:
Prohibits the use of facial recognition technology and biometric recognition programs in video-lottery terminals and online betting applications.

Prohibits the use of algorithmic programs that modify gaming based on a consumer's betting history to increase wagers.

Prohibits the use of artificial intelligence or automated decision systems to promote increased play through intermittent reinforcement based on cognitive-behavioral algorithms and prior gaming data.

Applies these prohibitions to sports betting applications and vendors of gambling products licensed by Rhode Island.

Excludes standardized rewards programs from these prohibitions.

Takes effect upon passage.

Official name: Pennsylvania HR 170
Casual name: Pennsylvania HR 170 ("AI Advisory Committee")
Short summary: Directs the Joint State Government Commission to study AI's impact in Pennsylvania, establish an advisory committee, and assess AI development, risks, and benefits. Requires legislative proposals, ethical guidelines, and an evaluation report within 18 months to state leaders.
Long summary:
Directs the Joint State Government Commission to establish an advisory committee to study artificial intelligence's impact in Pennsylvania.

Mandates an assessment of AI development and use, including benefits, risks, and fiscal impacts on the state.

Proposes legislation for state AI regulation and a definition for AI.

Recommends evaluating AI's effects on students' academic performance and addressing AI-driven disinformation.

Urges studying potential job displacement due to AI automation and assessing implications for organized labor.

Calls for a proposal on ethical and responsible AI development in Pennsylvania.

Advises considering the creation of a permanent commission for AI study.

Allows the Joint State Government Commission to request information from state agencies for the study.

Requires a report with findings and recommendations within 18 months to various governmental leaders and committees.

Official name: Protecting Kids on Social Media Act
Casual name: Protecting Kids on Social Media Act
Short summary: Prohibits social media platforms from using algorithmic recommendation systems for users under 18, and bans access of users under 13. Requires age verification and parental consent for minors on social media, and establishes enforcement mechanisms and a secure digital identification credential pilot program.
Long summary:
Requires social media platforms to verify the age of users beyond simple attestations, using current age verification technologies.

Prohibits social media platforms from allowing individuals under 13 from accessing their services.

Mandates parental or guardian consent for users under 18, requiring platforms to take reasonable steps to verify this consent.

Prohibits the use of algorithmic recommendation systems on individuals under 18, except for contextual suggestions or advertising.

Establishes a Pilot Program for secure digital identification credentials to facilitate age and parental consent verification, overseen by the Secretary of Commerce.

Empowers the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) and state attorneys general to enforce compliance, with civil penalties for violations.

Grants extraterritorial jurisdiction for violations involving individuals in the United States.

Official name: Transparent Automated Governance Act
Casual name: TAG Act
Short summary: Requires the Director to issue guidance on automated systems for critical decisions, including public notice and appeals processes. Obligates agencies to implement the guidance and mandates the Comptroller General to review compliance.
Long summary:
Requires the Director of the Office of Management and Budget to issue guidance for transparent automated governance, ensuring compliance with privacy, civil rights, and civil liberties protections.

Mandates agencies to disclose and allow appeals when utilizing automated systems for critical decisions affecting individuals’ rights or status.

Instructs agencies to design systems providing plain language notifications and establish appeals processes for decisions made using AI-augmented processes.

Obligates agencies to track the accuracy and reliability of AI systems, and share data with relevant authorities; encourages consultation with governmental and private sectors for guidance development.

Requires the Comptroller General to report on agency compliance, with reports submitted biennially.

Official name: Digital Defense Content Provenance Act of 2023
Casual name: Digital Defense Content Provenance Act
Short summary: Establishes a Defense Department education course on digital content provenance, training specialists on authentication standards and forgery risks. Launches a pilot program to test industry standards for verifying media authenticity in official DoD content, and requires reporting on progress, effectiveness, and potential permanence.
Long summary:
Requires the Department of Defense to establish a course at the Defense Information School to provide education on digital content provenance for DoD media content, covering challenges from digital content forgery, industry-open technical standards, and techniques for secure digital content documentation.

Mandates a report on the curriculum development and implementation plan for the course, including resource needs.

Initiates a pilot program to assess the feasibility of using industry-open technical standards for digital content provenance in DoD media.

Directs the pilot program to verify digital content provenance, apply technology solutions, assess historical records, and develop effectiveness measures.

Requires a report detailing findings, consultations, effectiveness, and recommendations on the pilot program's permanence.

Official name: Artificial Intelligence and the Information Privacy Principles
Casual name: Artificial Intelligence and the Information Privacy Principles
Short summary: Requires agencies using AI tools in New Zealand to conduct privacy impact assessments, ensure transparency, engage with Māori communities, and implement human review of AI outputs. Emphasizes compliance with the Privacy Act 2020 and its Information Privacy Principles.
Long summary:
Requires organizations in New Zealand using AI tools to comply with the Privacy Act 2020 and its Information Privacy Principles. 

Mandates senior leadership approval for AI tool usage and a privacy impact assessment (PIA) to evaluate privacy risks.

Demands transparency about AI tool usage, including engaging with Māori on potential impacts, assessing the necessity and proportionality of AI tools, and considering privacy implications.

Advises on proactive privacy measures, including developing privacy policies, engaging with affected communities, and evaluating training data provenance.

Highlights potential privacy issues with AI, such as automation blindness and overseas data disclosure risks.

Encourages organizations to secure personal information and comply with access, correction, and disclosure requirements.

Official name: Department of Energy Quantum Leadership Act of 2024, Section 2
Casual name: Department of Energy Quantum Leadership Act of 2024, Sec 2 (Quantum Information Science Research Program)
Short summary: Amends Section 401 of the National Quantum Initiative Act to require the Department of Energy to conduct quantum information science research and promote commercialization. Requires strategic plans for high-performance computing, cooperation with industry, and development of relevant supply chains. Prohibits funding partnerships with entities posing national security threats.
Long summary:
Amends the National Quantum Initiative Act to mandate the Secretary of Energy to conduct a program focusing on quantum information science research, development, and commercialization.

Operates National Quantum Information Science Research Centers to facilitate breakthroughs in quantum technologies.

Requires collaboration with industry, National Laboratories, and educational institutions for technology development.

Engages with the quantum technology industry to promote the commercialization of applications relevant to the Department of Energy's activities.

Submits a 10-year strategic plan to integrate quantum and AI with high-performance computing systems.

Establishes an early-stage quantum high-performance computing research program.

Conducts a study on the quantum supply chain to identify growth obstacles and recommends strategies to bolster domestic manufacturing and workforce development.

Launches a university-led traineeship program to develop the quantum workforce.

Coordinates activities with other federal entities to prevent duplication.

Restricts funding from foreign countries of concern and entities deemed harmful to U.S. national security.

Official name: Artificial Intelligence (Regulation) Act 2024
Casual name: Artificial Intelligence (Regulation) Act 2024
Short summary: Creates the AI Authority to oversee AI regulation, ensure safety, fairness, transparency, and compliance while supporting innovation, public engagement, and regulatory alignment across sectors. Businesses must disclose training data, ensure ethical AI use, allow independent audits, and comply with regulations, which may impose fines and require parliamentary approval.
Long summary:
Establishes the AI Authority to ensure regulators take accountability for AI, align regulatory approaches, review legislation, monitor regulatory frameworks, assess risks, support testbeds, and promote international interoperability.

Mandates the AI Authority to adhere to principles ensuring AI safety, transparency, fairness, accountability, compliance with laws, and inclusivity.

Requires the creation of regulatory sandboxes for testing AI innovations with consumer protections.

Obligates businesses using AI to appoint AI responsible officers to ensure ethical use and unbiased data.

Imposes transparency and IP obligations, requiring disclosing records of data used in AI training and independent audits.

Mandates the implementation of public engagement programs on AI opportunities and risks.

Official name: Candidate Voice Fraud Prohibition Act
Casual name: Candidate Voice Fraud Prohibition Act
Short summary: Amends the Federal Election Campaign Act to prohibit distributing AI-generated audio impersonating candidates' voices with intent to mislead voters, exempting certain media entities and satire. Imposes fines or imprisonment for violations, and requires the Federal Election Commission to issue regulations and reports on compliance.
Long summary:
Prohibits the distribution of political communications containing materially deceptive AI-generated audio that impersonates a candidate’s voice, with actual malice, intended to harm the candidate’s reputation or deceive voters.

Applies to communications distributed within 90 days of a general election or 60 days of a primary, special, or runoff election.

Exempts certain entities from liability such as radio or television stations that air deceptive AI-generated content as part of news coverage or communications that clearly constitute satire.

Defines "materially deceptive audio" as AI-generated impersonation of a candidate’s voice without approval and lacking a clear disclaimer.

Imposes criminal penalties of fines or up to 2 years imprisonment for individuals who knowingly and willfully violate the prohibition.

Requires the Federal Election Commission (FEC) to issue regulations to clarify disclaimer requirements and mandates the submission of annual compliance reports to Congress.

Official name: Integrating New Technologies to Empower Law Enforcement at Our Borders Act
Casual name: New Technologies at Borders Act
Short summary: Requires the Department of Homeland Security to plan integrating AI and emerging technologies for border awareness. Specifies assessments, cost estimates, capability gaps, and coordination with other agencies. Emphasizes transitioning new technologies and incentivizing private sector development.
Long summary:
Requires the Department of Homeland Security to develop a comprehensive plan within 180 days to identify, deploy, and integrate emerging and advanced technologies to enhance situational awareness along the northern and southern borders of the United States.

Mandates the inclusion of artificial intelligence, machine learning, automation, fiber-optic sensing, nanotechnology, optical and cognitive radar, modeling and simulation technology, hyperspectral and LIDAR sensors, and other similar technologies.

Includes an assessment of current technology utilization by U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) and how capability gaps can be addressed through technology integration.

Long summary:
Defines Artificial Intelligence

Creates a program called ‘‘AI Center of Excellence’’, (CoE) which shall facilitate the adoption of AI technologies in the Federal Government and improve cohesion in the adoption of artificial intelligence.

Stipulates certain functions of this program which includes; 
a.  Collaborating with individual agencies, federal laboratories, nonprofit organizations, institutions and other entities to discuss recent developments in AI.

b. Assisting agencies in applying Federal policies on the management and use of data in the use of AI.

c. Consulting with agencies, including the Department of Defense, the Department of Commerce, the Department of Energy, the Department of Homeland Security, the Office of Management and Budget, the Office of the Director of National Intelligence, and the National Science Foundation, on issues relating to AI.

Official name: Research and Development, Competition, and Innovation Act, Title III ("National Science Foundation for the Future")
Casual name: Research and Development, Competition, and Innovation Act, Title III ("National Science Foundation for the Future")
Short summary: Provides scholarships and fellowships for AI-related studies to recruit and train the next generation of AI professionals in US government. Grants competitive grants and fellowships for organizations advancing risk assessment through AI. Adds AI and machine learning to the list of research areas that the Director of the National Science Foundation can award grants to. Makes awards available for supporting basic research to advance critical minerals mining strategies that involve AI. Requires the development and testing of models to inform implementation of government-wide data infrastructure for statistical activities. Requires a study on U.S. higher education's AI research capacity and an annual update to key technology focus areas.
Long summary:
Instructs the Director of the National Science Foundation (Director) to develop a report on the need and feasibility as well as construct plans to implement a program to recruit and train the next generation of AI professionals in US government. Mandates that the report include statistical data on the federal AI workforce, assessments of current and future demand for AI expertise across the federal government and the ability of higher education institutions to prepare students to meet this demand, and an evaluation of the need for and feasibility of establishing a scholarship-for-service program for AI professionals in US government, as well as the possibility of leveraging existing programs such as the Federal Cyber Scholarship-for-Service Program. 

Authorizes the Director and other appropriate agency heads to establish a Federal AI scholarship-for-service program to recruit and train AI professionals to achieve the missions of US government. Requires the Director, in coordination with other agency heads, to establish criteria such as excellence in AI education and the ability to attract diverse students to designate qualified institutions of higher education that shall be eligible to participate in the program. Requires the program to provide scholarships through qualified higher education institutions to students pursuing fields of study related to AI, provide scholarship recipients with temporary appointments related to work on AI in the federal government, prioritize placement of scholarship recipients in executive agencies, identify opportunities to promote multidisciplinary programs of study, support capacity building for educational institutions to enable them to train the next-generation AI workforce, create courses in technology ethics for scholarship recipients, and award fellowships to masters and doctoral students who are pursuing degrees or research in AI and related fields. Instructs scholarship amounts to cover a student’s tuition and fees for not more than 3 years with an additional stipend, and obligates scholarship recipients to work for a period of time equivalent to the length of the scholarship in the AI mission of the US government entity or affiliated entity.

Instructs the Director to establish competitive awards to institutions to advance knowledge of risk assessment including the utilization of AI in our ability to understand and predict extreme events and natural hazards, create engineered system solutions and develop instrumentation for innovation in resilient infrastructure, and advance research on multidisciplinary wildfire science and behaviors related to increasing resilience.

Amends Section 506(a) of the America COMPETES Reauthorization Act of 2010 by adding AI and machine learning to the list of research areas that fall under information technologies for manufacturing that the Director can award grants to.

Instructs the Director to make competitive awards to institutions to support basic research to advance critical minerals mining strategies to make better use of domestic resources and eliminate national reliance on minerals subject to supply chain disruptions, including the application of AI for geological exploration of critical minerals and the application of machine learning for detection and sorting of critical minerals.

Requires the Director to conduct or support the development of a study on AI research capacity at US institutions of higher education. Requires the study to, at minimum, address which universities are putting out significant peer-reviewed AI research based on quantity and number of citations, which factors of these universities such as computing power enable their research, promising practices at these universities for advancing diversity, equity, and inclusion in AI research programs, geographic diversity of universities that have AI research-enabling factors, and how universities that are not currently top producers of AI research could improve, including potential pilot programs that the federal government could develop or support to help universities produce AI research.

Instructs the Director, in consultation with the Director of the Office of Management and Budget and the interagency committee established under the National AI Initiative Act of 2020, to develop and test models to inform the implementation of a government-wide data linkage and access infrastructure for statistical activities.

Requires the Director to identify, and annually review and update a list of not more than 10 key technology focus areas, which includes AI, machine learning, autonomy, and related advances.

Casual name: Research and Development, Competition, and Innovation Act, Title II ("National Institute of Standards and Technology for the Future"), Subtitle B ("Measurement Research")

Casual name: Research and Development, Competition, and Innovation Act, Title I ("Department of Energy Science for the Future")

Efforts on scholarships, fellowships, and ongoing research to nurture the next generation of artificial intelligence professionals and enhance biometric identification technologies.

Instructs the Secretary of the Advanced Computing Program to maintain foundational research programs in mathematical and computational sciences, including new and emerging computing needs within the mission of the Department like novel approaches to modelling and simulation, artificial intelligence, and machine learning.

Instructs the Director to carry out research and testing to improve the effectiveness of artificial intelligence-enabled cybersecurity.

Official name: 21 CFR § 864.3750 ("Software algorithm device to assist users in digital pathology")
Casual name: 21 CFR § 864.3750 ("Software algorithm device to assist users in digital pathology")
Collections: U.S. regulations, executive orders, and agency policies
Short summary: Requires a software algorithm device for assisting users in digital pathology -- an in vitro device intended to evaluate acquired scanned pathology images to assist in diagnosis -- to be subject to special control from the U.S. Food and Drug Administration. Those special controls require detailed product labeling; device performance assessments meeting specific standards; a detailed description of the device's software; and a risk management plan, including measures to protect cybersecurity.
Long summary:
Classifies a software algorithm device to assist users in digital pathology -- an in vitro device intended to evaluate acquired scanned pathology whole slide images to assist in diagnosis -- into the U.S. Food and Drug Administration's class II, requiring special controls. That set of special controls:

Requires detailed product labeling, including instructions for use and notices of the device's limitations.

Requires performance assessments of the device that meets pre-set standards for rigor, evaluate the device's performance when used by its intended users, and cover the entire spectrum of challenging cases likely to be encountered in the device's operation. 

Requires a detailed description of the workings of the device's software.

Requires a risk management plan, including mechanisms to mitigate cybersecurity risks.

Official name: 21 CFR § 882.1491 ("Pediatric Autism Spectrum Disorder diagnosis aid")
Casual name: 21 CFR § 882.1491 ("Pediatric Autism Spectrum Disorder diagnosis aid")
Collections: U.S. regulations, executive orders, and agency policies
Short summary: Classifies a machine-learning software program and device used in the diagnosis of Autism Spectrum Disorder into the U.S. Food and Drug Administration's class II, which mandates clinical performance testing; software verification, validation, and hazard analysis; usability assessment; and detailed product labeling.
Long summary:
Classifies the Cognoa ASD Diagnosis Aid, a machine-learning software program and device used in the diagnosis of Autism Spectrum Disorder, into the U.S. Food and Drug Administration's class II regulatory category requiring special controls. The mandated special controls are as follows:

Requires clinical performance testing to demonstrate that the device performs as intended under anticipated conditions of use, including an evaluation of sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value, and negative predictive value using a reference method of diagnosis and assessment of patient behavioral symptomology.

Requires software verification, validation, and hazard analysis to be provided for the device. Software documentation must include a detailed, technical description of the algorithm(s) used to generate device output(s), and a cybersecurity assessment of the impact of threats and vulnerabilities on device functionality and user(s).

Requires a usability assessment that must demonstrate that the intended user(s) can safely and correctly use the device.

Requires detailed product labeling that includes instructions for the device's use and a summary of any clinical testing done to demonstrate the device's functionality.

Requires disclosure of pre-specified performance testing protocols used to assess whether the device will improve reader performance as intended.

Requires disclosure of results from performance testing that follows pre-specified protocols.

Requires manufacturers to provide appropriate software documentation including device hazard analysis, software design specs, traceability analysis, and cybersecurity features.

Mandates labeling that details compatible imaging hardware and imaging protocols, as well as the intended reading protocol.

Official name: 21 CFR § 864.3750 ("Adjunctive hemodynamic indicator with decision point")
Casual name: 21 CFR § 864.3750 ("Adjunctive hemodynamic indicator with decision point")
Collections: U.S. regulations, executive orders, and agency policies
Short summary: Subjects the adjunctive hemodynamic indicator with decision point -- a device that monitors medical conditions relating to blood flow -- to special regulatory controls, including mandating a risk assessment and the reporting of clinical data proving the devices's efficacy.
Long summary:
Classifies the adjunctive hemodynamic indicator with decision point -- a device that monitors medical conditions relating to blood flow -- into the Food & Drug Administration's class II, requiring special controls. That set of controls:

Requires a full description of the technical parameters of the device's software and sensors.

Requires a risk assessment for the device.

Requires mechanisms designed to mitigate the impact of user error or failure of any subsystem components (signal detection and analysis, data display, and storage) on the accuracy of the device's output.

Requires statistical documentation, based on robust clinical data, of the device's efficacy. The accuracy of the device's algorithm must be tested using an independent dataset. 

Requires a usability assessment to demonstrate that the risk of misinterpretation of the status indicator is appropriately mitigated.

Requires detailed product labeling.

Official name: 15 CFR part 7 ("Securing the Information and Communications Technology and Services Supply Chain")
Casual name: 15 CFR part 7 ("Securing the Information and Communications Technology and Services Supply Chain")
Collections: U.S. regulations, executive orders, and agency policies
Short summary: Establishes procedures by which the Secretary of Commerce may evaluate, direct, and/or prohibit information and communications technology or service (ICTS) transactions with foreign adversaries. Relevant ICTS include drones or any other unmanned aerial systems, autonomous systems, and artificial intelligence and machine learning software.
Long summary:
Establishes procedures by which the Secretary of Commerce may determine whether any information and communications technology or service (ICTS) transaction that has been produced or supplied by foreign adversaries poses certain undue or unacceptable cybersecurity risks. Relevant ICTS include drones or any other unmanned aerial systems, autonomous systems, and artificial intelligence and machine learning software.

Establishes procedures by which the Secretary may prohibit and/or direct the timing and manner of the cessation of an ICTS transaction.

Establishes procedures by which the Secretary may consider factors that may mitigate the risks posed by the ICTS transaction. 

Details penalties for entities that violate any issued final determination or direction.

Casual name: 21 CFR § 870.2210 ("Adjunctive predictive cardiovascular indicator")
Collections: U.S. regulations, executive orders, and agency policies
Short summary: Requires assessments of the risks and efficacy of algorithms used in adjunctive predictive cardiovascular indicators to analyze cardiovascular vital signs and predict future cardiovascular health.
Long summary:
Requires that adjunctive predictive cardiovascular indicators -- medical devices used to predict cardiovascular health -- be subject to special controls in the U.S. Those controls include the following:

Requires a risk assessment of the device, including full disclosure of software specs and different inputs considered when making predictions about future cardiovascular health.

Requires scientific justification for the device's efficacy, including verification of algorithm calculations and validation using an independent data set.

Requires a human factors and usability engineering assessment to evaluate the risk of misinterpretation of device output.

Requires a clinical data assessment that specifies the clinical data used and an evaluation of output consistency.

Official name: 21 CFR § 870.2785 ("Software for optical camera-based measurement of pulse rate, heart rate, breathing rate, and/or respiratory rate")
Casual name: 21 CFR § 870.2785 ("Software for optical camera-based measurement of pulse rate, heart rate, breathing rate, and/or respiratory rate")
Collections: U.S. regulations, executive orders, and agency policies
Short summary: Classifies software for optical camera-based measurement of pulse rate, heart rate, breathing rate, and/or respiratory rate into the U.S. Food and Drug Administration's class II, requiring special controls. Those controls require comprehensive hazard analysis and risk assessment; clinical testing data demonstrating the software's efficacy; a usability assessment; and detailed product labeling.
Long summary:
Classifies the Oxehealth Vital Signs device -- which uses software to enable camera-based measurement of pulse rate, heart rate, breathing rate, and/or respiratory rate -- into the U.S. Food and Drug Administration's class II, requiring special controls. The set of special controls:

Requires a full characterization of the technical parameters of the device's software, as well as documentation of a cybersecurity vulnerability and management process to assure software functionality.

Requires the results of verification and validation testing based on a comprehensive hazard analysis and risk assessment of the software and associated device, to include a description of all mitigations for user error or failure of any subsystem components (including signal detection, signal analysis, data display, and storage) on output accuracy.

Requires clinical data demonstrating the software and device's efficacy, including comparing the device's output with a clinically accurate patient-contacting relevant comparator device in an accurate and reproducible manner.

Requires a human factors and usability engineering assessment that evaluates the risk of improper measurement.

Requires detailed product labeling, to include instructions for using the device and interpreting its output; warnings of factors that may impede proper functionality; and a description of the measurements the device takes.

Official name: Limited Applicability of Consumer Financial Protection Act's “Time or Space” Exception With Respect to Digital Marketing Providers
Casual name: Limited Applicability of Consumer Financial Protection Act's “Time or Space” Exception With Respect to Digital Marketing Providers
Collections: U.S. regulations, executive orders, and agency policies
Short summary: Clarifies Section 1002 of the Consumer Financial Protection Act of 2010's definition of "service provider" to include digital marketing providers that use algorithmic models or other analytics to commingle the targeting and delivery of advertisements to consumers.
Long summary:
Clarifies Section 1002 of the Consumer Financial Protection Act of 2010's definition of "service provider" to include digital marketing providers that use algorithmic models or other analytics to commingle the targeting and delivery of advertisements to consumers, rendering those providers subject to the Act's prohibition on unfair, deceptive, or abusive acts or practices.

Outlines the ways in which digital marketers provide additional services beyond "time or space" and are materially involved in the development of content strategy -- often through the use of targeting and delivering algorithms -- thus qualifying them as "service providers" under the law.

Outlines specific circumstances in which digital marketers are and are not considered service providers under the CFPA; they qualify as service providers when using algorithms and data to target and deliver advertisements with certain characteristics, or when using algorithms and business-specific data to determine when to display specific advertisements to specific consumers.

Official name: 21 CFR § 870.2345 ("Electrocardiograph software for over-the-counter use")
Casual name: 21 CFR § 870.2345 ("Electrocardiograph software for over-the-counter use")
Collections: U.S. regulations, executive orders, and agency policies
Short summary: Labels over-the-counter use of electrocardiograph software devices as Class II (special controls), which requires manufactures to conduct hazard analysis of detection algorithms in the devices.
Long summary:
Labels over-the-counter use of electrocardiograph software devices as Class II (special controls), which requires manufactures to verify, validate, and conduct hazard analysis of detection algorithms in the devices.

Mandates manufactures to produce clinical performance testing results that shows the performance characteristics of the detection algorithm by sensitivity and either specificity or positive predictive value.

Requires manufacturers who fail to conduct non-clinical performance testing to validate detection algorithm performance using a previously adjudicated data set.

Requires human factors and usability testing to demonstrate that the user can correctly interpret the device output and understand when to seek medical care.

Also clarifies that contributions made by a human to content that was created with the assistance of AI remain eligible for copyright protection.

Describes various situations in which content generated with the assistance of AI would, and would not, qualify for copyright protection. 

Instructs copyright applicants on how to apply for copyright protection for content generated with the assistance of AI.

Official name: An act to add Section 11546.45.5 to the Government Code, relating to automated decision systems
Casual name: California AB 302 (2023)
Short summary: Requires the California Department of Technology to analyze the benefits and risks of all high-risk automated decision systems that are in use by, or have been proposed for use by, state agencies.
Long summary:
Requires the California Department of Technology, in coordination with other agencies, to analyze the benefits and risks of all high-risk automated decision systems that have been proposed for use, development, or procurement by, or are being used, developed, or procured by, state agencies. 

Requires the analysis to include a description of, among other things, the categories of data and personal information the automated decision system uses to make its decisions. 

Requires the Department of Technology to submit an annual report on the above-described comprehensive analysis to specified committees of the California Legislature.

Official name: An act to amend and reenact subsection 8 of section 1-01-49 of the North Dakota Century Code, relating to the definition of person; and to declare an emergency
Casual name: North Dakota HB 1361 (2023)
Short summary: Clarifies that artificial intelligence cannot qualify as a "person" for the purpose of North Dakota state law.
Long summary: Clarifies that artificial intelligence cannot qualify as a "person" for the purpose of North Dakota state law.

Official name: An act to amend Chapter 12 of Title 31 of the Official Code of Georgia Annotated, relating to the control of hazardous conditions, preventable diseases, and metabolic disorders [...]
Casual name: Georgia House Bill 203 (2023)
Short summary: Regulates the use of artificial intelligence and other technology in eye assessments for medical purposes, including by requiring medical professionals to analyze any data collected or generated by automated systems.
Long summary:
Requires all eye assessments that utilize artificial intelligence or other advanced technology to conform to the traditional standards of in-person care.

Requires a medical professional to analyze any information collected or generated by an automated system during the eye assessment process.

Requires all eye assessments to involve the collection of a patient's relevant medical information, which must be stored in a medical record for at least 7 years.

Official name: An Act Concerning Artificial Intelligence, Automated Decision-Making and Personal Data Privacy
Casual name: Artificial Intelligence, Automated Decision-Making and Personal Data Privacy Act
Short summary: Requires the state government to establish a public inventory of its use of artificial intelligence systems and to mandate impact assessments of its AI systems to prevent unlawful discrimination; establishes a working group to make recommendations on the state government's use of AI systems
Long summary:
Requires the Department of Administrative Services to establish a public inventory of state agencies' existing use of AI systems; this inventory should include whether the system was employed to independently make or inform decisions, and whether it undergone an impact assessment before implementation

Requires the Office of Policy and Management to develop policies to govern state agencies' procurement and use of AI systems; this includes requiring state agencies to conduct impact assessments for these systems and to ensure systems do not unlawfully discriminate against individuals or groups

Bans state agencies from implementing any AI system unless it had performed an impact assessment to prevent unlawful discrimination, effective February 1 2024

Requires the Judicial Department to establish a public inventory of its systems that employ AI, including whether the AI system had undergone impact assessments and was used to make or support conclusions; also requires the department to introduce policies to govern the use of AI, and to prevent unlawful discrimination

Establishes a working group comprising industry representatives and academics to engage stakeholders and experts and make recommendations on the state government's use of AI; this includes recommendations on the adoption of a Connecticut artificial intelligence bill of rights based on the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy's 'Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights'
