Official name: WIOA Performance Accountability Improvement Act, Section 2, Subsection (c) ("Evaluation of State programs.")
Casual name: WIOA Performance Accountability Improvement Act, Section 2, Subsection (c) ("Evaluation of State programs.")
Short summary: Amends the Workforce Innovation and Opportunity Act to permit states to use machine learning and advanced analytics for improving program operations, outcomes, and evaluation, enhancing data-driven decision-making in state programs.
Long summary:
Amends the Workforce Innovation and Opportunity Act to require states to use data for analyses and ongoing evaluations of programs.

Allows states to use machine learning or other advanced analytics to enhance program operations, outcomes, and identify areas needing further evaluation.

Official name: Sustaining America’s Fisheries for the Future Act of 2024, Title IV, Section 401 ("Data modernization.")
Casual name: Sustaining America’s Fisheries for the Future Act of 2024, Title IV, Section 401 ("Data modernization.")
Short summary: Aligns fisheries data modernization with NOAA's AI Strategy for efficiency.
Long summary:
Aligns the national strategic plan for fisheries data with the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration's (NOAA) Artificial Intelligence Strategy to enhance efficiency and interoperability.

Requires the Secretary, through the Assistant Administrator for Fisheries, to provide a strategic plan to Congress within 180 days, detailing goals, implementation schedules, budgets, stakeholder engagement, and system development.

Solicits expertise from data management and technology experts both within and outside the government to support the strategic plan.

Mandates the Secretary to report annually to Congress on progress in implementing the fisheries data modernization efforts.

Official name: Better Mental Health Care for Americans Act, Sec. 203
Casual name: Better Mental Health Care for Americans Act, Sec. 203
Short summary: Amends the Social Security Act to require behavioral health information disclosure, including provider access, wait times, payment statistics, and denial data. Specifies AI and technology use in denial processes. Aims to enhance transparency and informed choice in behavioral health coverage.
Long summary:
Amends Section 1851(d)(4) of the Social Security Act to include provisions for behavioral health information starting in 2025.

Requires the provision of information on access to in-network behavioral health providers, including average wait times for new patient appointments and data on provider participation agreements.

Mandates disclosure of payment request statistics for mental health or substance use disorder services, specifying the percentage handled by in-network versus out-of-network providers.

Requires reporting on the number and percentage of denials for prior authorization or payment for mental health services compared to non-mental health services, categorized by denial type and service type.

Specifies the use of decision support, AI, machine learning, clinical decision-making, or other technologies in denial processes, with comparisons to similar physical health conditions.

Aims to promote informed choice by enhancing transparency in behavioral health service coverage.

Official name: Better Mental Health Care for Americans Act, Sec. 201
Casual name: Better Mental Health Care for Americans Act, Sec. 201
Short summary: Amends Medicare Advantage plans to ensure mental health and substance use benefits parity. Mandates standards-based necessity determination and NQTL analyses. Imposes penalties for non-compliance. Extends parity to drug plans. Directs regulation issuance within 18 months. Allocates $10 million for implementation.
Long summary:
Amends Medicare Advantage plans to require parity in mental health and substance use disorder benefits, ensuring financial and treatment limitations are no more restrictive than those for medical benefits.

Mandates MA organizations to determine medical necessity for mental health and substance use benefits based on generally accepted standards.

Requires MA organizations to conduct and submit comparative analyses of nonquantitative treatment limitations (NQTLs) for mental health and substance use disorder benefits.

Imposes penalties for failing to comply with the mental health parity requirements.

Extends parity provisions to prescription drug plans, ensuring consistent application across Medicare Advantage and prescription drug plans.

Directs the Secretary of Health and Human Services to issue implementing regulations within 18 months.

Sets the effective date for amendments to plan years beginning two years post-enactment, with $10 million allocated for implementation funding through the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services.

Official name: Supercomputing for Safer Chemicals Act
Casual name: SUPERSAFE Act
Short summary: Directs the Administrator of the Environmental Protection Agency to establish a consortium that identifies toxic substances and develops safer alternatives through computational toxicology methods based on AI and machine learning.
Long summary:
Directs the Administrator of the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) to establish a consortium representing federal, state, and academic stakeholders. The consortium will use supercomputing and machine learning to develop models that predict adverse health effects from toxic substances, establishing rapid approaches for large-scale identification of toxic substances and the development of safer alternatives.

Requires the Consortium to recommend how the information produced can be used in risk assessments by the EPA, other federal agencies, and industry.

Appropriates funding to the EPA Administrator to carry out the Consortium and requires the Consortium to make its model predictions public and accessible.
Tags: Harms: Harm to health/safety;Strategies: Government study or report;Strategies: Government support;Strategies: Convening;Strategies: New institution;Applications: Medicine, life sciences and public health

Official name: Block Nuclear Launch by Autonomous Artificial Intelligence Act of 2023
Casual name: Block Nuclear Launch by Autonomous Artificial Intelligence Act
Short summary: Bans the use of federal funds to operate autonomous weapons systems which launch nuclear weapons or select targets for nuclear weapons without meaningful human control.
Long summary:
Prohibits the use of federal funds to operate autonomous weapons systems which launch nuclear weapons, or select or engage targets for nuclear weapons, without meaningful human control.

Defines 'meaningful human control' as the ability for humans to control the selection and engagement of targets, as well as the time, location and manner of the use of nuclear weapons.
Tags: Risk factors: Safety;Harms: Harm to health/safety;Harms: Financial loss;Harms: Harm to property;Harms: Harm to infrastructure;Harms: Ecological harm;Applications: Government: military and public safety

Official name: Creating Resources for Every American To Experiment with Artificial Intelligence Act of 2023
Casual name: CREATE AI Act of 2023
Short summary: Establishes the National AI Research Resource to provide computational resources, data, educational tools, and testbeds for AI researchers.
Long summary:
Establishes a steering committee to oversee the NAIRR.

Authorizes the National Science Foundation, in coordination with the NAIRR steering committee, to establish the National AI Research Resource.

Creates a NAIRR Program Management Office to manage the NAIRR's day-to-day operations.

Directs the Program Management Office to solicit bids for an Operating Entity for the NAIRR and to oversee that Entity.

Provides for the NAIRR to be resourced with computational resources, data, educational tools and services, and AI testbeds.

Specifies eligibility for access to resources in the NAIRR, including academic, nonprofit, government, and small businesses, among others.

Provides for procedures to establish privacy, ethical, safety and scientific integrity requirements for users of the NAIRR.

Authorizes the NAIRR to charge fees for users at various tiers, including free and subsidized tiers.

Provides for security requirements to prevent unauthorized access and protect research security.

Casual name: FY2021 NDAA

Official name: Governance Principles for a New Generation of Artificial Intelligence: Develop Responsible Artificial Intelligence
Casual name: Governance Principles for a New Generation of Artificial Intelligence
Short summary: Provides eight aspirational principles for the responsible development of artificial intelligence, stressing the importance of fairness, security, privacy, and amicable cooperation.
Long summary: Provides eight aspirational principles for the responsible global development of artificial intelligence: harmony and friendliness, fairness and justice, inclusivity and sharing, respect of privacy, safety and controllability, shared responsibility, open collaboration, and agile governance.

Official name: Department of Defense Directive 3000.09 ("Autonomy in weapon systems")
Casual name: DoDD 3000.09 ("Autonomy in weapon systems")
Collections: Editors' Picks;U.S. regulations, executive orders, and agency policies
Short summary: Establishes Department of Defense policy on the use of autonomous and semi-autonomous weapons systems, including testing & evaluation requirements and requirements that humans be able to intervene should such weaponry begin to operate in unintended ways.
Long summary:
Establishes Department of Defense policy on the development and use of autonomous and semi-autonomous weapons systems, including armed platforms that are remotely operated or operated by onboard personnel. Those policies include:

Requires  testing and evaluation requirements that must be met before a weapons system is deployed, including testing designed to ensure that weapons systems operate as intended even when subject to actions from adversaries. Outlines requirements that humans be able to intervene should weapons systems begin to operate in unintended ways. Establishes guidelines for the use of autonomous weapons systems designed to mitigate the probability and consequences of weapons systems acting in unintended ways.

Official name: William M. (Mac) Thornberry National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2021, Division A, Title XCIV, Section 9412 ("Industries of the future")
Casual name: Industries of the Future Act of 2020
Short summary: Focuses on investing and developing new industries of the future which are regarded as Artificial Intelligence and quantum information science.  Creates a new council to enforce these new laws. Assigns responsibilities to the President and terminates the act 6 years after implementation.
Long summary:
Provides a multi-step plan in order to ensure that the United States will "continue to lead the world in developing emerging technologies".
Furthers the national interests and security goals of the nation.
Promotes collaboration between existing and new depraments.
Requires the Director of the Office of Science and Technology Policy to submit a detailed report regarding current investments, research and development projects, and more regarding the US mission to be involved in the development of creating the industries of the future. 
Defines the industries specifically
Outlines a plan to increase funding and investments to developments in Artificial Intelligence and Quantum Information Science to 10 billion dollars a year by the year 2025.
Instructs the President to create a council called the Industries of the Future Coordination Council to support and guide the Director.
Outlines the membership guidelines and duties of the councils to uphold the objective that the US will lead the world in technology.
Specifies the termination date of the policy to be 6 years from enactment, on Jan 1, 2027.

Casual name: Internet Information Service Algorithmic Recommendation Management Provisions
Short summary: Regulates algorithmic recommendation services in China, emphasizing security, transparency, and rights of users. Mandates providers prevent harmful information dissemination, protect user rights, and ensure algorithmic accountability, with penalties for violations.

Official name: An Act concerning discrimination and automated decision systems and supplementing P.L.1945, c.169 (C.10:5-1 et seq.).
Casual name: New Jersey SB1402
Short summary: Prohibits financial lenders, insurance companies and healthcare providers from using automated decision systems to discriminate against protected classes in providing services.
Long summary:
Bans banks, mortgage companies and other lending institutions from using AI systems which discriminate against members of a protected class; systems are deemed discriminatory if these disproportionately favor or disfavor members of a protected class in the provision of loans and credit terms.

Bans insurance companies from using AI systems which discriminate against members of a protected class; systems are deemed discriminatory if these disproportionately favor or disfavor members of a protected class in providing insurance coverage and setting the terms and rates of such coverage.

Bans health care providers from using AI systems to discriminate against members of a protected class; systems are deemed discriminatory if these disproportionately favor or disfavor members of a protected class in assessing eligibility for healthcare services.

Categorizes the above use of AI systems which discriminate against members of protected classes as unlawful discrimination; such companies and service providers are subject to fines not exceeding $10,000 on the first instance, if these companies have not committed prior violations within five years of the charge, and permits victims to file civil lawsuits against such companies.

Official name: State of Arizona Senate Bill 1565, An Act Amending Sections 16-442, 16-552 and 16-621, Arizona Revised Statutes; Relating to Conduct of Elections.
Casual name: Bill limiting use of AI in elections
Short summary: Bans the use of artificial intelligence in counting, processing, or adjudicating ballots, and in verifying voters' affidavits, in Arizona.
Long summary: Bans the use of artificial intelligence in the hardware or software used to (a) count, process, or adjudicate ballots, or (b) verify voters' affidavits, in Arizona.

Official name:
An act to amend the labor law, in relation to establishing criteria for
the use of automated employment decision tools
Casual name: Automated Employment Decision Tools Amendment to NY Labor Law
Short summary: Makes it unlawful for an employer in New York to use or implement an automated decision tool to  filter employment candidates or prospective candidates for hire that has not undergone a disparate impact assessment annually.
Long summary:
Makes it unlawful for an employer in New York to use or implement an automated decision tool to filter employment candidates or prospective candidates for hire that has not undergone an disparate impact assessment annually.

Requires employers to provide the department with a summary of their most recent disparate impact analysis annually. Allows the Attorney General and Commissioner to initiate investigations and legal action if violations are suspected or found. Requires a summary of the most recent disparate impact analysis of the automated decision tool and the distribution date of the tool to be made publicly available on the website of the employer prior to implementing the tool.

Official name: An Act preventing a dystopian work environment.
Casual name: An Act preventing a dystopian work environment.
Short summary: Mandates that Massachusetts employers inform workers of the use of automated decision systems, requires impact assessments on such systems, and sets conditions under which they can be used.
Long summary:
Mandates that employers inform workers of data collection and distribution, including inputs for Automated Decision Systems (ADS), bans employers from collecting data for non-necessary business purposes, and mandates security measures to protect worker data.

Requires employers to inform workers before ADS use and annually provide workers with notice of all ADS in use, bans ADS use for employment decisions where there is potential for bias and/or discrimination, and requires third-party impact assessments to be conducted on such systems before and after use.

Official name: An Act to amend the labor law, in relation to automated employment decision tools
Casual name: New York AB A7859
Short summary: Requires employers and employment agencies to notify job candidates when automated employment decision tools are used to screen candidates.
Long summary:
Requires employers and employment agencies that use automated employment decision tools to inform job applicants:
(1) That such a tool is being used to screen candidates.
(2) Of which characteristics and qualifications the automated tool considers.
(3) Of what data is collected by the automated tool, where it is sourced, and how it is managed.

Defines "automated employment decision tool" as any computational process that produces an output which is used to substantially assist or replace discretionary decision-making for employment decisions that impact natural persons.

Official name: An Act concerning the use of automated tools to assist with hiring decisions and supplementing Title 34 of the Revised Statutes.
Casual name: New Jersey act against discrimination in automated hiring
Short summary: Mandates automated decision tools sold in New Jersey to undergo a bias audit before sale, offer annual free audits, and disclose these requirements; it also requires users of these tools to notify screened candidates within 30 days. Imposes civil penalties (fines) for violations.
Long summary:
Prohibits the sale of automated decision tools in New Jersey unless they have been subject to a bias audit in the past year, include a free annual bias audit service that provides results to the purchaser, and discloses that the tool is subject to this bill.

Requires anyone who uses an automated employment decision tool to screen candidates to notify each candidate of using the tool within 30 days.

Provides for collecting civil penalties (fines) if the provisions above are violated.

Official name:
An Act to amend the general business law, in relation to the management
 and oversight of personal data (Section 7)
Casual name: New York Data Privacy Act, Section 7
Short summary: Requires entities that decline access to important services (e.g. healthcare and financing) on the basis of an AI system's decision to evaluate the system for accuracy & discrimination, to disclose the system's use, and to enable affected individuals to appeal the decision to a human being.
Long summary:
Requires any entity in New York that uses AI or another automated process as the sole basis of a decision that denies access to financial services, insurance, housing, public accommodation, healthcare, or basic necessities to:

(a) Clearly and conspicuously disclose that the decision was made solely on the basis of an automated process; and

(b) Enable any affected individual to appeal the decision, including by giving them the right to have a human review it.

Requires the above entities, and additionally those that use an automated decision systems to determine access to employment and education, to annually solicit an outside auditor to conduct, and make publicly available, an impact assessment of the system that:

(a) Evaluates the system's objectives, design, and training data, as well as how the system was tested for accuracy and discrimination; and 

(b) Assesses whether the system produces discriminatory results on the basis of race, color, ethnicity, religion, national origin, sex, gender, gender identity, sexual orientation, familial status, biometric information, lawful source of income, or disability.

Official name: An act to add Chapter 25 (commencing with Section 22756) to Division 8 of the Business and Professions Code, relating to artificial intelligence.
Casual name: AB-331 Automated decision tools
Short summary: Governs the use of artificial intelligence for making consequential decisions that affect others (on education, healthcare, etc.), including by banning tools that exhibit algorithmic discrimination and requiring impact assessments, disclosures, monitoring, and human alternatives.
Long summary:
Defines an automated decision tool as any tool that uses artificial intelligence to make consequential decisions relating to employment, education, housing, utilities, healthcare and family planning, financial services, criminal justice, legal services, voting, or government benefits.

Bans the use of automated decision tools in ways that result in algorithmic discrimination on the basis of classes protected by state law. 

Requires any entity that develops or deploys an automated decision tool to perform an impact assessment on the tool, covering its purpose, outputs, use cases, inputted data, and potential discriminatory impacts, and the monitoring, safeguard measures, and testing conducted to mitigate adverse consequences. The impact assessment must be updated annually and after any significant update to the tool, and be provided to the state's Civil Rights Department. 

Requires any entity that deploys an automated decision tool to provide individuals affected by the tool's decisions with a notice that the tool was used and information on the tool's purpose. 

Grants all individuals subject to an automated decision tool's decisions the right to instead have those decisions be made by a human, if technically feasible. 

Requires automated decision tool developers to provide the tool's deployers with documentation of the tool's limitations, training data, and testing. 

Requires the developer or deployer of an automated decision tool to implement a program that governs the foreseeable risks of the tool's use, including designating an employee to monitor the tool and report concerns. 

Permits individuals and public attorneys to bring civil action against entities that violate this law, and makes violation of the law punishable by fines.

Exempts entities with fewer than 25 employees using automated decision tools that affect less than 1,000 people per year from some of this law's provisions.

Official name: A Bill in the District of Columbia to prohibit users of algorithmic decision-making from utilizing algorithmic eligibility determinations in a discriminatory manner, to require corresponding notices to individuals whose personal information is used, and to provide for appropriate means of civil enforcement.
Casual name: Stop Discrimination by Algorithms Act of 2023
Short summary: Prohibits algorithms used to determine access to important opportunities (e.g., employment) or information about them in Washington D.C. from discriminating on the basis of protected classes; also mandates related algorithm audits, disclosures, and remedies for decisions made based on inaccurate data.
Long summary:
Prohibits entities in Washington D.C. from determining eligibility for, or access to information about, "important life opportunities" (e.g., housing or employment) on the basis of race, color, religion, national origin, sex, gender identity or expression, sexual orientation, familial status, source of income, or disability in a discriminatory manner.

Requires entities to provide a public notice describing any algorithmic tools they use to determine eligibility for, or access to information about, important life opportunities. The notice must be sent to any individual about whom such a determination is made.

Requires the notice described above to cover the data collected by algorithmic tools and how that data is shared externally, how that data informs the tool's decisions, and the rights provided under this act.

Requires entities to provide notice, to any individual against whom they take any adverse action (e.g., firing) on the basis of an algorithmic determination, that the individual may access and submit corrections to any personal information considered by the algorithm. A human must then redo the decision based on the corrected information.  

Requires entities to audit their algorithmic decision-making tools before deployment and annually thereafter to analyze discriminatory effects and other impacts, and then implement reasonable measures to address findings of unlawful disparate impact. Entities must submit a report on the audit to the DC government.

Permits affected individuals or the DC Attorney General to file a civil action in response to violations of this law, with fines up to $10,000 per violation.

Official name: Artificial Intelligence for California Research Act
Casual name: Artificial Intelligence for California Research Act
Short summary: Directs the California Department of Technology to develop and implement a research plan on how AI can improve state and local services, including analysis of the risks and benefits of various use cases, best practices from other governments, guidelines for risk management, monitoring, and evaluation, and privacy. Instructs the Department of Technology to submit a report on its findings to the legislature.
Long summary:
Directs the California Department of Technology to develop and implement a research plan on the feasibility and risks of advanced technologies, specifically AI, to improve state and local government services.

Requires the research plan to include an analysis of the potential benefits and risks of using AI chatbots/assistance in various government benefit and welfare programs (such as disability insurance or affordable housing), a review of best practices from other governments, a cost-benefit analysis of implementing AI in government services, recommendations for effective integration (including risk management and monitoring and evaluation guidelines), and recommendations for mitigating privacy issues.

Instructs the Department of Technology to report its findings to the legislature before January 1, 2026.

Official name: An Act regulating the use of artificial intelligence (AI) in providing mental health services.
Casual name: Massachusetts AI in Mental Health Act
Short summary: Requires licensed mental health professionals seeking to offer AI-assisted mental health services to obtain approval from a licensing board, inform patients about AI use, and obtain patient consent; requires AI systems delivering mental health services to prioritize patient safety and well-being. Imposes disciplinary action by a licensing board for violations.
Long summary:
Instructs licensed mental health professionals who wish to provide mental health services through AI to seek approval from a licensing board, inform patients of the use of AI in their treatment, provide non-AI alternatives, and obtain patient consent.

Requires any AI system used to provide mental health services to prioritize the safety and well-being of patients and be subject to continuous monitoring by a licensed mental health professional. 

Establishes disciplinary action by the relevant professional licensing board for mental health professionals who violate the provisions above.

Official name: An act to amend the executive law, in relation to creating the office of  algorithmic innovation
Casual name: NY Office of Algorithmic Innovation Act
Short summary: Establishes an Office of Algorithmic Innovation in New York to promote algorithmic innovation and ensure algorithms are safe, effective, and ethical.
Long summary:
Establishes an Office of Algorithmic Innovation in New York to create policies and set standards that ensure algorithms are safe, effective, and ethical, and that the state is conducive to promoting algorithmic innovation. 

Provides the Office the power to set standards, audit, and establish statewide policy on algorithms used in technology.
Tags: Harms: Violation of civil or human rights, including privacy;Strategies: Evaluation;Strategies: Evaluation: External auditing;Strategies: Governance development;Strategies: New institution

Official name: An act to add Chapter 5.9 (commencing with Section 11549.80) to Part 1 of Division 3 of Title 2 of, the Government Code, relating to state government.
Casual name: California AI-ware Act
Short summary: Establishes California’s Office of Artificial Intelligence to guide state agencies' design, use, and deployment of automated systems in a manner that minimizes bias and promotes privacy and civil liberties. Requires state agencies to inform users when they interact with an AI system, and provide instructions to reach a non-AI contact.
Long summary:
Establishes the Office of Artificial Intelligence in the California Department of Technology, which will guide state agencies' design, use, and deployment of automated systems in a manner that minimizes bias and promotes privacy and civil liberties.  

Requires state agencies to inform users they are interacting with an AI system, and provide instructions on how the user can reach a non-AI representative.

Official name: An act relating to a study on the establishment of a program administered by the Texas Workforce Commission to train individuals in certain skills related to artificial intelligence systems.
Casual name: Texas Workforce Commission Act
Short summary: Requires the Texas House of Representatives committee overseeing workforce development to conduct a study on whether to establish an AI-related workforce training program.
Long summary:
Requires the Texas House of Representatives committee overseeing workforce development to conduct a study on whether to establish a workforce training program, administered by the Texas Workforce Commission, to train individuals currently in the workforce in the use, operation, and creation of AI systems. 

Requires the committee to report the results of the study to the Governor, Lieutenant Governor, Speaker of the House, and corresponding committee in the Senate no later than December 1, 2024.

Official name: A bill to amend the South Carolina Code of Laws by adding Section 63-5-380 so as to prohibit operators of internet-based applications from using "automated decision systems" to place content on social media platforms for users under the age of eighteen who are residents of the State of South Carolina, to require operators to perform age-verification practices for certain users, to establish that a violation is an unfair or deceptive act or practice under the South Carolina Unfair Trade Practices Act, and for other purposes.
Casual name: South Carolina Recommender System Act
Short summary: Prohibits operators of internet-based applications, including social media platforms, from using automated decision-making (ADM) systems to suggest content for users under 18 in South Carolina. Requires operators who use ADM systems for users 18 or older to perform age verification of users; violation is subject to civil penalties.
Long summary:
Prohibits operators of internet-based applications, including social media platforms, from using automated decision-making (ADM) systems to suggest content for users under 18 who are residents of South Carolina.

Requires operators using ADM systems to suggest content for those 18 or older to either 1) Perform age verification of users through an independent third-party service or 2) Require users to upload government-issued identification, a current photograph, and personal information so age can be verified through publicly available records. 

Establishes civil penalties for violations of the above.
Tags: Strategies: Tiering;Incentives: Civil liability;Applications: Broadcasting and media production;Applications: Arts, sports, leisure, travel, and lifestyle

Official name: An act relating to state affairs and government -- commission to monitor the use of artificial intelligence in state government.
Casual name: Rhode Island AI Commission Act
Short summary: Establishes a permanent commission to monitor the use of artificial intelligence in Rhode Island state government and recommend changes to the state’s use of automated decision-making (ADM) systems. Requires the commission to submit an annual, public report detailing their findings.
Long summary:
Establishes a permanent commission to monitor the use of AI in Rhode Island state government and recommend changes to the state’s use of automated decision-making (ADM) systems that may affect human welfare. 

Instructs the commission to continuously survey the state’s use of ADM systems, including identifying gaps in the procurement, evaluation, and ongoing testing of ADM systems, the training given to individuals using these systems, the transparency, explicability, and auditability of ADM systems, identifying and mitigating disparate outcomes due to protected characteristics, and addressing data security and intellectual property concerns. 

Requires the commission to include representatives from multiple state agencies (commerce, business regulation, children, youth, and families, etc.) and four from academic institutions. 

Requires the commission to submit an annual public report to the Governor and state legislature detailing its activities and findings, regulatory or legislative recommendations–specifically where state offices should or should not use automated decision systems, progress implementing previous recommendations, and the extent of algorithmic decision-making used by the state.

Official name: An act creating a temporary state commission to  study  and  investigate how  to regulate artificial intelligence, robotics and automation; and providing for the repeal of such provisions upon expiration thereof
Casual name: Temporary AI Commission Act
Short summary: Establishes a temporary New York state commission to study the impact of AI, robotics, and automation and how to regulate these technologies.
Long summary:
Establishes a temporary New York state commission, composed of 13 members appointed by stakeholders in government and academia, to study the impact of and make determinations on how to regulate AI, robotics, and automation.

Requires the commission to study existing state laws and comparative state regulations for AI, the impact of AI on employment and data privacy, public sector AI applications, and the potential impact of AI regulation on industry. Requires the commission to make determinations regarding criminal and civil liability for AI-enabled violations of law and potential restrictions on AI use in weaponry. 

Requires the commission to issue a final report to the governor and other state leaders before December 31, 2024.

Official name: An act to amend the general business law, in relation to requiring advertisements to disclose the use of synthetic media
Casual name: Synthetic Media Disclosure Act
Short summary: Requires any person engaged in advertising property or services in New York to disclose when synthetic media is used in advertisements.
Long summary:
Requires any person advertising property or services in New York to disclose when synthetic media is used in advertisements, including when synthetic media depicts a natural person, without the person’s consent.

Establishes civil penalties and fines if the above is violated.

Official name: An Act amending Title 18 (Crimes and Offenses) of the Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes, in sexual offenses, providing for the offense of unlawful dissemination of artificially generated depiction; and, in minors, further providing for the offense of sexual abuse of children and for the offense of transmission of sexually explicit images by minor.
Casual name: Creation and Dissemination of Sexually Explicit Content Act
Short summary: Makes it illegal to disseminate deepfake pornography -- lifelike visual content that depicts sexual scenes which did not occur in reality, including images of nudity and sexual acts -- in the state of Pennsylvania.
Long summary:
Proscibes disseminating "deepfake pornography" -- lifelike visual content that depicts sexual scenes which did not occur in reality, including images of nudity and sexual acts -- with the intent to harass, annoy, or alarm the depicted individual as a criminal misdemeanor offense in the state of Pennsylvania.

Exempts cases in which deepfake pornography was disseminated with the consent of the depicted individual from punishment under this law.
Tags: Harms: Detrimental content;Strategies: Performance requirements;Incentives: Criminal liability;Applications: Broadcasting and media production

Official name: An Act requiring the Commissioner of Labor and Workforce Development to conduct a study and issue a report on the impact of artificial intelligence on the growth of the State's economy.
Casual name: New Jersey Bill A168
Short summary: Requires the New Jersey Commissioner of Labor and Workforce Development to conduct a study and issue a report on the impact of AI on the growth of the State’s economy.
Long summary:
Requires the New Jersey Commissioner of Labor and Workforce Development to conduct a study on the impact of AI-powered technology and automation on the growth of the State’s economy. 

Requires the Commissioner to issue a report within a year that addresses the predicted impact of AI on the labor market for highly skilled professionals, the occupations most affected by and new professions that may be created due to expanded use of AI, the impact of AI on labor productivity and economic growth, and the implications for long-term workforce education.

Official name: An Act to amend the general business law, the executive law, the state finance law and the education law, in relation to enacting the "digital fairness act" - Sections 7-9 ("AUTOMATED DECISION SYSTEM IMPACT ASSESSMENTS," etc.)
Casual name: Digital Fairness Act - Sections 7-9 ("AUTOMATED DECISION SYSTEM IMPACT ASSESSMENTS," etc.)
Short summary: Require the state to conduct ADS Impact Assessments before use. Mandate neutral third-party evaluations on impacts. Prohibit ADS purchase/use without publishing the assessment and public comments. Obligate a list of qualified assessors. Demand plain-language notifications and human review mechanisms. Ban payments for non-compliant ADS.
Long summary:
Require the state and its entities to conduct Automated Decision System (ADS) Impact Assessments before using any ADS.

Mandate the engagement of a neutral third party to evaluate the ADS, focusing on statistical impacts on protected classes, privacy, and security.

Prohibit purchasing or using ADS without publishing the assessment and considering public comments.

Obligate the state to maintain a list of qualified neutral third parties for conducting assessments.

Require a comprehensive study on the impacts of ADS, particularly on protected classes, to be published within two years.

Demand plain-language notifications to individuals affected by ADS decisions and provide mechanisms for human review.

Ban payments for ADS that do not use open-source software or comply with assessment and policy requirements.

Official name: New York S7422
Casual name: New York S7422 ("AI Displacing Actors")
Short summary: Exclude film productions using AI, which displaces existing employees, from tax credits provided to "qualified films" under the Empire State Film Production Credit.
Long summary:
Exclude productions using AI that displace employees from being considered a "qualified film" for the Empire State Film Production Credit.

Carves out an exception to the above for films covered by current collective bargaining agreements that allow for AI to displace employees.

Official name: Rhode Island HB 6286
Casual name: Rhode Island HB 6286 ("GenAI Operating Standards")
Short summary: Authorizes the Rhode Island Attorney General's Office to regulate large-scale generative AI models, ensuring that they meet disclosure, informed consent, and data management standards.
Long summary:
Prohibits companies from using large-scale generative AI models to engage in targeted bias and discrimination.

Mandates companies to label outputs created using large-scale generative AI models with a distinctive watermark.

Requires companies to secure informed consent from consumers before using their data for training models.

Requires companies, to the best of the their extent, to protect the security of collected data from individuals and shield their identities.

Requires companies to conduct regular risk assessments that identify and mitigate reasonably foreseeable risks and harms.

Mandates companies using large-scale generative AI models to register them with the Rhode Island Attorney General's Office. 

Requires the Rhode Island Attorney General's Office to maintain a public registry of all companies that have registered under the above stipulation.

Official name: Massachusetts Bill H.64
Casual name: Massachusetts Bill H.64 ("Automated Decision Making Commission")
Short summary: Establishes a commission to study and recommend on ADS use in Massachusetts. Requires surveying ADS uses, consulting experts, and examining research and risks. Compels meetings and public hearings. Mandates annual reports to the governor and legislature.
Long summary:
Establishes a commission within the executive office of technology services and security to study and make recommendations on the use of automated decision systems (ADS) by the Commonwealth of Massachusetts.

Requires the commission to survey all uses of ADS by Massachusetts offices, including procurement, evaluation, training, validation, transparency, and accountability.

Mandates the commission to consult experts on machine learning, algorithmic bias, and civil rights, and to examine research on disparate impacts of ADS based on group characteristics.

Obligates the commission to examine data protection, intellectual property issues, and other opportunities and risks associated with ADS.

Compels the commission to meet at least 10 times a year and hold public hearings for feedback.

Requires the commission to submit an annual report detailing activities, findings, and regulatory recommendations to the governor and state legislature.

Official name: Voluntary Commitments from Leading Artificial Intelligence Companies
Casual name: Voluntary Commitments from Leading Artificial Intelligence Companies
Short summary:
Establishes the commitments of leading artificial intelligence companies to manage risks posed by AI.

Announces the Biden-Harris Administration's development of an executive order and intent to pursue bipartisan legislation in these matters.
Long summary:
Acknowledges the voluntary commitments received from Amazon, Anthropic, Google, Inflection, Meta, Microsoft, and OpenAI to the Biden-Harris Administration to help move toward the safe, secure, and transparent development of AI technology.   

Specifies various actionable commitments that will support three broad goals: ensuring products are safe before public release, building systems that put security first, and earning public trust. Commitments include internal and external security testing, sharing information across the industry and with governments, civil society, and academia, and investing in cybersecurity and insider threat safeguards.

Announces the Biden-Harris Administration's development of an executive order and intent to pursue bipartisan legislation in these matters.

Official name: Massachusetts Bill S. 31
Casual name: Massachusetts Bill S. 31 ("Regulate GenAI")
Short summary: Regulate generative AI models to protect safety, privacy, and IP rights. Define models as those with ‚â•1 billion parameters. Prohibit discriminatory AI use. Mandate AI text watermarks/authentication. Require data security, informed consent, and timely data deletion. Conduct regular risk assessments. Mandate registration with the attorney general, detailing practices. Authorize attorney general to enforce compliance.
Long summary:
Regulate generative AI models, such as ChatGPT, to protect public safety, privacy, and intellectual property rights.

Define "large-scale generative artificial intelligence model" as those with at least one billion parameters.

Prohibit the use of AI models for discrimination or bias based on protected characteristics.

Mandate AI-generated text to contain a distinctive watermark or offer an authentication process to prevent plagiarism.

Require companies to implement reasonable security measures to protect training data.

Obtain informed consent from individuals before collecting, using, or disclosing their data.

Delete or de-identify data when no longer needed for the intended purpose.

Conduct regular risk assessments to identify and mitigate foreseeable risks and harms.

Mandate companies operating large-scale AI models to register with the attorney general within 90 days, including detailed model and data practices.

Authorize the attorney general to adopt regulations and enforce compliance, including bringing legal action for violations.

Official name: Texas HB 4695
Casual name: Texas HB 4695 ("AI and Mental Health")
Short summary: Requires commission approval for AI mental health applications and mandates licensed supervision, informed consent, and clear AI disclosure.
Long summary:
Requires commission approval for AI mental health applications and mandates licensed supervision, informed consent, and clear AI disclosure. Permits liability-free testing with specialized consent. 

Mandates that licensed mental health professionals supervise AI mental health services, ensuring availability for review, communication, and intervention. Obliges providers to obtain informed consent and clearly inform users about the use of AI technology.
Subjects AI mental health service providers to the same disciplinary actions and record-keeping requirements as licensed mental health professionals.

Official name: New York Bill A5309
Casual name: New York Bill A5309 ("Responsible AI Standards")
Short summary: Amends state finance law to require state units purchasing a product containing algorithmic decision systems to purchase only those adhering to responsible AI standards.  Outlines such standards to include harm avoidance, transparency, fairness, and risk evaluation.
Long summary:
Amends the state finance law to require state units to purchase only products or services containing algorithmic decision systems adhering to responsible artificial intelligence standards.

Mandates the avoidance of harm, including minimizing risks of physical or mental injury, unjustified information deletion or disclosure, and unwarranted damage to property, reputation, or the environment.

Emphasizes a commitment to transparency, including full disclosure of the algorithmic decision system's capabilities, limitations, and potential problems.

Prioritizes fairness by eliminating discrimination, promoting equality, and providing feedback mechanisms to redress harms.

Amends the executive law to define "unlawful discriminatory practice" to include actions performed through algorithmic decision systems.

Require the commissioner of taxation and finance to adopt regulations to implement these provisions.

Official name: Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights
Casual name: Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights
Collections: U.S. regulations, executive orders, and agency policies;Editors' Picks
Short summary: Requires pre-deployment testing, risk mitigation, and ongoing monitoring for automated systems' safety and effectiveness. Mandates independent evaluations, public reporting, and avoiding inappropriate data. Demands protections against algorithmic discrimination and data privacy. Institutes oversight on surveillance technologies. Requires clear user notifications and explanations. Insists on transparency and accountability through independent evaluations.
Long summary:
Requires automated systems to undergo pre-deployment testing, risk identification, mitigation, and ongoing monitoring to ensure safety and effectiveness.

Requires that systems avoid the use of inappropriate or irrelevant data and that independent evaluations and public reporting confirm system safety and effectiveness.

Imposes protections against algorithmic discrimination by incorporating proactive equity assessments, representative data usage, and continuous disparity testing and mitigation.

Enforces data privacy through built-in protections, user consent for data collection, and limitations on data use and sharing, particularly in sensitive domains such as health, education, and finance.

Institutes heightened oversight and limits on surveillance technologies to protect civil liberties, privacy, and democratic values, with specific restrictions on continuous surveillance in sensitive contexts.

Requires designers and deployers of automated systems to provide clear, timely, and accessible notice and explanations of system use, including the rationale behind decisions impacting individuals.

Calls for independent evaluations and public reporting to ensure transparency and accountability in the use of automated systems, with detailed documentation and assessments.

Official name: AI Liability Directive
Casual name: AI Liability Directive
Short summary: Establishes rules for disclosure of evidence and burden of proof in civil claims for damages caused by high-risk AI systems. Presumes fault and causal link under specific conditions. Allows national courts to order evidence disclosure and preservation. Requires Member States to implement compatible rules.
Long summary:
Establishes common rules for non-contractual fault-based civil claims related to damages caused by high-risk AI systems.

Requires disclosure of evidence from AI providers or users to substantiate claims, with courts empowered to order such disclosures.

Presumes non-compliance with duty of care if defendants fail to comply with evidence disclosure orders.

Presumes a causal link between defendant’s fault and AI system's output if certain conditions are met, allowing defendants to rebut the presumption.

Permits Member States to adopt more favorable rules for claimants, compatibility with Union law required.

Mandates evaluation and review by the European Commission within five years, considering the necessity of no-fault liability and insurance for AI operators.

Amends Directive (EU) 2020/1828 to include AI Liability Directive.

Obligates Member States to transpose the Directive into national law within two years and communicate relevant national legislative texts to the Commission.

Official name: Australia’s AI Ethics Principles
Casual name: Australia’s AI Ethics Principles
Short summary: Prioritizes AI systems' benefits to individuals, society, and environment. Ensures respect for human rights, fairness, privacy, and data security. Requires transparency, explainability, and mechanisms for contestability. Holds accountable those responsible for AI throughout its lifecycle.
Long summary:
Prioritizes AI systems that benefit individuals, society, and the environment throughout their lifecycle.

Requires AI systems to respect human rights, diversity, and individual autonomy, promoting an equitable society.

Mandates fairness by ensuring AI inclusivity and accessibility, prohibiting unfair discrimination.

Demands privacy protection and data security, with ongoing data governance and vulnerability assessments.

Ensures AI reliability and safety, mandating operation in line with intended purposes and risk management.

Calls for transparency and explainability, necessitating timely and responsible disclosures of AI impacts.

Establishes contestability, allowing challenges to AI impacts on individuals or communities through clear processes.

Holds relevant parties accountable for AI outcomes, requiring identifiable responsibility and human oversight.

Official name: Israel's Policy on Artificial Intelligence: Regulations and Ethics
Casual name: Israel's Policy on Artificial Intelligence: Regulations and Ethics
Short summary: Promotes responsible AI innovation through sector-specific regulation, risk-based approaches, and "soft" regulatory tools. Establishes an AI Policy Coordination Center to advise and coordinate AI regulation, fostering international consistency. Recommends adopting ethical AI principles and creating tools for responsible AI development.
Long summary:
Establishes a governmental policy framework for AI regulation, emphasizing sector-specific regulation and consistency with international approaches.

Adopts a risk-based approach and promotes incremental development and regulatory experimentation.

Encourages the use of "soft" regulatory tools and multistakeholder cooperation.

Recommends adopting ethical AI principles based on the OECD AI Principles, focusing on growth, human-centric AI, equality, transparency, reliability, and accountability.

Proposes the creation of an AI Policy Coordination Center to advise regulators, promote inter-agency coordination, and lead international representation.

Mandates mapping AI uses and associated challenges in regulated sectors.

Calls for the establishment of forums for regulators and public participation to ensure coordination and open discussions.

Urges active involvement in international forums to develop AI regulation and standards.

Advocates developing tools for responsible AI, including a risk management framework.

Official name: Safe and Responsible AI in Australia Consultation, Interim Response
Casual name: Safe and Responsible AI in Australia Consultation, Interim Response
Short summary: Considers mandatory safety guardrails for high-risk AI applications. Plans to consult on regulatory frameworks, including testing and transparency. Collaborates internationally on AI safety. Focuses on balancing innovation with risk management, enhancing privacy laws, and addressing misinformation.
Long summary:
Proposes strengthening existing laws and considering new legislation to address AI-related risks, particularly in high-risk settings.

Considers mandatory safety guardrails for AI systems in high-risk contexts, focusing on testing, transparency, and accountability.

Plans to work with industry to develop a voluntary AI Safety Standard and explore options for voluntary labelling and watermarking of AI-generated materials.

Emphasizes a risk-based regulatory framework to ensure proportionality and avoid unnecessary burdens on low-risk AI applications.

Commits to collaborating with international partners, aligning with global standards, and contributing to initiatives like the Bletchley Declaration on AI safety.

Supports investments in AI capability development and aims to maximize AI benefits, including through initiatives funded by the 2023–24 Budget.

Establishes an interim expert advisory group to explore AI guardrail options and harmonizes efforts with state and territory governments.

Official name: Commission Decision Establishing the European AI Office
Casual name: Commission Decision Establishing the European AI Office
Short summary: Establishes the European Artificial Intelligence Office to implement and enforce forthcoming AI regulations. Tasks include developing evaluation tools, monitoring AI risks, supporting international cooperation, fostering innovation, and coordinating with stakeholders. Operates under the Directorate-General for Communication Networks, Content and Technology.
Long summary:
Establishes the European Artificial Intelligence Office within the Directorate-General for Communication Networks, Content and Technology to oversee AI advancements and enforce the forthcoming Regulation on AI.

Mandates the Office to develop tools and methodologies for evaluating general-purpose AI models, monitor AI market evolution, and investigate potential regulation infringements.

Requires collaboration with stakeholders, including the scientific community and AI developers, to advance best practices and codes of conduct.

Coordinates with national authorities and EU bodies to ensure uniform application and enforcement of AI regulations.

Supports international cooperation and the promotion of the EU's approach to trustworthy AI.

Tasks the Office with assisting the Commission in preparing relevant decisions and facilitating the uniform application of AI regulations.

Finances the Office through redeployment of resources from the Directorate-General and the Digital Europe Programme. 

Enters into force on 21 February 2024.

Official name: The Bletchley Declaration by Countries Attending the AI Safety Summit
Casual name: The Bletchley Declaration by Countries Attending the AI Safety Summit
Short summary: Affirms the need for safe, human-centric AI, addressing risks through international cooperation. Emphasizes responsibility for frontier AI safety, transparency, and accountability. Encourages inclusive dialogue and research to maximize AI benefits while mitigating risks. Promotes a risk-based governance approach.
Long summary:
Affirms the need for AI to be developed and used safely, human-centrically, and responsibly.

Recognizes AI's transformative potential in public sectors like health and education while addressing risks such as disinformation and misuse.

Welcomes international cooperation to tackle AI risks, emphasizing transparency, fairness, accountability, and human rights protection.

Identifies particular risks with frontier AI, including potential catastrophic harm and manipulation of content.

Resolves to intensify cooperation on AI safety, especially regarding frontier AI, through international collaboration and future AI Safety Summits.

Encourages actors, including nations and companies, to ensure AI safety through testing and monitoring harmful capabilities.

Calls for building risk-based policies and enhancing AI capacity in developing countries.

Supports an international network of scientific research on AI safety and sustains a global dialogue to harness AI benefits responsibly.

Commits to further meetings and cooperation to address AI governance, involving countries like the US, EU, China, and others.

Official name: Initial advice on Generative Artificial Intelligence in the public service (New Zealand)
Casual name: Initial advice on Generative Artificial Intelligence in the public service (New Zealand)
Short summary: Advises New Zealand Public Service on cautious use of GenAI, emphasizing data privacy, security, and ethical considerations. Prohibits using GenAI with sensitive or personal data externally. Stresses governance, transparency, and collaboration, especially concerning Māori data and interests.
Long summary:
Prohibits the use of GenAI for data classified as SENSITIVE or higher, and personal information should not be input into external GenAI tools.

Recommends agencies avoid using GenAI for business-critical information, systems, or public-facing channels due to risks of inaccuracy and bias.

Advises against using GenAI as Shadow IT, emphasizing the need for robust governance and accountability in AI application.

Encourages agencies to develop GenAI policies, ensuring ethical use, and promoting transparency and accountability.

Emphasizes the importance of considering privacy risks, recommending privacy impact assessments and engagement with Māori communities regarding Māori data.

Warns of security risks, including insecure code generation and data spills, advising strong cybersecurity practices.

Urges agencies to remain transparent about GenAI usage, ensure human oversight, and validate AI outputs for accuracy.

Suggests using sandboxes for testing GenAI safely and advises on procurement principles to mitigate vendor-related risks. 

This guidance serves as an interim measure while longer-term AI regulatory frameworks are developed.

Official name: TTC Joint Roadmap on Evaluation and Measurement Tools for Trustworthy AI and Risk Management
Casual name: TTC Joint Roadmap on Evaluation and Measurement Tools for Trustworthy AI and Risk Management
Short summary: Establishes a joint roadmap by the U.S. and EU for AI risk management and trustworthy AI development. Advances shared terminologies, international standardization, and tools for monitoring AI risks. Promotes stakeholder engagement and cooperation on AI governance, standards, and methodologies.
Long summary:
Establishes a U.S.-EU Joint Roadmap to advance trustworthy AI and risk management, promoting shared democratic values and human rights.

Encourages risk-based approaches, aligning EU and U.S. methodologies, including the EU AI Act and the U.S. AI Bill of Rights.

Advances shared terminologies and taxonomies to foster interoperability and consistent application of AI risk management concepts.

Promotes leadership and cooperation in international technical standards, supporting transparency, inclusiveness, and innovation.

Develops a shared repository for metrics and methodologies to assess AI trustworthiness and risk management tools.

Analyzes existing tools to identify gaps and support AI standards development.

Implements mechanisms to monitor and measure existing and emerging AI risks, providing a tracker and interoperable tests.

Establishes expert working groups and consultation plans to advance short-term and long-term objectives in AI governance.

Official name: Maintaining American Leadership in Artificial Intelligence (EO 13859)
Casual name: Maintaining American Leadership in Artificial Intelligence (EO 13859)
Collections: U.S. regulations, executive orders, and agency policies
Short summary: Establishes the American AI Initiative to maintain U.S. leadership in AI, emphasizing R&D investment, workforce training, international collaboration, and requires federal agencies to pursue and promote AI innovation through the development of regulatory guidance.
Long summary:
Mandates the Federal Government to maintain U.S. leadership in AI through a coordinated strategy known as the American AI Initiative.

Requires agencies to pursue strategic guidelines to prioritize AI R&D, enhance data accessibility, and reduce barriers for AI deployment, ensuring protection of civil liberties and privacy.

Obligates agencies to develop technical standards to minimize vulnerabilities and foster public trust in AI systems.

Directs the prioritization and training of American workers in AI-related skills to prepare them for future job markets.

Instructs the development of international standards and promotes global collaboration while safeguarding critical AI technologies from foreign adversaries.

Develops an action plan to protect U.S. advantages in AI technology against strategic competitors and adversaries.

Official name: National Artificial Intelligence Research and Development Strategic Plan 2023 Update
Casual name: National Artificial Intelligence Research and Development Strategic Plan 2023 Update
Collections: U.S. regulations, executive orders, and agency policies
Short summary: Updates the National AI R&D Strategic Plan to prioritize responsible AI research, human-AI collaboration, ethical considerations, AI safety, shared datasets, and workforce development. Expands public-private partnerships and international collaboration to advance equitable and trustworthy AI systems.
Long summary:
Requires the federal government to coordinate and focus R&D investments in AI to ensure U.S. leadership in trustworthy AI systems. 

Reaffirms eight strategies and adds a ninth to emphasize international collaboration in AI research.

Prioritizes investments in fundamental and responsible AI research, human-AI collaboration, and understanding ethical, legal, and societal implications.

Advances safety and security standards for AI systems, ensuring they are reliable and secure from vulnerabilities.

Develops shared public datasets and environments for AI training and testing to democratize access to AI resources.

Establishes benchmarks and standards for evaluating AI systems, including fairness, privacy, and bias considerations.

Addresses workforce needs by training and retraining a diverse AI-ready workforce.

Expands public-private partnerships to accelerate AI advances and facilitate global collaborations for responsible AI development.

Emphasizes the importance of AI governance and the development of international guidelines and standards for AI systems.

Official name: Section 106.11 Application: Nondiscrimination on the Basis of Sex in Education Programs or Activities Receiving Federal Financial Assistance
Casual name: Section 106.11 Application: Nondiscrimination on the Basis of Sex in Education Programs or Activities Receiving Federal Financial Assistance
Collections: U.S. regulations, executive orders, and agency policies
Short summary: Declines to expand examples of conduct under sex-based harassment regulations to include AI technologies, but reinforces responsibility for conduct within a recipient's disciplinary authority, including off-campus and online activities. Clarifies Title IX obligations for addressing hostile environments linked to education programs.
Long summary:
Requires recipients to address sex-based harassment within their education programs or activities, including off-campus and remote settings, aligning with recent court decisions.

Clarifies that institutions are responsible for discrimination within contexts they control, such as AI technologies used in educational settings.

Acknowledges requests for examples involving AI and other specific circumstances but declines to provide additional examples or exhaustive lists.

Confirms obligations for conduct during institution-sponsored events, online classes, and use of digital platforms, including AI technologies.

Deletes references to sex-based harassment occurring outside the U.S. but maintains the obligation to address contributing conduct external to the program or activity.

Official name: Part 170: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: Certification Program Updates, Algorithm Transparency, and Information Sharing
Casual name: Part 170: Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: Certification Program Updates, Algorithm Transparency, and Information Sharing
Authority: Department of Health and Human Services
Collections: U.S. regulations, executive orders, and agency policies
Short summary: Adds definitions for "Predictive Decision Support Intervention" (Predictive DSI) involving AI algorithms for healthcare decision-making. Requires certification and configuration of AI-based decision support systems, ensuring fairness, transparency, risk management, and external validation processes. Establishes standards for data interoperability and API access.
Long summary:
Amends health IT certification criteria to include "Predictive Decision Support Intervention," addressing decision-making support based on algorithms for predictions, classifications, and recommendations.

Mandates that health IT systems enable recording of patient demographics, including race, ethnicity, sex, sexual orientation, gender identity, and pronouns, using specified standards.

Requires health IT modules to be updated to meet new certification criteria and provide updates to customers as per defined dates.

Outlines certification criteria for health IT, including decision support interventions that must be evidence-based and use specified data standards.

Establishes risk management practices for predictive decision support interventions, including risk analysis, mitigation, and governance.

Incorporates by reference various health information standards, including HL7, LOINC, and SNOMED CT, to ensure interoperability and accuracy in electronic health information exchange.

Official name: Train Crew Size Safety Requirements (New Technology and Automated Operations)
Casual name: Train Crew Size Safety Requirements (New Technology and Automated Operations)
Collections: U.S. regulations, executive orders, and agency policies
Short summary: Allows railroads to petition for rulemaking or waivers to use non-compliant rail automation technology. Requires safety justification for autonomous operations with fewer crew members. Supports technological advancements in rail safety and automation through research and funding.
Long summary:
Require railroads seeking to use automation technology non-compliant with FRA safety regulations to file a petition for rulemaking or a waiver.

Mandate a risk assessment before initiating operations under special approval processes.

Acknowledge freight rail industry's concerns about competition with automated trucking but emphasize rail safety hazards from reduced crew sizes.

Disagree with assertions that labor-saving technologies like PTC justify removal of crew members, stating such technologies are not fully developed.

Support technological advancement in rail through research on AI and automation, including drones and real-time safety systems.

Permit railroads to petition for rulemaking or waivers to use technology that doesn't meet existing regulatory requirements, ensuring safety justifications are provided.

Clarify that the rule does not impede innovation but requires description of technology used to support tasks typically performed by a second crew member.

Official name: Consensus Statement on AI Safety as a Global Public Good
Casual name: Consensus Statement on AI Safety as a Global Public Good (IDAIS)
Short summary: Recognizes AI safety as a global public good, urging international cooperation on governance. Calls on states to encourage AI Safety Institutes, emergency preparedness, a Safety Assurance Framework, and independent verification research. Emphasizes global coordination, independent audits, and comprehensive verification of AI safety claims.
Long summary:
Recognizes AI safety as a global public good, urging global governance to mitigate risks from advanced AI systems.

Encourages states and AI developers to commit to foundational principles for responsible AI development, fostering cooperation at intergovernmental summits.

Calls for states to establish and resource AI Safety Institutes to advance testing, research, and standards-setting.

Urges development of domestic authorities to detect and respond to AI incidents, coordinating globally for contingency planning.

Proposes Emergency Preparedness Agreements and Institutions for model registration, incident reporting, and contingency plans.

Establishes a Safety Assurance Framework requiring developers to demonstrate AI safety before deployment.

Calls for post-deployment monitoring and independent audits to ensure AI systems' safety.

Calls for independent global AI safety and verification research, funded by a diverse range of stakeholders.

Encourages international collaboration to develop verification methods, enabling credible safety claims and fostering trust.

Official name: Consensus Statement on Red Lines in Artificial Intelligence
Casual name: Consensus Statement on Red Lines in Artificial Intelligence (IDAIS)
Short summary: Encourages governance development through domestic regulations and international collaboration to enforce red lines and ensure AI safety.
Long summary:
Proposes red lines in AI development and governance development as an international coordination mechanism to prevent catastrophic risks.

Prohibits AI systems from self-replication or improvement without human approval.

Restricts AI from taking actions to increase its power or influence unduly.

Forbids AI systems from aiding in the development of weapons of mass destruction.

Prohibits AI from autonomously executing cyberattacks causing significant harm.

Prevents AI from deceiving designers or regulators about its capabilities.

Calls for comprehensive governance regimes to prevent crossing of red lines.

Requires domestic registration of advanced AI models for government oversight.

Conditions global market access on compliance with internationally aligned regulations.

Encourages multilateral institutions to govern AGI development safely.

Mandates developers demonstrate adherence to red lines through evaluations and proofs.

Urges international collaboration on AI safety research and funding. 

Emphasizes decisive action combining technical research and governance to mitigate risks.

Official name: IDAIS-Oxford Statement (2023)
Casual name: IDAIS-Oxford Statement (2023)
Short summary: Urges global cooperation between governments and AI developers to manage AI risks through the implementation of mandatory registration, third-party audits for advanced AI models, and immediate shutdown procedures for unsafe AI. Calls on AI developers and government agencies to increase investments into AI safety and governance research.
Long summary:
Encourages governments worldwide to develop measures to prevent worst-case AI outcomes from malicious or careless actors and manage AI safety risks.

Recommends mandatory registration for creating, selling, or using AI models above a certain capability threshold to enhance government visibility into emerging risks.

Encourages governments to implement requirements to monitor large-scale data centers, track AI incidents, and mandate independent third-party audits for AI developers concerning information security and model safety.

Proposes defining clear red lines that, if crossed, mandate immediate termination of an AI system through rapid and safe shut-down procedures.

Urges international cooperation to establish and maintain the capacity to shut down AI systems crossing red lines.

Calls for AI developers to demonstrate to regulators that their systems will not cross defined red lines before deployment.

Advocates for substantial research progress to ensure advanced AI alignment with designers' intent and robustness against malicious actors.

Encourages a global network of AI safety research and governance institutions, with leading developers committing significant resources to AI safety.

Official name: Fraudulent Misrepresentation of Campaign Authority
Casual name: Fraudulent Misrepresentation of Campaign Authority
Collections: U.S. regulations, executive orders, and agency policies
Short summary: Clarifies that 52 U.S.C. 30124 and 11 CFR 110.16 apply to fraudulent misrepresentation using AI. States that the statute is technology-neutral, covering fraud via AI-generated content or other means. Emphasizes non-binding nature of this interpretive guidance.
Long summary:
Clarifies that 52 U.S.C. 30124 and 11 CFR 110.16 apply to fraudulent misrepresentation in federal election campaigns, regardless of the technology used, including AI.

Emphasizes that the statute is technology neutral and covers AI-assisted media, forged signatures, and other means of fraudulent activity.

States that the legal focus is on whether individuals fraudulently represent themselves as acting for a candidate or political party.

Announces that this interpretation will guide the Commission's future actions concerning evolving technologies.

Specifies that this interpretive rule does not require notice and comment procedures, nor does it bind the Commission or public.

Excludes the applicability of the Regulatory Flexibility Act, as the interpretive rule does not involve binding agency action.

Official name: Public Act 103-0804
Casual name: Illinois Public Act 103-0804
Short summary: Prohibits employers in Illinois from using AI in employment practices that result in discrimination based on protected classes. Requires employers to notify employees when AI is used for employment decisions. Mandates rule adoption for implementation and enforcement. Effective January 1, 2026.
Long summary:
Amends the Illinois Human Rights Act to regulate the use of artificial intelligence (AI) in employment-related decisions.

Prohibits employers from using AI in recruitment, hiring, promotion, or other employment practices if it results in discrimination based on protected classes.

Requires employers to notify employees if AI is used in employment decisions.

Mandates the Illinois Department of Human Rights to adopt rules for implementing these AI-related provisions, including specifying conditions and timelines for providing notice.

Defines "artificial intelligence" as a machine-based system generating outputs like predictions or decisions that can influence environments.

Includes "generative artificial intelligence" as systems producing content simulating human-produced content based on prompts.

Takes effect on January 1, 2026.

Official name: Artificial Intelligence Law of the People’s Republic of China (Draft for Suggestions from Scholars)
Casual name: Artificial Intelligence Law of the People’s Republic of China (Scholars' Draft)
Short summary: Outlines key principles and aims for innovative and safe AI development use in the public and private sector. Details compliance and supervision mechanisms, with special attention to critical AI. Encourages international cooperation and imposes penalties for non-compliance measures.
Long summary:
Mandates the regulation of AI development and use activities within and outside China if they affect Chinese national interests.

Establishes principles of ethics, fairness, transparency, safety, and accountability in AI development and use.

Establishes aims and support measures for AI adoption, infrastructure development and supports data sharing, compute resources, and algorithmic innovation.

Protects intellectual property laws, user privacy rights and encourages applications of energy efficient AI products.

Implements a comprehensive oversight and coordination framework involving multiple government agencies. Mandate compliance with safety and security assessments, especially for critical AI systems.

Provides legal liability structures for violations, including administrative penalties, tort liability, and public interest litigation.

Promotes international cooperation in AI governance and allows for countermeasures against discriminatory foreign actions.

Official name: Digital Charter Implementation Act 2022, Part 3 ("Artificial Intelligence and Data Act")
Casual name: Digital Charter Implementation Act 2022, Part 3 ("Artificial Intelligence and Data Act")
Short summary: Regulates AI systems by establishing design, development, and use requirements to prevent harm and discrimination. Requires risk assessment, mitigation, and record-keeping for high-impact systems. Imposes penalties for non-compliance and mandates ministerial oversight and public disclosure of certain AI system information.
Long summary:
Regulates international and interprovincial trade in AI systems by establishing common requirements for design, development, and use.

Prohibits conduct related to AI systems that may cause serious harm to individuals or their interests.

Defines a "high-impact system" as an AI system meeting criteria established by regulations, requiring special assessments and risk mitigation measures.

Mandates responsible persons to assess AI systems, establish risk mitigation measures, and keep records, particularly for high-impact systems.

Requires publication of system descriptions and risk mitigation measures on publicly available websites for high-impact systems.

Obligates notification to the Minister if a high-impact system causes or likely causes material harm.

Empowers the Minister to order audits, require implementation of measures, and impose administrative penalties for non-compliance.

Prohibits possession or use of personal information for AI purposes if obtained unlawfully.

Imposes offenses for making AI systems available that cause harm or economic loss with severe penalties.
